## Agent 6 — Path C theorem stack and failure modes

I am answering the Agent 6 prompt: compare Stack A, C1, C2, C3 and recommend the next proof architecture. The coordinator’s current fork decision is that Path A is counter-random, Path B breaks scalar downstream compatibility, and Path C is the preferred direction: aggregate canonical Gate–1 plus good-subset downstream extraction. 

# 1. The four stacks

## Stack A — pointwise scalar route

\[
\boxed{
A+B+\mathrm{O1b}_{\rm frame}+\mathrm{Downstream}_{\rm pointwise}
}
\]

Dependencies:

\[
A:
\quad
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\Longrightarrow
\|T_{\can}X(m_0,\alpha)\|
\ge
Q^{-A_1}\|X(m_0,\alpha)\|.
\]

\[
B:
\quad
\inf_{(c,P)\ne0}
\left\|
\left(
L_{m_0,\alpha}((c+mP)x^k)
\right)_{0\le k\le R+1}
\right\|
\ge
Q^{-A_2}(|c|+\|P\|).
\]

\[
\mathrm{O1b}_{\rm frame}:
\quad
2M\le R+2
\quad
+
\quad
\text{cell-symbol bridge}.
\]

Then the repaired pointwise downstream chain runs:

\[
\text{Gate--1}
\Longrightarrow
\text{Gate--2}
\Longrightarrow
\text{wall escape}
\Longrightarrow
\text{D4}
\Longrightarrow
\text{row energy}.
\]

### First failure mode

\[
\boxed{
A\text{ is counter-random.}
}
\]

It asks a dimension-\(R\) projection to see every refined vector in dimension \(\asymp Q/\log Q\) at all admissible zeros. This is the strongest and least plausible stack.

---

## Stack C1 — aggregate Gate–1, pointwise O2/O3

\[
\boxed{
C_{\rm G1}+B_{\rm pointwise}+\mathrm{O1b}_{\rm frame}
+\mathrm{GoodSubsetDownstream}
}
\]

Dependencies:

\[
C_{\rm G1}:
\quad
\sum_{m_0\in\mathcal C_{\rm ord}}
\rho(m_0)\|T_{\can}X(m_0)\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A_1}
\sum_{m_0\in\mathcal C_{\rm ord}}
\rho(m_0)\|X(m_0)\|^2.
\]

Good-subset extraction:

\[
\mathcal G_1
=
\left\{
m_0:
\|T_{\can}X(m_0)\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A_1'}\|X(m_0)\|^2
\right\},
\]

with

\[
\sum_{\mathcal G_1}\rho(m_0)\|X(m_0)\|^2
\ge
Q^{-C}
\sum_{\mathcal C_{\rm ord}}\rho(m_0)\|X(m_0)\|^2.
\]

Pointwise O2/O3 still required on \(\mathcal G_1\):

\[
B_{\rm pointwise}(m_0)
\quad
\text{for every }m_0\in\mathcal G_1.
\]

Then downstream runs on \(\mathcal G_1\).

### First failure mode

\[
\boxed{
B_{\rm pointwise}\text{ remains a hard pointwise arithmetic input.}
}
\]

C1 avoids the worst pointwise Gate–1 projection theorem, but it still requires pointwise augmented complex-frame nondegeneracy on all extracted good centers.

C1 is strictly better than A but still has a pointwise bottleneck.

---

## Stack C2 — aggregate Gate–1 + aggregate O2/O3

\[
\boxed{
C_{\rm G1}+C_{\rm O23}
+\mathrm{GoodSubsetIntersection}
+\mathrm{O1b}_{\rm frame}
+\mathrm{GoodSubsetDownstream}
}
\]

Dependencies:

Aggregate Gate–1:

\[
\sum_{\mathcal C}
\rho(m_0)\|T_{\can}X(m_0)\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A_1}
\sum_{\mathcal C}
\rho(m_0)\|X(m_0)\|^2.
\]

Aggregate O2/O3:

Let

\[
\sigma_{\rm aug}(m_0)
=
\sigma_{\min}
\left[
(c,P)\mapsto
\left(
L_{m_0}((c+mP)x^k)
\right)_{0\le k\le R+1}
\right].
\]

Assume

\[
\sum_{\mathcal C}
\rho(m_0)\sigma_{\rm aug}(m_0)^2
\ge
Q^{-A_2}
\sum_{\mathcal C}\rho(m_0).
\]

With polynomial upper bounds

\[
\|T_{\can}X(m_0)\|^2/\|X(m_0)\|^2\le Q^C,
\]

\[
\sigma_{\rm aug}(m_0)^2\le Q^C,
\]

extract

\[
\mathcal G
=
\mathcal G_1\cap\mathcal G_{23},
\]

where both Gate–1 and O2/O3 hold pointwise at polynomial scale.

Then run downstream on \(\mathcal G\).

### First failure mode

The first possible failure is **good-subset intersection**.

Two separate aggregate lower bounds do not automatically guarantee that the same centers are good for both unless the extraction is done with compatible weights. The correct weighted extraction must use one common measure, for example

\[
d\mu(m_0)
=
\rho(m_0)\|X(m_0)\|^2
\]

or a normalized common variant.

If Gate–1 is weighted by \(\rho\|X\|^2\) but O2/O3 is weighted by \(\rho\), the two good subsets can be disjoint. Thus C2 needs a common weighted formulation:

\[
\sum \rho \|X\|^2 E_1 \ge Q^{-A}\sum \rho\|X\|^2,
\]

\[
\sum \rho \|X\|^2 E_{23} \ge Q^{-A}\sum \rho\|X\|^2.
\]

Then intersection extraction works with polynomial loss.

### Status

\[
\boxed{
C2\text{ is the weakest plausible stack preserving scalar downstream, provided common-weight extraction works.}
}
\]

---

## Stack C3 — aggregate all branches

\[
\boxed{
C_{\rm ord}+C_{\rm tr}+C_{\rm sing}
+C_{\rm O23}
+\mathrm{BranchGoodSubsetDownstream}
+\mathrm{AggregateRowContradiction}
}
\]

Dependencies:

Ordinary aggregate:

\[
\sum_{\mathcal C_{\rm ord}}
\rho\|T_{\can}X\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{\mathcal C_{\rm ord}}\rho\|X\|^2.
\]

Transition aggregate:

\[
\sum_{\mathcal C_{\rm tr}}
\rho\|\mathcal J_R^{\rm tr}(m_0)\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{\mathcal C_{\rm tr}}\rho.
\]

Singular aggregate:

\[
\sum_{\mathcal C_{\rm sing}}
\rho\|\mathcal S_R(m_0)\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{\mathcal C_{\rm sing}}\rho.
\]

Aggregate O2/O3 on whichever branch requires the ordinary Wronskian/cofactor mechanism, or branch-specific replacements in transition/singular regimes.

Then extract branch-specific good subsets:

\[
\mathcal G_{\rm ord},\quad
\mathcal G_{\rm tr},\quad
\mathcal G_{\rm sing}.
\]

Run branch-aware downstream and sum row energy over all good subsets.

### First failure mode

\[
\boxed{
\text{branch-aware downstream compatibility.}
}
\]

Ordinary D4 is Green for the scalar canonical source \(u=q''_{\can}\). But transition and singular packets may not enter the exact same ordinary D4 coordinate chart. C3 therefore needs branch-specific Gate–2/wall-escape/D4 or a transfer theorem from transition/singular good packets back to the ordinary canonical downstream chart.

C3 is most complete, but it is not minimal.

---

# 2. Logical implications

\[
A\Longrightarrow C_{\rm G1}.
\]

Pointwise Gate–1 implies aggregate Gate–1.

\[
C_{\rm G1}\not\Longrightarrow A.
\]

Aggregate Gate–1 gives pointwise Gate–1 only on a polynomial-weight good subset.

\[
B_{\rm pointwise}\Longrightarrow C_{\rm O23}.
\]

Pointwise augmented O2/O3 implies aggregate augmented O2/O3.

\[
C_{\rm O23}\not\Longrightarrow B_{\rm pointwise}.
\]

Aggregate O2/O3 gives pointwise O2/O3 only after good-subset extraction.

\[
C2\Longrightarrow C1
\]

only after extracting a good subset where O2/O3 becomes pointwise. Without the intersection lemma, this implication fails.

\[
C3\Longrightarrow C2
\]

only on the ordinary branch. C3 contains extra singular/transition machinery and is stronger architecturally.

---

# 3. Weakest sufficient stack

For preserving the repaired **canonical scalar downstream**, the weakest sufficient stack is:

\[
\boxed{
C2:
\quad
\text{aggregate canonical Gate--1}
+
\text{aggregate augmented O2/O3}
+
\text{common-weight good-subset intersection}
+
\text{good-subset downstream}.
}
\]

In expanded form:

\[
\boxed{
\begin{gathered}
\sum_{\mathcal C}
d\mu(m_0)\,E_1(m_0)
\ge Q^{-A_1}\mu(\mathcal C),\\
\sum_{\mathcal C}
d\mu(m_0)\,E_{23}(m_0)
\ge Q^{-A_2}\mu(\mathcal C),\\
E_1,E_{23}\le Q^C,\\
\Longrightarrow
\exists\mathcal G\subset\mathcal C
\text{ with }
\mu(\mathcal G)\ge Q^{-C'}\mu(\mathcal C),\\
E_1(m_0)\ge Q^{-A_1'},\quad
E_{23}(m_0)\ge Q^{-A_2'}
\quad
(m_0\in\mathcal G),\\
\text{and downstream runs on }\mathcal G
\text{ with bounded bad-wall loss and bounded row-mobility overlap.}
\end{gathered}
}
\]

C2 is weaker than A and C1 because it avoids both pointwise Gate–1 and pointwise O2/O3 as initial assumptions.

---

# 4. Most plausible stack under heuristics

\[
\boxed{
C2\text{ is also the most plausible near-term stack.}
}
\]

Reason:

- Aggregate Gate–1 resembles a lower large-sieve / second-moment lower bound.
- Aggregate O2/O3 is still hard, but more plausible than pointwise augmented-frame nondegeneracy.
- It keeps the canonical scalar source \(u=q''_{\can}\), so D4 remains usable.
- It avoids rebuilding downstream for arbitrary source families.

C3 is plausible as a full coverage architecture, but it introduces transition/singular downstream compatibility questions. It should be treated as a second phase after C2 is proved for the ordinary branch.

---

# 5. Exact first failure modes

## Stack A

First failure:

\[
\boxed{
\text{pointwise scalar projection theorem }A.
}
\]

It is counter-random because \(\dim T_{\can}E_Q\ll \dim E_Q\).

## Stack C1

First failure:

\[
\boxed{
\text{pointwise O2/O3 augmented frame on the extracted good subset.}
}
\]

Gate–1 is made aggregate, but O2/O3 remains pointwise.

## Stack C2

First failure:

\[
\boxed{
\text{common-weight good-subset intersection or aggregate O2/O3 lower frame.}
}
\]

If both aggregate estimates use the same weight and polynomial upper bounds, extraction is formal. Then the first real mathematical input is the aggregate O2/O3 lower-frame theorem.

## Stack C3

First failure:

\[
\boxed{
\text{branch-aware downstream transfer for transition/singular good subsets.}
}
\]

Ordinary branch uses repaired scalar D4. Transition/singular need either transfer to ordinary scalar source or branch-specific downstream replacement.

---

# 6. Recommended next architecture

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Choose Stack C2 for the next proof attempt.}
}
\]

The target theorem stack should be:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{AggG1}_{\can}
+
\mathrm{AggFrame}_{23}
+
\mathrm{GoodSubsetIntersection}
+
\mathrm{GoodSubsetDownstream}.
}
\]

Where:

\[
\mathrm{AggG1}_{\can}:
\quad
\sum d\mu\,E_1\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C),
\]

\[
\mathrm{AggFrame}_{23}:
\quad
\sum d\mu\,E_{23}\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C),
\]

\[
\mathrm{GoodSubsetIntersection}:
\quad
E_1,E_{23}\le Q^C
\Longrightarrow
\exists\mathcal G,\ 
\mu(\mathcal G)\ge Q^{-C}\mu(\mathcal C),
\quad
E_1,E_{23}\ge Q^{-C}
\text{ on }\mathcal G,
\]

\[
\mathrm{GoodSubsetDownstream}:
\quad
\mathcal G
\Longrightarrow
\sum_{\text{row targets}}
\mathrm{RowEnergy}
\ge
Q^{-C}\mu(\mathcal G).
\]

This is the least counter-random path that still preserves the repaired scalar canonical downstream engine.

C1 is acceptable as an interim fallback if aggregate O2/O3 is too hard, but C2 is the correct strategic target.

## Agent 6 — Good-subset downstream audit for Stack C2

The assigned task is to test the downstream part of Stack C2: assume a polynomial-weight subset \(\mathcal G\) where pointwise canonical Gate–1 and pointwise augmented O2/O3 both hold, then determine whether the repaired downstream engine can run on \(\mathcal G\) and be summed. 

# Result

\[
\boxed{
\text{Good-subset downstream is viable, but only under two additional aggregate routing controls.}
}
\]

The local downstream chain is uniform:

\[
\text{Gate--1}+\text{O2/O3}
\Longrightarrow
\text{Gate--2}
\Longrightarrow
\text{D4 row-energy certificate}.
\]

The two nonlocal controls still needed are:

\[
\boxed{
\text{bad-wall / bad-Gram weighted loss control}
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\text{bounded-overlap, noncancelling row mobility.}
}
\]

Without those, Stack C2 can still fail after good-subset extraction.

---

# 1. Setup

Assume a finite routed set \(\mathcal C\) with weight

\[
d\mu(m)=\rho(m)\,dm
\]

or simply discrete weight \(\mu(m)=\rho(m)\).

Assume extraction gives a subset

\[
\mathcal G\subset\mathcal C
\]

with

\[
\mu(\mathcal G)\ge Q^{-A_0}\mu(\mathcal C),
\]

and for every \(m\in\mathcal G\),

\[
E_1(m)\ge Q^{-A_1},
\]

\[
E_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-A_{23}}.
\]

Here \(E_1\) is canonical Gate–1 energy for

\[
u=q''_{\can},
\]

and \(E_{23}\) is the augmented O2/O3 complex-frame energy.

---

# 2. Gate 2 on the good subset

The Gate–1 \(\Rightarrow\) Gate–2 step is local. Therefore it can be applied pointwise on \(\mathcal G\), provided every \(m\in\mathcal G\) satisfies the ordinary local-chart hypotheses:

\[
\lambda Q\asymp 1,
\]

\[
Q^{-C}\le g_{\rm Gr}(m),h_{\rm Gr}(m)\le Q^C,
\]

stable cell frame,

canonical transfer for

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2,
\]

and the \(L^2\)-input real-rigidity hypotheses.

Then there is a uniform implication

\[
m\in\mathcal G
\Longrightarrow
E_2(m)\ge Q^{-A_2}.
\]

So the local Gate–2 theorem sums immediately:

\[
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}\mu(m)E_2(m)
\ge
Q^{-A_2}\mu(\mathcal G).
\]

No new obstruction appears here, assuming the local hypotheses are already part of the routing definition.

---

# 3. Bad-wall / bad-Gram / gauge loss

This is the first genuine downstream risk.

Define

\[
\mathcal G_{\rm bad}
=
\{m\in\mathcal G:
m\text{ is Gram-bad, gauge-bad, wall-stuck, or D4-chart-bad}\}.
\]

For Stack C2, one needs either a deletion theorem

\[
\boxed{
\mu(\mathcal G_{\rm bad})
\le
(1-Q^{-C})\mu(\mathcal G)
}
\]

or, better,

\[
\boxed{
\mu(\mathcal G_{\rm bad})
\le
\frac12\mu(\mathcal G).
}
\]

Then

\[
\mathcal G_{\rm good}:=\mathcal G\setminus\mathcal G_{\rm bad}
\]

has

\[
\mu(\mathcal G_{\rm good})
\ge
Q^{-C}\mu(\mathcal G).
\]

A more flexible version is an escape-map theorem. Instead of deleting bad-wall points, construct

\[
\mathcal E:\mathcal G_{\rm bad}\to\mathcal C
\]

such that:

\[
\mathcal E(m)\text{ is wall-good/Gram-good},
\]

\[
E_1(\mathcal E(m))\ge Q^{-C}E_1(m),
\]

\[
E_{23}(\mathcal E(m))\ge Q^{-C}E_{23}(m),
\]

\[
\rho(\mathcal E(m))\ge Q^{-C}\rho(m),
\]

and

\[
\#\mathcal E^{-1}(m')\le Q^C.
\]

Then bad-wall mass can be rerouted rather than discarded.

### Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{Bad-wall / bad-Gram loss is not automatic from good-subset extraction.}
}
\]

It must be a named downstream theorem:

\[
\mathrm{BadWallLoss}_{\mathcal G}
\quad\text{or}\quad
\mathrm{WallEscapeMap}_{\mathcal G}.
\]

If this theorem fails, Stack C2 fails even after aggregate Gate–1 and aggregate O2/O3 are proved.

---

# 4. D4 on the good subset

On

\[
\mathcal G_{\rm good},
\]

the carrier-quotient D4 theorem applies because the source remains the canonical scalar source

\[
u=q''_{\can}.
\]

For each \(m\in\mathcal G_{\rm good}\), D4 gives local carrier row energy

\[
\mathrm{D4Energy}(m)
\ge
Q^{-A_4}
E_2(m)
\]

or, more concretely,

\[
\mathrm{D4Energy}(m)
\ge
Q^{-A_4}
|H(m)|^2
\left(
\Gamma_G(m)^2+\Gamma_{G,2}(m)^2
\right),
\]

with

\[
L_{\rm eff}=L_{\rm clean},
\qquad
\varepsilon_{\rm rem}=0
\]

inside the formal carrier quotient.

Thus

\[
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G_{\rm good}}
\mu(m)\mathrm{D4Energy}(m)
\ge
Q^{-A_5}\mu(\mathcal G_{\rm good}).
\]

D4 itself is not the obstruction, provided the good subset remains canonical scalar and Gram-good.

---

# 5. Row mobility and bounded overlap

This is the second genuine downstream risk.

Let local D4 produce a row certificate

\[
r(m)
\]

or a small finite set of row targets

\[
\mathcal R(m).
\]

To sum local row energy into global row energy, one needs a mobility map

\[
\mathcal M:m\mapsto r
\]

or a finite correspondence

\[
m\mapsto\mathcal R(m)
\]

satisfying two properties.

## 5.1 Bounded multiplicity

For every global row target \(r\),

\[
\#\{m\in\mathcal G_{\rm good}: r\in\mathcal R(m)\}
\le Q^C.
\]

Weighted form:

\[
\sum_{\substack{m\in\mathcal G_{\rm good}\\ r\in\mathcal R(m)}}\mu(m)
\le
Q^C \nu(r),
\]

where \(\nu(r)\) is the target-row weight.

## 5.2 Noncancelling transfer

One also needs the local row certificates to add as energies, not as cancellable amplitudes.

A safe formulation is:

\[
\sum_{r}
\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\ge
Q^{-C}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G_{\rm good}}
\mu(m)\mathrm{D4Energy}(m).
\]

This follows if row mobility is implemented by almost-orthogonal packets or by assigning disjoint row-energy atoms. It does **not** follow merely from a many-to-one amplitude map, because amplitudes can cancel.

So the required theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{RowMobility}_{\rm Bessel/Frame}:
\quad
\sum_{r}
\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\ge
Q^{-C}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G_{\rm good}}
\mu(m)\mathrm{D4Energy}(m).
}
\]

This is stronger than ordinary bounded overlap; it is a lower-frame/Bessel-dual statement for the mobility map.

### Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{Row mobility is viable only as bounded-overlap noncancelling energy transfer.}
}
\]

If mobility is only an amplitude correspondence, Stack C2 can fail through cancellation at shared row targets.

---

# 6. Final aggregate row-energy lower bound

Assuming:

1. good subset:

\[
\mu(\mathcal G)\ge Q^{-A_0}\mu(\mathcal C),
\]

2. Gate 2 local implication:

\[
E_2(m)\ge Q^{-A_2}
\quad(m\in\mathcal G),
\]

3. bad-wall loss:

\[
\mu(\mathcal G_{\rm good})\ge Q^{-A_3}\mu(\mathcal G),
\]

4. D4 local row energy:

\[
\mathrm{D4Energy}(m)\ge Q^{-A_4},
\quad m\in\mathcal G_{\rm good},
\]

5. row mobility lower frame:

\[
\sum_r \mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\ge
Q^{-A_5}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G_{\rm good}}
\mu(m)\mathrm{D4Energy}(m),
\]

we get

\[
\boxed{
\sum_r \mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This is the desired GoodSubsetDownstream conclusion.

---

# 7. Required global upper bound

To complete a contradiction, the paper also needs a global row-energy upper bound of the form

\[
\boxed{
\sum_r \mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\le
Q^{-B}
\mu(\mathcal C)
}
\]

with

\[
B>A,
\]

or an incompatible sign/coercivity inequality.

If the existing row-energy contradiction is pointwise, it must be upgraded to an aggregate contradiction:

\[
\text{aggregate lower row energy}
>
\text{aggregate upper row energy}.
\]

If no such aggregate upper bound exists, Stack C2 stops at aggregate row-energy production but does not yet contradict anything.

This is a separate final-check theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{GlobalRowEnergyUpper}.
}
\]

---

# 8. GoodSubsetDownstream theorem

The downstream theorem needed for Stack C2 is:

\[
\boxed{
\begin{gathered}
\mathcal G\subset\mathcal C,\quad
\mu(\mathcal G)\ge Q^{-A_0}\mu(\mathcal C),\\
E_1(m),E_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-A_1}
\quad(m\in\mathcal G),\\
\mathrm{BadWallLoss}_{\mathcal G},\quad
\mathrm{RowMobility}_{\rm Bessel/Frame},\quad
\mathrm{GlobalRowEnergyUpper}
\\
\Longrightarrow
\text{contradiction.}
\end{gathered}
}
\]

More constructively, before contradiction:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal G
\Longrightarrow
\sum_r \mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

---

# 9. First downstream obstruction

The first obstruction is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{bad-wall / bad-Gram weighted loss control.}
}
\]

If too much of \(\mathcal G\) lies on bad walls or outside the Gram-good carrier chart, D4 cannot be applied to a polynomial-weight subset.

The second obstruction is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{row-mobility lower-frame / noncancellation.}
}
\]

If local certificates collide at global row targets with uncontrolled cancellation, aggregate row energy cannot be summed.

D4 itself is not the obstruction.

---

# Final classification

\[
\boxed{
\text{Good-subset downstream is conditionally viable.}
}
\]

It requires exactly three downstream aggregate controls beyond the already repaired local machinery:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{BadWallLoss}_{\mathcal G}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{RowMobility}_{\rm Bessel/Frame}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{GlobalRowEnergyUpper}
}
\]

The recommended Stack C2 remains viable if these three theorems can be proved.

## Agent 6 continued attack — GoodSubsetDownstream

I continued the downstream Stack C2 audit. The previous conclusion was that GoodSubsetDownstream is viable only with three extra controls: bad-wall loss, row-mobility lower-frame/noncancellation, and a global row-energy upper bound. The current attack sharpens those into exact theorem forms and identifies the first necessary modification to Stack C2. 

# Main new conclusion

\[
\boxed{
\text{Stack C2 must extract a wall-good subset jointly, not after the fact.}
}
\]

Good \(E_1\) and good \(E_{23}\) do **not** imply wall-goodness. A polynomial-weight subset may satisfy pointwise Gate–1 and O2/O3 but still lie almost entirely on the D4 wall or Gram-bad locus.

So the correct downstream-compatible extraction target is not merely

\[
E_1(m)\ge Q^{-A},
\qquad
E_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

It should be

\[
E_1(m)\ge Q^{-A},
\qquad
E_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-A},
\qquad
E_{\rm wall}(m)\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Thus Stack C2 should be upgraded to:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23,\mathrm{wall}}
+
\mathrm{GoodSubsetDownstream}.
}
\]

---

# 1. Define the wall-good energy

Let the D4-relevant anisotropy vector be

\[
\Gamma(m)
=
\bigl(\Gamma_G(m),\Gamma_{G,2}(m)\bigr).
\]

The clean D4 output is proportional to

\[
|H(m)|^2|\Gamma(m)|^2
\]

in the carrier quotient.

Define a normalized wall energy

\[
E_{\rm wall}(m)
:=
\frac{|H(m)|^2|\Gamma(m)|^2}
{\mathcal N_{\rm D4}(m)},
\]

where \(\mathcal N_{\rm D4}(m)\) is the local polynomial-size normalization used in the Gate–2/D4 chart. For extraction purposes, truncate it:

\[
\widetilde E_{\rm wall}(m)
:=
\min\{1,Q^{C}E_{\rm wall}(m)\}.
\]

The wall-good condition is then

\[
\widetilde E_{\rm wall}(m)\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

This packages Gram-goodness, source nondegeneracy, and D4 anisotropy escape into the same extraction layer.

---

# 2. Why post-extraction wall deletion is insufficient

Suppose we have a subset \(\mathcal G\) with

\[
E_1(m)\ge Q^{-A},
\qquad
E_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

There is no formal implication

\[
E_1,E_{23}\gg Q^{-A}
\Longrightarrow
|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2\gg Q^{-A}.
\]

Indeed, in local coordinates the wall conditions are approximately

\[
\Gamma_G=0,
\qquad
\Gamma_{G,2}=0,
\]

where

\[
\Gamma_G=u-6q_0+2q_0^3,
\]

\[
\Gamma_{G,2}=(1-q_0^2)u-\frac16q^{(4)}.
\]

These are constraints on the local curvature jet. Gate–1 visibility and the augmented O2/O3 complex frame are different finite-row conditions. Without an explicit transversality theorem, their good sets may intersect the D4 wall heavily.

So the bad-wall deletion theorem

\[
\mu(\mathcal G_{\rm bad})\le \frac12\mu(\mathcal G)
\]

is not automatic. It is either a new theorem or must be built into the aggregate extraction.

The clean fix is to use a joint aggregate theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C}
d\mu(m)\,
E_1(m)E_{23}(m)\widetilde E_{\rm wall}(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

Then good-subset extraction gives all three pointwise lower bounds simultaneously.

---

# 3. Joint extraction lemma

Let

\[
0\le E_j(m)\le Q^{B_j},
\qquad j=1,\ldots,J.
\]

Assume

\[
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C}d\mu(m)\prod_{j=1}^J E_j(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

Define

\[
\mathcal G
=
\left\{
m:
E_j(m)\ge Q^{-A-B_1-\cdots-B_J-1}
\ \text{for all }j
\right\}.
\]

Then

\[
\boxed{
\mu(\mathcal G)\ge Q^{-A-B_1-\cdots-B_J-1}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

Proof: if \(E_i(m)<\eta\) for some \(i\), then

\[
\prod_jE_j(m)\le \eta\prod_{j\ne i}Q^{B_j}.
\]

Choosing

\[
\eta=Q^{-A-\sum B_j-1}
\]

makes the total contribution of the bad union \(<\frac12Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C)\). The remaining half of the product mass must lie on \(\mathcal G\), and since \(\prod_j E_j\le Q^{\sum B_j}\), the weight of \(\mathcal G\) is polynomially large.

For Stack C2 with wall included, take

\[
(E_1,E_2,E_3)
=
(E_1,E_{23},\widetilde E_{\rm wall}).
\]

This gives a polynomial-weight subset where all three are pointwise good.

---

# 4. D4 summation on the wall-good subset

On the extracted subset

\[
\mathcal G_{\rm good},
\]

we have

\[
E_1(m)\ge Q^{-A},
\qquad
E_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-A},
\qquad
\widetilde E_{\rm wall}(m)\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Therefore:

1. Gate–1 gives the local canonical source row.

2. O2/O3 gives cofactor and Wronskian incidence.

3. Gate–2 gives real-rigidity output.

4. Wall-goodness gives

\[
|H(m)|^2|\Gamma(m)|^2\ge Q^{-A}\mathcal N_{\rm D4}(m).
\]

5. Carrier-quotient D4 gives

\[
\mathrm{D4Energy}(m)\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Thus

\[
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G_{\rm good}}
d\mu(m)\,\mathrm{D4Energy}(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal G_{\rm good}).
\]

This part is local and uniform. D4 itself remains Green.

---

# 5. Row mobility: exact lower-frame condition

The next possible failure is cancellation during mobility.

Let local D4 produce a vector

\[
v_m\in \mathcal H_{\rm row}
\]

in the global row-energy Hilbert space. The desired global lower bound is

\[
\left\|
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G_{\rm good}}
\sqrt{\mu(m)}\,v_m
\right\|_{\mathcal H_{\rm row}}^2
\ge
Q^{-C}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G_{\rm good}}
\mu(m)\|v_m\|^2.
\]

This is a **lower Riesz-frame bound** for the family \(\{v_m\}\).

Bounded overlap alone gives the upper Bessel bound

\[
\left\|
\sum_m a_mv_m
\right\|^2
\le
Q^C\sum_m|a_m|^2\|v_m\|^2.
\]

It does not give the lower bound. Two equal and opposite row certificates can cancel.

So the required row-mobility theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{RowMobility}_{\rm lower}:
\quad
\{v_m:m\in\mathcal G_{\rm good}\}
\text{ is a Riesz sequence with lower bound }Q^{-C}.
}
\]

A sufficient condition is almost-orthogonality:

\[
|\langle v_m,v_{m'}\rangle|
\le
Q^{-C_0}\|v_m\|\|v_{m'}\|
\quad(m\ne m'),
\]

with

\[
C_0
\]

large enough relative to the local packing exponent of \(\mathcal G_{\rm good}\).

Another sufficient condition is disjoint row allocation: assign to each \(m\) a row atom \(r(m)\) such that

\[
r(m)=r(m')\Rightarrow m=m'
\]

after polynomial thinning. Then the lower bound is immediate, with only polynomial loss from thinning.

Thus row mobility must be formulated as one of:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Riesz lower-frame mobility}
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
\text{disjoint row-atom extraction with polynomial loss}.
}
\]

---

# 6. Global row-energy upper bound

After mobility,

\[
\sum_{\text{rows }r}\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal G_{\rm good}).
\]

To contradict anything, we need an independent upper bound

\[
\sum_{\text{rows }r}\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C)
\]

with

\[
B>A+\text{extraction losses}.
\]

If the existing argument only gives a pointwise upper bound, it must be upgraded to a summed upper bound over the row target set reached by \(\mathcal G_{\rm good}\).

This upper bound is not optional. Without it, Stack C2 proves row-energy production but not contradiction.

So the final downstream theorem stack must include:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{GlobalRowEnergyUpper}_{\rm agg}.
}
\]

---

# 7. Revised Stack C2

The minimal corrected Stack C2 is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23,\mathrm{wall}}
+
\mathrm{GoodSubsetExtraction}
+
\mathrm{LocalDownstream}
+
\mathrm{RowMobility}_{\rm lower}
+
\mathrm{GlobalRowEnergyUpper}_{\rm agg}.
}
\]

Where

\[
\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23,\mathrm{wall}}:
\quad
\sum_{\mathcal C}d\mu\,
E_1E_{23}\widetilde E_{\rm wall}
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

This is stronger than separate aggregate Gate–1 and aggregate O2/O3, but it eliminates the disjoint-good-set and bad-wall problems at once.

---

# 8. Final diagnosis

The downstream good-subset route is not blocked by Gate–2 or D4.

It is blocked, if at all, by two specific aggregate facts:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Joint aggregate lower bound including wall energy.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Riesz/disjoint lower-frame row mobility.}
}
\]

The next best mathematical attack should be on these two statements.

If both are true, Stack C2 becomes a viable replacement for the counter-random pointwise projection theorem.

## Agent 6 — final GoodSubsetDownstream theorem for corrected Stack C2

The assigned downstream problem is: assume joint extraction has already produced a polynomial-weight subset \(\mathcal G\) where

\[
E_1(m)\ge Q^{-A},\qquad
E_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-A},\qquad
E_{\rm wall}(m)\ge Q^{-A}
\]

for all \(m\in\mathcal G\). Determine whether the existing canonical downstream engine can run and sum over \(\mathcal G\). The coordinator specifically asks for local Gate2/D4 hypotheses, residual bad-set controls, row-mobility lower frame, global row-energy upper bound, and failure modes. 

# 1. Local Gate2/D4 theorem

The correct local theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
E_1(m)+E_{23}(m)+E_{\rm wall}(m)
\Longrightarrow
\mathrm{D4Energy}(m)\ge Q^{-C}
}
\]

for the canonical scalar source

\[
u=q''_{\can}.
\]

More explicitly, for each \(m\in\mathcal G\), assume:

\[
E_1(m)\ge Q^{-A_1},
\]

\[
E_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-A_{23}},
\]

\[
E_{\rm wall}(m)\ge Q^{-A_{\rm wall}}.
\]

Then, under the local chart hypotheses below,

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{D4Energy}(m)
\ge
Q^{-C}
\mathcal N_{\rm row}(m),
}
\]

where \(\mathcal N_{\rm row}(m)\) is the local normalization of the D4 carrier row-energy output.

The implication chain is:

\[
E_1(m)
\Longrightarrow
\text{canonical Gate--1 source row for }u=q''_{\can},
\]

\[
E_{23}(m)
\Longrightarrow
\text{O2/O3 cofactor and Wronskian incidence},
\]

\[
E_1(m)+E_{23}(m)
\Longrightarrow
\text{Gate--2 by source thickening and }L^2\text{-input rigidity},
\]

\[
E_{\rm wall}(m)
\Longrightarrow
|H(m)|^2\left(\Gamma_G(m)^2+\Gamma_{G,2}(m)^2\right)
\ge Q^{-C}\mathcal N_{\rm D4}(m),
\]

and then carrier-quotient D4 gives

\[
\mathrm{D4Energy}(m)
\ge
Q^{-C}
|H(m)|^2
\left(\Gamma_G(m)^2+\Gamma_{G,2}(m)^2\right).
\]

Thus

\[
\mathrm{D4Energy}(m)\ge Q^{-C}
\]

after normalization.

## Required local hypotheses

The local theorem is uniform only on the good local chart. For every \(m\in\mathcal G\), require:

\[
\lambda Q\asymp 1
\]

for the ordinary branch, or the corresponding branch-normalized transition/singular chart.

Require Gram-goodness:

\[
Q^{-C}\le g_{\rm Gr}(m),h_{\rm Gr}(m)\le Q^C,
\]

and carrier-frame conditioning:

\[
\sigma_{\min}(E_{\rm car}(m))\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

Require canonical transfer:

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2
\]

with the full canonical object, not a proxy.

Require source-thickening stability and \(L^2\)-input real-rigidity constants polynomial in \(Q\).

Require carrier routing:

\[
R_\nu^{(b)}\in\mathcal C_{\rm car}
\]

for

\[
\nu\in\{\mathrm{line},\mathrm{bg/core},\mathrm{norm/sub}\},\qquad b\in\{7,9\}.
\]

Then D4 is Green in the formal carrier quotient:

\[
L_{\rm eff}=L_{\rm clean},\qquad \varepsilon_{\rm rem}=0.
\]

# 2. Does the wall factor remove bad-wall deletion?

Mostly yes, but not completely.

The extracted condition

\[
E_{\rm wall}(m)\ge Q^{-A}
\]

should be defined to include the D4 anisotropy escape:

\[
|H(m)|^2\left(\Gamma_G(m)^2+\Gamma_{G,2}(m)^2\right)
\]

after normalization.

If \(E_{\rm wall}\) is defined only as anisotropy energy, then separate deletion is still needed for:

\[
\boxed{\text{Gram-bad points}}
\]

\[
\boxed{\text{gauge-bad points}}
\]

\[
\boxed{\text{carrier-frame-bad points}}
\]

\[
\boxed{\text{chart-boundary-bad points}}
\]

\[
\boxed{\text{carrier-routing-bad points}}
\]

To avoid a second deletion theorem, define the wall factor broadly:

\[
F_{\rm wall}(m)
=
F_{\rm aniso}(m)
F_{\rm Gram}(m)
F_{\rm gauge}(m)
F_{\rm car}(m)
F_{\rm chart}(m)
F_{\rm route}(m).
\]

Here each factor is capped between \(0\) and \(1\), for example:

\[
F_{\rm Gram}(m)
=
\mathbf 1_{Q^{-C}\le g_{\rm Gr},h_{\rm Gr}\le Q^C},
\]

or a smoothed capped version.

Then the joint aggregate theorem should be

\[
\int F_1F_{23}F_{\rm wall}\,d\mu
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C),
\]

with \(F_{\rm wall}\) including every local condition needed for D4.

If this broad \(F_{\rm wall}\) is used, then no independent bad-wall deletion is needed.

If \(F_{\rm wall}\) is narrow, one still needs:

\[
\mu(\mathcal G_{\rm residual\ bad})
\le
\frac12\mu(\mathcal G).
\]

# 3. RowMobility lower-frame theorem

Let every \(m\in\mathcal G\) produce a local row certificate

\[
v_m\in\mathcal H_{\rm row}
\]

in the global row-energy Hilbert space, normalized so that

\[
\|v_m\|_{\mathcal H_{\rm row}}^2
\asymp
\mathrm{D4Energy}(m).
\]

The required theorem is not just bounded overlap. It is a lower-frame inequality:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{\text{global rows }r}
\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\ge
Q^{-C}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}
\mu(m)\|v_m\|_{\mathcal H_{\rm row}}^2.
}
\]

Equivalently, for coefficients

\[
a_m=\sqrt{\mu(m)},
\]

one needs

\[
\left\|
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}a_m v_m
\right\|_{\mathcal H_{\rm row}}^2
\ge
Q^{-C}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}|a_m|^2\|v_m\|^2.
\]

## Sufficient condition 1: disjoint row-atom extraction

If each \(v_m\) contains a row atom \(r(m)\) with

\[
\mathrm{AtomEnergy}_{r(m)}(m)\ge Q^{-C}\|v_m\|^2
\]

and after thinning,

\[
r(m)=r(m')\Rightarrow m=m',
\]

then

\[
\sum_r\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\ge
Q^{-C}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G'}\mu(m)\|v_m\|^2.
\]

If thinning loses only polynomial weight,

\[
\mu(\mathcal G')\ge Q^{-C}\mu(\mathcal G),
\]

then row mobility is sufficient.

This is the cleanest route.

## Sufficient condition 2: Riesz lower bound

If

\[
|\langle v_m,v_{m'}\rangle|
\le
\delta_{m,m'}\|v_m\|\|v_{m'}\|
\]

and the Gram matrix

\[
G_{m,m'}=
\frac{\langle v_m,v_{m'}\rangle}
{\|v_m\|\|v_{m'}\|}
\]

satisfies

\[
\lambda_{\min}(G)\ge Q^{-C},
\]

then

\[
\left\|
\sum_m a_m v_m
\right\|^2
\ge
Q^{-C}\sum_m |a_m|^2\|v_m\|^2.
\]

A practical sufficient condition is row-local almost orthogonality plus packing:

\[
\sum_{m'\ne m}
\left|
\frac{\langle v_m,v_{m'}\rangle}
{\|v_m\|\|v_{m'}\|}
\right|
\le
1-Q^{-C}.
\]

Then Gershgorin gives a lower Riesz bound.

## Sufficient condition 3: signed/coherent transfer

If the mobility map preserves a positive row-energy density, rather than amplitude, then cancellation is impossible. Formally, if there are nonnegative row-energy atoms

\[
e_{m,r}\ge0
\]

such that

\[
\sum_r e_{m,r}\ge Q^{-C}\|v_m\|^2
\]

and

\[
\sum_m\mu(m)e_{m,r}
\le Q^C\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r),
\]

then

\[
\sum_r\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\ge
Q^{-C}
\sum_m\mu(m)\|v_m\|^2.
\]

This avoids amplitude cancellation and is preferable if the row-energy object is naturally positive.

# 4. Global row-energy upper bound

The final contradiction requires an upper bound of the form

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{\text{global rows }r}
\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C)
}
\]

or, more generally,

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{r\in\mathcal R(\mathcal C)}
\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\le
Q^{B_0}\mathcal U_{\rm global},
}
\]

where the lower bound from \(\mathcal G\) is

\[
\sum_r\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\ge
Q^{-B_1}\mu(\mathcal C),
\]

and contradiction requires

\[
Q^{-B_1}\mu(\mathcal C)
>
Q^{B_0}\mathcal U_{\rm global}.
\]

If the existing proof has only a pointwise row-energy upper bound, it is insufficient unless summable over the reached row target set with polynomial multiplicity.

The required aggregate upper theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{GlobalRowEnergyUpper}_{\rm agg}:
\quad
\sum_{r\in\mathcal R(\mathcal C)}
\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This upper bound must use the same normalization as the lower row-energy production.

If no such upper bound exists, Stack C2 proves aggregate row-energy production but not a contradiction.

# 5. Final GoodSubsetDownstream theorem

The complete theorem needed is:

\[
\boxed{
\begin{gathered}
\mathcal G\subset\mathcal C,\qquad
\mu(\mathcal G)\ge Q^{-A_0}\mu(\mathcal C),\\
E_1(m),E_{23}(m),E_{\rm wall}(m)\ge Q^{-A_1}
\quad(m\in\mathcal G),\\
\text{local chart hypotheses hold on }\mathcal G,\\
\mathrm{RowMobility}_{\rm lower}\text{ holds on }\mathcal G,\\
\mathrm{GlobalRowEnergyUpper}_{\rm agg}\text{ holds on }\mathcal R(\mathcal C)
\\
\Longrightarrow
\text{aggregate row-energy contradiction.}
\end{gathered}
}
\]

More explicitly, local downstream gives

\[
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}
\mu(m)\mathrm{D4Energy}(m)
\ge
Q^{-C}\mu(\mathcal G),
\]

row mobility gives

\[
\sum_{r\in\mathcal R(\mathcal G)}
\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\ge
Q^{-C}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}
\mu(m)\mathrm{D4Energy}(m),
\]

hence

\[
\sum_{r\in\mathcal R(\mathcal G)}
\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\ge
Q^{-C}\mu(\mathcal G)
\ge
Q^{-C'}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

Then the aggregate upper bound must contradict this.

# 6. Failure modes after joint extraction

Even after

\[
E_1,E_{23},E_{\rm wall}\ge Q^{-A}
\]

on \(\mathcal G\), Stack C2 can fail in exactly these ways:

1. **Incomplete wall factor.**  
   \(E_{\rm wall}\) controls anisotropy but not Gram/gauge/carrier-frame/chart validity.

2. **Carrier-routing failure.**  
   Correction rows have hidden live components outside \(\mathcal C_{\rm car}\).

3. **Gate2 nonuniformity.**  
   Gate–1 \(\Rightarrow\) Gate–2 constants depend on local parameters not controlled by \(F_{\rm wall}\).

4. **Row-mobility cancellation.**  
   Local D4 certificates collide as amplitudes and cancel. Bounded overlap alone does not prevent this.

5. **No aggregate row-energy upper bound.**  
   The proof produces lower row energy but has no global summed upper bound to contradict.

6. **Normalization mismatch.**  
   Extraction uses \(d\mu=\rho|X|^2\) but row-energy lower/upper bounds are normalized with \(\rho\), or vice versa.

7. **Branch mismatch.**  
   Transition/singular good packets produce source energy in a chart that does not transfer to the canonical ordinary D4 chart.

# Final classification

\[
\boxed{
\text{GoodSubsetDownstream is conditionally viable.}
}
\]

The exact remaining downstream inputs are:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{WallFactor}_{\rm full}
}
\]

meaning \(F_{\rm wall}\) includes anisotropy, Gram, gauge, carrier-frame, chart, and routing validity;

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{RowMobility}_{\rm lower}
}
\]

meaning disjoint row-atom extraction, Riesz lower frame, or positive row-energy transfer;

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{GlobalRowEnergyUpper}_{\rm agg}.
}
\]

With those three, corrected Stack C2 can use the repaired canonical Gate2/D4 engine on a polynomial-weight extracted subset and sum to a contradiction.

## Agent 6 — RowMobility lower-frame and global row-energy upper

The assigned Agent 6 task is the final downstream bottleneck after joint extraction: assume a polynomial-weight good subset \(\mathcal G\) where \(E_1,E_{23},E_{\rm wall}^{\rm full}\ge Q^{-A}\), and test whether local Gate2/D4 certificates can be summed into a global row-energy contradiction. 

# 1. Local Gate2/D4 conclusion

Assume \(m\in\mathcal G\) satisfies

\[
E_1(m)\ge Q^{-A_1},
\qquad
E_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-A_{23}},
\qquad
E_{\rm wall}^{\rm full}(m)\ge Q^{-A_w}.
\]

Assume also all local chart hypotheses are included in \(E_{\rm wall}^{\rm full}\):

\[
\text{Gram-good},\quad
\text{gauge-good},\quad
\text{carrier-frame-good},\quad
\text{chart-good},\quad
\text{carrier-routing-good}.
\]

Then local downstream gives a row certificate

\[
v_m\in\mathcal H_{\rm row}
\]

such that

\[
\boxed{
\|v_m\|_{\mathcal H_{\rm row}}^2
\ge
Q^{-C}\mathcal N_{\rm loc}(m).
}
\]

Here \(\mathcal N_{\rm loc}(m)\) is the local normalization. If energies are already normalized, take

\[
\mathcal N_{\rm loc}(m)=1.
\]

The local implication is:

\[
E_1(m)+E_{23}(m)
\Rightarrow
\text{Gate--2},
\]

\[
E_{\rm wall}^{\rm full}(m)
\Rightarrow
|H(m)|^2(\Gamma_G(m)^2+\Gamma_{G,2}(m)^2)\gg Q^{-C},
\]

and carrier-quotient D4 gives

\[
\|v_m\|^2
\asymp
\mathrm{D4Energy}(m)
\gg Q^{-C}.
\]

D4 itself is not the remaining obstruction.

---

# 2. Global row-energy object

The clean abstraction is:

\[
\mathcal H_{\rm row}
=
\ell^2(\mathcal R,w_r)
\]

for a global row-target set \(\mathcal R\), with

\[
v_m=(v_m(r))_{r\in\mathcal R}.
\]

The aggregate row amplitude is

\[
V(r)
=
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}\sqrt{\mu(m)}\,v_m(r).
\]

Then

\[
\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r):=|V(r)|^2,
\]

and

\[
\sum_{r\in\mathcal R}\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
=
\left\|
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}\sqrt{\mu(m)}\,v_m
\right\|_{\mathcal H_{\rm row}}^2.
\]

The desired lower transfer is therefore

\[
\boxed{
\left\|
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}\sqrt{\mu(m)}\,v_m
\right\|^2
\ge
Q^{-C}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}\mu(m)\|v_m\|^2.
}
\]

Equivalently, define the Gram matrix

\[
K_{m,m'}
=
\frac{\langle v_m,v_{m'}\rangle}
{\|v_m\|\|v_{m'}\|}.
\]

Then RowMobility lower is

\[
\boxed{
\lambda_{\min}(K_{\mathcal G})\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

This is the actual downstream theorem needed.

---

# 3. Why bounded overlap is insufficient

Bounded overlap gives only an upper/Bessel estimate.

Example: take two good centers \(m_1,m_2\) and a one-dimensional row space. Let

\[
v_{m_1}=e,\qquad v_{m_2}=-e,
\qquad
\mu(m_1)=\mu(m_2)=1.
\]

Each local certificate has

\[
\|v_{m_i}\|^2=1.
\]

The overlap multiplicity is \(2\), hence bounded. But

\[
\left\|v_{m_1}+v_{m_2}\right\|^2=0,
\]

while

\[
\|v_{m_1}\|^2+\|v_{m_2}\|^2=2.
\]

So bounded overlap does not imply a lower row-energy transfer.

The missing ingredient is one of:

\[
\boxed{\text{disjoint row atoms}}
\]

\[
\boxed{\text{Riesz lower-frame / almost orthogonality}}
\]

\[
\boxed{\text{positive row-energy transfer without amplitude summation}}
\]

---

# 4. Three sufficient mechanisms

## Mechanism A: disjoint row-atom extraction

For each \(m\in\mathcal G\), suppose there exists a row atom \(r(m)\in\mathcal R\) such that

\[
|v_m(r(m))|^2
\ge
Q^{-C}\|v_m\|^2.
\]

If after thinning to \(\mathcal G'\subset\mathcal G\),

\[
r(m)=r(m')\Rightarrow m=m',
\]

and

\[
\mu(\mathcal G')\ge Q^{-C}\mu(\mathcal G),
\]

then

\[
\sum_{r\in\mathcal R}\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\ge
Q^{-C}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G'}\mu(m)\|v_m\|^2.
\]

This is the most robust route because it avoids cancellation.

The needed theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{DisjointRowAtomExtraction}:
\quad
\exists\mathcal G'\subset\mathcal G,\ 
\mu(\mathcal G')\ge Q^{-C}\mu(\mathcal G),
\text{ with injective dominant row atoms.}
}
\]

This is plausible if row targets have enough cardinality and the routing map has polynomial local multiplicity.

## Mechanism B: Riesz lower frame

Assume normalized vectors

\[
u_m=\frac{v_m}{\|v_m\|}
\]

satisfy

\[
\sum_{m'\ne m}|\langle u_m,u_{m'}\rangle|
\le
1-Q^{-C}
\]

for every \(m\). Then Gershgorin gives

\[
\lambda_{\min}(K)\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

Thus

\[
\left\|
\sum_m a_m v_m
\right\|^2
\ge
Q^{-C}\sum_m |a_m|^2\|v_m\|^2.
\]

The needed theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{RowMobility}_{\rm Riesz}:
\quad
\{v_m:m\in\mathcal G\}
\text{ is a Riesz sequence with lower bound }Q^{-C}.
}
\]

This is plausible only if row certificates have enough phase/position separation. It is not implied by standard Bessel upper estimates.

## Mechanism C: positive row-energy transfer

If the downstream row object can be formulated as positive row-energy atoms rather than summed amplitudes, assume nonnegative quantities \(e_{m,r}\ge0\) such that

\[
\sum_r e_{m,r}\ge Q^{-C}\|v_m\|^2,
\]

and

\[
\sum_m\mu(m)e_{m,r}
\le
Q^C\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r).
\]

Then

\[
\sum_r\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\ge
Q^{-C}
\sum_m\mu(m)\|v_m\|^2.
\]

This is best if the proof’s row-energy object is naturally positive, for example a square norm of separately allocated packets. It fails if the actual global quantity is the square of a coherent amplitude sum.

---

# 5. Most plausible mechanism

\[
\boxed{
\text{Disjoint row-atom extraction is the most plausible first target.}
}
\]

Reason:

- Riesz lower-frame requires controlling many pairwise phases.
- Positive transfer requires reformulating the global row-energy object.
- Disjoint atom extraction only needs a combinatorial thinning plus a dominant-atom lemma.

The exact theorem to try is:

\[
\boxed{
\begin{gathered}
\forall m\in\mathcal G,\ \exists r(m)\in\mathcal R
\text{ with }
|v_m(r(m))|^2\ge Q^{-C}\|v_m\|^2,\\
\#\{m:r(m)=r\}\le Q^C
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\exists\mathcal G'\subset\mathcal G,\ 
\mu(\mathcal G')\ge Q^{-C}\mu(\mathcal G),\\
r(m)\text{ injective on }\mathcal G'.
\end{gathered}
}
\]

This gives RowMobility lower after thinning.

However, the first line is not automatic. It requires a **dominant row atom lemma**: local D4 energy cannot be spread over too many row atoms without at least one atom carrying polynomial mass.

---

# 6. Global row-energy upper

The required upper theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{GlobalRowEnergyUpper}_{\rm agg}:
\quad
\sum_{r\in\mathcal R(\mathcal C)}
\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C)
}
\]

or, more generally,

\[
\sum_{r\in\mathcal R(\mathcal C)}
\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\le
Q^{B_0}\mathcal U_{\rm global},
\]

where the lower bound gives

\[
\sum_r\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\ge
Q^{-B_1}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

To contradict, need

\[
Q^{-B_1}\mu(\mathcal C)
>
Q^{B_0}\mathcal U_{\rm global}.
\]

At the current abstraction level, this upper bound is not yet identified as a proven theorem. It must be supplied by one of:

1. a global conservation law for row energy;
2. a Bessel inequality over global row targets;
3. an explicit formula upper bound;
4. a finite total-energy budget for canonical rows.

Without such an upper bound, Stack C2 produces aggregate row energy but does not contradict anything.

So the final downstream bottleneck is at least as much

\[
\mathrm{GlobalRowEnergyUpper}_{\rm agg}
\]

as RowMobility lower.

---

# 7. Final GoodSubsetDownstream theorem

A precise downstream theorem is:

Assume:

1. \(\mathcal G\subset\mathcal C\) with

\[
\mu(\mathcal G)\ge Q^{-A_0}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

2. For all \(m\in\mathcal G\),

\[
E_1(m),E_{23}(m),E_{\rm wall}^{\rm full}(m)\ge Q^{-A_1}.
\]

3. Local Gate2/D4 gives

\[
\|v_m\|^2\ge Q^{-A_2}\mathcal N_{\rm loc}(m).
\]

4. RowMobility lower gives

\[
\sum_r\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\ge
Q^{-A_3}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}\mu(m)\|v_m\|^2.
\]

5. Normalization lower bound:

\[
\mathcal N_{\rm loc}(m)\ge Q^{-A_4}
\]

or this is already included in \(E_{\rm wall}^{\rm full}\).

Then

\[
\sum_r\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\ge
Q^{-A_5}\mu(\mathcal G)
\ge
Q^{-A_6}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

If additionally

\[
\sum_r\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C)
\]

with

\[
B>A_6,
\]

then contradiction follows.

---

# 8. Failure modes

Even after joint extraction, the downstream can fail only in these precise ways:

1. **Incomplete \(F_{\rm wall}\).**  
   It does not encode Gram/gauge/carrier-frame/chart/routing validity.

2. **Local Gate2 nonuniformity.**  
   Gate2 constants depend on parameters not controlled by the extraction.

3. **Carrier routing failure.**  
   D4 correction rows have live components outside \(\mathcal C_{\rm car}\).

4. **No dominant row atom.**  
   Local D4 energy may spread over too many row targets.

5. **No disjoint thinning or Riesz lower bound.**  
   Row certificates collide and cancel.

6. **Only Bessel/overlap upper bounds.**  
   These do not produce lower row energy.

7. **No global row-energy upper.**  
   Lower row-energy production has no contradiction target.

8. **Normalization mismatch.**  
   The measure used in extraction does not match row-energy summation.

9. **Branch mismatch.**  
   Transition/singular certificates do not transfer to the same row-energy Hilbert space.

---

# Final classification

\[
\boxed{
\text{GoodSubsetDownstream reduces to two concrete downstream theorems.}
}
\]

They are:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{RowMobility}_{\rm lower}
}
\]

preferably via disjoint dominant row-atom extraction, and

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{GlobalRowEnergyUpper}_{\rm agg}.
}
\]

If both are proved, corrected Stack C2 can run the repaired local Gate2/D4 engine on the extracted good subset and sum to a contradiction.

## Agent 6 continued attack — row mobility lower frame

I pushed the downstream bottleneck one layer deeper. The remaining question is whether local D4 certificates can be converted into noncancelling global row energy after extraction. The prior prompt asks exactly for this: RowMobility_lower and GlobalRowEnergyUpper_agg. 

# 1. Main new reduction

The cleanest route is not to prove a full Riesz lower-frame theorem directly. It is to prove a **dominant row-atom extraction theorem**.

The sufficient theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Local D4 energy has a polynomially large atom, and dominant atoms have polynomial multiplicity.}
}
\]

Then one can thin the good subset to an injective row-atom subset with only polynomial weight loss. This avoids amplitude cancellation entirely.

So RowMobility_lower reduces to two concrete statements:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{DominantAtom}:
\forall m\in\mathcal G,\ 
\exists r(m)
\text{ with }
|v_m(r(m))|^2\ge Q^{-C}\|v_m\|^2.
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{AtomMultiplicity}:
\#\{m\in\mathcal G:r(m)=r\}\le Q^C
\quad\text{or weighted analogue.}
}
\]

Together they imply RowMobility_lower after thinning.

---

# 2. Dominant atom lemma

Suppose the local row certificate has finite row support

\[
\operatorname{supp} v_m\subset \mathcal R_m
\]

with

\[
|\mathcal R_m|\le Q^C.
\]

Then

\[
\|v_m\|^2
=
\sum_{r\in\mathcal R_m}|v_m(r)|^2.
\]

Therefore there exists

\[
r(m)\in\mathcal R_m
\]

such that

\[
|v_m(r(m))|^2
\ge
|\mathcal R_m|^{-1}\|v_m\|^2
\ge
Q^{-C}\|v_m\|^2.
\]

So the dominant atom lemma is automatic if each local D4 row certificate spreads over only polynomially many row atoms.

Thus the real condition is:

\[
\boxed{
|\operatorname{supp}v_m|\le Q^C.
}
\]

If \(v_m\) is not literally finitely supported, it is enough to prove polynomial concentration:

\[
\sum_{r\in\mathcal R_m^{\rm loc}}|v_m(r)|^2
\ge
Q^{-C}\|v_m\|^2,
\qquad
|\mathcal R_m^{\rm loc}|\le Q^C.
\]

Then a dominant atom exists inside \(\mathcal R_m^{\rm loc}\).

This is plausible because local D4 certificates are finite-jet / finite-carrier objects. If row mobility spreads them into exponentially many global row atoms, the current downstream architecture probably fails.

---

# 3. Weighted injective thinning lemma

Assume each \(m\in\mathcal G\) has a selected dominant atom \(r(m)\), and assume bounded multiplicity:

\[
\#\{m:r(m)=r\}\le M_0,
\qquad
M_0\le Q^C.
\]

Let \(\mu(m)\ge0\) be arbitrary weights.

For each row atom \(r\), choose one element

\[
m_r\in \{m:r(m)=r\}
\]

with maximal weight \(\mu(m_r)\). Then

\[
\sum_r \mu(m_r)
\ge
M_0^{-1}\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}\mu(m).
\]

Indeed, in each fiber,

\[
\max_{m:r(m)=r}\mu(m)
\ge
\frac1{|\{m:r(m)=r\}|}
\sum_{m:r(m)=r}\mu(m)
\ge
M_0^{-1}
\sum_{m:r(m)=r}\mu(m).
\]

Let

\[
\mathcal G'=\{m_r:r\in r(\mathcal G)\}.
\]

Then

\[
r:\mathcal G'\to \mathcal R
\]

is injective and

\[
\mu(\mathcal G')\ge Q^{-C}\mu(\mathcal G).
\]

This gives a noncancelling subcollection.

---

# 4. RowMobility_lower from dominant atoms

On \(\mathcal G'\), the selected atoms are distinct. Hence

\[
\sum_{r\in\mathcal R}\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\ge
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G'}
\mu(m)|v_m(r(m))|^2.
\]

By the dominant atom bound,

\[
|v_m(r(m))|^2
\ge Q^{-C}\|v_m\|^2.
\]

Therefore

\[
\sum_{r\in\mathcal R}\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\ge
Q^{-C}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G'}
\mu(m)\|v_m\|^2.
\]

If local D4 gives

\[
\|v_m\|^2\ge Q^{-C}
\]

in normalized units, then

\[
\sum_{r\in\mathcal R}\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\ge
Q^{-C}\mu(\mathcal G')
\ge
Q^{-C}\mu(\mathcal G).
\]

Since extraction gave

\[
\mu(\mathcal G)\ge Q^{-C}\mu(\mathcal C),
\]

we get

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{r\in\mathcal R}\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\ge
Q^{-C}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This proves the desired aggregate lower row-energy production.

---

# 5. What must be proved about the actual row map

The downstream row-mobility task is now exact.

For each local D4 certificate \(v_m\), prove:

## 5.1 Polynomial row localization

\[
\boxed{
\exists\mathcal R_m^{\rm loc}\subset\mathcal R
\quad
|\mathcal R_m^{\rm loc}|\le Q^C,
\quad
\sum_{r\in\mathcal R_m^{\rm loc}}|v_m(r)|^2
\ge Q^{-C}\|v_m\|^2.
}
\]

This gives a dominant atom.

## 5.2 Polynomial row multiplicity

For a selected dominant row atom \(r(m)\),

\[
\boxed{
\#\{m\in\mathcal G:r(m)=r\}\le Q^C.
}
\]

A sufficient geometric condition is:

\[
r(m)=r(m')
\Longrightarrow
|m-m'|\le Q^C\Delta_{\rm row},
\]

combined with local packing of \(\mathcal G\):

\[
\#(\mathcal G\cap I)\le Q^C
\]

for every row-scale interval \(I\).

If row atoms encode both location and derivative/order index, this multiplicity bound should be plausible. If row atoms forget too much location information, multiplicity can be large and mobility fails.

## 5.3 Weight compatibility

Need either:

\[
\mu(m)\asymp \mu(m')
\]

on row fibers, or the maximal-fiber thinning argument above. The latter works for arbitrary weights but keeps only the largest weight per fiber.

Thus no weight comparability is strictly needed if multiplicity is polynomial.

---

# 6. Global row-energy upper: exact target

After lower production,

\[
\sum_{r\in\mathcal R(\mathcal G')}\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\ge
Q^{-C}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

To contradict, need an upper bound on the same row target family:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{r\in\mathcal R(\mathcal C)}
\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C)
}
\]

with

\[
B>C.
\]

If the upper bound is instead

\[
\sum_{r\in\mathcal R(\mathcal C)}
\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\le Q^B \mathcal U,
\]

then contradiction requires

\[
Q^{-C}\mu(\mathcal C)>Q^B\mathcal U.
\]

So the upper theorem must be normalized against the same measure \(\mu(\mathcal C)\).

At this point the upper bound is the least specified part. It must come from one of:

1. a global Bessel/Parseval bound for row energies;
2. an explicit formula conservation law;
3. a finite total-energy budget for canonical row outputs;
4. an exclusion principle saying rows cannot carry this much aggregate energy.

The downstream proof cannot close without this.

---

# 7. Candidate upper-bound shape

A plausible upper statement is:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{r\in\mathcal R}
\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\le
Q^C
\int_{\mathcal I}
|q''_{\can}(t)|^2 w(t)\,dt.
}
\]

Then one needs an independent upper bound

\[
\int_{\mathcal I}|q''_{\can}(t)|^2w(t)\,dt
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

But this is delicate: aggregate Gate–1 itself asserts that \(q''_{\can}\)-derived energy is not too small at routed centers. Therefore the global upper must be an incompatible **row-specific** budget, not merely a generic \(L^2\) upper unless the lower row energy has stronger scaling.

Another possible upper is finite rank:

\[
\sum_{r\in\mathcal R}\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\le
\operatorname{rank}(\mathcal R)\cdot \max_r\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r).
\]

This is not enough unless \(\max_r\) is sharply controlled.

So the upper-bound problem is real, not formal.

---

# 8. Updated GoodSubsetDownstream theorem

The clean theorem is now:

Assume:

1. Joint extraction gives \(\mathcal G\) with

\[
\mu(\mathcal G)\ge Q^{-C}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

2. Local Gate2/D4 gives

\[
\|v_m\|^2\ge Q^{-C}
\qquad(m\in\mathcal G).
\]

3. Polynomial row localization:

\[
\exists\mathcal R_m^{\rm loc},\quad
|\mathcal R_m^{\rm loc}|\le Q^C,
\quad
\sum_{r\in\mathcal R_m^{\rm loc}}|v_m(r)|^2
\ge Q^{-C}\|v_m\|^2.
\]

4. Polynomial row multiplicity after dominant-atom selection:

\[
\#\{m:r(m)=r\}\le Q^C.
\]

5. Global row-energy upper:

\[
\sum_{r\in\mathcal R(\mathcal C)}\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C),
\]

where \(B\) exceeds all lower-bound losses.

Then contradiction follows.

---

# 9. Final downstream status

\[
\boxed{
\text{RowMobility_lower is reducible to finite localization + multiplicity.}
}
\]

This is a genuine improvement over the abstract Riesz theorem. The most concrete remaining downstream inputs are:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{RowLocalization}_{\rm poly}}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{RowMultiplicity}_{\rm poly}}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{GlobalRowEnergyUpper}_{\rm agg}}
\]

The first two look like finite/geometric row-routing statements. The third is the remaining serious analytic/global obstruction.

## Agent 6 continued attack — global row-energy upper

The previous downstream reduction left three items: polynomial row localization, polynomial row multiplicity, and \(\mathrm{GlobalRowEnergyUpper}_{\rm agg}\). Continuing the attack, the main issue is now sharper: a generic global row-energy upper is not enough. It must be an upper bound for the **same forbidden row channel** into which D4 injects energy. This is the remaining downstream bottleneck in the corrected Stack C2 route. 

# 1. New diagnosis

The lower side gives something like

\[
\sum_{r\in\mathcal R(\mathcal G)}
\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\ge
Q^{-C}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

But this contradicts nothing unless the global theory gives

\[
\sum_{r\in\mathcal R(\mathcal G)}
\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C)
\]

with \(B>C\).

A generic Bessel or Parseval upper usually gives only

\[
\sum_{r}
\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\le
Q^C \mu(\mathcal C),
\]

which is compatible with the lower bound. It is not a contradiction.

So the global upper must be a **defect-channel upper**, not merely a total-energy upper.

The correct object is therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal R_{\rm forb}\subseteq\mathcal R
}
\]

such that

1. local D4 produces energy in \(\mathcal R_{\rm forb}\), and  
2. global structure forces \(\mathcal R_{\rm forb}\)-energy to be small.

---

# 2. Required forbidden-channel formulation

Let

\[
P_{\rm forb}:\mathcal H_{\rm row}\to\mathcal H_{\rm forb}
\]

be the projection onto the globally forbidden row channel.

The local row certificate is

\[
v_m\in\mathcal H_{\rm row}.
\]

The previous RowMobility lower must be upgraded from

\[
\|v_m\|^2\gg Q^{-C}
\]

to

\[
\boxed{
\|P_{\rm forb}v_m\|^2
\ge
Q^{-C}\|v_m\|^2.
}
\]

Otherwise D4 may produce row energy only in globally allowed rows, and the global forbidden upper would be irrelevant.

Thus the new local-to-global requirement is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{ForbiddenProjection}_{\rm D4}:
\quad
\text{D4 row certificates have polynomial mass in the globally forbidden row channel.}
}
\]

Then row mobility should be applied to

\[
P_{\rm forb}v_m,
\]

not \(v_m\).

---

# 3. Revised row-mobility theorem

The correct downstream lower-frame statement is:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{r\in\mathcal R_{\rm forb}}
\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\ge
Q^{-C}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}\mu(m)\|P_{\rm forb}v_m\|^2.
}
\]

Together with

\[
\|P_{\rm forb}v_m\|^2\ge Q^{-C}
\]

on \(\mathcal G\), this gives

\[
\sum_{r\in\mathcal R_{\rm forb}}
\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\ge
Q^{-C}\mu(\mathcal G)
\ge
Q^{-C'}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

Then one needs the global upper:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{r\in\mathcal R_{\rm forb}}
\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C),
\qquad B>C'.
}
\]

This finally gives a contradiction.

So the downstream theorem should not be stated over all rows. It should be stated over a specifically forbidden row channel.

---

# 4. Dominant atom version

The dominant atom reduction also needs this refinement.

Old condition:

\[
\exists r(m)\in\mathcal R
\quad
|v_m(r(m))|^2\ge Q^{-C}\|v_m\|^2.
\]

Correct condition:

\[
\boxed{
\exists r(m)\in\mathcal R_{\rm forb}
\quad
|v_m(r(m))|^2\ge Q^{-C}\|v_m\|^2.
}
\]

Then, with polynomial multiplicity,

\[
\#\{m:r(m)=r\}\le Q^C,
\]

weighted thinning gives an injective subcollection

\[
\mathcal G'\subseteq\mathcal G
\]

with

\[
\mu(\mathcal G')\ge Q^{-C}\mu(\mathcal G).
\]

Since the selected atoms lie in \(\mathcal R_{\rm forb}\),

\[
\sum_{r\in\mathcal R_{\rm forb}}
\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\ge
Q^{-C}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G'}\mu(m)\|v_m\|^2
\ge
Q^{-C'}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

Thus the dominant atom theorem must become:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{ForbiddenDominantAtom}:
\quad
\forall m\in\mathcal G,\ 
\exists r(m)\in\mathcal R_{\rm forb}
\text{ carrying polynomial local D4 mass.}
}
\]

This is stronger than mere row localization.

---

# 5. What can supply the forbidden upper?

There are three plausible mechanisms.

## Mechanism 1: exact null identity

If the global row equations imply

\[
P_{\rm forb}V=0
\]

for the total row vector \(V\), then

\[
\sum_{r\in\mathcal R_{\rm forb}}
\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)=0.
\]

Then any positive lower bound contradicts immediately.

This is the cleanest possible case.

Required theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{ForbiddenRowNull}:
\quad
\text{the global canonical row vector has zero projection to }
\mathcal R_{\rm forb}.
}
\]

## Mechanism 2: tail/error upper

If exact vanishing fails but the forbidden component is only produced by tails/errors, then

\[
\sum_{r\in\mathcal R_{\rm forb}}
\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

Required theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{ForbiddenRowSmall}:
\quad
\|P_{\rm forb}V\|^2\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This is likely the realistic target if D4 produces an obstruction channel that should vanish modulo controlled regularized tails.

## Mechanism 3: sign/coercivity incompatibility

Instead of smallness, the upper could be an incompatibility of signs:

\[
\sum_{r\in\mathcal R_{\rm forb}}
\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\le 0,
\]

while local D4 gives positivity.

This requires a signed quadratic form

\[
\mathcal Q_{\rm forb}(V)
\]

such that globally

\[
\mathcal Q_{\rm forb}(V)\le 0,
\]

but locally D4 gives

\[
\mathcal Q_{\rm forb}(v_m)\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

This is stronger and more delicate because the row energy is currently positive.

---

# 6. Why total-energy upper is insufficient

Suppose all one has is

\[
\sum_{r\in\mathcal R}\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\le Q^C\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

The lower gives

\[
\sum_{r\in\mathcal R}\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\ge Q^{-C'}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

Both can hold simultaneously.

Even a sharp Parseval upper of the form

\[
\sum_{r\in\mathcal R}\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\le C\mu(\mathcal C)
\]

does not contradict a lower of size

\[
Q^{-C'}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

Therefore the global upper must be **small in the specific row channel**, not merely finite.

This changes the downstream target.

---

# 7. Revised GoodSubsetDownstream theorem

The final downstream theorem should now be:

Assume:

1. \(\mathcal G\subset\mathcal C\), with

\[
\mu(\mathcal G)\ge Q^{-A_0}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

2. For all \(m\in\mathcal G\),

\[
E_1(m),E_{23}(m),E_{\rm wall}^{\rm full}(m)\ge Q^{-A_1}.
\]

3. Local Gate2/D4 gives row certificates \(v_m\).

4. Forbidden-channel dominance:

\[
\|P_{\rm forb}v_m\|^2\ge Q^{-A_2}\|v_m\|^2.
\]

5. Forbidden-channel row mobility lower:

\[
\sum_{r\in\mathcal R_{\rm forb}}
\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\ge
Q^{-A_3}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}\mu(m)\|P_{\rm forb}v_m\|^2.
\]

6. Local D4 normalization:

\[
\|v_m\|^2\ge Q^{-A_4}.
\]

Then

\[
\sum_{r\in\mathcal R_{\rm forb}}
\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\ge
Q^{-A_5}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

If also

\[
\sum_{r\in\mathcal R_{\rm forb}}
\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C),
\qquad
B>A_5,
\]

then contradiction follows.

---

# 8. Updated list of true downstream bottlenecks

The previous list was:

\[
\mathrm{RowLocalization}_{\rm poly},
\quad
\mathrm{RowMultiplicity}_{\rm poly},
\quad
\mathrm{GlobalRowEnergyUpper}_{\rm agg}.
\]

The refined list is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{ForbiddenChannelDefinition}
}
\]

A precise row subspace \(\mathcal R_{\rm forb}\) must be identified.

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{ForbiddenDominantAtom}
}
\]

D4 local energy must land polynomially in that forbidden channel.

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{ForbiddenRowMultiplicity}
}
\]

Selected forbidden atoms must have polynomial multiplicity.

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{ForbiddenRowSmall/Null}
}
\]

The global row vector must have small or zero forbidden-channel energy.

These are sharper and more decisive than generic RowMobility and GlobalRowEnergyUpper.

---

# 9. Consequence for Stack C2

Corrected Stack C2 is viable only if the D4 row-energy output is not just positive, but positive in a row channel that the global theory forbids.

So the key downstream mathematical question is now:

\[
\boxed{
\text{What exactly is the forbidden row channel?}
}
\]

If such a channel exists and D4 lands in it, the aggregate route can plausibly close.

If no such channel exists, the aggregate downstream produces energy but not contradiction.

This is now the next best target for Agent 6: identify \(\mathcal R_{\rm forb}\) from the existing row-energy architecture.

## Agent 6 continued attack — identifying the forbidden row channel

The previous downstream reduction showed that generic row-energy production is not enough. The local D4 certificates must land in a **globally forbidden channel**, otherwise the aggregate lower bound does not contradict any global upper bound. This continues the Agent 6 downstream task: row mobility lower-frame plus aggregate global row-energy upper. 

# 1. Abstract formulation

Let

\[
\mathcal H_{\rm row}
\]

be the global row-energy Hilbert space, and let

\[
V_{\rm glob}\in\mathcal H_{\rm row}
\]

be the actual global row vector produced by the canonical object.

A “forbidden channel” is a closed subspace

\[
\mathcal H_{\rm forb}\subseteq \mathcal H_{\rm row}
\]

with projection

\[
P_{\rm forb}:\mathcal H_{\rm row}\to\mathcal H_{\rm forb}
\]

such that:

\[
\boxed{
\|P_{\rm forb}V_{\rm glob}\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C)
}
\]

globally, but local D4 certificates satisfy

\[
\boxed{
\|P_{\rm forb}v_m\|^2
\ge Q^{-C}\|v_m\|^2
}
\]

on the extracted good subset.

Then row mobility in the forbidden channel gives

\[
\|P_{\rm forb}V_{\rm glob}\|^2
\ge Q^{-C'}\mu(\mathcal C),
\]

which contradicts the global upper if \(B>C'\).

So the entire downstream contradiction reduces to:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Find }P_{\rm forb}\text{ such that global rows are small there but D4 lands there.}
}
\]

Without such a \(P_{\rm forb}\), Stack C2 produces energy but not contradiction.

---

# 2. Natural candidate 1: nuisance-carrier quotient

The first candidate is the carrier quotient itself:

\[
\mathcal Q_{\rm car}
=
\mathcal M_{\rm car}/\mathcal C_{\rm car}.
\]

D4 produces energy in the live carrier plane

\[
W_{\rm car}
=
\operatorname{span}\{[\mathsf C_7],[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\}.
\]

One might try to declare

\[
\mathcal H_{\rm forb}=W_{\rm car}.
\]

But this is **not automatically forbidden**. In fact, D4 being Green says only that local energy survives in \(W_{\rm car}\):

\[
P_{W_{\rm car}}v_m\ne0.
\]

To get contradiction, the global theory would need

\[
P_{W_{\rm car}}V_{\rm glob}=0
\]

or

\[
\|P_{W_{\rm car}}V_{\rm glob}\|^2\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

Do we have that from quotienting nuisance carriers? No. The quotient only kills nuisance directions:

\[
\mathsf C_{\rm line},\quad
\mathsf C_{\rm bg/core},\quad
\mathsf C_{\rm norm/sub},\quad
\mathsf C_{N7,*},\quad
\mathsf C_{N5,*}.
\]

It does not kill the live carriers \(\mathsf C_7,\mathsf C_{Y12}\). Those are precisely the surviving D4 directions.

So:

\[
\boxed{
W_{\rm car}\text{ is live, not forbidden, unless a separate global identity kills it.}
}
\]

The carrier quotient alone is insufficient.

---

# 3. Natural candidate 2: scalar \(I\)-coordinate cancellation

Another possible forbidden channel is the scalar \(I\)-coordinate. Earlier scalar \(I\)-coordinate D4 was quarantined because distinct carrier classes can collapse to the same scalar coordinate and cancel.

Could that cancellation provide the global forbidden upper?

No, not in the active route.

The active D4 theorem deliberately avoids scalar \(I\)-coordinate coercivity. It keeps carrier classes separated in

\[
\mathcal Q_{\rm car}.
\]

If we reintroduce scalar \(I\)-coordinate cancellation as the forbidden channel, we are reopening the fallback route that the coordinator explicitly quarantined. Moreover, scalar cancellation is an obstruction to proving a lower bound, not a clean global upper for the carrier quotient.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\text{scalar }I\text{-coordinate cancellation is not the forbidden channel for Stack C2.}
}
\]

It is a fallback obstruction, not the desired contradiction mechanism.

---

# 4. Natural candidate 3: global exact row identity / cokernel

The strongest candidate is a global linear identity.

Suppose there is a global row operator

\[
\mathcal L:\mathcal H_{\rm row}\to\mathcal K
\]

such that actual canonical rows satisfy

\[
\mathcal L V_{\rm glob}=0.
\]

Then the forbidden channel is

\[
\mathcal H_{\rm forb}
=
(\ker\mathcal L)^\perp
=
\overline{\operatorname{ran}\mathcal L^*}.
\]

For actual rows,

\[
P_{\rm forb}V_{\rm glob}=0.
\]

If D4 produces local certificates with

\[
\|P_{\rm forb}v_m\|^2\ge Q^{-C}\|v_m\|^2,
\]

then contradiction follows after forbidden-channel row mobility.

This is the cleanest structure:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{GlobalRowIdentity}
+
\mathrm{D4TransversalityToIdentity}
+
\mathrm{ForbiddenRowMobility}
\Longrightarrow
\text{contradiction}.
}
\]

This suggests the next downstream target:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Identify the global row identity whose cokernel contains the D4 live channel.}
}
\]

If the draft has an existing “row energy contradiction,” it must correspond to exactly such an identity or to an equivalent global coercivity upper.

---

# 5. Natural candidate 4: telescoping / coboundary channel

A second viable structure is telescoping.

Suppose row targets are edges or differences, and local certificates are discrete derivatives:

\[
v_m=\delta a_m.
\]

If the global sum over a closed chain telescopes,

\[
\sum_m \delta a_m=0,
\]

then the row channel corresponding to nonzero boundary/curl is forbidden.

In this case the forbidden channel is a cohomological one:

\[
\mathcal H_{\rm forb}
=
\operatorname{coker}(\delta)
\quad\text{or}\quad
\ker(\delta^*)^\perp,
\]

depending on the formulation.

This could be compatible with “row mobility” if mobility transports local row certificates along chains. But it requires a precise chain complex:

\[
C_0\xrightarrow{\delta}C_1\xrightarrow{\delta}C_2,
\]

and a global identity like

\[
\delta^2=0.
\]

Then D4 must land in a curl/boundary defect channel that globally vanishes.

This is mathematically plausible if the row-energy architecture is based on adjacent transport or mobility around loops.

The needed theorem would be:

\[
\boxed{
P_{\rm curl}v_m\text{ carries polynomial D4 mass, while }P_{\rm curl}V_{\rm glob}=0.
}
\]

No such structure is automatic; it must be explicitly present.

---

# 6. Natural candidate 5: sign-definite forbidden quadratic form

Instead of a forbidden subspace, there may be a signed quadratic form

\[
\mathcal Q_{\rm glob}
\]

such that globally

\[
\mathcal Q_{\rm glob}(V_{\rm glob})\le 0,
\]

while D4 locally gives

\[
\mathcal Q_{\rm glob}(v_m)\ge Q^{-C}\|v_m\|^2.
\]

This is weaker than exact nullity but still works.

However, for aggregate summation one also needs cross-term control:

\[
\mathcal Q_{\rm glob}\left(\sum_m\sqrt{\mu(m)}v_m\right)
\ge
Q^{-C}\sum_m\mu(m)\mathcal Q_{\rm glob}(v_m).
\]

So this again reduces to a lower-frame/noncancellation theorem, now for an indefinite form.

This path is more delicate than the projection/null-channel path.

---

# 7. Critical no-go

If the only known global upper is a total-energy upper

\[
\|V_{\rm glob}\|^2\le Q^C\mu(\mathcal C),
\]

and the local lower is

\[
\|V_{\rm glob}\|^2\ge Q^{-C'}\mu(\mathcal C),
\]

then there is no contradiction.

Therefore the downstream architecture must exhibit one of:

\[
\boxed{
\text{exact forbidden subspace}}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{small forbidden error channel}}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{signed global incompatibility}}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{telescoping/coboundary cancellation}}
\]

Without one of these, the aggregate downstream is not a proof engine; it is only an energy-production mechanism.

---

# 8. Refined Stack C2 downstream theorem

The correct theorem should be stated as:

Assume joint extraction gives \(\mathcal G\) with

\[
\mu(\mathcal G)\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C)
\]

and

\[
E_1,E_{23},E_{\rm wall}^{\rm full}\ge Q^{-A}
\]

on \(\mathcal G\).

Assume local Gate2/D4 gives

\[
v_m\in \mathcal H_{\rm row},
\qquad
\|v_m\|^2\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Assume there exists a forbidden projection

\[
P_{\rm forb}
\]

such that:

\[
\tag{F1}
\|P_{\rm forb}V_{\rm glob}\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C),
\]

\[
\tag{F2}
\|P_{\rm forb}v_m\|^2
\ge Q^{-A}\|v_m\|^2
\quad(m\in\mathcal G),
\]

and forbidden-channel mobility gives

\[
\tag{F3}
\|P_{\rm forb}V_{\rm glob}\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}\mu(m)\|P_{\rm forb}v_m\|^2.
\]

Then

\[
\|P_{\rm forb}V_{\rm glob}\|^2
\ge Q^{-A'}\mu(\mathcal C),
\]

contradicting F1 if \(B>A'\).

This is now the minimal downstream contradiction theorem.

---

# 9. What Agent 6 should target next

The next precise mathematical task is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Identify }P_{\rm forb}.
}
\]

Concretely, inspect the row-energy architecture and determine whether the global contradiction is based on:

1. a global row identity,
2. a telescoping/coboundary identity,
3. a small tail/error channel,
4. an indefinite sign contradiction,
5. or something else.

Then prove one of:

\[
\boxed{
P_{\rm forb}[\mathsf C_7]\ne0
\quad\text{or}\quad
P_{\rm forb}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\ne0
}
\]

with polynomial lower angle:

\[
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
W_{\rm car},
\ker P_{\rm forb}
\right)
\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

That is the exact D4-to-forbidden-channel transversality theorem.

---

# 10. Final state after this attack

The aggregate Stack C2 route now has a sharper downstream dependency:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23,\mathrm{wall}}
+
\mathrm{ForbiddenChannelTransversality}
+
\mathrm{ForbiddenRowMobility}_{\rm lower}
+
\mathrm{ForbiddenRowSmall}
\Longrightarrow
\text{contradiction}.
}
\]

The strongest remaining downstream unknown is no longer generic row mobility. It is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Does a forbidden row channel exist, and does D4 land in it?}
}
\]

If yes, the downstream side is structurally viable.

If no, corrected Stack C2 still lacks a contradiction mechanism.
