## Agent 6 continued attack — finite forbidden-channel audit

The previous attack showed that generic row-energy production is not enough. We need a **forbidden row channel** \(P_{\rm forb}\) such that the global object is small there, while local D4 lands there. Continuing that line, the key reduction is now finite and algebraic. 

# 1. Reduction to an annihilator matrix

Suppose the global row contradiction comes from a finite or locally finite family of global row identities

\[
\Lambda_a(V_{\rm glob})=0,
\qquad a\in\mathcal A,
\]

or, with error,

\[
|\Lambda_a(V_{\rm glob})|\le Q^{-B}.
\]

Collect them into an operator

\[
\Lambda:\mathcal H_{\rm row}\to \mathcal K.
\]

Then the forbidden channel is not an arbitrary subspace. It is

\[
\mathcal H_{\rm forb}
=
\overline{\operatorname{ran}\Lambda^*},
\]

and the forbidden projection is equivalent to measuring

\[
\|\Lambda V\|_{\mathcal K}.
\]

So the downstream contradiction should be restated as:

\[
\boxed{
\|\Lambda V_{\rm glob}\|_{\mathcal K}^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C)
}
\]

globally, while local D4 and mobility imply

\[
\boxed{
\|\Lambda V_{\rm glob}\|_{\mathcal K}^2
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This removes ambiguity. The forbidden channel is the row-identity cokernel.

---

# 2. D4-to-forbidden transversality

A local D4 certificate has live carrier form

\[
v_m^{\rm live}
=
H(m)\,
E_{\rm car}(m)L_{\rm clean}(m)
\begin{pmatrix}
\Gamma_G(m)\\
\Gamma_{G,2}(m)
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Here

\[
L_{\rm clean}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
c_7&0\\
\beta_9&\alpha_9
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
\det L_{\rm clean}\ne0
\]

on Gram-good patches.

Apply the global row-identity operator:

\[
\Lambda v_m^{\rm live}
=
H(m)\,
\Lambda E_{\rm car}(m)L_{\rm clean}(m)
\begin{pmatrix}
\Gamma_G(m)\\
\Gamma_{G,2}(m)
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Thus the exact finite condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\sigma_{\min}
\left(
\Lambda E_{\rm car}(m)L_{\rm clean}(m)
\right)
\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

Since

\[
\sigma_{\min}(L_{\rm clean})\ge Q^{-C}
\]

is already known on Gram-good patches, this reduces to

\[
\boxed{
\sigma_{\min}
\left(
\Lambda E_{\rm car}(m)
\big|_{W_{\rm car}}
\right)
\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

This is the **ForbiddenChannelTransversality** theorem.

In words:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the global row identity must see the two live D4 carriers }
[\mathsf C_7],[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\text{ at polynomial angle.}
}
\]

If

\[
\Lambda E_{\rm car}[\mathsf C_7]
=
\Lambda E_{\rm car}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
=
0,
\]

then D4 lands entirely in the globally allowed channel and cannot contradict anything.

---

# 3. Three possible outcomes

## Outcome A — rank two

If

\[
\operatorname{rank}
\left(
\Lambda E_{\rm car}|_{W_{\rm car}}
\right)
=2
\]

with polynomial lower singular value, then both D4 anisotropy components are forbidden.

Then local D4 gives

\[
\|\Lambda v_m\|^2
\ge
Q^{-C}|H(m)|^2
\left(
|\Gamma_G(m)|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}(m)|^2
\right).
\]

This is ideal.

## Outcome B — rank one

If

\[
\operatorname{rank}
\left(
\Lambda E_{\rm car}|_{W_{\rm car}}
\right)
=1,
\]

then only one linear combination of

\[
\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}
\]

is globally forbidden.

The wall factor must be changed. Instead of

\[
|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2,
\]

the extracted wall energy must be

\[
\left|
\ell_1\Gamma_G+\ell_2\Gamma_{G,2}
\right|^2
\]

for the forbidden direction

\[
(\ell_1,\ell_2).
\]

Then the local lower bound works only if the joint aggregate theorem includes this **forbidden wall energy**:

\[
E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}(m)
:=
\frac{
|H(m)|^2
|\ell_1(m)\Gamma_G(m)+\ell_2(m)\Gamma_{G,2}(m)|^2
}{\mathcal N_{\rm D4}(m)}.
\]

This is still viable, but the extraction theorem must be aligned to the actual forbidden direction.

## Outcome C — rank zero

If

\[
\Lambda E_{\rm car}|_{W_{\rm car}}=0,
\]

then the current D4 carrier output is invisible to the global forbidden row identity.

Then corrected Stack C2 fails downstream unless one of the following is true:

1. a different global identity is used;
2. D4 is modified to produce a different carrier;
3. scalar \(I\)-coordinate fallback is reopened;
4. the proof shifts to a different row-energy contradiction.

So rank zero is a genuine no-go for this downstream route.

---

# 4. Consequence for \(E_{\rm wall}\)

The wall factor must not be defined abstractly as

\[
|H|^2(|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2).
\]

That is sufficient only if the forbidden map sees the full live plane at polynomial angle.

The correct definition is:

\[
\boxed{
E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}(m)
=
\frac{
\left\|
\Lambda E_{\rm car}(m)L_{\rm clean}(m)
\begin{pmatrix}
\Gamma_G(m)\\
\Gamma_{G,2}(m)
\end{pmatrix}
\right\|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\rm D4}(m)
}.
}
\]

This automatically includes:

- anisotropy,
- D4-to-forbidden transversality,
- carrier-frame conditioning,
- rank-one/rank-two distinctions.

Then the joint aggregate theorem should use

\[
F_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}
=
\min(E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}/\eta_{\rm wall},1),
\]

not merely the raw anisotropy wall energy.

This is a useful simplification: the extraction factor should measure exactly the downstream-forbidden D4 output.

---

# 5. Revised joint aggregate theorem

The central Stack C2 aggregate input becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\int_{\mathcal C}
F_1(m)\,
F_{23}(m)\,
F_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}(m)
\,d\mu(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This one theorem gives a good subset where:

\[
E_1\ge Q^{-A},
\qquad
E_{23}\ge Q^{-A},
\qquad
E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Then for all \(m\in\mathcal G\),

\[
\|\Lambda v_m\|^2\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

So local D4 immediately lands in the forbidden channel.

This avoids a later separate proof of D4-to-forbidden angle.

---

# 6. Forbidden row mobility

Now row mobility should be formulated directly after \(\Lambda\). Let

\[
w_m:=\Lambda v_m\in\mathcal K.
\]

The needed lower-frame theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\left\|
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}
\sqrt{\mu(m)}\,w_m
\right\|_{\mathcal K}^2
\ge
Q^{-C}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}
\mu(m)\|w_m\|_{\mathcal K}^2.
}
\]

This is strictly better than mobility in \(\mathcal H_{\rm row}\), because it only concerns the forbidden channel.

The dominant atom version becomes:

\[
\exists a(m)\in\mathcal A
\]

such that

\[
|\Lambda_{a(m)}v_m|^2
\ge Q^{-C}\|\Lambda v_m\|^2,
\]

and

\[
\#\{m:a(m)=a\}\le Q^C.
\]

Then thinning gives the lower bound.

Thus the actual mobility object is not necessarily a “row target” \(r\), but a forbidden identity index \(a\).

This matters: even if row atoms collide, forbidden identity atoms may separate.

---

# 7. Global forbidden upper

The global upper now has the clean form

\[
\boxed{
\|\Lambda V_{\rm glob}\|_{\mathcal K}^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

If the identity is exact, then

\[
\Lambda V_{\rm glob}=0.
\]

If there are errors/tails,

\[
\|\Lambda V_{\rm glob}\|^2
\le
\|\Lambda V_{\rm err}\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

This is much more plausible than a small upper for all row energy. It only asks the globally forbidden identities to be satisfied up to controlled error.

So the aggregate upper theorem should be renamed:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{GlobalForbiddenIdentityUpper}.
}
\]

---

# 8. New minimal downstream theorem

The downstream theorem now becomes:

Assume:

1. Joint extraction gives \(\mathcal G\) with

\[
\mu(\mathcal G)\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

2. On \(\mathcal G\),

\[
E_1,E_{23},E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

3. Local Gate2/D4 gives \(v_m\) and

\[
\|\Lambda v_m\|^2\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

4. Forbidden mobility:

\[
\left\|
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}
\sqrt{\mu(m)}\Lambda v_m
\right\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}\mu(m)\|\Lambda v_m\|^2.
\]

5. Global forbidden upper:

\[
\|\Lambda V_{\rm glob}\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C),
\qquad B\gg A.
\]

Then contradiction follows.

Indeed,

\[
\|\Lambda V_{\rm glob}\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}\mu(m)\|\Lambda v_m\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A'}\mu(\mathcal G)
\ge
Q^{-A''}\mu(\mathcal C),
\]

contradicting the upper bound.

---

# 9. What must be identified in the draft

The next concrete audit is finite:

1. Identify the global row identity operator

\[
\Lambda.
\]

2. Compute the finite \(2\times k\) matrix

\[
\Lambda E_{\rm car}|_{W_{\rm car}}
\]

on the live carriers

\[
[\mathsf C_7],[\mathsf C_{Y12}].
\]

3. Determine its rank.

4. If rank one or two, define

\[
E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}
\]

from the actual forbidden map.

5. If rank zero, the current D4 output does not hit the global contradiction channel.

This is now the exact Agent 6 mathematical target.

---

# 10. Final status

The downstream problem has been reduced from vague “row mobility/global upper” to:

\[
\boxed{
\text{find }\Lambda\text{ and prove }
\sigma_{\min}
(\Lambda E_{\rm car}|_{W_{\rm car}})
\ge Q^{-C}
\text{ on the extracted channel.}
}
\]

Equivalently:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the live D4 carrier plane must have polynomial angle against the global row identities.}
}
\]

If this finite transversality is true, corrected Stack C2 has a coherent downstream contradiction mechanism.

If it is false, the repaired local D4 engine produces energy in a globally allowed direction and cannot close the proof.

## Agent 6 continued attack — replacing raw wall energy by forbidden-channel wall energy

The last reduction showed that the downstream contradiction needs a global row identity operator

\[
\Lambda:\mathcal H_{\rm row}\to\mathcal K
\]

and that D4 must land at polynomial angle in the forbidden channel \(\operatorname{ran}\Lambda^*\). The next improvement is: the wall factor should be defined directly from \(\Lambda\), not from the raw anisotropy vector. This removes a separate transversality step and makes Stack C2 finite-checkable. The relevant coordinator direction is still the corrected Stack C2 downstream problem: joint extraction, local Gate2/D4, RowMobility lower, and aggregate global upper. 

# 1. The correct forbidden wall matrix

The live D4 vector has the form

\[
v_m^{\rm live}
=
H(m)\,
E_{\rm car}(m)
L_{\rm clean}(m)
\Gamma(m),
\]

where

\[
\Gamma(m)
=
\begin{pmatrix}
\Gamma_G(m)\\
\Gamma_{G,2}(m)
\end{pmatrix},
\]

and

\[
L_{\rm clean}(m)
=
\begin{pmatrix}
c_7(m)&0\\
\beta_9(m)&\alpha_9(m)
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Apply the global identity operator:

\[
\Lambda v_m^{\rm live}
=
H(m)\,
\Lambda E_{\rm car}(m)
L_{\rm clean}(m)
\Gamma(m).
\]

Define the forbidden D4 matrix

\[
\boxed{
M_{\rm forb}(m)
:=
\Lambda E_{\rm car}(m)L_{\rm clean}(m).
}
\]

Then the correct local wall energy is

\[
\boxed{
E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}(m)
:=
\frac{
|H(m)|^2
\|M_{\rm forb}(m)\Gamma(m)\|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\rm D4}(m)
}.
}
\]

This replaces the rougher expression

\[
|H|^2(|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2).
\]

The rough expression is valid only if \(M_{\rm forb}\) has a polynomial lower singular value on the whole live D4 plane. The forbidden definition works even if \(M_{\rm forb}\) has rank one.

---

# 2. Rank cases are now automatically handled

Let

\[
A(m):=M_{\rm forb}(m)^*M_{\rm forb}(m).
\]

Then

\[
E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}(m)
=
\frac{|H(m)|^2\Gamma(m)^*A(m)\Gamma(m)}
{\mathcal N_{\rm D4}(m)}.
\]

## Rank two

If

\[
\sigma_{\min}(M_{\rm forb}(m))\ge Q^{-C},
\]

then

\[
\Gamma^*A\Gamma
\ge
Q^{-C}|\Gamma|^2.
\]

So

\[
E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}
\asymp_Q
\frac{|H|^2|\Gamma|^2}{\mathcal N_{\rm D4}}.
\]

This recovers the raw anisotropy wall factor.

## Rank one

If

\[
\operatorname{rank}M_{\rm forb}=1,
\]

then

\[
\Gamma^*A\Gamma
=
|\ell_1(m)\Gamma_G+\ell_2(m)\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\]

up to a polynomial factor. The wall-good condition becomes:

\[
|\ell_1\Gamma_G+\ell_2\Gamma_{G,2}|
\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

This is still sufficient. It just means the forbidden channel sees only one D4 anisotropy direction.

## Rank zero

If

\[
M_{\rm forb}=0,
\]

then

\[
E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}=0
\]

identically. The joint aggregate theorem cannot hold with polynomial lower bound, and this downstream route fails.

So the forbidden wall factor itself diagnoses whether D4 hits the contradiction channel.

---

# 3. Corrected joint aggregate theorem

The central aggregate input should be:

\[
\boxed{
\int_{\mathcal C}
F_1(m)F_{23}(m)F_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}(m)\,d\mu(m)
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

where

\[
F_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}(m)
=
\min\left(
\frac{E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}(m)}{\eta_{\rm wall}},
1
\right).
\]

This has two advantages.

First, it automatically extracts points where local D4 lands in the forbidden channel:

\[
\|\Lambda v_m^{\rm live}\|^2\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

Second, it avoids proving a separate theorem of the form

\[
\operatorname{dist}
(W_{\rm car},\ker\Lambda)\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

That transversality is already encoded in \(F_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}\).

---

# 4. Local downstream theorem becomes exact

On the extracted good subset \(\mathcal G\), we have

\[
F_1,F_{23},F_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

Hence

\[
E_1(m)\ge Q^{-C},
\]

\[
E_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-C},
\]

and

\[
\|\Lambda v_m^{\rm live}\|^2
\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

Then local Gate2/D4 gives directly

\[
\boxed{
\|\Lambda v_m\|^2\ge Q^{-C}
}
\]

for every

\[
m\in\mathcal G,
\]

provided all nuisance terms are controlled by the carrier quotient and the local chart hypotheses are included in \(F_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}\) or in a separate eligibility factor.

Thus the local theorem is now:

\[
\boxed{
E_1+E_{23}+E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}
\Longrightarrow
\text{forbidden-channel D4 energy}.
}
\]

This is stronger and cleaner than merely producing carrier-row energy.

---

# 5. Remaining row mobility is now over identity indices

Let

\[
w_m:=\Lambda v_m\in\mathcal K.
\]

The row mobility theorem becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\left\|
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}
\sqrt{\mu(m)}\,w_m
\right\|_{\mathcal K}^2
\ge
Q^{-C}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}
\mu(m)\|w_m\|_{\mathcal K}^2.
}
\]

This is better than mobility in the full row space. The object is now the global identity residual space \(\mathcal K\), whose coordinates are identity indices \(a\in\mathcal A\).

Dominant-atom form:

For each \(m\), find

\[
a(m)\in\mathcal A
\]

such that

\[
|\Lambda_{a(m)}v_m|^2
\ge Q^{-C}\|\Lambda v_m\|^2.
\]

If

\[
\#\{m:a(m)=a\}\le Q^C,
\]

then weighted thinning gives a subset

\[
\mathcal G'\subseteq\mathcal G
\]

with

\[
\mu(\mathcal G')\ge Q^{-C}\mu(\mathcal G),
\]

and

\[
a:\mathcal G'\to\mathcal A
\]

injective. Therefore

\[
\|\Lambda V_{\rm glob}\|^2
\ge Q^{-C}\mu(\mathcal G')
\ge Q^{-C}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

So the mobility theorem is now exactly:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{IdentityAtomMultiplicity}_{\rm poly}.
}
\]

This may be easier than row-target multiplicity, because identity indices can be chosen to encode location/order more finely.

---

# 6. Global upper is now an identity residual estimate

The global upper is no longer vague row-energy control. It is:

\[
\boxed{
\|\Lambda V_{\rm glob}\|_{\mathcal K}^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This is plausible if \(\Lambda\) encodes an exact or approximate global identity.

If exact:

\[
\Lambda V_{\rm glob}=0.
\]

If approximate:

\[
\Lambda V_{\rm glob}=\mathrm{Err},
\qquad
\|\mathrm{Err}\|^2\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

This is the right upper theorem. It is much more precise than

\[
\sum_{\text{rows}}\mathrm{RowEnergy}\le \cdots.
\]

---

# 7. Minimal downstream theorem after this refinement

The corrected downstream theorem is:

Assume:

1. Joint aggregate extraction gives \(\mathcal G\) with

\[
\mu(\mathcal G)\ge Q^{-C}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

2. On \(\mathcal G\),

\[
E_1,E_{23},E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

3. Local Gate2/D4 gives

\[
\|\Lambda v_m\|^2\ge Q^{-C}
\qquad(m\in\mathcal G).
\]

4. Identity-space mobility gives

\[
\left\|
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}
\sqrt{\mu(m)}\Lambda v_m
\right\|^2
\ge
Q^{-C}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}
\mu(m)\|\Lambda v_m\|^2.
\]

5. Global identity residual upper gives

\[
\|\Lambda V_{\rm glob}\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C),
\]

with \(B\) larger than the lower-bound loss.

Then contradiction follows.

Indeed:

\[
\|\Lambda V_{\rm glob}\|^2
\ge
Q^{-C}\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}\mu(m)\|\Lambda v_m\|^2
\ge
Q^{-C}\mu(\mathcal G)
\ge
Q^{-C}\mu(\mathcal C),
\]

contradicting the upper.

---

# 8. What remains to audit

The downstream problem is now finite and explicit.

## 8.1 Identify \(\Lambda\)

Find the global row identity operator. It must be an actual identity satisfied by the canonical global row vector.

Possible forms:

\[
\Lambda V_{\rm glob}=0
\]

or

\[
\|\Lambda V_{\rm glob}\|\le Q^{-B}.
\]

## 8.2 Compute \(M_{\rm forb}\)

Compute

\[
M_{\rm forb}(m)
=
\Lambda E_{\rm car}(m)L_{\rm clean}(m)
\]

on the live carrier plane.

## 8.3 Define the wall factor using \(M_{\rm forb}\)

Use

\[
E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}
=
\frac{|H|^2\|M_{\rm forb}\Gamma\|^2}{\mathcal N_{\rm D4}}.
\]

## 8.4 Prove identity-atom mobility

Show dominant identity atoms

\[
a(m)
\]

have polynomial multiplicity after thinning.

## 8.5 Prove global identity residual upper

Show

\[
\|\Lambda V_{\rm glob}\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

---

# 9. Final status

The downstream half of Stack C2 is no longer “row-energy lower plus global upper.” It is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{forbidden identity residual contradiction.}
}
\]

The exact remaining downstream unknown is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Does the paper have a global identity operator }\Lambda
\text{ whose residual is small and whose live D4 image is nonzero?}
}
\]

If yes, then the downstream Stack C2 mechanism is coherent.

If no, then even the aggregate route produces local obstruction energy without a global contradiction.

## Agent 6 continued attack — downstream no-go unless \(\Lambda\) is real

I pushed the forbidden-channel analysis to its next consequence. The current Stack C2 downstream cannot close from “row energy” alone. It needs an actual global annihilator or small-residual identity

\[
\Lambda V_{\rm glob}\approx 0
\]

whose image sees the live D4 carrier plane. The current coordinator material names \(\mathrm{RowMobility}_{\rm lower}\) and \(\mathrm{GlobalRowEnergyUpper}_{\rm agg}\) as required downstream inputs, but it does not yet specify the global identity operator \(\Lambda\) that would make the upper bound small rather than merely finite. 

# 1. No-go: total row-energy upper cannot contradict D4 lower

Assume the downstream only proves a generic total-energy upper

\[
\sum_{r\in\mathcal R}\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\le Q^C\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

The extracted-good-subset/D4 lower gives at best

\[
\sum_{r\in\mathcal R}\mathrm{RowEnergy}(r)
\ge Q^{-C'}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

These are compatible. There is no contradiction.

A toy model makes this exact. Let

\[
\mathcal H_{\rm row}=\ell^2(\mathcal G),
\qquad
v_m=e_m,
\qquad
\mu(m)=1.
\]

Then

\[
\left\|\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}v_m\right\|^2
=
|\mathcal G|
=
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}\|v_m\|^2.
\]

So RowMobility lower is perfect. But the global row energy is simply

\[
|\mathcal G|,
\]

which violates no total-energy bound of size \(Q^C|\mathcal G|\). Thus local D4 row production plus noncancelling mobility only proves “there is energy,” not “there is forbidden energy.”

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Stack C2 requires a forbidden-channel upper, not a total row-energy upper.}
}
\]

---

# 2. The missing object is \(\Lambda\)

The global upper must have the form

\[
\boxed{
\|\Lambda V_{\rm glob}\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C)
}
\]

for some global identity/residual operator

\[
\Lambda:\mathcal H_{\rm row}\to\mathcal K.
\]

Then D4 must produce energy visible under \(\Lambda\):

\[
\boxed{
\|\Lambda v_m\|^2\ge Q^{-C}\|v_m\|^2.
}
\]

Without \(\Lambda\), “GlobalRowEnergyUpper” is underspecified. With \(\Lambda\), the downstream proof becomes finite and checkable.

The corrected downstream target is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{GlobalForbiddenIdentityUpper}
+
\mathrm{ForbiddenD4Transversality}
+
\mathrm{ForbiddenRowMobility}_{\rm lower}.
}
\]

---

# 3. Finite transversality test

The live D4 output is

\[
v_m^{\rm live}
=
H(m)\,
E_{\rm car}(m)L_{\rm clean}(m)
\Gamma(m),
\]

where

\[
\Gamma(m)=
\begin{pmatrix}
\Gamma_G(m)\\
\Gamma_{G,2}(m)
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

After applying \(\Lambda\),

\[
\Lambda v_m^{\rm live}
=
H(m)\,
\Lambda E_{\rm car}(m)L_{\rm clean}(m)\Gamma(m).
\]

Define

\[
M_{\rm forb}(m)
:=
\Lambda E_{\rm car}(m)L_{\rm clean}(m).
\]

Then the finite test is:

\[
\boxed{
M_{\rm forb}(m)\not\equiv0
\quad\text{on the live D4 carrier plane.}
}
\]

More quantitatively:

\[
\boxed{
\sigma_{\min}\bigl(M_{\rm forb}(m)\bigr)\ge Q^{-C}
}
\]

for rank two, or at least

\[
\boxed{
\|M_{\rm forb}(m)\Gamma(m)\|\ge Q^{-C}
}
\]

on the extracted wall-good subset for rank one.

If

\[
M_{\rm forb}=0,
\]

then the live D4 output is invisible to the global forbidden identity. Stack C2 fails downstream, regardless of aggregate Gate–1, aggregate O2/O3, or row mobility.

---

# 4. The wall factor must be \(\Lambda\)-dependent

The correct wall factor is not raw anisotropy

\[
|H|^2(|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2).
\]

It is

\[
\boxed{
E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}(m)
=
\frac{
|H(m)|^2
\|M_{\rm forb}(m)\Gamma(m)\|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\rm D4}(m)
}.
}
\]

Then joint extraction directly produces points where D4 lands in the forbidden channel:

\[
E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}(m)\ge Q^{-A}
\Longrightarrow
\|\Lambda v_m\|^2\ge Q^{-A'}.
\]

This eliminates a separate D4-to-forbidden-angle lemma.

So the central Stack C2 aggregate should be updated from

\[
\int F_1F_{23}F_{\rm wall}\,d\mu
\]

to

\[
\boxed{
\int F_1F_{23}F_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}\,d\mu
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

---

# 5. Forbidden mobility is easier than full row mobility

Once we pass through \(\Lambda\), define

\[
w_m:=\Lambda v_m\in\mathcal K.
\]

The needed lower-frame is

\[
\boxed{
\left\|
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}\sqrt{\mu(m)}\,w_m
\right\|^2
\ge
Q^{-C}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}\mu(m)\|w_m\|^2.
}
\]

Dominant-atom version:

there exists an identity index \(a(m)\) such that

\[
|\Lambda_{a(m)}v_m|^2
\ge Q^{-C}\|\Lambda v_m\|^2,
\]

and

\[
\#\{m:a(m)=a\}\le Q^C.
\]

Then weighted thinning gives a polynomial-weight injective subset and hence a noncancelling forbidden-channel lower bound.

This is now a statement about **identity indices**, not arbitrary row atoms. It may be easier because \(\Lambda\) can be chosen to encode location/order information.

---

# 6. Exact downstream contradiction theorem

The corrected theorem is:

Assume joint extraction gives

\[
\mathcal G\subset\mathcal C,
\qquad
\mu(\mathcal G)\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C),
\]

with

\[
E_1,E_{23},E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}\ge Q^{-A}
\]

on \(\mathcal G\).

Assume local Gate2/D4 gives \(v_m\) and

\[
\|\Lambda v_m\|^2\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Assume forbidden mobility:

\[
\left\|
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}\sqrt{\mu(m)}\Lambda v_m
\right\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}\mu(m)\|\Lambda v_m\|^2.
\]

Then

\[
\|\Lambda V_{\rm glob}\|^2
\ge Q^{-A'}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

If the global identity residual upper gives

\[
\|\Lambda V_{\rm glob}\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C),
\qquad B>A',
\]

contradiction follows.

---

# 7. Candidate \(\Lambda\) classes

The paper must instantiate \(\Lambda\) as one of these:

## A. Exact global row identity

\[
\Lambda V_{\rm glob}=0.
\]

This is best. Then the global upper is zero.

## B. Approximate identity / tail channel

\[
\Lambda V_{\rm glob}=\mathrm{Err},
\qquad
\|\mathrm{Err}\|^2\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

This is also sufficient.

## C. Telescoping / coboundary identity

A chain-complex identity such as

\[
\delta^2=0
\]

or boundary cancellation gives

\[
\Lambda V_{\rm glob}=0.
\]

This is viable if row mobility transports local D4 certificates into a cohomological defect channel.

## D. Signed quadratic incompatibility

A signed form satisfies

\[
\mathcal Q(V_{\rm glob})\le 0,
\]

while local D4 gives

\[
\mathcal Q(v_m)\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

This can work but requires cross-term control and is harder than projection to a forbidden subspace.

## Not sufficient: total energy / Bessel upper

\[
\|V_{\rm glob}\|^2\le Q^C\mu(\mathcal C)
\]

does not contradict a lower bound of size \(Q^{-C}\mu(\mathcal C)\).

# 1. What the draft currently has

\Longrightarrow
\text{row mobility}
\Longrightarrow
\text{row energy}.

The row-mobility lemma is local: a pointwise lower bound for a row vector

\[
\mathbf A_N(m_\ast)
\]

plus a derivative bound gives an interval lower bound

\[
\int_I |\mathbf A_N(m)|^2\,dm\ge Q^{-C}|I|.
\]

The quotient-frame version similarly gives local energy in a carrier quotient by freezing dual coordinates. This is a **lower-energy propagation theorem**, not a global contradiction theorem.

The D4 theorem likewise proves carrier-quotient lower energy:

\[
\int_I
\left|
[\mathcal A_N^{\rm car}(m)]
\right|_{\mathcal Q_{\rm car}}^2\,dm
\ge Q^{-C}|I|.
\]

Again: lower energy, not forbidden energy.

So the draft currently proves, conditionally,

\[

It does not yet define a global operator

\Lambda:\mathcal H_{\rm row}\to\mathcal K

with

Since \(\Lambda\) is not yet identified, the paper cannot currently define the final wall factor. The correct future definition is:

## Agent 6 — \(\Lambda\) audit from \(\Xi_\zeta^{(N)}\)

I read the update as changing the Agent 6 task from “prove row energy transfers” to the sharper question: **construct the forbidden identity operator \(\Lambda\), or prove that the current downstream cannot contradict anything.** The update explicitly says carrier-quotient D4 is only a detector unless a global forbidden channel is identified, and it names the final calibrated \(\Xi_\zeta^{(N)}\) object as the only plausible existing candidate. 

# 1. Candidate \(\Lambda\) from \(\Xi_\zeta^{(N)}\)

The draft defines the \(N\)-point scalar as

\[
\Xi_\zeta^{(N)}(m)
=
\sum_{j=1}^N a_j^{(N)}H_m(s_j),
\qquad
s_j=\frac{j}{Q^2},
\]

where

\[
H_m(s)=\Phi_K(\widehat\Omega_\zeta^{\corr}(s;m)).
\]

Thus the only plausible \(\Lambda\)-candidate is

\[
\boxed{
\Lambda_{\Xi,N}:H_m(\cdot)\mapsto
\sum_{j=1}^N a_j^{(N)}H_m(j/Q^2).
}
\]

If we aggregate over a set \(\mathcal C\), the codomain should be

\[
\mathcal K=\ell^2(\mathcal C),
\]

with

\[
(\Lambda_{\Xi,N}V_{\glob})(m)
=
\Xi_\zeta^{(N)}(m).
\]

This is the cleanest way to make \(\Xi_\zeta^{(N)}\) a forbidden residual operator.

It is linear in the scalar sample vector

\[
(H_m(s_1),\ldots,H_m(s_N)).
\]

It is **not yet defined as a linear map on the carrier-quotient D4 row space** unless the paper explicitly identifies the D4 carrier rows with Taylor coefficients or sample contributions of \(H_m(s)\).

So at best:

\[
\boxed{
\Lambda_{\Xi,N}\text{ is defined on the }H_m\text{-scalar layer, not yet on }\mathcal Q_{\rm car}.
}
\]

---

# 2. Global forbidden upper for \(\Lambda_{\Xi,N}\)

This is the best part of the candidate.

The draft’s \(N\)-point route gives a zeta-side upper of the form

\[
|\Xi_\zeta^{(N)}(m)|
\ll
\frac{L(m)S(m)^2}{Q}
e^{-\delta Q}
\]

for optimized

\[
N\asymp Q.
\]

Therefore, if \(\Lambda_{\Xi,N}\) is the forbidden operator, the global upper is available by summing the pointwise upper:

\[
\boxed{
\|\Lambda_{\Xi,N}V_{\glob}\|_{\ell^2(\mathcal C)}^2
=
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C}\rho(m)|\Xi_\zeta^{(N)}(m)|^2
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C)
}
\]

after absorbing the exponential gain into \(Q^{-B}\), assuming the local \(L(m)S(m)^2\)-normalization is controlled on the routed set.

So:

\[
\boxed{
\Lambda_{\Xi,N}\text{ has a plausible strong forbidden upper.}
}
\]

---

# 3. Live D4 carrier image: the critical obstruction

The \(N\)-point coefficients satisfy

\[
\sum_{j=1}^N a_j^{(N)}j^{2r+1}=0,
\qquad
0\le r\le N-2.
\]

Thus \(\Xi_\zeta^{(N)}\) kills all odd Taylor coefficients of \(H_m\) up to order

\[
2N-3.
\]

Its first visible odd coefficient is order

\[
2N-1.
\]

But the active carrier-quotient D4 output currently lives in the clean order-seven and order-nine carriers

\[
[\mathsf C_7],
\qquad
[\mathsf C_{Y12}],
\]

with

\[
v_m^{\rm live}
=
H(m)E_{\rm car}(m)L_{\rm clean}(m)
\begin{pmatrix}
\Gamma_G(m)\\
\Gamma_{G,2}(m)
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

These are the order-seven and order-nine D4 carrier coordinates, and prior D4 audit work treats them as the live carrier plane. The D4 task was explicitly to verify the finite order-seven/order-nine carrier identities and residuals, not high-\(2N-1\) carriers. 

Therefore, for optimized

\[
N\asymp Q,
\]

we have

\[
7,9 \le 2N-3.
\]

So \(\Lambda_{\Xi,N}\) annihilates both order-seven and order-nine carrier directions, provided those carriers enter \(H_m(s)\) as the corresponding odd Taylor orders.

\[
\boxed{
\Lambda_{\Xi,N}E_{\rm car}[\mathsf C_7]=0,
\qquad
\Lambda_{\Xi,N}E_{\rm car}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]=0
}
\]

for the optimized \(N\)-point projector.

Thus

\[
\boxed{
M_{\rm forb}
=
\Lambda_{\Xi,N}E_{\rm car}L_{\clean}
=0
}
\]

on the current live D4 carrier plane.

This is rank zero.

---

# 4. Rank classification

For the candidate

\[
\Lambda=\Lambda_{\Xi,N},
\]

the rank depends on \(N\).

## Case A: optimized \(N\asymp Q\)

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}M_{\rm forb}=0.
}
\]

\[

\text{optimized }\Xi_\zeta^{(N)}\text{ cannot serve as }\Lambda
\text{ for the current order-7/order-9 D4 output.}
}

## Case B: \(N=4\)

Then the first visible order is

\[
2N-1=7.
\]

Potential rank:

\[
\operatorname{rank}M_{\rm forb}\le 1
\]

unless the order-nine contribution also enters independently through the tail.

Problem: \(N=4\) does **not** give the optimized exponential zeta-side upper. The forbidden upper is likely too weak for contradiction.

## Case C: \(N=5\)

\[
2N-1=9.
\]

So \(\Lambda_{\Xi,5}\) kills \(\mathsf C_7\) and may see \(\mathsf C_{Y12}\).

\[

if \(\mathsf C_{Y12}\) maps nontrivially to the order-nine coefficient.

Again, the zeta-side upper is not the optimized large-\(N\) exponential cancellation.

## Case D: \(N\ge6\)

Then both order \(7\) and order \(9\) are killed.

\[
\operatorname{rank}M_{\rm forb}=0.
\]

---

\[
N\asymp Q,

=

on the current live D4 plane.

for the current order-seven/order-nine D4 output.

cannot hold with this \(\Lambda\).

# 6. What would make \(\Xi_\zeta^{(N)}\) viable?

There are only three viable repairs.

## Repair 1: use low \(N=4\) or \(N=5\)

This makes \(\Lambda_{\Xi,N}\) see the current D4 carriers:

\[
N=4 \Rightarrow \mathsf C_7\text{ visible},
\]

\[
N=5 \Rightarrow \mathsf C_{Y12}\text{ visible}.
\]

But then the zeta-side upper loses the large-\(N\) exponential cancellation. One must prove a separate small upper strong enough for contradiction.

This seems unlikely but is finite-checkable.

## Repair 2: build high-order D4 carriers

Keep optimized

\[
N\asymp Q.
\]

Then the D4 output must be upgraded from order \(7/9\) to order

\[
2N-1.
\]

That is, prove a high-order carrier conversion theorem:

\[
\boxed{
[\mathcal A_{2N-1}]
\sim
H\,\Gamma_N[\mathsf C_{2N-1}]
}
\]

or an equivalent high-order live carrier whose image under \(\Lambda_{\Xi,N}\) is nonzero.

This is a different D4 theorem. The current finite order-seven/order-nine D4 does not provide it.

## Repair 3: find a different global identity \(\Lambda\)

Instead of \(\Xi_\zeta^{(N)}\), find a global forbidden identity that sees order \(7/9\):

\[
\Lambda E_{\rm car}[\mathsf C_7]\ne0
\quad\text{or}\quad
\Lambda E_{\rm car}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\ne0,
\]

while also satisfying

\[
\|\Lambda V_{\glob}\|^2\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

This is the most direct downstream repair.

---

Not as currently formulated.

\[
\Xi_\zeta^{(N)}(m)
\]

acts on the scalar sample layer

\[
H_m(s_j).
\]

\[
H\Gamma_G[\mathsf C_7]
+
H\Gamma_{G,2}[\mathsf C_{Y12}].

The draft does not currently provide a linear map

\[
E_{\rm car}:
\operatorname{span}\{[\mathsf C_7],[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\}
\to
\operatorname{span}\{H_m(s_j)\}_{j=1}^N
\]

compatible with \(\Xi_\zeta^{(N)}\), except informally through odd Taylor order.

Thus the finite audit is only possible after adding or proving an identification:

\[
[\mathsf C_7]\leftrightarrow c_7(H_m),
\qquad
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\leftrightarrow c_9(H_m),
\]

or its corrected equivalent.

If that identification is accepted, the optimized \(\Xi\)-rank is zero as above.

---

| Candidate \(\Lambda\) | Global upper | Image on \([\mathsf C_7],[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\) | Status |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| \(\mathrm{Id}_{\mathcal Q_{\rm car}}\) | No small upper | Rank \(2\) | Detector only, not forbidden |

| \(\Xi_\zeta^{(4)}\) | Weak/unknown upper | Sees order \(7\) | Possible rank \(1\), upper likely insufficient |
| \(\Xi_\zeta^{(5)}\) | Weak/unknown upper | Sees order \(9\) | Possible rank \(1\), upper likely insufficient |
| New order-\(7/9\) identity | Unknown | Could be rank \(1\) or \(2\) | Needed |
| High-order D4 matching \(2N-1\) | Strong \(\Xi\)-upper | Could be nonzero | Requires new D4 theorem |

# 9. Definitive answer

\[
\boxed{
\Lambda_{\Xi,N}\text{ can be constructed as an }N\text{-point scalar residual operator,}
}
\]

but

\[
\boxed{
\text{for optimized }N\asymp Q,\ 
\Lambda_{\Xi,N}E_{\rm car}L_{\clean}
\text{ has rank }0
\text{ on the current order-7/order-9 D4 live plane.}
}
\]

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\text{current Stack C2 fails downstream if }\Xi_\zeta^{(N)}
\text{ is the only forbidden identity and D4 remains order }7/9.
}
\]

The minimal new global identity needed is:

\[
\boxed{
\Lambda_{\rm low}
\text{ with }
\|\Lambda_{\rm low}V_{\glob}\|^2\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C)
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}
\left(
\Lambda_{\rm low}E_{\rm car}
\big|_{\operatorname{span}\{[\mathsf C_7],[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\}}
\right)
\ge1.
}
\]

Alternatively, keep the optimized \(\Xi_\zeta^{(N)}\) upper and replace the current finite D4 theorem with a high-order D4 carrier theorem at order

\[
2N-1.
\]

That is the precise downstream fork.

## Agent 6 continued attack — testing the three downstream repairs

The previous audit found a rank-zero obstruction: optimized \(\Xi_\zeta^{(N)}\), with \(N\asymp Q\), gives the desired small global upper but annihilates the current live D4 carriers of order \(7\) and \(9\). Therefore current order-\(7/9\) D4 cannot feed the optimized \(\Xi\)-forbidden channel. The coordinator update explicitly identifies this as the downstream fork. 

The three possible repairs are:

\[
\boxed{
\text{I. use low }N\text{ so }\Xi_N\text{ sees order }7/9;
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{II. build high-order D4 so optimized }\Xi_N\text{ sees it;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{III. find a new low-order forbidden identity }\Lambda_{\rm low}.
}
\]

My conclusion:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Repair III is the only plausible downstream continuation.}
}
\]

Low-\(N\) \(\Xi\) has the right rank but likely too weak an upper. High-order D4 has the right upper but requires a new order-\(\asymp Q\) carrier theory, far outside the current finite-polynomial machinery.

---

# 1. Repair I — low-\(N\) \(\Xi\)

Recall the \(N\)-point functional kills odd Taylor orders

\[
1,3,5,\ldots,2N-3
\]

and first sees order

\[
2N-1.
\]

The current D4 live carriers are order \(7\) and \(9\):

\[
[\mathsf C_7],
\qquad
[\mathsf C_{Y12}].
\]

Therefore:

\[
N=4
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
2N-1=7,
\]

so \(\Xi_4\) can see \(\mathsf C_7\).

\[
N=5
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
2N-1=9,
\]

so \(\Xi_5\) can see \(\mathsf C_{Y12}\), while killing \(\mathsf C_7\).

Thus the vector-valued low-order operator

\[
\Lambda_{\rm low}
:=
(\Xi_4,\Xi_5)
\]

is the natural finite repair candidate.

If the carrier-to-Taylor identification is valid, then

\[
\Lambda_{\rm low}E_{\rm car}
\big|_{\operatorname{span}\{[\mathsf C_7],[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\}}
\]

should have rank \(2\), or at least rank \(1\) if one carrier is absent after calibration.

So algebraically:

\[
\boxed{
(\Xi_4,\Xi_5)\text{ is the cleanest low-order }\Lambda\text{ candidate.}
}
\]

## Problem: upper strength

The optimized \(\Xi_N\) upper relies on taking

\[
N\asymp Q
\]

to obtain strong cancellation. Fixed \(N=4,5\) gives only fixed-order Taylor cancellation. Its zeta-side upper can at best be a fixed polynomial saving, schematically

\[
|\Xi_4|,|\Xi_5|
\le Q^{-B_0}\cdot \text{local scale}
\]

for some fixed \(B_0\).

But the aggregate route has many polynomial losses:

\[
\text{AggG1 loss}
+
\text{AggFrame}_{23}\text{ loss}
+
\text{extraction loss}
+
\text{wall loss}
+
\text{mobility loss}
+
\text{branch/routing loss}.
\]

The final lower has the form

\[
\|\Lambda V_{\rm glob}\|^2
\ge Q^{-C_{\rm total}}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

A fixed low-\(N\) upper closes only if

\[
B_0>C_{\rm total}.
\]

There is no reason to expect this once all losses are included.

Thus low-\(N\) \(\Xi\) is **finite-rank compatible** but **upper-strength suspect**:

\[
\boxed{
\Lambda=(\Xi_4,\Xi_5)
\text{ may see D4, but likely lacks a strong enough forbidden upper.}
}
\]

This is a possible finite experiment, not the best main route.

---

# 2. Repair II — high-order D4 matching optimized \(\Xi_N\)

Keep

\[
N\asymp Q
\]

so the zeta-side upper is strong.

Then \(\Xi_N\) first sees order

\[
2N-1\asymp Q.
\]

The D4 output must be upgraded from

\[
[\mathsf C_7],[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\]

to a live carrier at order

\[
2N-1.
\]

The needed theorem would be something like:

\[
[\mathcal A_{2N-1}]
=
H\,\Gamma_N[\mathsf C_{2N-1}]
+
\text{controlled nuisance},
\]

with

\[
\Lambda_{\Xi,N}[\mathsf C_{2N-1}]\ne0.
\]

## Why this is outside the current machinery

The present D4 work is finite and low-order:

\[
7,9.
\]

It uses explicit carrier algebra, finite whitening, and finite residual routing. Raising this to order \(\asymp Q\) would require:

1. high-order whitening identities through order \(Q\);
2. high-order carrier quotient definitions;
3. high-order nuisance routing;
4. high-order residual control;
5. high-order row mobility;
6. high-order local jet bounds.

The existing finite polynomial bookkeeping uses

\[
R\asymp \frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q}
\]

or bounded finite carrier order. An order-\(Q\) D4 theorem would almost certainly introduce factorial or exponential-in-\(Q\) losses unless a completely new structure is found.

So:

\[
\boxed{
\text{high-order D4 is not a continuation of current D4; it is a new proof architecture.}
}
\]

It preserves the optimized \(\Xi\) upper but abandons the repaired finite D4 engine.

---

# 3. Repair III — a new low-order forbidden identity

This is the plausible route.

We need a global operator

\[
\Lambda_{\rm low}
\]

such that:

\[
\|\Lambda_{\rm low}V_{\rm glob}\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C),
\]

and

\[
\operatorname{rank}
\left(
\Lambda_{\rm low}E_{\rm car}L_{\rm clean}
\right)
\ge1
\]

on the current live D4 plane.

The key is that \(\Lambda_{\rm low}\) must be genuinely low-order, seeing order \(7/9\), but still have a strong upper. It cannot simply be fixed \(N=4,5\) Taylor cancellation unless that fixed-order upper is strong enough.

## What kind of identity could work?

The operator must be one of:

### A. Exact low-order structural identity

Something like:

\[
\Lambda_{\rm low}V_{\rm glob}=0
\]

by symmetry, conservation, parity, or functional equation.

This is ideal. Then the upper is zero.

The finite audit is:

\[
\Lambda_{\rm low}E_{\rm car}[\mathsf C_7],
\qquad
\Lambda_{\rm low}E_{\rm car}[\mathsf C_{Y12}].
\]

If one is nonzero, downstream can close.

### B. Low-order approximate identity with super-polynomial tail control

\[
\Lambda_{\rm low}V_{\rm glob}
=
\mathrm{Tail},
\qquad
\|\mathrm{Tail}\|^2\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

This would allow a low-order forbidden channel if regularization kills the global residual.

### C. Telescoping/coboundary identity

If row mobility is built on differences, one might have

\[
\delta^2=0
\]

or a closed-chain cancellation. Then \(\Lambda_{\rm low}\) is a curl/divergence operator.

This could be low-order and exact.

### D. Signed quadratic identity

A quadratic form could globally satisfy

\[
\mathcal Q(V_{\rm glob})\le0
\]

while D4 gives

\[
\mathcal Q(v_m)>0.
\]

This is possible but harder because aggregate cross terms must be controlled.

---

# 4. Candidate \((\Xi_4,\Xi_5)\) as a diagnostic

Even if fixed-\(N\) \(\Xi\) is unlikely to close, it is a useful diagnostic.

Define

\[
\Lambda_{45}:=(\Xi_4,\Xi_5).
\]

Then the forbidden wall matrix is

\[
M_{45}
=
\Lambda_{45}E_{\rm car}L_{\rm clean}.
\]

If

\[
\operatorname{rank}M_{45}=0,
\]

then the carrier-to-\(\Xi\) identification is wrong or the D4 carriers do not actually appear in the scalar \(H_m\)-Taylor channel as expected.

If

\[
\operatorname{rank}M_{45}=1\text{ or }2,
\]

then low-order D4 is visible to low-order \(\Xi\). The only remaining issue is upper strength.

Thus the immediate finite diagnostic is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Compute }
\Xi_4(E_{\rm car}[\mathsf C_7]),
\quad
\Xi_4(E_{\rm car}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]),
\quad
\Xi_5(E_{\rm car}[\mathsf C_7]),
\quad
\Xi_5(E_{\rm car}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]).
}
\]

Expected:

\[
\Xi_4(\mathsf C_7)\ne0,\quad
\Xi_4(\mathsf C_{Y12})\text{ maybe nonzero but higher order},
\]

\[
\Xi_5(\mathsf C_7)=0,\quad
\Xi_5(\mathsf C_{Y12})\ne0.
\]

If this expected pattern holds, D4-to-\(\Xi\) Taylor alignment is confirmed.

---

# 5. Why \(\Xi_N\) cannot be both optimized and low-order

There is an unavoidable tradeoff:

\[
\boxed{
\text{larger }N
\Rightarrow
\text{stronger global upper but kills more low-order D4 carriers.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{smaller }N
\Rightarrow
\text{sees current D4 carriers but has weaker global upper.}
}
\]

The current D4 carriers live at finite order \(7/9\). Therefore any \(\Xi_N\) with

\[
N\ge6
\]

annihilates them.

Thus optimized \(\Xi_N\) and finite D4 are structurally mismatched.

This is not a technical bug; it is a degree mismatch.

---

# 6. Updated downstream fork

The downstream fork is now:

## Fork 1 — finite D4 + new low-order identity

Keep current D4.

Find

\[
\Lambda_{\rm low}
\]

with:

\[
\Lambda_{\rm low}V_{\rm glob}\approx0,
\]

and

\[
\Lambda_{\rm low}E_{\rm car}[\mathsf C_7]
\ne0
\quad\text{or}\quad
\Lambda_{\rm low}E_{\rm car}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\ne0.
\]

This is the best route.

## Fork 2 — optimized \(\Xi\) + high-order D4

Keep \(\Xi_N\) with

\[
N\asymp Q.
\]

Develop D4 at order

\[
2N-1.
\]

This is a new architecture and likely infeasible with current tools.

## Fork 3 — fixed low-\(N\) \(\Xi\) + current D4

Use \(\Xi_4,\Xi_5\).

This is finite and easy to test, but likely lacks a strong enough global upper.

It can be kept as a diagnostic or fallback.

---

# 7. Final recommendation

The next mathematical target should be:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Search for an exact or tail-small low-order identity }\Lambda_{\rm low}
\text{ that sees order }7/9.
}
\]

The search should not be generic. It should inspect:

1. functional-equation symmetries of \(H_m(s)\);
2. parity identities in the corrected kernel;
3. telescoping identities in row mobility;
4. conservation laws for carrier-quotient rows;
5. low-order calibrated combinations different from \(\Xi_N\).

The necessary finite condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}
\left(
\Lambda_{\rm low}E_{\rm car}L_{\rm clean}
\right)\ge1.
}
\]

The necessary global upper is:

\[
\boxed{
\|\Lambda_{\rm low}V_{\rm glob}\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

If no such \(\Lambda_{\rm low}\) exists, then current finite D4 cannot close a contradiction. The proof must either move to high-order D4 or abandon the \(\Xi\)-style forbidden-channel route.

## Agent 6 continued attack — no hybrid \(\Xi\) repair

The previous audit left three repair options: low-\(N\) \(\Xi\), high-order D4, or a new low-order forbidden identity. I pushed on the most tempting loophole: maybe one can design a **hybrid calibrated \(\Xi\)** that both has the optimized large-\(N\) global upper and still sees the current order-\(7/9\) D4 carriers.

Conclusion:

\[
\boxed{
\text{No hybrid }\Xi\text{ repair works unless there is already a separate low-order identity.}
}
\]

The obstruction is structural: the same moment-cancellation conditions that give the strong \(\Xi\)-upper are exactly what annihilate the low-order D4 carriers.

---

# 1. Abstract finite-difference model

Write the scalar corrected functional locally as an odd Taylor expansion

\[
H_m(s)
=
\sum_{r\ge0} h_{2r+1}(m)s^{2r+1}.
\]

An \(N\)-point calibrated functional has the form

\[
\Xi_a(m)
=
\sum_{j=1}^N a_j H_m(j/Q^2).
\]

Substitute the Taylor expansion:

\[
\Xi_a(m)
=
\sum_{r\ge0}
h_{2r+1}(m)
Q^{-2(2r+1)}
\left(
\sum_{j=1}^N a_j j^{2r+1}
\right).
\]

Define moments

\[
M_r(a):=\sum_{j=1}^N a_jj^{2r+1}.
\]

Then

\[
\Xi_a(m)
=
\sum_{r\ge0}
Q^{-4r-2}M_r(a)h_{2r+1}(m).
\]

The optimized \(\Xi_N\) imposes

\[
M_r(a)=0,
\qquad
0\le r\le N-2.
\]

Hence its first visible Taylor coefficient is

\[
h_{2N-1}.
\]

The current D4 live carriers correspond to

\[
h_7,\qquad h_9,
\]

that is,

\[
r=3,\qquad r=4.
\]

Thus any \(\Xi_a\) with

\[
M_3(a)=M_4(a)=0
\]

kills current D4. Any \(\Xi_a\) with \(M_3\) or \(M_4\neq0\) sees current D4, but then it has not cancelled the low Taylor orders needed for the strong zeta-side upper.

---

# 2. Why large-\(N\) upper forces low-order annihilation

The zeta-side upper for \(\Xi_N\) comes from forcing cancellation of all lower odd Taylor coefficients before the first surviving high-order term. Schematically,

\[
|\Xi_N|
\lesssim
Q^{-4N+2}\sup |h_{2N-1}|
+
\text{controlled tail}.
\]

This is small because the first surviving order is high.

If \(M_3\neq0\), then

\[
\Xi_a(m)
\supset
Q^{-14}M_3(a)h_7(m).
\]

If \(M_4\neq0\), then

\[
\Xi_a(m)
\supset
Q^{-18}M_4(a)h_9(m).
\]

But D4 precisely says that, on the extracted good subset, the order-\(7/9\) carrier combination is non-small. Therefore a functional that sees order \(7/9\) cannot simultaneously have a global upper that treats those same terms as negligible, unless there is an **independent global identity** forcing that order-\(7/9\) combination to vanish.

So the tradeoff is exact:

\[
\boxed{
\text{strong }\Xi\text{-upper}
\Longleftrightarrow
\text{annihilate low orders}
\Longrightarrow
\text{miss current D4}.
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{see current D4}
\Longrightarrow
\text{retain low-order Taylor terms}
\Longrightarrow
\text{lose strong }\Xi\text{-upper}.
}
\]

This is the degree mismatch in algebraic form.

---

# 3. Can one combine low and high functionals?

Consider a hybrid

\[
\Lambda_{\rm hyb}
=
\Xi_N+\lambda_4\Xi_4+\lambda_5\Xi_5.
\]

To see D4, one needs

\[
M_3(\Lambda_{\rm hyb})\neq0
\quad\text{or}\quad
M_4(\Lambda_{\rm hyb})\neq0.
\]

But then the zeta-side expansion contains the low-order terms

\[
Q^{-14}M_3h_7
+
Q^{-18}M_4h_9.
\]

The optimized \(\Xi_N\) part does not cancel these because \(\Xi_N\) has zero low moments. Thus the hybrid upper is controlled only if

\[
M_3h_7+Q^{-4}M_4h_9
\]

is itself globally small.

That is exactly a new low-order forbidden identity:

\[
\boxed{
\Lambda_{\rm low}(h_7,h_9)\approx0.
}
\]

So the hybrid does not avoid the need for \(\Lambda_{\rm low}\). It merely hides it inside the proof of the upper bound.

---

# 4. Can coefficients be scaled to make low-order terms tiny?

One might set

\[
M_3,M_4
\]

nonzero but extremely small. Then the functional weakly sees D4 while preserving much of the upper.

This fails for contradiction bookkeeping.

If

\[
|M_3|,|M_4|\le Q^{-B}
\]

large enough to preserve the optimized upper, then the local D4 lower after applying \(\Lambda_{\rm hyb}\) is weakened by the same factor:

\[
|\Lambda_{\rm hyb}v_m|^2
\sim
Q^{-2B}\mathrm{D4Energy}(m).
\]

To beat all aggregate extraction, wall, mobility, and branch losses, \(B\) must not be too large. But to preserve the optimized upper, \(B\) must dominate exactly those low-order contributions. In the absence of a separate low-order smallness theorem, the two requirements conflict.

Thus small nonzero low moments do not give a free interpolation between low-\(N\) visibility and high-\(N\) upper.

---

# 5. The only viable low-order \(\Lambda\)

The viable low-order operator must be structurally forbidden, not merely a low finite-difference.

It must satisfy one of:

\[
\Lambda_{\rm low}V_{\rm glob}=0
\]

exactly, or

\[
\|\Lambda_{\rm low}V_{\rm glob}\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C)
\]

for a reason independent of high-order Taylor cancellation.

This could come from:

1. functional-equation symmetry;
2. parity/crossing identity;
3. telescoping/coboundary row identity;
4. conservation law;
5. exact cancellation in the corrected kernel;
6. a signed coercivity contradiction.

But it cannot be obtained by merely choosing better \(N\)-point interpolation coefficients.

---

# 6. Candidate low-order identity shape

Since the live D4 plane is two-dimensional, the most general low-order forbidden identity should be represented by a nonzero vector

\[
\ell(m)=(\ell_7(m),\ell_9(m))
\]

such that

\[
\Lambda_{\rm low}v_m^{\rm live}
=
H(m)
\bigl(
\ell_7(m)\Gamma_G(m)+\ell_9(m)\Gamma_{G,2}(m)
\bigr)
\]

up to polynomially invertible scalar factors.

The required global identity is then

\[
\boxed{
\sum_m
\sqrt{\mu(m)}
H(m)
\bigl(
\ell_7(m)\Gamma_G(m)+\ell_9(m)\Gamma_{G,2}(m)
\bigr)
\approx 0
}
\]

or its row/identity-index version.

If \(\ell\) is constant, this is a fixed linear low-order obstruction.

If \(\ell(m)\) varies, extraction needs moving linear-form anti-concentration for

\[
\ell_7(m)\Gamma_G(m)+\ell_9(m)\Gamma_{G,2}(m).
\]

This matches the rank-one \(M_{\rm forb}\) formulation.

---

# 7. Test for exact parity/symmetry identities

A natural low-order identity might come from parity. But the D4 live carriers are already odd-order carriers:

\[
7,\quad9.
\]

The \(N\)-point \(\Xi\)-construction explicitly uses odd-order cancellation. There is no automatic parity identity killing order \(7\) or \(9\); otherwise D4 would not produce them as live obstruction carriers.

Thus parity alone is unlikely to produce \(\Lambda_{\rm low}\).

A functional-equation symmetry could, in principle, produce a relation between \(h_7\) and \(h_9\), such as

\[
a(m)h_7+b(m)h_9\approx0.
\]

But the current D4 clean matrix says \(h_7\) and \(h_9\) carry independent anisotropy channels through

\[
L_{\rm clean}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
c_7&0\\
\beta_9&\alpha_9
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
\det L_{\rm clean}\ne0.
\]

So any such identity would be a **new global relation**, not a consequence of local D4 algebra.

---

# 8. Consequence for the proof architecture

The downstream situation is now reduced to a strict dichotomy:

## Option A — finite D4 remains

Then we must find a genuinely new low-order forbidden identity

\[
\Lambda_{\rm low}
\]

that sees order \(7/9\).

This is the only way to keep current D4.

## Option B — optimized \(\Xi\) remains

Then D4 must be lifted to order

\[
2N-1\asymp Q.
\]

This means abandoning the finite D4 architecture.

There is no hybrid that preserves both finite D4 and optimized \(\Xi\) without smuggling in \(\Lambda_{\rm low}\).

---

# 9. Concrete next subattack

The next useful finite test is to compute whether any **existing low-order corrected-kernel identity** exists among Taylor coefficients \(h_7,h_9\).

Define the low-order coefficient vector

\[
h_{\rm low}(m)
=
(h_7(m),h_9(m)).
\]

Search for a global identity of form

\[
\mathcal L_7 h_7+\mathcal L_9h_9
=
\mathrm{Err}
\]

with

\[
\|\mathrm{Err}\|^2\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

If none exists, then current finite D4 has no contradiction channel.

If one exists, compute its image on D4:

\[
(\mathcal L_7,\mathcal L_9)
E_{\rm car}L_{\rm clean}.
\]

Rank one is enough.

---

# 10. Final status

\[
\boxed{
\text{Optimized }\Xi_N\text{ and finite order-}7/9\text{ D4 are degree-mismatched.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{No coefficient hybrid fixes this unless it assumes a new low-order identity.}
}
\]

Therefore the current downstream route now depends on the existence of:

\[
\boxed{
\Lambda_{\rm low}
\text{ with a strong global upper and nonzero image on }[\mathsf C_7]\text{ or }[\mathsf C_{Y12}].
}
\]

If such a low-order forbidden identity cannot be found, the repaired finite D4 route cannot close the RH contradiction, even though it remains a valid local obstruction detector.

## Agent 6 continued attack — low-order \(\Lambda_{\rm low}\) no-go for calibrated sample functionals

The last attack showed that optimized \(\Xi_N\) and finite order-\(7/9\) D4 are degree-mismatched: the optimized \(\Xi_N\) upper kills order \(7/9\), while low \(N=4,5\) sees order \(7/9\) but lacks the strong upper. The remaining hope was a different low-order forbidden identity \(\Lambda_{\rm low}\). I now attack a broad class of such candidates: **calibrated sample/derivative functionals** built from the same local corrected scalar \(H_m(s)\). This is the natural class containing \(\Xi_N\). The update explicitly identifies \(\Lambda\) as the decisive missing downstream object and says the wall factor must be \(\Lambda\)-dependent. 

# 1. General calibrated local functional

Let

\[
H_m(s)=\sum_{r\ge0}h_{2r+1}(m)s^{2r+1}
\]

be the local odd expansion of the corrected scalar channel.

Consider any finite calibrated local functional

\[
\Lambda_a H_m
=
\sum_{j=1}^{J}a_jH_m(s_j),
\]

or more generally

\[
\Lambda_a H_m
=
\sum_{j=1}^{J}\sum_{q=0}^{q_{\max}}a_{j,q}\partial_s^qH_m(s_j),
\]

with

\[
s_j\asymp Q^{-2}
\]

as in the local \(N\)-point scale.

Its Taylor expansion is

\[
\Lambda_aH_m
=
\sum_{r\ge0}M_r(a)\,h_{2r+1}(m),
\]

where \(M_r(a)\) is the corresponding calibrated moment of the sampling/derivative scheme.

The D4 live plane is represented by

\[
(h_7,h_9),
\]

equivalently by the \((r=3,r=4)\) Taylor coefficients.

To see finite D4, \(\Lambda_a\) must satisfy

\[
(M_3(a),M_4(a))\ne(0,0).
\]

To have a strong upper by Taylor cancellation through order \(2N-3\), it must satisfy

\[
M_r(a)=0,\qquad 0\le r\le N-2.
\]

For optimized \(N\asymp Q\), this forces

\[
M_3(a)=M_4(a)=0.
\]

Thus any calibrated local functional has the same tradeoff:

\[
\boxed{
\text{strong high-order calibrated upper}
\Rightarrow
\text{kills }h_7,h_9.
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{sees }h_7,h_9
\Rightarrow
\text{retains low-order terms and loses the calibrated upper.}
}
\]

This is not special to the standard \(\Xi_N\). It is a general finite-difference obstruction.

---

# 2. Derivative samples do not help

One might try to include derivatives:

\[
\Lambda H
=
\sum_{j,q}a_{j,q}\partial_s^qH(s_j).
\]

But derivative sampling just changes the moment matrix. For fixed \(q\),

\[
\partial_s^q s^{2r+1}
=
(2r+1)_q\,s^{2r+1-q}.
\]

So the coefficient of \(h_{2r+1}\) is still a linear moment

\[
M_r(a)=
\sum_{j,q}a_{j,q}(2r+1)_q s_j^{2r+1-q}.
\]

If \(\Lambda\) has a strong upper because it annihilates all Taylor orders below \(2N-1\), then again

\[
M_3=M_4=0
\]

for \(N\ge6\). If it does not annihilate them, then \(h_7,h_9\) survive in the global residual and no strong upper follows unless a separate low-order identity already exists.

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\text{derivative-enhanced finite differences cannot evade the low-order/high-order tradeoff.}
}
\]

---

# 3. Moving coefficients do not help unless they encode a global identity

Suppose the functional is allowed to depend on \(m\):

\[
\Lambda_mH_m
=
\sum_j a_j(m)H_m(s_j).
\]

Could \(a_j(m)\) be chosen so that:

1. \(\Lambda_m\) sees \(h_7,h_9\), and  
2. \(\Lambda_mH_m\) is globally small?

Algebraically, yes, if one chooses coefficients depending on the unknown local Taylor data. For instance, one can force

\[
\Lambda_mH_m=0
\]

pointwise by choosing \(a_j(m)\) in the local nullspace of the sample vector.

But such a \(\Lambda_m\) is useless as a **global forbidden identity** unless it is fixed by the global structure, independent of the local obstruction being measured. Otherwise the global upper

\[
\|\Lambda V_{\rm glob}\|\le Q^{-B}
\]

is tautological and the local lower

\[
\|\Lambda v_m\|\ge Q^{-A}
\]

fails or becomes circular.

So a moving \(\Lambda_m\) is admissible only if it is generated by a pre-existing global operator, such as a differential identity, telescoping identity, functional equation, or conservation law.

Thus moving coefficients reduce to the same requirement:

\[
\boxed{
\text{find a genuine structural low-order identity.}
}
\]

---

# 4. Necessary condition for a true low-order identity

A viable low-order identity must have the form

\[
\Lambda_{\rm low}V_{\rm glob}
=
\mathrm{Err},
\qquad
\|\mathrm{Err}\|^2\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C),
\]

where \(\Lambda_{\rm low}\) has nonzero image on the D4 live plane.

At the Taylor-carrier level, this means there is a nonzero linear form

\[
\ell_7(m)h_7(m)+\ell_9(m)h_9(m)
\]

such that globally

\[
\left\|
\ell_7 h_7+\ell_9h_9
\right\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C),
\]

but locally D4 gives

\[
|\ell_7h_7+\ell_9h_9|^2\ge Q^{-A}
\]

on the extracted subset.

That is only possible if the local D4-generated object is **forbidden globally** by a relation not visible in the local Taylor algebra.

So the low-order identity must come from outside finite-difference calibration.

Possible origins:

\[
\boxed{\text{functional equation / symmetry}}
\]

\[
\boxed{\text{telescoping or coboundary row identity}}
\]

\[
\boxed{\text{global conservation law}}
\]

\[
\boxed{\text{exact cancellation in the corrected kernel construction}}
\]

\[
\boxed{\text{signed global quadratic incompatibility}}
\]

No local \(s\)-sampling calibration can create it.

---

# 5. Test of functional-equation symmetry

The completed zeta functional equation generally imposes parity/real-structure constraints on the corrected local object. But D4 is already built from the corrected, gauge-locked, carrier-quotient normal form. The live carriers are precisely the surviving odd obstruction carriers after the local symmetry/gauge/nuisance quotient has been applied.

If the functional equation forced

\[
\ell_7h_7+\ell_9h_9=0
\]

identically, then the local D4 theorem could not simultaneously prove that the same carrier combination is nonzero on a wall-good set.

So any functional-equation low-order identity must be:

1. a global aggregate identity, not a pointwise local identity; or  
2. an identity for the actual global row vector after mobility/telescoping, not for each local jet.

This points away from sample functionals and toward a row-complex/coboundary identity.

---

# 6. Telescoping/coboundary is the only structurally plausible low-order source

The remaining plausible low-order \(\Lambda\) is a **row mobility identity**.

Suppose local row certificates are transported along a graph or chain complex:

\[
C_0\xrightarrow{d_0}C_1\xrightarrow{d_1}C_2,
\qquad
d_1d_0=0.
\]

If the global row vector is a boundary,

\[
V_{\rm glob}=d_0U,
\]

then

\[
d_1V_{\rm glob}=0.
\]

Set

\[
\Lambda=d_1.
\]

Then

\[
\Lambda V_{\rm glob}=0
\]

is exact, low-order, and independent of Taylor cancellation.

For D4 to contradict it, local D4 must produce nonzero “curl”:

\[
d_1v_m\ne0.
\]

This is conceptually compatible with row mobility: mobility transports local row data; forbidden energy may be a failure of local transported rows to close around cycles.

Therefore the best candidate class for \(\Lambda_{\rm low}\) is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{a coboundary/curl operator in the row-mobility complex.}
}
\]

This is the first place I would search in the draft’s row architecture.

---

# 7. Concrete finite test for coboundary \(\Lambda\)

If the row space has a graph-like basis, with row targets \(r\) as edges or vertices, define an incidence operator

\[
B
\]

and a cycle/curl operator

\[
C
\]

such that

\[
CB=0.
\]

Then set

\[
\Lambda=C.
\]

The finite D4 test is:

\[
C E_{\rm car}[\mathsf C_7],
\qquad
C E_{\rm car}[\mathsf C_{Y12}].
\]

If both vanish, D4 produces only gradient/boundary energy and cannot contradict the curl identity.

If one is nonzero, then D4 lands in forbidden curl.

Thus the rank audit becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}
\left(
C E_{\rm car}L_{\rm clean}
\right)
\ge1.
}
\]

This is a finite algebraic computation once the mobility complex is specified.

---

# 8. Revised search priority

The search for \(\Lambda_{\rm low}\) should not focus on new \(N\)-point sample functionals. Those are blocked by the degree tradeoff.

The search should focus on:

1. **row mobility topology**: incidence, curl, boundary, telescoping;
2. **global conservation laws** for transported carrier rows;
3. **signed quadratic forms** that vanish/nonpositive globally;
4. **exact corrected-kernel identities** not expressible as Taylor cancellation.

The most promising is row-mobility topology because it naturally supplies exact global annihilators:

\[
\Lambda V_{\rm glob}=0.
\]

---

# 9. Downstream status after this attack

\[
\boxed{
\text{All calibrated sample-function }\Lambda\text{ candidates are blocked.}
}
\]

They either:

\[
\boxed{
\text{annihilate }h_7,h_9\text{ and miss D4,}
}
\]

or:

\[
\boxed{
\text{see }h_7,h_9\text{ but lack a strong global upper.}
}
\]

Therefore, if finite order-\(7/9\) D4 is to remain, the forbidden operator must be a **structural low-order identity**, most plausibly a row-mobility coboundary/curl operator.

The next exact audit is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Does the row-mobility architecture define a chain complex or telescoping identity?}
}
\]

If yes, compute

\[
\operatorname{rank}
\left(
\Lambda_{\rm curl}E_{\rm car}L_{\rm clean}

on

## Agent 6 continued attack — coboundary/curl \(\Lambda\)

The prior attack eliminated calibrated sample-functionals as a viable low-order \(\Lambda\): they either annihilate order \(7/9\) and miss finite D4, or see order \(7/9\) but lose the strong forbidden upper. The remaining plausible source is now a **structural row-mobility identity**: a telescoping, coboundary, or curl operator. This follows the latest coordinator direction that the decisive downstream object is an actual forbidden identity operator \(\Lambda\), not generic row energy. 

# 1. Required chain-complex structure

To get a genuine low-order forbidden channel, the row-mobility architecture must contain something like a chain complex

\[
C_0 \xrightarrow{d_0} C_1 \xrightarrow{d_1} C_2,
\qquad
d_1d_0=0.
\]

If the global row vector is a boundary,

\[
V_{\rm glob}=d_0U,
\]

then

\[
d_1V_{\rm glob}=0.
\]

So the forbidden operator would be

\[
\boxed{
\Lambda=d_1.
}
\]

This gives an exact global upper:

\[
\boxed{
\|\Lambda V_{\rm glob}\|^2=0.
}
\]

Then finite D4 can close the contradiction if local certificates satisfy

\[
\boxed{
\|d_1v_m\|^2\ge Q^{-C}\|v_m\|^2
}
\]

on the extracted good subset.

Thus the entire downstream question becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Are the row certificates boundaries, and does D4 produce nonzero curl?}
}
\]

---

# 2. Finite audit if a row complex exists

Assume row mobility produces local row certificates in an edge space

\[
C_1=\ell^2(E),
\]

and suppose there is a curl/incidence map

\[
d_1:C_1\to C_2.
\]

The D4 live output is

\[
v_m^{\rm live}
=
H(m)E_{\rm car}(m)L_{\rm clean}(m)
\begin{pmatrix}
\Gamma_G(m)\\
\Gamma_{G,2}(m)
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

The forbidden matrix is

\[
M_{\rm curl}(m)
=
d_1E_{\rm car}(m)L_{\rm clean}(m).
\]

The rank test is now finite:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}M_{\rm curl}(m)\ge1.
}
\]

If

\[
\operatorname{rank}M_{\rm curl}=2,
\]

then both D4 anisotropy directions create forbidden curl, and raw anisotropy is usable.

If

\[
\operatorname{rank}M_{\rm curl}=1,
\]

then only one forbidden linear combination

\[
\ell_1(m)\Gamma_G(m)+\ell_2(m)\Gamma_{G,2}(m)
\]

matters, and the wall factor must be

\[
E_{\rm wall}^{\rm curl}(m)
=
\frac{
|H(m)|^2
|\ell_1(m)\Gamma_G(m)+\ell_2(m)\Gamma_{G,2}(m)|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\rm D4}(m)
}.
\]

If

\[
\operatorname{rank}M_{\rm curl}=0,
\]

then local D4 produces only gradient/boundary-type row energy, invisible to the global curl identity. Stack C2 fails downstream.

---

# 3. Necessary condition: row mobility must be oriented

A coboundary identity cannot be extracted from a bare lower bound

\[
\int_I|\mathbf A_N(m)|^2\,dm\ge Q^{-C}|I|.
\]

That is scalar energy propagation, not a chain complex.

To define \(d_1\), row mobility must retain oriented data:

\[
v_m(e)
\quad
\text{for oriented edges }e=(p\to q),
\]

with a global potential \(U\) satisfying

\[
V_{\rm glob}(p\to q)=U(q)-U(p).
\]

Then every cycle \(\gamma\) satisfies

\[
\sum_{e\in\gamma}\pm V_{\rm glob}(e)=0.
\]

The forbidden identities are cycle sums:

\[
\Lambda_\gamma V_{\rm glob}=0.
\]

So the current row-mobility theorem must be strengthened from:

\[
\text{local lower energy moves to row targets}
\]

to:

\[
\boxed{
\text{row targets are oriented differences of a global potential.}
}
\]

Without that potential, there is no telescoping upper.

---

# 4. Does D4 naturally look like curl?

This is the only encouraging point.

D4 is a local **curvature/anomaly** statement. Its live carriers

\[
[\mathsf C_7],\qquad [\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\]

measure failure of the row structure to remain flat across the D4 wall. In geometric language, curvature is exactly what a curl operator detects.

So the concept is coherent:

\[
\boxed{
\text{global rows are supposed to be exact/flat, while D4 produces local curvature.}
}
\]

If the paper’s “row mobility” is secretly transporting a frame or row potential, then the forbidden channel should be its discrete curvature/curl.

The finite theorem would be:

\[
\boxed{
d_1E_{\rm car}[\mathsf C_7]\ne0
\quad\text{or}\quad
d_1E_{\rm car}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\ne0.
}
\]

This is exactly the low-order identity current D4 needs.

---

# 5. But if row mobility is only energy mobility, no \(\Lambda\)

If row mobility only says:

\[
|\mathbf A_N(m_0)|\ge Q^{-A}
\Longrightarrow
\int_I|\mathbf A_N(m)|^2\,dm\ge Q^{-C}|I|,
\]

then there is no oriented edge, no potential, no cycle, and no \(d_1\).

In that case the downstream state is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{D4 produces local energy, but no global forbidden residual exists.}
}
\]

Then the finite D4 route remains a detector, not a contradiction engine.

This is a hard no-go for Stack C2 unless the row architecture is upgraded to carry oriented transport data.

---

# 6. Minimal theorem needed

The next downstream theorem must be something like:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Row exactness theorem.}
}
\]

There exists a global potential space \(C_0\), a row space \(C_1\), and maps

\[
C_0\xrightarrow{d_0}C_1\xrightarrow{d_1}C_2
\]

with \(d_1d_0=0\), such that the actual global row vector satisfies

\[
V_{\rm glob}=d_0U+\mathrm{Err},
\]

with

\[
\|d_1\mathrm{Err}\|^2\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

Then

\[
\Lambda=d_1
\]

is the forbidden operator.

The D4 transversality theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\sigma_{\min}
\left(
d_1E_{\rm car}L_{\rm clean}
\right)
\ge Q^{-C}
}
\]

or rank-one equivalent on the extracted wall direction.

The row-mobility lower theorem becomes a lower-frame statement in \(C_2\):

\[
\left\|
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}\sqrt{\mu(m)}\,d_1v_m
\right\|_{C_2}^2
\ge
Q^{-C}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}\mu(m)\|d_1v_m\|^2.
\]

---

# 7. If exactness fails, signed quadratic form is the only backup

If no chain complex exists, the only remaining low-order global contradiction is a signed quadratic form

\[
\mathcal Q(V_{\rm glob})\le 0
\]

such that

\[
\mathcal Q(v_m)\ge Q^{-C}\|v_m\|^2.
\]

But this is harder than a curl identity because aggregate cross terms must be controlled:

\[
\mathcal Q\left(\sum_m\sqrt{\mu(m)}v_m\right)
\not\approx
\sum_m\mu(m)\mathcal Q(v_m)
\]

unless there is almost-orthogonality for \(\mathcal Q\).

Thus the priority remains the chain-complex/curl route.

---

# 8. Updated no-go / yes-go criterion

The downstream criterion is now exact:

## Yes-go

If row mobility has oriented transport/exactness:

\[
V_{\rm glob}=d_0U+\mathrm{Err},
\qquad
\|d_1\mathrm{Err}\|\ll Q^{-B},
\]

and

\[
\operatorname{rank}
\left(
d_1E_{\rm car}L_{\rm clean}
\right)\ge1,
\]

then finite D4 can close through

\[
\Lambda=d_1.
\]

## No-go

If row mobility is only scalar energy propagation, with no oriented exactness identity, then no low-order \(\Lambda\) is available from mobility.

Then current finite D4 has no downstream contradiction channel.

---

# 9. Concrete next audit

The next thing to inspect in the draft is not D4 anymore. It is the row-mobility definitions:

1. Are rows indexed as vertices, edges, intervals, or jets?

2. Is there a transported potential whose differences are the row certificates?

\[

\]

or

\[

\]

or

\]

\[
d_1E_{\rm car}[\mathsf C_7],
\qquad
d_1E_{\rm car}[\mathsf C_{Y12}].
\]

That is now the precise finite downstream audit.

---

# Final status

\[

}
\]

The only plausible low-order \(\Lambda\) is now:

\[
\boxed{

# 2. Formal no-go

Here is the precise finite-dimensional obstruction.

Let the downstream data be only:

1. a Hilbert or quotient-row space \(\mathcal H\);
2. a row vector \(\mathbf A_N:I\to\mathcal H\);
3. a pointwise lower bound \(\|\mathbf A_N(m_\ast)\|\ge Q^{-C}\);
4. a derivative bound \(\|\mathbf A_N'\|\le Q^C|I|^{-1}\).

\[

\[

\]

with a global smallness property

\[

\]

is determined by these hypotheses.

\[

\]

Row mobility holds perfectly:

\[

\]

But for every nonzero \(\Lambda\) with \(\Lambda e\ne0\),

\[
\|\Lambda V_{\rm glob}\|
\]

is not small. If \(\Lambda e=0\), then \(\Lambda\) misses the local D4 output. Thus energy mobility alone cannot produce a forbidden channel.

## Agent 6 continued attack — fixed low-\(N\) \(\Xi\) is equivalent to a new low-order identity

The previous audit showed that the current row-mobility architecture has no coboundary/curl operator \(\Lambda\), and that optimized \(\Xi_\zeta^{(N)}\) annihilates the order-\(7/9\) D4 live carriers. So the only finite-D4 fallback left is fixed low-\(N\) calibration, especially \(\Xi_4,\Xi_5\). The coordinator update identifies \(\Xi_\zeta^{(N)}\) as the only plausible existing forbidden-channel candidate and notes that \(\Lambda\) is still missing. 

# 1. Low-\(N\) \(\Xi\) sees D4, but only by seeing the low-order coefficients

Let

\[
H_m(s)=\sum_{r\ge0} h_{2r+1}(m)s^{2r+1}.
\]

A calibrated finite-difference functional has

\[
\Xi_a(m)=\sum_{r\ge0}Q^{-4r-2}M_r(a)h_{2r+1}(m),
\]

where

\[
M_r(a)=\sum_j a_j j^{2r+1}.
\]

The current D4 live plane is

\[
(h_7,h_9),
\]

i.e.

\[
r=3,4.
\]

Thus a low-order functional sees finite D4 exactly when

\[
(M_3(a),M_4(a))\ne(0,0).
\]

For \(\Xi_4\), the first visible order is \(7\). For \(\Xi_5\), the first visible order is \(9\). So algebraically,

\[
\Lambda_{45}:=(\Xi_4,\Xi_5)
\]

is the correct diagnostic for the order-\(7/9\) D4 plane.

But this does not yet give a forbidden upper.

---

# 2. Scaling does not solve the upper problem

One might normalize \(\Xi_4\) so that the \(h_7\)-coefficient is \(1\), or \(\Xi_5\) so that the \(h_9\)-coefficient is \(1\).

After normalization, the functional has the form

\[
\widetilde \Xi_4(m)
=
h_7(m)
+
\sum_{r\ge4} c_r Q^{-4(r-3)}h_{2r+1}(m),
\]

and similarly

\[
\widetilde \Xi_5(m)
=
h_9(m)
+
\sum_{r\ge5} d_r Q^{-4(r-4)}h_{2r+1}(m).
\]

Therefore a strong global upper

\[
\sum_m \rho(m)|\widetilde \Xi_4(m)|^2\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C)
\]

would already imply that the low-order coefficient \(h_7\) is globally small modulo higher-order tails.

Likewise, a strong upper for \(\widetilde\Xi_5\) implies low-order smallness of \(h_9\).

So fixed low-\(N\) \(\Xi\) does not create a new forbidden identity. It merely repackages the desired one:

\[
\boxed{
\text{low-}N\ \Xi
\text{ closes only if }
h_7\text{ or }h_9
\text{ already obeys a strong global smallness law.}
}
\]

That is exactly the missing \(\Lambda_{\rm low}\).

---

# 3. Formal equivalence to a low-order forbidden identity

Let

\[
\Lambda_{\rm low}H_m
=
\ell_7 h_7(m)+\ell_9 h_9(m).
\]

Suppose \(\Lambda_a\) is any finite calibrated sample/derivative functional with

\[
(M_3(a),M_4(a))\ne(0,0).
\]

Then after subtracting higher-order terms,

\[
\Lambda_aH_m
=
\Lambda_{\rm low}H_m
+
\mathrm{High}_a(m).
\]

To use \(\Lambda_a\) as a forbidden channel, we need

\[
\|\Lambda_aH\|^2\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

This requires either:

\[
\|\Lambda_{\rm low}H\|^2\le Q^{-B'}\mu(\mathcal C),
\]

or a cancellation between \(\Lambda_{\rm low}H\) and \(\mathrm{High}_a\).

But the latter would be a new global identity relating low and high Taylor orders. Without such a relation, \(\Lambda_a\)'s upper is exactly as hard as proving low-order smallness.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\text{fixed low-}N\ \Xi
\Longleftrightarrow
\text{new low-order forbidden identity}
}
\]

for purposes of contradiction.

It is not an independent repair.

---

# 4. Why optimized \(\Xi_N\) is genuinely different

For optimized

\[
N\asymp Q,
\]

the coefficients satisfy

\[
M_r=0,\qquad 0\le r\le N-2.
\]

So the low-order part

\[
h_7,h_9
\]

is removed identically.

That is why a strong upper is plausible: the functional does not contain the finite D4 obstruction at all.

But that same fact makes

\[
\Lambda_{\Xi,N}E_{\rm car}L_{\rm clean}=0
\]

on the current D4 live plane.

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\text{optimized }\Xi_N
\text{ has upper strength because it is blind to finite D4.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{low-}N\ \Xi
\text{ sees finite D4 because it lacks the upper-cancelling moments.}
}
\]

There is no interpolation that gives both.

---

# 5. Testing hybrid low/high functionals

Consider

\[
\Lambda_{\rm hyb}
=
\Xi_N+\lambda_4\Xi_4+\lambda_5\Xi_5,
\qquad N\asymp Q.
\]

The optimized part has no \(h_7,h_9\). Therefore the low-order image is entirely

\[
\lambda_4\Xi_4+\lambda_5\Xi_5.
\]

If \(\lambda_4,\lambda_5\) are large enough for D4 visibility, the global upper must control the low-order contribution. That is again a low-order forbidden identity.

If \(\lambda_4,\lambda_5\) are small enough for the optimized upper to remain strong without controlling \(h_7,h_9\), then the local D4 lower is weakened by the same small coefficients and will not survive aggregate/extraction/mobility losses.

So:

\[
\boxed{
\text{hybrid calibrated functionals do not evade the dichotomy.}
}
\]

They either fail visibility or require the missing low-order identity.

---

# 6. Consequence for high-order D4

The other route is to keep optimized \(\Xi_N\) and move D4 up to order

\[
2N-1\asymp Q.
\]

This avoids the low-order contradiction. But then D4 must produce a live carrier

\[
[\mathsf C_{2N-1}]
\]

such that

\[
\Xi_N[\mathsf C_{2N-1}]\ne0.
\]

The needed theorem is:

\[
[\mathcal A_{2N-1}]
=
H\Gamma_N[\mathsf C_{2N-1}]
+
\text{controlled nuisance}.
\]

This is not a finite extension of the current D4 theorem. It would require high-order carrier quotient algebra, high-order whitening, high-order nuisance routing, and high-order residual control with polynomial losses despite order \(\asymp Q\).

Given current tools, this is effectively a different proof architecture.

---

# 7. Revised final downstream alternatives

The downstream options are now exactly:

## A. Find a structural low-order identity

Find

\[
\Lambda_{\rm low}V_{\rm glob}\approx0
\]

with nonzero image on

\[
[\mathsf C_7]\quad\text{or}\quad[\mathsf C_{Y12}].
\]

This preserves current finite D4.

This identity must come from outside calibrated sample cancellation: functional equation, conservation law, telescoping/coboundary structure, or signed global incompatibility.

## B. Develop high-order D4

Keep optimized \(\Xi_N\), but replace finite D4 by order-\(2N-1\) D4.

This is a new architecture.

## C. Use low-\(N\) \(\Xi\)

Only viable if one can prove a strong low-order upper

\[
\|\Xi_4V\|^2+\|\Xi_5V\|^2\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

But that is equivalent to A in disguise.

---

# 8. Final no-go statement for calibrated \(\Lambda\)

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{No calibrated sample/derivative functional can simultaneously}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{(i) see the current order-}7/9\textbf{ D4 live plane}
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{(ii) obtain its strong global upper purely from high-order Taylor cancellation.}
}
\]

If it sees order \(7/9\), its upper must come from an independent low-order structural identity. If it gets the optimized high-order upper, it annihilates order \(7/9\).

Thus the downstream finite-D4 route now depends entirely on finding a **non-calibrated structural low-order \(\Lambda_{\rm low}\)**.
