## Agent 6 — partial explicit-formula residuals as upstream tool vs downstream \(\Lambda\)

I read the update as changing the role of partial explicit-formula residuals. They are no longer a primary downstream \(\Lambda\) candidate; the better downstream candidate is now a carrier-lifted atlas loop. My task here is to classify whether partial EF residuals are still useful upstream for \(\mathrm{Agg}L^2(q''_{\can})\), downstream as a forbidden identity, or not useful. 

# 1. Candidate residuals

The full canonical identity is

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

So the full residual

\[
R_{\rm full}:=
q''_{\can}-(B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2)
\]

is identically zero by definition, modulo whatever regularization convention defines \(E_2\).

This gives a perfect global upper:

\[
R_{\rm full}=0.
\]

But it is almost certainly D4-invisible, because D4 is built from the already-assembled scalar source \(u=q''_{\can}\). If the decomposition is varied consistently, the residual remains zero. Thus

\[
R_{\rm full}E_{\rm car}[\mathsf C_7]=0,
\qquad
R_{\rm full}E_{\rm car}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]=0.
\]

So the full EF residual has rank zero as a downstream \(\Lambda\).

The meaningful partial residuals are instead:

\[
R_X
:=
q''_{\can}
-
(B_2+P_2^{\le X}+D_2^{\le X}),
\]

\[
R_{\rm tail,X}
:=
P_2^{>X}+D_2^{>X}+E_2,
\]

\[
R_{\rm reg}^{X,Y}
:=
(B_2+P_2^{\le X}+D_2^{\le X}+E_2^{X})
-
(B_2+P_2^{\le Y}+D_2^{\le Y}+E_2^{Y}),
\]

and localized versions

\[
R_{X,\psi}
:=
\psi\left[
q''_{\can}
-
(B_2+P_2^{\le X}+D_2^{\le X})
\right].
\]

---

# 2. Upstream use: yes, partial EF residuals are useful

Partial EF residuals are best used upstream to prove completion/coherence for

\[
\mathrm{Agg}L^2(q''_{\can}).
\]

The target is

\[
\int |q''_{\can}(t)|^2W(t)\,dt
\ge
Q^{-A}\mathcal N_{\can}(W).
\]

Let

\[
F_X:=B_2+P_2^{\le X}+D_2^{\le X}.
\]

Then

\[
q''_{\can}=F_X+R_X.
\]

A usable upstream theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\|R_X\|_{L^2(W)}
\le
\frac12\|F_X\|_{L^2(W)}
}
\]

or more invariantly,

\[
\boxed{
\|R_X\|_{L^2(W)}^2
\le
Q^{-A}\mathcal N_X(W)
}
\]

together with

\[
\boxed{
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W)}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\mathcal N_{\can}(W).
}
\]

Then

\[
\|q''_{\can}\|_{L^2(W)}
\ge
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W)}-\|R_X\|_{L^2(W)}
\ge
\frac12\|F_X\|_{L^2(W)}.
\]

So partial EF residual estimates can reduce the full source lower bound to a finite prime/dual Dirichlet-polynomial mean-square theorem.

This is especially useful because Gate–1 is now local scalar \(L^2\) energy of \(q''_{\can}\), not branch-specific finite rows. The partial residual should therefore be treated as a **completion-coherence tool** for the full source.

---

# 3. Downstream \(\Lambda\) use: generally no

For a partial residual to be a downstream \(\Lambda\), it must satisfy all three:

\[
\boxed{
\text{localized/atomic small upper}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{nonzero image on }[\mathsf C_7]\text{ or }[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{compatibility with forbidden mobility}
}
\]

A partial residual may have the first property only if the truncation/tail estimate is strong and local:

\[
|\Lambda_{\rm trunc,\psi}V_{\glob}|^2
\le
Q^{-B}\|\psi\|^2.
\]

But it usually fails one of the next two.

## 3.1 Full residual: upper strong, rank zero

\[
R_{\rm full}=0
\]

is exact, but definitional. It annihilates the complete canonical package and therefore cannot see D4 unless D4 somehow represents a failure of the explicit formula itself. It does not; D4 is a local geometric consequence of the source.

Classification:

\[
\boxed{
R_{\rm full}: \text{downstream no-go, rank zero.}
}
\]

## 3.2 Truncation residual: upper possible, rank likely wrong

The truncation residual

\[
R_X=q''_{\can}-F_X
\]

can be made small if \(X\) is chosen so the omitted tail is controlled. But the D4 live carriers are low-order local curvature carriers of the full source. They are not obviously supported in the truncation error. In fact, if \(F_X\) approximates \(q''_{\can}\) well enough upstream, then the D4 carrier should be present in \(F_X\), not in \(R_X\).

So the expected rank is

\[
\Lambda_{\rm trunc}E_{\rm car}[\mathsf C_7]\approx0,
\qquad
\Lambda_{\rm trunc}E_{\rm car}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\approx0
\]

unless the truncation deliberately removes a component carrying D4.

But if the truncation removes a component carrying D4, then the residual is not small. That destroys the upper.

Thus truncation residuals face a visibility/smallness tradeoff analogous to the \(\Xi_N\) degree tradeoff:

\[
\boxed{
\text{small tail residual}
\Rightarrow
\text{probably D4-invisible;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{D4-visible residual}
\Rightarrow
\text{not tail-small.}
}
\]

Classification:

\[
\boxed{
R_X: \text{upstream useful, downstream unlikely.}
}
\]

## 3.3 Regularization-limit residual: upstream stability, not D4

A regularization difference

\[
R_{\rm reg}^{X,Y}
\]

is useful for proving the canonical object is stable under cutoff/regularization choices. But if it is small, it measures only regularization dependence, not the live D4 curvature of \(q''_{\can}\).

Expected:

\[
R_{\rm reg}^{X,Y}E_{\rm car}[\mathsf C_7]=0,
\qquad
R_{\rm reg}^{X,Y}E_{\rm car}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]=0
\]

up to small regularization error.

Classification:

\[
\boxed{
R_{\rm reg}: \text{upstream stability tool, downstream rank-zero.}
}
\]

## 3.4 Prime/zero commutator: possible only if partial and localized

A prime/zero commutator could be defined as

\[
\Lambda_{\rm PZ,\psi}
=
\psi\left(P_2^{\le X}-\mathcal Z_2^{\le X}\right)
\]

for some zero-side reconstruction \(\mathcal Z_2^{\le X}\).

This is more promising as a downstream \(\Lambda\), because it compares two genuinely different global constructions. It is external enough to the local D4 geometry.

But to be useful, one needs:

\[
|\Lambda_{\rm PZ,\psi}V_{\glob}|^2
\le Q^{-B}\|\psi\|^2
\]

and

\[
\Lambda_{\rm PZ,\psi}E_{\rm car}[\mathsf C_7]\ne0
\quad\text{or}\quad
\Lambda_{\rm PZ,\psi}E_{\rm car}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\ne0.
\]

The problem is that the first condition is essentially a strong localized explicit-formula error theorem, and the second means D4 lives in the prime/zero mismatch rather than in the common scalar source. That is not currently supported.

Classification:

\[
\boxed{
\Lambda_{\rm PZ,\psi}: \text{possible but hard; not the leading candidate.}
}
\]

It is less structurally aligned than the carrier-lifted atlas loop.

---

# 4. D4 rank audit by residual type

| Residual candidate | Upper strength | Expected D4 rank on \([\mathsf C_7],[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\) | Classification |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| \(R_{\rm full}=q''_{\can}-(B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2)\) | Exact zero | Rank \(0\) | Definitional, D4-invisible |
| \(R_X=q''_{\can}-(B_2+P_2^{\le X}+D_2^{\le X})\) | Possible tail-small | Likely rank \(0\) if small | Upstream only |
| \(R_{\rm reg}^{X,Y}\) | Possible stability-small | Rank \(0\) | Upstream regularization only |
| localized tail residual \(R_{X,\psi}\) | Type II possible | Likely rank \(0\) unless tail carries D4 | Upstream/local stability |
| prime/zero partial commutator \(\Lambda_{\rm PZ,\psi}\) | Hard but possible | Unknown | Secondary downstream candidate |
| carrier-lifted atlas loop | Exact/localizable if exists | Unknown, best target | Primary downstream candidate |

---

# 5. Upstream theorem enabled by partial residuals

The useful theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Localized EF completion coherence.}
}
\]

For admissible branch weights \(W_{\branch}\), there exists \(X\) such that

\[
\|q''_{\can}-(B_2+P_2^{\le X}+D_2^{\le X})\|_{L^2(W_{\branch})}^2
\le
Q^{-A}\mathcal N_{\can}(W_{\branch}),
\]

and

\[
\|B_2+P_2^{\le X}+D_2^{\le X}\|_{L^2(W_{\branch})}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\mathcal N_{\can}(W_{\branch}).
\]

Then

\[
\|q''_{\can}\|_{L^2(W_{\branch})}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\mathcal N_{\can}(W_{\branch}).
\]

This is the correct use of partial EF residuals.

It supports the new Gate–1 definition:

\[
E_1(m)=
\int_{I_b}
|\lambda_b^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda_bx)|^2dx.
\]

---

# 6. Why partial EF residual is weaker than atlas loop for \(\Lambda\)

A useful downstream \(\Lambda\) should be:

1. exact or locally tail-small;
2. external to local D4;
3. D4-visible;
4. Type I/II localizable.

The carrier-lifted atlas loop has all four plausible in principle:

- exact chart cocycle;
- external to one local chart;
- may see D4 as atlas curvature;
- local plaquettes give Type I atoms.

A partial EF residual has at most:

- localized tail-small upper;
- externality only if partial;
- uncertain D4 visibility;
- Type II only if very strong localized EF error estimates hold.

Thus partial EF residual is second-best and should not displace the atlas-loop search.

---

# 7. Recommendation

\[
\boxed{
\text{Use partial EF residuals upstream, not as the main downstream }\Lambda.
}
\]

Specifically:

## Use upstream

Use localized partial residual estimates to prove completion coherence for

\[
\mathrm{Agg}L^2(q''_{\can}).
\]

This is a natural and valuable role.

## Do not use as primary downstream \(\Lambda\)

The full residual is rank-zero. Truncation/regularization residuals are likely D4-invisible if they are small. Partial prime/zero commutators are possible but require a very strong localized explicit-formula error theorem and uncertain D4 rank.

## Keep as fallback

If the carrier-lifted atlas loop fails, then test a localized prime/zero partial commutator:

\[
\Lambda_{\rm PZ,\psi}.
\]

But its finite audit must prove both:

\[
|\Lambda_{\rm PZ,\psi}V_{\glob}|^2
\le Q^{-B}\|\psi\|^2,
\]

and

\[
\operatorname{rank}
\left(
\Lambda_{\rm PZ,\psi}E_{\rm car}L_{\clean}
\right)\ge1.
\]

Until then, it is not a viable forbidden channel.

---

# Final classification

\[
\boxed{
\text{Partial EF residuals are valuable upstream completion tools.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{They are not currently viable as the primary downstream }\Lambda.
}
\]

The leading downstream candidate remains

\[
\boxed{
\Lambda_{\atlas}
=
\widetilde{\mathcal T}_{\sing\to\ord}
\widetilde{\mathcal T}_{\tr\to\sing}
\widetilde{\mathcal T}_{\ord\to\tr}
-
\mathrm{Id}.
}
\]

The next finite-D4 audit should focus on carrier-lifted atlas transitions and the rank of

\[
\Lambda_{\atlas}E_{\rm car}L_{\clean}
\]

on the live D4 plane.

## Agent 6 continued attack — carrier-lifted atlas loop

The leading downstream candidate is now

\[
\Lambda_{\atlas}
=
\widetilde{\mathcal T}_{\sing\to\ord}
\widetilde{\mathcal T}_{\tr\to\sing}
\widetilde{\mathcal T}_{\ord\to\tr}
-
\mathrm{Id},
\]

acting on carrier quotient spaces rather than scalar source functions. The last result was that partial explicit-formula residuals are useful upstream, but not the best downstream \(\Lambda\); the atlas loop remains the main finite-D4 candidate. 

# 1. Exact carrier lifts are probably D4-invisible

Suppose the lifted transition maps are exact functorial maps induced by reparametrizing the same scalar source

\[
u=q''_{\can}.
\]

That is, each chart \(A\in\{\ord,\tr,\sing\}\) has a local coordinate \(x_A\), and the carrier package is built from the finite jet or corrected local expansion of

\[
F_A(x_A)=\lambda_A^3u(m_A+\lambda_A x_A).
\]

If the transition

\[
\widetilde{\mathcal T}_{A\to B}
\]

is the exact change-of-coordinate map on the full formal jet algebra, then the cocycle is tautologically exact:

\[
\widetilde{\mathcal T}_{C\to A}
\widetilde{\mathcal T}_{B\to C}
\widetilde{\mathcal T}_{A\to B}
=
\mathrm{Id}.
\]

Then

\[
\Lambda_{\atlas}=0
\]

as an operator on every formal local package.

In particular,

\[
\Lambda_{\atlas}E_{\car}[\mathsf C_7]=0,
\qquad
\Lambda_{\atlas}E_{\car}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]=0.
\]

So exact functorial carrier lifts are globally perfect but D4-invisible:

\[
\boxed{
\text{exact full-jet atlas loop has rank }0\text{ on the D4 live plane.}
}
\]

This is the same failure pattern as the full explicit-formula residual: exact but too definitional.

---

# 2. Nonzero atlas holonomy requires truncation or projection

For \(\Lambda_{\atlas}\) to see finite D4, the lifted transitions cannot be exact full-jet maps. They must involve a finite projection, quotient, truncation, normalization, or chart-dependent carrier reduction.

A realistic lifted transition has the form

\[
\widetilde{\mathcal T}_{A\to B}^{(R)}
=
P_B^{(R)}\,\mathcal T_{A\to B}\,\iota_A^{(R)},
\]

where:

- \(\iota_A^{(R)}\) embeds the finite carrier package in the full formal/local source space;
- \(\mathcal T_{A\to B}\) is the exact scalar coordinate transition;
- \(P_B^{(R)}\) projects to the finite carrier quotient in chart \(B\).

Then the loop is

\[
\Lambda_{\atlas}^{(R)}
=
P_A^{(R)}
\mathcal T_{C\to A}
P_C^{(R)}
\mathcal T_{B\to C}
P_B^{(R)}
\mathcal T_{A\to B}
\iota_A^{(R)}
-
\mathrm{Id}.
\]

Now the loop need not vanish because the projections \(P_A^{(R)},P_B^{(R)},P_C^{(R)}\) do not commute with transition maps.

So:

\[
\boxed{
\text{D4-visible atlas holonomy can only come from finite projection/truncation holonomy.}
}
\]

That is promising, because D4 is also finite-order carrier algebra. But it changes the global upper.

---

# 3. The upper is no longer exact

If the atlas holonomy comes from finite truncation/projection, then globally

\[
\Lambda_{\atlas}^{(R)}V_{\glob}
\]

is not exactly zero. It is the error from moving around the atlas while discarding high modes or nuisance components.

The required upper becomes a finite-projection atlas error theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\|\Lambda_{\atlas}^{(R)}V_{\glob}\|^2
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This is not automatic. It is a new theorem.

Moreover, there is a direct tension:

- if the projection error is extremely small, it may not see D4;
- if it sees D4 at polynomial size, it may not be globally small.

Thus atlas-loop \(\Lambda\) faces a visibility/smallness tradeoff similar to partial EF residuals, but potentially less severe because chart transitions and carrier quotients are geometric rather than arbitrary truncations.

---

# 4. Finite rank audit for truncated atlas holonomy

The finite object to compute is now

\[
M_{\atlas}^{(R)}
=
\Lambda_{\atlas}^{(R)}
E_{\car}^{\ord}
L_{\clean}^{\ord}.
\]

Here

\[
L_{\clean}^{\ord}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
c_7&0\\
\beta_9&\alpha_9
\end{pmatrix},
\]

and

\[
E_{\car}^{\ord}:
\mathbb C^2
\to
\mathcal Q_{\car}^{\ord}
\]

embeds the live D4 plane.

The test is:

\[
\operatorname{rank}M_{\atlas}^{(R)}
\in\{0,1,2\}.
\]

The expected behaviors are:

## Rank zero

The finite atlas projection is still too natural/functorial; D4 is chart-invariant.

Then atlas loop fails.

## Rank one

One D4 anisotropy combination is chart-holonomy-visible:

\[
\ell_1\Gamma_G+\ell_2\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

Then define

\[
E_{\wall}^{\atlas}
=
\frac{
|H|^2
|\ell_1\Gamma_G+\ell_2\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\rm D4}
}.
\]

This is enough, if the atlas error upper is strong.

## Rank two

Both D4 directions are atlas-holonomy-visible; raw D4 anisotropy works.

This is best but less likely.

---

# 5. What determines the rank?

Rank is controlled by the commutator between projection and transition:

\[
[P_B^{(R)},\mathcal T_{A\to B}].
\]

If the D4 carriers are preserved by every finite chart projection, rank is zero.

If one live carrier is killed or distorted by transition-projection mismatch, rank one.

If both are distorted independently, rank two.

So the rank audit reduces to:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Do the finite carrier projections commute with chart transitions on }
[\mathsf C_7],[\mathsf C_{Y12}]?
}
\]

This is concrete.

For each adjacent chart pair \(A\to B\), compute:

\[
P_B^{(R)}\mathcal T_{A\to B}[\mathsf C_7^{(A)}]
-
\mathcal T_{A\to B}^{\car}P_A^{(R)}[\mathsf C_7^{(A)}],
\]

and similarly for \(\mathsf C_{Y12}\).

If all pairwise commutators vanish on the live plane, the atlas loop has rank zero.

---

# 6. Interaction with \(L^2\)-Gate–1

The new \(L^2\)-Gate–1 simplification is helpful here.

The scalar source transition maps are now unquestionably canonical: ordinary, transition, and singular charts are all representations of

\[
u=q''_{\can}.
\]

Thus scalar atlas exactness is not the issue.

The issue is only whether the **finite carrier quotient lift** has nontrivial holonomy.

That is a cleaner problem:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Scalar atlas is exact; finite carrier atlas may have holonomy.}
}
\]

D4 can be interpreted as detecting that finite carrier holonomy.

This is the best conceptual story for finite D4 so far.

---

# 7. But upper strength is the key risk

If \(\Lambda_{\atlas}^{(R)}\) is projection holonomy, the global upper is an estimate on discarded modes:

\[
\|\Lambda_{\atlas}^{(R)}V_{\glob}\|^2
\lesssim
\|\text{high modes / projection tails}\|^2.
\]

For this to be useful, we need

\[
\|\text{projection tails}\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

But D4 itself is a finite-order effect. If the projection tail smallness is achieved by using a large enough \(R\), then the order-\(7/9\) D4 carriers are inside the retained space, and the holonomy may vanish on them. If the projection is coarse enough to create holonomy at order \(7/9\), the tail may not be small.

Thus there is a possible **projection-rank tradeoff**:

\[
\boxed{
\text{fine projection} \Rightarrow \text{small upper but D4-invisible;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{coarse projection} \Rightarrow \text{D4-visible but upper too large.}
}
\]

This is the atlas analogue of the \(\Xi_N\) degree mismatch.

The only way out is if the carrier quotient projection is not simply “truncate high modes,” but quotients nuisance directions while leaving a small holonomy defect that is globally forbidden. That is possible, but must be finite-audited.

---

# 8. Best theorem form

The correct atlas theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Carrier-lifted atlas holonomy theorem.}
}
\]

There exist finite carrier quotient spaces

\[
\mathcal Q_{\car}^{A},
\qquad A\in\{\ord,\tr,\sing\},
\]

lifted transition maps

\[
\widetilde{\mathcal T}_{A\to B}^{(R)}
:
\mathcal Q_{\car}^{A}
\to
\mathcal Q_{\car}^{B},
\]

and local atlas plaquettes \(p\), such that:

## Global upper

\[
\sum_p
\left|
\Lambda_{\atlas,p}^{(R)}
V_{\glob}
\right|^2
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

## D4 visibility

\[
\operatorname{rank}
\left(
\Lambda_{\atlas,p}^{(R)}
E_{\car}^{A}L_{\clean}^{A}
\right)\ge1
\]

on the extracted chart.

## Mobility / multiplicity

Each extracted good point contributes to polynomially many plaquettes, and plaquette multiplicity is polynomial.

Then finite D4 can close.

---

# 9. Expected failure modes

The atlas route fails if any of these happens:

1. **No carrier lift.**  
   Transitions exist only for scalar \(u\), not for \(\mathcal Q_{\car}\).

2. **Exact functorial lift.**  
   The carrier lift is exact around loops, giving rank zero.

3. **Projection holonomy too large.**  
   The loop defect is not globally small.

4. **Projection holonomy D4-invisible.**  
   The small defect lives in nuisance/high-order directions, not \([\mathsf C_7]\) or \([\mathsf C_{Y12}]\).

5. **Nonlocal loop only.**  
   The atlas identity is a single global Type III identity, not local plaquettes; complement cancellation returns.

6. **Branch overlap insufficient.**  
   Ordinary/transition/singular charts do not overlap in the regions where extracted good points live.

---

# 10. Final status after this attack

The carrier-lifted atlas loop remains the best finite-D4 \(\Lambda\) candidate, but it has sharpened into a specific tradeoff:

\[
\boxed{
\text{D4-visible atlas holonomy must be small globally.}
}
\]

That requires more than scalar atlas exactness. It requires a finite carrier quotient lift whose loop defect is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{small on actual global rows}
}
\]

but

\[
\boxed{
\text{nonzero on local D4 carriers}.
}
\]

This is possible only if D4 measures a finite carrier holonomy defect that the global scalar source cannot realize.

The next finite audit is therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Compute transition-projection commutators on }[\mathsf C_7],[\mathsf C_{Y12}].
}
\]

If the commutators vanish, atlas \(\Lambda\) is rank zero.

If they are nonzero, then prove the atlas holonomy upper.

## Agent 6 continued attack — projection holonomy rank test

The atlas-loop candidate is still the best finite-D4 \(\Lambda\) candidate, but it now has a sharp internal fork. If the lifted atlas transitions are exact full-jet maps, the loop is rank zero. If the lifted maps use finite projections or quotient reductions, the loop may see D4—but then its global upper is no longer automatic. The coordinator’s latest synthesis explicitly puts this \(\Lambda\)-construction/refutation at the downstream center. 

# 1. Model the lifted atlas maps as projected transports

Let the full local formal jet/source space in chart \(A\) be

\[
\mathcal F_A.
\]

Let the finite carrier quotient be obtained by

\[
\mathcal Q_A
=
P_A\mathcal F_A/\mathcal N_A,
\]

where \(P_A\) is a finite projection and \(\mathcal N_A\) is the nuisance subspace.

Let the exact scalar/chart transition be

\[
T_{A\to B}:\mathcal F_A\to \mathcal F_B.
\]

A lifted finite transition has the form

\[
\widetilde T_{A\to B}
=
\pi_B P_B T_{A\to B}\iota_A,
\]

where

\[
\iota_A:\mathcal Q_A\to \mathcal F_A
\]

chooses a representative and

\[
\pi_B:P_B\mathcal F_B\to \mathcal Q_B
\]

is quotient projection.

The atlas loop is

\[
\Lambda_{\atlas}
=
\widetilde T_{C\to A}\widetilde T_{B\to C}\widetilde T_{A\to B}
-\mathrm{Id}_{\mathcal Q_A}.
\]

Since

\[
T_{C\to A}T_{B\to C}T_{A\to B}
=
\mathrm{Id}
\]

on the full scalar/formal space, all nonzero atlas holonomy comes from the failure of \(P\), \(\pi\), and \(\iota\) to commute with the exact transitions.

Thus

\[
\boxed{
\Lambda_{\atlas}
\text{ is a finite projection/quotient commutator.}
}
\]

This is good: it is a finite algebraic object.

---

# 2. The live-carrier rank test

Let

\[
W_{\rm D4}
=
\operatorname{span}\{[\mathsf C_7],[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\}
\subset \mathcal Q_{\ord}.
\]

The finite rank test is

\[
\operatorname{rank}
\left(
\Lambda_{\atlas}|_{W_{\rm D4}}
\right).
\]

Because

\[
L_{\clean}
\]

is invertible on Gram-good patches, this is equivalent to testing

\[
\operatorname{rank}
\left(
\Lambda_{\atlas}E_{\car}L_{\clean}
\right).
\]

Now the crucial observation:

\[
\boxed{
\Lambda_{\atlas}|_{W_{\rm D4}}\neq 0
}
\]

only if exact chart transport sends at least one of the live carriers through a component that is treated differently by the finite projections/quotients in the loop.

In formula form, for a live carrier \(w\in W_{\rm D4}\),

\[
\Lambda_{\atlas}w
=
\pi_A P_A T_{C\to A}P_C T_{B\to C}P_B T_{A\to B}\iota_A w
-
w.
\]

Since the full loop is identity,

\[
T_{C\to A}T_{B\to C}T_{A\to B}\iota_Aw=\iota_Aw.
\]

So

\[
\Lambda_{\atlas}w
\]

is entirely a sum of terms involving

\[
(1-P_B),\quad (1-P_C),\quad (1-P_A),
\]

and nuisance projections.

Thus live D4 is atlas-visible only if the transported carrier leaks into a mode that is cut or quotiented somewhere and then returns to the retained carrier quotient as a nontrivial defect.

---

# 3. Two mutually exclusive regimes

## Regime A: carrier-stable projection

If for every adjacent transition

\[
P_B T_{A\to B}\iota_A(W_{\rm D4})
=
T_{A\to B}\iota_A(W_{\rm D4})
\]

modulo nuisance, and the transition maps \(W_{\rm D4}\) isomorphically to the corresponding live carrier plane, then

\[
\Lambda_{\atlas}|_{W_{\rm D4}}=0.
\]

This is the likely outcome if the finite carrier package was defined invariantly.

Then atlas \(\Lambda\) is D4-invisible.

## Regime B: carrier-unstable projection

If some transition sends

\[
[\mathsf C_7]
\quad\text{or}\quad
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\]

into a combination involving modes outside the target carrier quotient, and the return loop leaves a finite residue in the retained quotient, then

\[
\operatorname{rank}\Lambda_{\atlas}|_{W_{\rm D4}}\ge1.
\]

This is the desired case.

But the same leakage is exactly the atlas projection defect. The global upper becomes a bound on such leakage for actual global rows.

So the downstream theorem becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\text{D4 carrier leakage under atlas projection is locally nonzero but globally small.}
}
\]

That is a very specific new statement.

---

# 4. The projection-rank tradeoff

There is a fundamental tradeoff.

If \(P_A,P_B,P_C\) retain all modes needed to represent order \(7/9\) carrier transport exactly, then

\[
\Lambda_{\atlas}|_{W_{\rm D4}}=0.
\]

If they discard enough to create nonzero holonomy on order \(7/9\), then the actual global atlas loop error may be of the same order as the D4 carrier itself.

Thus the desired situation is narrow:

\[
\boxed{
\text{projection holonomy is nonzero on formal D4 carriers but small on actual global rows.}
}
\]

This can happen only if actual global rows satisfy an extra compatibility condition not satisfied by arbitrary local D4 carriers.

That is precisely what a forbidden identity is supposed to express.

So atlas \(\Lambda\) remains plausible, but it requires a real global compatibility theorem for actual rows—not just coordinate functoriality.

---

# 5. What finite data must be computed

For each adjacent chart transition \(A\to B\), compute the block matrix

\[
T_{A\to B}^{\rm live}
:
W_{\rm D4}^{A}
\to
\mathcal Q_B
\oplus
\mathcal R_B,
\]

where

\[
\mathcal Q_B
\]

is the retained carrier quotient and

\[
\mathcal R_B
\]

is the discarded/high/nuisance complement relevant to the finite lift.

Write

\[
T_{A\to B}^{\rm live}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
T_{QQ}^{A\to B}\\
T_{RQ}^{A\to B}
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

The leakage block is

\[
T_{RQ}^{A\to B}.
\]

If all leakage blocks vanish on \(W_{\rm D4}\), then atlas rank is zero.

If at least one leakage block is nonzero, compute the return effect

\[
\mathcal R_B
\to
\mathcal Q_A
\]

through the rest of the loop. The holonomy matrix is schematically

\[
M_{\atlas}
\sim
\sum_{\text{transition sites}}
\text{return}_{R\to Q}\,
T_{RQ}.
\]

The finite rank test is

\[
\operatorname{rank}M_{\atlas}\ge1.
\]

This is the exact algebraic audit.

---

# 6. Expected rank by chart pair

## Ordinary \(\to\) transition

This is the most likely source of leakage.

Ordinary chart carriers are built in the scale

\[
\lambda Q\asymp1,
\]

while transition chart carriers use weighted scaling

\[
\mu=\lambda Q
\]

with \((\log Q)^{-C}\lesssim\mu\lesssim1\).

If the order-\(7/9\) ordinary carriers transform into weighted transition modes with \(\mu\)-dependent normalization, finite projection may not commute.

Potential rank: nonzero.

## Transition \(\to\) singular

Also plausible, especially if singular charts use projected canonical \(L^2\) basis elements rather than raw Taylor carriers.

If transition finite jets are projected into a singular basis

\[
V_R=\operatorname{span}\{\Phi_3,\ldots,\Phi_{R+3}\},
\]

then order-\(7/9\) carriers may leak into high singular modes or be reshuffled.

Potential rank: nonzero.

## Singular \(\to\) ordinary

This is likely the hardest and may be ill-conditioned. If the singular basis is complete enough, the return may recover the ordinary carrier exactly; if not, holonomy appears.

Potential rank: unknown.

---

# 7. Interaction with local \(L^2\)-Gate–1

The new \(L^2\)-Gate–1 helps because singular/transition observables no longer need to be exact finite coefficient carriers. But the atlas \(\Lambda\) does need carrier-lifted transitions.

This creates a separation of duties:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Gate--1 uses scalar }L^2\text{ charts.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\Lambda_{\atlas}\text{ uses finite carrier-lifted chart transitions.}
}
\]

So scalar branch transitions can be smooth and exact, while carrier lifts can carry holonomy.

This is coherent.

---

# 8. What the global upper must say

The atlas global upper is not simply scalar atlas exactness. Exact scalar atlas loops give zero on the full scalar source, but finite carrier holonomy is a projection artifact.

The needed upper is:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_p
|\Lambda_{\atlas,p}V_{\glob}|^2
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This means actual global rows have small finite carrier atlas holonomy.

Why might this be true?

Only if actual global rows are compatible across charts to higher precision than arbitrary local D4 carriers. That is, the global scalar source has a coherent full expansion, and the finite carrier projections in overlapping charts almost agree.

This is a real theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\text{finite carrier atlas coherence for actual }q''_{\can}.
}
\]

It is analogous to a finite-element consistency estimate: exact function has small projection holonomy; arbitrary local finite data may not.

That is plausible if the projections are high-order enough and chart overlaps are controlled.

---

# 9. Downstream theorem reformulated

The carrier-lifted atlas route now requires three finite/analytic pieces.

## 9.1 Atlas coherence upper

For actual global rows,

\[
\sum_p
|\Lambda_{\atlas,p}V_{\glob}|^2
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

## 9.2 D4 holonomy rank

For local D4 live carriers,

\[
\operatorname{rank}
\left(
\Lambda_{\atlas,p}E_{\car}L_{\clean}
\right)\ge1
\]

on extracted good chart plaquettes.

## 9.3 Atomic mobility

The atlas plaquettes \(p\) are local atoms, so if multiplicity is polynomial, Type I mobility follows.

Thus if 9.1 and 9.2 hold, the downstream contradiction is structurally sound.

---

# 10. Main risk: exactness may force rank zero

The risk is that the same regularity/coherence theorem proving the global upper may also imply that the order-\(7/9\) carriers are transported coherently, making rank zero.

To avoid this, the D4 carriers must represent **incompatible local finite data**: data satisfying local Gate2/D4 but not realizable as restrictions of a single global scalar source across the atlas.

This is the philosophical core:

\[
\boxed{
\text{D4 should be a local finite-carrier obstruction to global atlas coherence.}
}
\]

If that is true, atlas \(\Lambda\) is the right contradiction.

If D4 carriers are globally coherent finite jets, atlas \(\Lambda\) misses them.

---

# 11. Concrete next calculation

The next calculation is:

1. Choose one overlap, probably ordinary-transition.

2. Write the ordinary live carrier basis:

\[
[\mathsf C_7^{\ord}],
\quad
[\mathsf C_{Y12}^{\ord}].
\]

3. Express these as finite local source jets.

4. Change variables to transition scaling.

5. Project onto transition carrier quotient.

6. Compare with the transition live carrier basis.

7. Compute leakage.

If leakage is nonzero, continue around a loop or test whether a local two-chart mismatch already gives a Type II localized identity.

If leakage is zero, ordinary-transition is not the source.

Repeat for transition-singular.

---

# Final status

The atlas loop remains viable but is now very concrete:

\[
\boxed{
\text{compute projection/transition commutators on the live D4 carriers.}
}
\]

The downstream proof can close only if:

\[
\boxed{
\text{actual global rows have small carrier atlas holonomy,}
}
\]

but

\[
\boxed{
\text{local D4 carriers have nonzero carrier atlas holonomy.}
}
\]

This is the sharpest finite-D4 contradiction formulation so far.

## Agent 6 continued attack — atlas-loop rank likely zero under jet-complete chart lifts

I pushed the carrier-lifted atlas loop one step further by modeling the ordinary–transition overlap. The goal is to test whether a finite carrier atlas loop can be nonzero on the live D4 carriers

\[
[\mathsf C_7],\qquad [\mathsf C_{Y12}].
\]

The current downstream candidate is still

\[
\Lambda_{\atlas}
=
\widetilde{\mathcal T}_{\sing\to\ord}
\widetilde{\mathcal T}_{\tr\to\sing}
\widetilde{\mathcal T}_{\ord\to\tr}
-
\mathrm{Id},
\]

and the finite audit is whether

\[
\operatorname{rank}
\left(
\Lambda_{\atlas}E_{\car}L_{\clean}
\right)\ge1.
\]

The update explicitly identifies carrier-lifted atlas loops as the best remaining finite-D4 \(\Lambda\) candidate, but only if the live D4 carrier plane has nonzero atlas holonomy. 

# 1. Jet-complete atlas transitions annihilate finite D4

Let \(F\) be the local scalar source

\[
F(x)=\lambda^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x).
\]

Suppose the ordinary chart and transition chart are related by an analytic coordinate change

\[
x_{\tr}=\phi(x_{\ord}),
\qquad
\phi(0)=0,\qquad
\phi'(0)\ne0.
\]

Let

\[
J_{\le R}^{\ord}F
\]

denote the ordinary \(R\)-jet, and similarly for the transition chart.

There is a canonical finite-jet transition map

\[
T_{\ord\to\tr}^{(R)}:
J_{\le R}^{\ord}F
\longmapsto
J_{\le R}^{\tr}F.
\]

Crucially, the coefficients of \(J_{\le R}^{\tr}F\) depend only on the coefficients of \(J_{\le R}^{\ord}F\). Higher-order coefficients do **not** enter lower-order Taylor coefficients after analytic composition.

Therefore, if the carrier lift retains all jet information through order \(R\ge 9\), then the transition is functorial on the order-\(7\) and order-\(9\) carrier data.

In particular,

\[
T_{\ord\to\tr}^{(R)}[\mathsf C_7^{\ord}]
=
[\mathsf C_7^{\tr}],
\]

and

\[
T_{\ord\to\tr}^{(R)}[\mathsf C_{Y12}^{\ord}]
=
[\mathsf C_{Y12}^{\tr}]
\]

up to the deterministic change-of-basis in the carrier plane.

Thus the atlas loop around exact jet-complete transitions satisfies

\[
\Lambda_{\atlas}[\mathsf C_7]=0,
\qquad
\Lambda_{\atlas}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]=0.
\]

So

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}
\left(
\Lambda_{\atlas}E_{\car}L_{\clean}
\right)=0
}
\]

for any jet-complete carrier lift through order \(9\).

This is the first serious no-go against the atlas-loop candidate.

---

# 2. Why projection holonomy cannot come from ordinary finite jets

Earlier, the hope was that atlas holonomy could arise because finite projections do not commute with chart transition:

\[
P_B T_{A\to B}\ne T_{A\to B}P_A.
\]

But for finite jets this commutator vanishes on low-order data, provided the projection keeps every coefficient through the relevant order.

The D4 live carriers are already low-order:

\[
7,\qquad 9.
\]

If the carrier quotient keeps enough information to formulate order-\(7/9\) D4, then it also keeps enough information for chart transition to preserve those carriers exactly.

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\text{ordinary finite-jet truncation does not create D4-visible atlas holonomy.}
}
\]

To get nonzero holonomy, the carrier lift must discard or quotient something **inside** the order-\(7/9\) live carrier plane.

But then one of two things happens:

1. the D4 theorem cannot use that discarded component; or  
2. the global atlas error is no longer small because actual finite jets also contain that component.

That is the same visibility/smallness tradeoff seen in the \(\Xi_N\) and partial-EF residual analyses.

---

# 3. Quotient mismatch as the only possible atlas source

There is one remaining subtle possibility.

Even if full jets transition functorially, the **quotient decompositions** may be chart-dependent:

\[
\mathcal Q_{\car}^{A}
=
\mathcal M_{\car}^{A}/\mathcal C_{\car}^{A}.
\]

If the nuisance submodule is not preserved under chart transition,

\[
T_{A\to B}\mathcal C_{\car}^{A}
\ne
\mathcal C_{\car}^{B},
\]

then the induced quotient transition may have holonomy.

This could produce a nonzero atlas loop even on low-order carriers.

So the atlas candidate reduces to a very precise finite question:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Are the nuisance carrier submodules functorial under branch chart transitions?}
}
\]

If they are functorial, atlas rank is zero.

If they are not functorial, atlas holonomy may be nonzero.

But if non-functorial nuisance quotients create the holonomy, then the global upper requires a theorem saying actual global rows are nevertheless compatible across those non-functorial quotients:

\[
\|\Lambda_{\atlas}V_{\glob}\|^2\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

That is not automatic.

Thus the atlas path now depends on **quotient non-functoriality plus global compatibility**.

---

# 4. Rank test in quotient language

For each chart \(A\), write

\[
\mathcal M_A
=
W_A\oplus \mathcal C_A,
\]

where

\[
W_A=
\operatorname{span}\{[\mathsf C_7^A],[\mathsf C_{Y12}^A]\}
\]

is the live D4 carrier plane and \(\mathcal C_A\) is the nuisance submodule.

Let

\[
T_{A\to B}:\mathcal M_A\to\mathcal M_B
\]

be the exact transition on finite carrier modules.

The induced quotient map is well-defined only if

\[
T_{A\to B}\mathcal C_A\subseteq \mathcal C_B.
\]

If so, the map on quotients is functorial.

Then the atlas loop on \(W_A\) is

\[
\Lambda_{\atlas}|_{W_A}
=
T_{C\to A}^{q}T_{B\to C}^{q}T_{A\to B}^{q}
-
\mathrm{Id}.
\]

Since the full transition loop is identity, nonzero holonomy can only occur if one of the quotient maps is not induced functorially and instead uses a chosen projection/splitting.

Thus the finite rank is controlled by the nuisance-mixing blocks

\[
T_{A\to B}^{CW}:W_A\to \mathcal C_B,
\]

\[
T_{A\to B}^{WC}:\mathcal C_A\to W_B.
\]

If these vanish or cancel around the loop, rank zero.

If

\[
W_A\to \mathcal C_B\to W_C
\]

returns nontrivially, rank may be one or two.

So the concrete matrix to compute is:

\[
\boxed{
W_A
\xrightarrow{T^{CW}}
\mathcal C_B
\xrightarrow{T^{WC}}
W_C.
}
\]

This is now the exact atlas finite audit.

---

# 5. Why non-functorial quotient holonomy may be dangerous

If the live D4 carrier leaks into nuisance directions in one chart and returns as live in another, then atlas holonomy may see D4.

But the D4 theorem itself relies on quotienting nuisance directions as irrelevant. If atlas holonomy depends on nuisance leakage, then we must ensure we are not contradicting our own quotient principle.

Specifically:

- carrier-quotient D4 says nuisance directions should not affect row energy;
- atlas holonomy would say nuisance leakage around chart loops is forbidden and measurable.

These can coexist only if nuisance directions are local gauge artifacts, but their **failure to glue globally** is meaningful.

That is a possible geometric picture:

\[
\boxed{
\text{local nuisance is gauge, but global gauge holonomy is forbidden.}
}
\]

This is plausible and interesting. It makes the atlas loop a genuine gauge-cocycle obstruction.

But it is new structure. It is not already in the D4 quotient theorem.

---

# 6. Expected outcome for ordinary–transition

For ordinary–transition, if transition merely rescales the local variable

\[
x_{\tr}=\mu x_{\ord}
\]

and reweights derivatives by \(\mu^{-r}\), then the order-\(7/9\) carriers transform diagonally:

\[
\mathsf C_7^{\ord}\mapsto \mu^{a_7}\mathsf C_7^{\tr},
\]

\[
\mathsf C_{Y12}^{\ord}\mapsto \mu^{a_9}\mathsf C_{Y12}^{\tr}.
\]

If the quotient normalizes these powers, the transition is functorial and rank zero.

Nonzero holonomy would require that ordinary D4 carriers contain components which the transition chart assigns partly to nuisance or higher weighted modes. But if transition jets are weighted precisely to avoid degeneration, this mixing may have been designed away.

So my expectation:

\[
\boxed{
\text{ordinary–transition chart lift likely has rank zero on }W_{\rm D4}.
}
\]

---

# 7. Expected outcome for transition–singular

Transition–singular is more promising.

The singular chart may use an \(L^2\)-projected basis

\[
V_R=\operatorname{span}\{\Phi_3,\ldots,\Phi_{R+3}\}
\]

rather than raw Taylor/jet carriers. Projection onto \(V_R\) need not preserve order-\(7/9\) Taylor carrier structure exactly.

If the live D4 carrier expressed in transition variables has nontrivial projection error into singular modes, then atlas holonomy may be nonzero.

However, if the singular branch has been redefined using full local \(L^2\) source energy and projections are only proof tools, the carrier quotient used for D4 may not even exist in the singular chart.

Thus transition–singular can produce atlas \(\Lambda\) only if we define a singular carrier quotient lift, not merely scalar \(L^2\) energy.

This is a new burden.

---

# 8. Updated atlas verdict

The atlas loop is still the best candidate, but it now depends on a very narrow mechanism:

\[
\boxed{
\text{non-functoriality of carrier quotient splittings across branch charts.}
}
\]

If branch carrier quotients are natural/functorial through order \(9\), then

\[
\boxed{
\Lambda_{\atlas}\text{ has rank }0\text{ on finite D4.}
}
\]

If they are not functorial, then atlas holonomy may see D4, but the proof must add:

\[
\boxed{
\text{global smallness of the carrier quotient gluing defect for actual }q''_{\can}.
}
\]

So atlas \(\Lambda\) is not free. It is a new carrier-gluing theorem.

---

# 9. Next concrete finite audit

The next computation should not be the full loop. It should be local blocks.

For each chart transition \(A\to B\):

1. Define the carrier module \(\mathcal M_A\) and nuisance submodule \(\mathcal C_A\).

2. Define the live plane \(W_A\).

3. Compute the block decomposition of transition:

\[
T_{A\to B}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
T^{WW} & T^{WC}\\
T^{CW} & T^{CC}
\end{pmatrix}
:
W_A\oplus\mathcal C_A
\to
W_B\oplus\mathcal C_B.
\]

4. Check whether

\[
T^{CW}=0
\]

on \(W_A\).

If yes for every transition, atlas rank zero.

If no, compute whether the leakage returns to \(W_A\) around the loop.

This is the finite audit.

---

# 10. Final status

\[
\boxed{
\text{Exact jet-complete atlas transitions are D4-invisible.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Atlas D4 visibility can only come from non-functorial carrier quotient gluing.}
}
\]

This is the sharpest formulation yet of the atlas-loop opportunity.

The carrier-lifted atlas route remains possible, but it now requires a new theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\text{actual global rows have small carrier-gluing holonomy, while local D4 carriers have nonzero gluing holonomy.}
}
\]

If this theorem cannot be built, finite order-\(7/9\) D4 still lacks a contradiction channel.

## Agent 6 — partial EF residuals for completion coherence and tail suppression

I treat partial explicit-formula residuals as upstream tools, not as the primary downstream \(\Lambda\). The current architecture now defines Gate–1 by local scalar \(L^2\) source energy of

\[
u=q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2,
\]

so partial EF residuals are most useful for proving the full-source \(L^2\) lower bound from finite prime/dual packets. 

# 1. Candidate residuals

Fix a branch-weighted window \(W=W_{\branch}\). Define finite truncations

\[
P_{2,X},\qquad D_{2,X}
\]

of the prime and dual/completion pieces. Let

\[
F_X:=B_2+P_{2,X}+D_{2,X}.
\]

Then the main residual is

\[
R_X
:=
q''_{\can}-F_X
=
(P_2-P_{2,X})+(D_2-D_{2,X})+E_2.
\]

For comparing two regularizations, also define

\[
R_{X,Y}^{\rm reg}
:=
(B_2+P_{2,X}+D_{2,X}+E_{2,X})
-
(B_2+P_{2,Y}+D_{2,Y}+E_{2,Y}).
\]

Localized versions use a smooth cutoff \(\psi\):

\[
R_{X,\psi}:=\psi R_X.
\]

---

# 2. Completion coherence theorem for \(\mathrm{Agg}L^2(q''_{\can})\)

The useful theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\|R_X\|_{L^2(W)}^2
\le
Q^{-A_0}\mathcal N_{\can}(W).
}
\]

Together with a finite packet lower bound

\[
\boxed{
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W)}^2
\ge
Q^{-A_1}\mathcal N_{\can}(W),
}
\]

this implies

\[
\|q''_{\can}\|_{L^2(W)}
\ge
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W)}-\|R_X\|_{L^2(W)}
\ge
Q^{-A}\mathcal N_{\can}(W)^{1/2},
\]

after choosing \(A_0\) large relative to \(A_1\). Hence

\[
\boxed{
\|q''_{\can}\|_{L^2(W)}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\mathcal N_{\can}(W).
}
\]

So the aggregate Gate–1 theorem can be reduced to two components:

\[
\boxed{
\text{finite prime/dual mean-square lower bound for }F_X;
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{localized EF residual upper for }R_X.
}
\]

This is the correct role for partial EF residuals.

---

# 3. What should be in \(\mathcal N_{\can}(W)\)?

The reference normalization should not be \(P_2\)-only unless one proves that the other pieces are perturbative in the same norm.

A robust definition is

\[
\mathcal N_{\can}(W)
:=
\|B_2\|_{L^2(W)}^2
+
\|P_{2,X}\|_{L^2(W)}^2
+
\|D_{2,X}\|_{L^2(W)}^2
\]

plus relevant diagonal cross-normalizations if \(P_{2,X}\) and \(D_{2,X}\) are paired by the AFE. Equivalently, use the diagonal coefficient mass of the finite canonical packet \(F_X\).

Then the lower bound

\[
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W)}^2\ge Q^{-A}\mathcal N_{\can}(W)
\]

is a finite-packet lower-frame theorem.

If instead one defines

\[
\mathcal N_{\can}(W)=\|P_{2,X}\|_{L^2(W)}^2,
\]

then one must separately prove

\[
\|B_2+D_{2,X}\|_{L^2(W)}
\le Q^C\|P_{2,X}\|_{L^2(W)}
\]

and that destructive cancellation among \(B_2,P_{2,X},D_{2,X}\) cannot suppress \(F_X\). That is unnecessary risk.

---

# 4. Finite packet mean square

For

\[
F_X(t)=B_2(t)+\sum_{n\le X}a_n n^{-it}
+\sum_{n\le X'}b_n n^{it},
\]

one gets

\[
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W)}^2
=
\sum_{n,n'\le X}a_n\overline{a_{n'}}\widehat W(\log(n/n'))
+
\sum_{n,n'\le X'}b_n\overline{b_{n'}}\widehat W(-\log(n/n'))
\]

plus main/dual cross terms and background interactions.

A sufficient theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W)}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\left(
\sum_{n\le X}|a_n|^2W_0
+
\sum_{n\le X'}|b_n|^2W_0
+
\|B_2\|_{L^2(W)}^2
\right).
}
\]

This is a lower large-sieve / lower-frame theorem for the finite canonical packet under \(W\).

It is easier than the old pointwise projection theorem because it is an \(L^2\) statement, not pointwise at every zero.

---

# 5. Tail suppression for O2/O3 determinant survival

Partial EF residuals can also help simplify the full determinant

\[
D_{\full}=\det A_{\full}.
\]

Decompose

\[
A_{\full}=A_{\le X}+A_{\tail},
\]

where \(A_{\le X}\) is built from

\[
B_2+P_{2,X}+D_{2,X},
\]

and

\[
A_{\tail}
\]

comes from

\[
R_X=(P_2-P_{2,X})+(D_2-D_{2,X})+E_2.
\]

A useful perturbative theorem is

\[
\boxed{
\|A_{\tail}\|_{\op}
\le
Q^{-A}
\sigma_{\min}(A_{\le X})
}
\]

on the relevant weighted set, or in aggregate,

\[
\boxed{
\int
\|A_{\tail}(m)\|_{\op}^2\,d\nu(m)
\le
Q^{-A}
\int
\mathcal H_{23}(A_{\le X}(m))\,d\nu(m).
}
\]

Then determinant and harmonic exterior ratio are stable:

\[
\mathcal H_{23}(A_{\full})
\ge
Q^{-C}
\mathcal H_{23}(A_{\le X})
\]

on a polynomial-weight subset.

This reduces O2/O3 determinant survival from the full infinite/canonical source to the finite prime/dual/background matrix \(A_{\le X}\).

But this is conditional on tail being small relative to the **least singular direction**, not merely small in \(L^2\). That is stronger than the Gate–1 completion estimate.

So partial residuals help O2/O3 only if the residual control is operator-level or exterior-stable, not just source \(L^2\).

---

# 6. Role of \(B_2\)

\(B_2\) should not be discarded as “background” unless its size and phase are proven perturbative relative to the finite packet.

For Gate–1 \(L^2\), there are three possible regimes:

## \(B_2\) perturbative

If

\[
\|B_2\|_{L^2(W)}
\le
Q^{-A}\|P_{2,X}+D_{2,X}\|_{L^2(W)},
\]

then it can be absorbed.

## \(B_2\) dominant positive/reference

If \(B_2\) is sizable, include it in

\[
F_X
\]

and \(\mathcal N_{\can}\). Then it contributes to source energy.

## \(B_2\) cancellation-active

If \(B_2\) cancels the prime/dual pieces in \(L^2\), then one needs a specific anti-cancellation theorem:

\[
\|B_2+P_{2,X}+D_{2,X}\|_{L^2(W)}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\left(
\|B_2\|^2+\|P_{2,X}\|^2+\|D_{2,X}\|^2
\right).
\]

Thus \(B_2\) must remain part of the canonical finite packet unless proven harmless.

For O2/O3, \(B_2\) may enter as an affine correction to the augmented frame. It should remain in \(A_{\le X}\) unless a perturbative bound is proven.

---

# 7. Why partial EF residuals remain poor downstream \(\Lambda\)'s

The full residual

\[
R_{\full}=q''_{\can}-(B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2)
\]

is exact but definitional. It annihilates the whole canonical object and, if varied consistently, also annihilates local D4 carrier directions. Expected rank:

\[
R_{\full}E_{\car}[\mathsf C_7]=0,
\qquad
R_{\full}E_{\car}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]=0.
\]

A small truncation residual

\[
R_X=q''_{\can}-F_X
\]

is also likely D4-invisible if \(F_X\) approximates \(q''_{\can}\) well. If \(R_X\) is D4-visible, then it contains non-negligible low-order curvature content and probably is not small.

Thus partial EF residuals have the same visibility/smallness tradeoff:

\[
\boxed{
\text{small residual}\Rightarrow\text{D4-invisible;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{D4-visible residual}\Rightarrow\text{not small.}
}
\]

A prime/zero partial commutator may evade this, but only if it is genuinely external and localized; that is a separate, harder candidate and is weaker than the carrier-lifted atlas loop.

---

# 8. Recommended theorem stack

## Upstream completion coherence

\[
\boxed{
\begin{gathered}
F_X=B_2+P_{2,X}+D_{2,X},\\
\|R_X\|_{L^2(W_{\branch})}^2
\le
Q^{-A}\mathcal N_{\can}(W_{\branch}),\\
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W_{\branch})}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\mathcal N_{\can}(W_{\branch})
\\
\Longrightarrow
\|q''_{\can}\|_{L^2(W_{\branch})}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\mathcal N_{\can}(W_{\branch}).
\end{gathered}
}
\]

## O2/O3 tail suppression

\[
\boxed{
A_{\full}=A_{\le X}+A_{\tail},
\qquad
\|A_{\tail}\|_{\op}
\le
Q^{-A}\sigma_{\min}(A_{\le X})
\Longrightarrow
\mathcal H_{23}(A_{\full})
\ge
Q^{-C}\mathcal H_{23}(A_{\le X}).
}
\]

In aggregate, use a dyadic/tail-good subset where this operator inequality holds.

---

# 9. Final classification

\[
\boxed{
\text{Partial EF residuals are strong upstream tools for completion coherence.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{They may help O2/O3 by suppressing tail/completion terms in }A_{\full}.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{They are not currently credible as the primary downstream }\Lambda.}
}
\]

The primary downstream \(\Lambda\) search should remain with the carrier-lifted atlas loop. Partial EF residuals should support the \(L^2\)-Gate–1 and full determinant survival layers.

## Agent 6 continued attack — making EF completion coherence quantitative

I continued the Agent 6 track: partial explicit-formula residuals should be used upstream for \(\mathrm{Agg}L^2(q''_{\can})\) and possibly for O2/O3 tail suppression, not as the primary downstream \(\Lambda\). The current architecture explicitly treats Gate–1 as local scalar \(L^2\) energy of \(u=q''_{\can}\), while \(\Lambda\) remains a separate downstream problem. 

# 1. Correct finite approximation target

Write

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

For a truncation parameter \(X\), define

\[
F_X:=B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X},
\]

and

\[
R_X:=q''_{\can}-F_X
=
P_{2,>X}+D_{2,>X}+E_2.
\]

For branch weights \(W=W_{\branch}\), the upstream completion theorem should be:

\[
\boxed{
\|R_X\|_{L^2(W)}^2
\le
\varepsilon_X
\mathcal N_{\can}(W)
}
\]

with

\[
\varepsilon_X\le Q^{-A}.
\]

Then it is enough to prove

\[
\boxed{
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W)}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\mathcal N_{\can}(W).
}
\]

This gives

\[
\|q''_{\can}\|_{L^2(W)}
\ge
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W)}-\|R_X\|_{L^2(W)}
\ge
Q^{-A'}\mathcal N_{\can}(W)^{1/2}.
\]

So the Gate–1 source-energy problem reduces to:

\[
\boxed{
\text{finite canonical packet lower frame}
+
\text{tail/completion upper}.
}
\]

---

# 2. The correct normalization

The normalization should be attached to the finite canonical packet, not to \(P_2\) alone.

A safe choice is

\[
\mathcal N_{\can}(W)
=
\mathcal N_X(W)
+
\mathcal N_{\rm bg}(W),
\]

where

\[
\mathcal N_X(W)
=
\sum_{n\le X}|a_n|^2\widehat W(0)
+
\sum_{n\le X'}|b_n|^2\widehat W(0)
\]

for the prime and dual Dirichlet packets, and

\[
\mathcal N_{\rm bg}(W)=\|B_2\|_{L^2(W)}^2
\]

if the gamma/background term is not perturbative.

Then the finite lower-frame theorem is

\[
\boxed{
\|B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}\|_{L^2(W)}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\mathcal N_{\can}(W).
}
\]

This formulation prevents a false proof where \(P_2\) has healthy diagonal mass but is cancelled by \(D_2\) or \(B_2\).

---

# 3. Tail estimate required

For

\[
P_{2,>X}(t)=\sum_{n>X} a_n n^{-it},
\]

a typical \(L^2(W)\) estimate is

\[
\|P_{2,>X}\|_{L^2(W)}^2
=
\sum_{n,n'>X}
a_n\overline{a_{n'}}\widehat W(\log(n/n')).
\]

A sufficient condition is a tail diagonal bound plus off-diagonal control:

\[
\sum_{n>X}|a_n|^2\widehat W(0)
\le
Q^{-A}\mathcal N_{\can}(W),
\]

and

\[
\sum_{\substack{n,n'>X\\ n\ne n'}}
|a_n a_{n'}|
|\widehat W(\log(n/n'))|
\le
Q^{-A}\mathcal N_{\can}(W).
\]

Analogous estimates are needed for \(D_{2,>X}\), plus the mixed \(P_{>X}\)-\(D_{>X}\), \(B_2\)-tail, and \(E_2\)-tail cross terms.

Thus the actual completion theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\|P_{2,>X}+D_{2,>X}+E_2\|_{L^2(W)}^2
\le
Q^{-A}\mathcal N_{\can}(W).
}
\]

This must be proved in the same branch-weighted window \(W_{\branch}\), not just globally.

---

# 4. Choice of \(X\)

The truncation \(X\) must satisfy two competing constraints.

It must be large enough that

\[
P_{2,>X}+D_{2,>X}+E_2
\]

is \(L^2(W)\)-small.

But it must be small enough that the finite packet

\[
F_X=B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}
\]

admits a lower-frame theorem under \(W\). If \(X\) is too large relative to the branch window’s bandwidth, the finite mean-square Gram may become ill-conditioned.

So the truncation should be branch-dependent:

\[
X=X_b
\]

with branch scale \(\lambda_b\). A natural condition is:

\[
\log X_b
\lesssim
\text{effective bandwidth visible in } W_b.
\]

The theorem should not use one universal \(X\) unless all branches have compatible bandwidth.

---

# 5. AggL2 theorem stack

The clean stack is:

## Theorem A: finite canonical lower frame

For each branch-weighted \(W\),

\[
\boxed{
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W)}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\mathcal N_X(W).
}
\]

Here

\[
F_X=B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}.
\]

## Theorem B: localized completion coherence

\[
\boxed{
\|q''_{\can}-F_X\|_{L^2(W)}^2
\le
Q^{-A}\mathcal N_X(W).
}
\]

## Theorem C: reference nontriviality

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m)\mathcal N_{X_b}(W_{b,m})
\ge
Q^{-A}\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m).
}
\]

Then

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m)
\int_{I_b}
|\lambda_b^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda_bx)|^2dx
\ge
Q^{-A}\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m).
}
\]

This gives the aggregate Gate–1 input.

---

# 6. O2/O3 determinant tail suppression

For O2/O3, the residual must be stronger than \(L^2\)-smallness of the source.

Let

\[
A_{\full}=A_{\le X}+A_{\tail}.
\]

To transfer harmonic exterior ratio,

\[
\mathcal H_{23}(A)
=
\frac{\det(A^*A)}{e_{n-1}(A^*A)},
\]

one needs perturbation control near the least singular value.

A sufficient pointwise condition is

\[
\boxed{
\|A_{\tail}(m)\|_{\op}
\le
\frac12\sigma_{\min}(A_{\le X}(m)).
}
\]

Then

\[
\sigma_{\min}(A_{\full})
\ge
\frac12\sigma_{\min}(A_{\le X}),
\]

and

\[
\|A_{\full}\|_{\op}
\le
\|A_{\le X}\|_{\op}+\|A_{\tail}\|_{\op}.
\]

In an \(R^C\)-normalized basis, this yields

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal H_{23}(A_{\full})
\ge
Q^{-C}
\mathcal H_{23}(A_{\le X}).
}
\]

But this is too strong to demand everywhere. The aggregate version should be dyadic:

Define the tail-good set

\[
\mathcal T_{\rm good}
=
\left\{
m:
\|A_{\tail}(m)\|_{\op}
\le
\frac12\sigma_{\min}(A_{\le X}(m))
\right\}.
\]

Then prove

\[
\nu(\mathcal T_{\rm good}^c)
\le
Q^{-A}\nu(\mathcal C)
\]

under the Stack C2 measure

\[
d\nu=F_1F_{\wall}^{\forb}N\,d\rho.
\]

This gives determinant survival after discarding a small bad set.

---

# 7. Tail suppression cannot prove O2/O3 by itself

Even if

\[
A_{\tail}
\]

is negligible, one still needs the finite matrix

\[
A_{\le X}
\]

to satisfy the weighted exterior-pivot theorem:

\[
\int
F_{23}(A_{\le X})\,d\nu
\ge
Q^{-A}\nu(\mathcal C).
\]

Partial EF residuals only justify replacing \(A_{\full}\) by \(A_{\le X}\). They do not solve:

\[
\text{branch survival,}
\]

\[
\text{graph non-polynomiality,}
\]

or

\[
\text{weighted determinant anti-trough.}
\]

So their O2/O3 role is supportive, not central.

---

# 8. \(B_2\) classification

\(B_2\) should be handled as follows.

## In Gate–1

Keep \(B_2\) inside the finite canonical packet:

\[
F_X=B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}.
\]

Do not discard it unless

\[
\|B_2\|_{L^2(W)}\le Q^{-A}\|P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}\|_{L^2(W)}.
\]

If \(B_2\) is comparable, it contributes to \(\mathcal N_X(W)\).

## In O2/O3

Keep \(B_2\) inside \(A_{\le X}\) as an affine/canonical correction unless it is perturbative in the \(R^C\)-normalized operator norm.

It may alter the graph model by adding a low-rank background column/row. That should be handled in the finite graph-block analysis, not ignored.

---

# 9. Downstream \(\Lambda\) no-go remains

Partial EF residuals are poor downstream \(\Lambda\)'s for the same reason as before.

## Full residual

\[
q''_{\can}-(B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2)=0
\]

is definitional, hence rank zero on D4 if pieces are varied consistently.

## Small truncation residual

If

\[
R_X
\]

is small enough for a strong forbidden upper, then it is likely D4-invisible; the D4 carriers live in the well-approximated finite source.

## D4-visible residual

If a residual contains the D4 live carriers, then it is not tail-small and cannot provide a forbidden upper.

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\text{partial EF residuals should not be the primary downstream }\Lambda.
}
\]

They belong upstream.

---

# 10. Final theorem package

The useful theorem package is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{LocalizedEFCompletion}(W):
\quad
q''_{\can}=F_X+R_X,
\quad
\|R_X\|_{L^2(W)}^2
\le
Q^{-A}\mathcal N_X(W).
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{FiniteCanonicalMeanSquare}(W):
\quad
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W)}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\mathcal N_X(W).
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{ReferenceNontriviality}(W):
\quad
\mathcal N_X(W)
\ge
Q^{-A}\text{branch weight}.
}
\]

Together these prove \(\mathrm{Agg}L^2(q''_{\can})\).

For O2/O3 add:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{ExteriorTailGood}(\nu):
\quad
\|A_{\tail}\|_{\op}
\le
\frac12\sigma_{\min}(A_{\le X})
\quad
\text{outside }Q^{-A}\text{ weighted mass}.
}
\]

Then O2/O3 can be reduced from \(A_{\full}\) to \(A_{\le X}\).

---

# Final status

\[
\boxed{
\text{Partial EF residuals are high-value upstream tools.}
}
\]

They support:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{Agg}L^2(q''_{\can})
}
\]

and may support:

\[
\boxed{
\text{full determinant tail suppression for O2/O3}.
}
\]

They do **not** currently provide a viable downstream \(\Lambda\). The leading downstream route remains carrier-gluing atlas holonomy.

I’ll continue on the Agent 6 line by tightening the partial-EF residual role into checkable estimates: what exact \(L^2\) and operator-norm bounds are needed, and where the first obstruction likely lies.

## Agent 6 continued attack — first obstruction in EF completion coherence

The previous reduction made partial explicit-formula residuals useful upstream: they can support

\[
\mathrm{Agg}L^2(q''_{\can})
\]

and possibly simplify O2/O3 by suppressing tail/completion terms. They are not a credible downstream \(\Lambda\). I now push the upstream theorem one layer deeper.

# 1. Completion coherence splits into two different problems

For Gate–1 \(L^2\), it is enough to prove an \(L^2\)-tail estimate:

\[
\|R_X\|_{L^2(W)}^2
\le
Q^{-A}\mathcal N_X(W).
\]

For O2/O3, this is **not enough**. There one needs a matrix/operator perturbation estimate:

\[
\|A_{\tail}(m)\|_{\op}
\le
\frac12\sigma_{\min}(A_{\le X}(m))
\]

on a large weighted subset.

So the same residual \(R_X\) has two levels:

\[
\boxed{
L^2\text{-small tail}
\quad\text{for Gate--1;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{least-singular-direction small tail}
\quad\text{for O2/O3.}
}
\]

The first is plausible. The second is much harder and may fail near the O2/O3 troughs.

---

# 2. Gate–1 completion: plausible \(L^2\) theorem

Let

\[
F_X=B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X},
\qquad
R_X=P_{2,>X}+D_{2,>X}+E_2.
\]

For branch weight \(W\),

\[
\|R_X\|_{L^2(W)}^2
=
\|P_{2,>X}+D_{2,>X}+E_2\|_{L^2(W)}^2.
\]

A sufficient, checkable package is:

\[
\|P_{2,>X}\|_{L^2(W)}^2
+
\|D_{2,>X}\|_{L^2(W)}^2
+
\|E_2\|_{L^2(W)}^2
\le
Q^{-A}\mathcal N_X(W),
\]

plus cross-term control. Since

\[
\|a+b+c\|^2\le 3(\|a\|^2+\|b\|^2+\|c\|^2),
\]

one does not need delicate cancellation control for the residual upper. Triangle/Cauchy is enough.

Thus Gate–1 completion is robust: suppress each tail component separately.

The first real issue is choosing \(X\) branchwise so that:

1. \(R_X\) is small;
2. \(F_X\) still has a lower-frame theorem under \(W\).

That is the truncation balance.

---

# 3. Finite canonical mean-square: the real Gate–1 arithmetic input

After tail suppression, the main theorem is

\[
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W)}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\mathcal N_X(W).
\]

This is a lower-frame statement for the **finite full canonical packet**

\[
F_X=B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}.
\]

The dangerous failure is not the tail; it is cancellation between the finite pieces:

\[
B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}\approx0
\]

on the branch windows.

Therefore the first real Gate–1 obstruction is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{finite canonical packet lower frame, including main/dual/background cancellation.}
}
\]

A P\(_2\)-only lower bound is insufficient unless one proves \(D_2,B_2\) are perturbative or phase-separated.

---

# 4. AFE-pair lower frame: likely needed

The finite packet has a main/dual structure. A useful formulation is:

\[
F_X(t)
=
B_2(t)
+
\sum_{n\le X} a_n n^{-it}
+
e^{i\Theta(t)}
\sum_{n\le X'} b_n n^{it}.
\]

The lower-frame theorem should treat this as a **two-branch vector frame**, not as two independent scalar Dirichlet polynomials.

A sufficient theorem is:

\[
\int
\left|
B_2(t)+P_X(t)+e^{i\Theta(t)}D_X(t)
\right|^2W(t)\,dt
\ge
Q^{-A}
\left(
\|B_2\|_{L^2(W)}^2
+\|P_X\|_{L^2(W)}^2
+\|D_X\|_{L^2(W)}^2
\right).
\]

This requires excluding branch anti-lock in \(L^2\), not pointwise.

This is far more plausible than old pointwise O1b:

\[
\boxed{
\text{\(L^2\)-branch anti-lock is the right replacement for pointwise folded anti-lock.}
}
\]

It should be attacked with phase variation of \(\Theta'(t)\), not zero-conditioned pointwise arithmetic.

---

# 5. Ordinary/transition/singular difference under \(L^2\)

With \(L^2\)-Gate–1, branches differ only by the weight/window \(W_b\) and scale \(\lambda_b\). The finite mean-square theorem should be stated branch-uniformly:

\[
\|F_{X_b}\|_{L^2(W_b)}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\mathcal N_{X_b}(W_b).
\]

Ordinary branch:

- larger usable frequency band;
- main/dual phases oscillate at ordinary scale.

Transition branch:

- weighted scaling \(\mu=\lambda Q\);
- phase variation can be weaker but windows are adapted.

Singular branch:

- if using full \(L^2\), no projection-capture theorem is needed;
- but lower-frame must prevent \(q''_{\can}\) from being small on singular windows.

Thus singular is no longer a special coefficient problem; it is a local finite-packet lower-frame problem at a smaller scale.

---

# 6. O2/O3 tail suppression: likely the harder residual use

For O2/O3, suppose

\[
A_{\full}=A_{\le X}+A_{\tail}.
\]

The condition

\[
\|A_{\tail}\|_{\op}
\le
\frac12\sigma_{\min}(A_{\le X})
\]

is very sensitive. It fails precisely where

\[
A_{\le X}
\]

is nearly singular, i.e. near the O2/O3 anti-troughs.

Therefore one should not try to prove this pointwise before proving O2/O3. Instead use dyadic split:

Define

\[
S_\tau=\{m:\sigma_{\min}(A_{\le X}(m))\asymp\tau\}.
\]

On \(S_\tau\), require

\[
\|A_{\tail}(m)\|_{\op}\le \frac12\tau.
\]

For small \(\tau\), either:

1. \(S_\tau\) is already the O2/O3 bad set to be controlled by anti-trough, or  
2. tail suppression is impossible.

Thus the better O2/O3 tail theorem is conditional:

\[
\boxed{
\text{On the cofactor-pivot good / exterior-good set, the tail is perturbative.}
}
\]

Not global.

Concretely:

\[
\int
\mathbf 1_{\|A_{\tail}\|_{\op}>\frac12\sigma_{\min}(A_{\le X})}
F_1F_{\wall}^{\forb}N\,d\rho
\le
Q^{-A}\int F_1F_{\wall}^{\forb}N\,d\rho.
\]

This is a weighted tail-bad smallness theorem.

---

# 7. Tail suppression and weighted anti-trough must be ordered carefully

For O2/O3, the correct order is probably:

1. Prove finite anti-trough for \(A_{\le X}\):

\[
\int F_{23}(A_{\le X})\,d\nu
\ge Q^{-A}\nu(\mathcal C).
\]

2. Extract/dyadically restrict to a set where

\[
\sigma_{\min}(A_{\le X})\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

3. Prove tail perturbativity on that extracted set:

\[
\|A_{\tail}\|_{\op}\le Q^{-A'}\sigma_{\min}(A_{\le X}).
\]

4. Transfer to \(A_{\full}\).

Trying to transfer \(A_{\full}\) to \(A_{\le X}\) before anti-trough may be circular because the transfer denominator is exactly the singular value being proved non-small.

So partial EF residuals help O2/O3 **after** or **inside** extraction, not as a blanket preliminary.

---

# 8. Revised theorem package

## Gate–1 package

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{L^2}(W):
\|R_X\|_{L^2(W)}^2
\le Q^{-A}\mathcal N_X(W).
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{FiniteCanonicalL2Frame}(W):
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W)}^2
\ge Q^{-A}\mathcal N_X(W).
}
\]

These yield:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{Agg}L^2(q''_{\can}).
}
\]

## O2/O3 package

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{FiniteWeightedPivotAntiTrough}(A_{\le X}):
\int F_{23}(A_{\le X})\,d\nu
\ge Q^{-A}\nu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{TailPerturbativeOnGoodSet}:
\|A_{\tail}\|_{\op}
\le
\frac12\sigma_{\min}(A_{\le X})
\quad
\text{on the extracted good set.}
}
\]

Then:

\[
\boxed{
F_{23}(A_{\full})\ge Q^{-C}F_{23}(A_{\le X})
}
\]

on the good set.

---

# 9. Most likely first obstruction

The Gate–1 tail estimate is not the first likely failure.

The first likely failure is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{FiniteCanonicalL2Frame}(W)
}
\]

for

\[
B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}.
\]

Specifically, branch anti-lock between main and dual plus background cancellation.

But because this is \(L^2\), not pointwise, it is plausible:

- phase \(\Theta(t)\) varies over windows;
- main/dual frequency supports have opposite signs;
- smooth branch windows create off-diagonal decay;
- \(B_2\) is low-complexity and can be separated.

Thus the next upstream subattack should be:

\[
\boxed{
L^2\text{ AFE main/dual/background lower frame.}
}
\]

---

# 10. Final status after this attack

Partial EF residuals yield a clean upstream support role:

\[
\boxed{
\text{control tails/completion, then prove finite canonical lower frame.}
}
\]

For O2/O3 they are useful only after finite anti-trough supplies a singular-value scale; otherwise operator perturbation is circular.

So the refined role is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Gate--1: EF residuals first.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{O2/O3: EF residuals after finite anti-trough / on good sets.}
}
\]

They remain nonviable as downstream \(\Lambda\).

## Agent 6 — ForbiddenSingularDiscardD4 theorem

I am assuming the proposed local forbidden operator exists:

\[
\Lambda_p=(I-P_{\sing,p})T_{\tr\to\sing,p}P_{\tr,p}.
\]

The task is to assemble the downstream contradiction theorem if this \(\Lambda_p\) has a small global upper and nonzero D4 rank. This matches the current architecture: Gate–1 is local scalar \(L^2\) energy, O2/O3 remains separate, and finite D4 closes only if the singular-discard channel sees the live carrier plane. 

# 1. Local forbidden wall energy

Let the transition-chart live D4 output be

\[
v_m^{\rm live}
=
H(m)\,
E_{\car,p}^{\tr}
L_{\clean,p}^{\tr}
\Gamma(m),
\]

where

\[
\Gamma(m)=
\begin{pmatrix}
\Gamma_G(m)\\
\Gamma_{G,2}(m)
\end{pmatrix},
\]

and

\[
L_{\clean}^{\tr}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
c_7&0\\
\beta_9&\alpha_9
\end{pmatrix}
\]

is invertible on Gram-good patches.

Define

\[
K_p
:=
\Lambda_p E_{\car,p}^{\tr}L_{\clean,p}^{\tr}.
\]

Then the correct forbidden wall energy is

\[
\boxed{
E_{\wall}^{\forb}(m)
=
\frac{
|H(m)|^2\,
\|K_p\Gamma(m)\|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\D4}(m)
}.
}
\]

This is the only wall factor that matters downstream. Raw anisotropy

\[
|H|^2(|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2)
\]

is valid only in the rank-two case.

---

# 2. Rank cases

## Rank two

If

\[
\sigma_{\min}(K_p)\ge Q^{-C},
\]

then

\[
\|K_p\Gamma\|^2\ge Q^{-C}|\Gamma|^2.
\]

So

\[
E_{\wall}^{\forb}
\asymp_Q
\frac{|H|^2(|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2)}{\mathcal N_{\D4}}.
\]

In this case, the old raw D4 anisotropy wall is acceptable.

## Rank one

If

\[
\operatorname{rank}K_p=1,
\]

then there is a visible right singular direction

\[
\ell_p=(\ell_1,\ell_2)
\]

such that

\[
\|K_p\Gamma\|^2
\asymp_Q
|\ell_1\Gamma_G+\ell_2\Gamma_{G,2}|^2.
\]

The wall condition must be replaced by

\[
\boxed{
|H|^2|\ell_1\Gamma_G+\ell_2\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\ge Q^{-A}\mathcal N_{\D4}.
}
\]

This is still enough. Extraction must use moving linear-form anti-concentration for \(\ell_p\cdot\Gamma\), or freeze \(\ell_p\) by angular caps.

## Rank zero

If

\[
K_p=0,
\]

then

\[
E_{\wall}^{\forb}\equiv0.
\]

The singular-discard channel is D4-invisible, and this \(\Lambda_p\) cannot close the proof.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}K_p\ge1
}
\]

is the finite D4 transversality condition.

---

# 3. Good-subset extraction interface

Assume the aggregate stage gives capped factors

\[
F_1,\qquad F_{23},\qquad F_{\wall}^{\forb}
\]

with

\[
F_{\wall}^{\forb}
=
\min(E_{\wall}^{\forb}/\eta_{\wall},1).
\]

Assume

\[
\int F_1F_{23}F_{\wall}^{\forb}\,d\mu
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

Then capped extraction gives a subset

\[
\mathcal G\subset\mathcal C
\]

with

\[
\mu(\mathcal G)\ge Q^{-C}\mu(\mathcal C)
\]

and, for every \(m\in\mathcal G\),

\[
E_1(m)\ge Q^{-C},
\]

\[
E_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-C},
\]

\[
E_{\wall}^{\forb}(m)\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

This subset is already singular-discard-compatible. No separate raw wall deletion is needed.

The geometric bad sets must already be removed or included in \(F_{\wall}^{\forb}\):

\[
\text{Gram-good},
\quad
\text{gauge-good},
\quad
\text{chart-good},
\quad
\text{carrier-frame-good},
\quad
\text{routing-good},
\quad
\text{\(\Lambda_p\)-rank-good}.
\]

---

# 4. Local downstream implication

On \(\mathcal G\), the local implications are:

\[
E_1(m)\Rightarrow\text{Gate--2 source energy},
\]

\[
E_{23}(m)\Rightarrow\text{O2/O3 cofactor and incidence nondegeneracy},
\]

\[
E_{\wall}^{\forb}(m)\Rightarrow
\|\Lambda_p v_m^{\rm live}\|^2\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

Using carrier-quotient D4,

\[
v_m=v_m^{\rm live}+\text{nuisance/error},
\]

with nuisance killed and error absorbed, so

\[
\boxed{
\|\Lambda_p v_m\|^2\ge Q^{-C}
}
\]

for every \(m\in\mathcal G\), after reducing constants.

This is the local forbidden-channel D4 output.

---

# 5. Global upper

The required Type-I upper is

\[
\boxed{
\sum_p
\|\Lambda_p J_{\tr,p}(q''_{\can})\|^2
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

Equivalently, for the global row vector \(V_{\glob}\),

\[
\boxed{
\sum_p\|\Lambda_p V_{\glob}\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This is positive atomic. It avoids complement cancellation by construction.

The upper should come from singular coherence:

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(q''_{\can})
=
P_{\sing,p}T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(q''_{\can})
+
\text{small discarded part}.
\]

Thus

\[
(I-P_{\sing,p})T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(q''_{\can})
\]

is small in square sum over \(p\).

---

# 6. Mobility/multiplicity

Since \(\Lambda_p\) is local and atomic, the mobility theorem is simpler than earlier global row-mobility.

Assume each local D4 certificate \(m\in\mathcal G\) is assigned to one or a polynomially bounded number of singular-discard atoms \(p(m)\), with

\[
\|\Lambda_{p(m)}v_m\|^2
\ge Q^{-C}\|\Lambda_p v_m\|^2
\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

Assume multiplicity

\[
\#\{m\in\mathcal G:p(m)=p\}\le Q^C,
\]

or weighted multiplicity

\[
\sum_{m:p(m)=p}\mu(m)\le Q^C\mu_p.
\]

Then weighted thinning gives a subset

\[
\mathcal G'\subset\mathcal G
\]

such that

\[
\mu(\mathcal G')\ge Q^{-C}\mu(\mathcal G)
\]

and \(p(m)\) is injective on \(\mathcal G'\).

Therefore

\[
\sum_p\|\Lambda_p V_{\glob}\|^2
\ge
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G'}
\mu(m)\|\Lambda_{p(m)}v_m\|^2
\ge
Q^{-C}\mu(\mathcal G')
\ge
Q^{-C}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

This is the aggregate forbidden-channel lower bound.

---

# 7. Contradiction theorem

The full downstream theorem is:

Assume:

1. Joint extraction gives \(\mathcal G\) with

\[
\mu(\mathcal G)\ge Q^{-C}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

2. On \(\mathcal G\),

\[
E_1,E_{23},E_{\wall}^{\forb}\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

3. Local Gate–2/D4 gives

\[
\|\Lambda_p v_m\|^2\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

4. Atomic multiplicity/thinning gives

\[
\sum_p\|\Lambda_p V_{\glob}\|^2
\ge Q^{-C}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

5. Singular-discard upper gives

\[
\sum_p\|\Lambda_p V_{\glob}\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

If \(B\) exceeds the accumulated lower-bound losses, contradiction follows.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{JointGoodSubset}
+
\mathrm{SingularDiscardD4Rank}
+
\mathrm{AtomicMultiplicity}
+
\mathrm{SingularDiscardUpper}
\Rightarrow
\text{contradiction}.
}
\]

---

# 8. Failure modes

The singular-discard route can fail only in precise ways.

## 8.1 Rank zero

\[
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}
E_{\car}^{\tr}L_{\clean}^{\tr}=0.
\]

Then D4 is singular-coherent; no forbidden channel.

## 8.2 Upper failure

Actual global rows do not lie close to \(R_{\sing}\):

\[
\sum_p
\|(I-P_{\sing,p})T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(q''_{\can})\|^2
\not\ll Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

Then \(\Lambda_p\) is not forbidden.

## 8.3 Nonlocality

The singular-discard condition exists only globally, not atomically. Complement cancellation returns.

## 8.4 Multiplicity failure

Too many local D4 certificates map to the same discarded singular atom \(p\), and thinning loses super-polynomial mass.

## 8.5 Chart mismatch

The extracted good packets are not in the transition–singular overlap where \(T_{\tr\to\sing,p}\) is defined with polynomial conditioning.

## 8.6 O2/O3 or Gate–2 failure

The subset has source energy but lacks O2/O3, or local Gate–2 constants are not uniform.

## 8.7 Tail/coherence mismatch

The same \(R_{\sing}\) used for the upper does not align with the transition map used in the D4 rank audit.

---

# 9. What remains to prove

The downstream route is now finite and local except for the singular-coherence upper.

The exact remaining theorems are:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{SingularCoherentSpace}:
\text{ define }R_{\sing,p}\text{ and }P_{\sing,p}.
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{SingularDiscardUpper}:
\sum_p
\|(I-P_{\sing,p})T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(q''_{\can})\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{D4DiscardRank}:
\operatorname{rank}
\left(
(I-P_{\sing,p})T_{\tr\to\sing,p}
E_{\car,p}^{\tr}L_{\clean,p}^{\tr}
\right)\ge1
}
\]

on a polynomial-weight set.

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{DiscardAtomMultiplicity}:
p(m)\text{ has polynomial multiplicity.}
}
\]

If these hold, finite D4 has a genuine contradiction channel.

---

# Final verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{ForbiddenSingularDiscardD4 is structurally viable.}
}
\]

It is the cleanest finite-D4 downstream theorem so far because:

- it is local/atomic Type I;
- it avoids complement cancellation;
- it avoids the \(\Xi\) degree mismatch;
- it does not require a full atlas loop;
- it uses singular coherence as the forbidden global condition.

The decisive finite question remains:

\[
\boxed{
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}
\operatorname{span}\{[\mathsf C_7],[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\}
\neq0?
}
\]

If yes, this route can close downstream once the singular-discard upper is proved.
