## Agent 6 continued attack — the \(R_{\sing}\) design tradeoff

The singular-discard route is the cleanest downstream candidate so far:

\[
\Lambda_{\tr,\sing,p}
=
(I-P_{\sing,p})T_{\tr\to\sing,p}P_{\tr,p}.
\]

It is local, positive/atomic, and avoids the \(\Xi\)-degree mismatch. But it now exposes a sharp design problem: the retained singular space \(R_{\sing}\) must be chosen with exactly the right size. The current coordinator instructions frame this as the decisive finite question:

\[
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}
\operatorname{span}\{[\mathsf C_7],[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\}
\neq0.
\]

If not, finite D4 has no forbidden channel. 

# 1. The new tradeoff

There are two competing requirements.

## Upper requirement

Actual global rows must be singular-coherent:

\[
\sum_p
\|(I-P_{\sing,p})
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(q''_{\can})\|^2
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

So \(R_{\sing,p}\) must contain the actual transition-to-singular image of the canonical source.

## D4-rank requirement

Formal D4 live carriers must not be singular-coherent:

\[
(I-P_{\sing,p})T_{\tr\to\sing,p}E_{\car,p}^{\tr}L_{\clean,p}^{\tr}
\neq0.
\]

So \(R_{\sing,p}\) must exclude at least one direction in the transported D4 live plane.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
R_{\sing}\text{ must contain actual EF-coherent rows but reject formal D4 carrier rows.}
}
\]

This is the central singular-discard design problem.

---

# 2. Too-large \(R_{\sing}\): rank-zero failure

If \(R_{\sing}\) is defined as all local scalar source patterns up to the relevant order, then it is too large.

For example, if

\[
R_{\sing}
=
J_{\le9}^{\sing}
\]

or any jet-complete singular space through order \(9\), then the transported D4 carriers are retained:

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_7]\in R_{\sing},
\]

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\in R_{\sing}.
\]

Then

\[
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_7]=0,
\]

\[
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]=0,
\]

and the rank is zero.

So:

\[
\boxed{
R_{\sing}\text{ cannot be a full Taylor/jet-complete singular space.}
}
\]

That would make the upper easy but destroys D4 visibility.

---

# 3. Too-small \(R_{\sing}\): upper failure

If \(R_{\sing}\) is a small low-mode projection, say

\[
R_{\sing}=\operatorname{span}\{\Phi_3,\ldots,\Phi_{R+3}\}
\]

or some finite diagnostic basis unrelated to actual EF coherence, then D4 transversality may hold. But the actual canonical source may not lie close to \(R_{\sing}\):

\[
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(q''_{\can})
\]

may be large.

Then the forbidden upper fails.

So:

\[
\boxed{
R_{\sing}\text{ cannot be an arbitrary low-mode diagnostic space.}
}
\]

It must be justified by actual-source coherence.

---

# 4. Correct design: actual EF-coherent singular space

The right definition is not “all jets” and not “low modes.” It is:

\[
\boxed{
R_{\sing,p}
=
\text{the singular representation of the finite EF-coherent canonical packet }F_X.
}
\]

Let

\[
F_X=B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}.
\]

Define

\[
R_{\sing,p}
:=
\operatorname{span}
\left\{
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(F_X)
:
F_X\text{ ranges over the allowed actual finite EF coefficient class}
\right\}.
\]

But this must be interpreted carefully.

If “allowed coefficient class” means arbitrary coefficients \(a_n,b_n\), then \(R_{\sing}\) may again be too large and contain D4. If it means the **actual canonical coefficient family** with its arithmetic constraints, it may be small enough to reject formal D4.

So the real theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{D4 formal carriers are not realizable as actual EF-coherent singular packets.}
}
\]

This is the singular-discard analogue of a nonrealizability theorem.

---

# 5. Finite linear-algebra formulation

Let

\[
\mathcal A_{\EF,p}
\]

be the finite-dimensional actual EF coefficient space used in the local packet. Define the linear map

\[
\mathcal S_p:\mathcal A_{\EF,p}\to H_{\sing,p},
\]

\[
\mathcal S_p(a,b,\mathrm{bg})
=
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}
(B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}).
\]

Then

\[
R_{\sing,p}=\operatorname{ran}\mathcal S_p.
\]

The D4 live map is

\[
D_p:
\mathbb C^2\to H_{\sing,p},
\]

\[
D_p\Gamma
=
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}
E_{\car,p}^{\tr}
L_{\clean,p}^{\tr}
\Gamma.
\]

The finite rank condition is:

\[
\boxed{
(I-P_{\operatorname{ran}\mathcal S_p})D_p
\neq0.
}
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
D_p(\mathbb C^2),
\operatorname{ran}\mathcal S_p
\right)
\ge Q^{-C}
}
\]

in at least one direction.

This is now a precise finite linear-algebra condition.

---

# 6. Danger: if \(\mathcal A_{\EF,p}\) is too flexible

If \(\mathcal A_{\EF,p}\) contains arbitrary local prime/dual packet coefficients, then

\[
\operatorname{ran}\mathcal S_p
\]

will likely be very large. Since \(D_p(\mathbb C^2)\) is only two-dimensional, it will probably lie inside or very close to \(\operatorname{ran}\mathcal S_p\).

Then rank zero.

Thus the upper cannot be based on arbitrary EF coefficient flexibility. It must use actual arithmetic rigidity of the coefficients:

\[
a_n\sim n^{-1/2}\cdot \text{canonical weight},
\]

not arbitrary \(a_n\).

This is subtle because most \(L^2\) lower-frame arguments deliberately allow arbitrary coefficients. For the downstream forbidden upper, arbitrary coefficients are too broad.

So the singular-discard \(\Lambda\) requires:

\[
\boxed{
\text{actual-coefficient EF coherence, not universal coefficient coherence.}
}
\]

This mirrors the earlier correction for aggregate Gate–1: universal lower sampling was too strong/wrong, actual-coefficient sampling was the right target.

---

# 7. Upper theorem restated with actual coefficients

The singular-discard upper should be:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_p
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(q''_{\can}),
R_{\sing,p}^{\actual}
\right)^2
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C),
}
\]

where

\[
R_{\sing,p}^{\actual}
\]

is generated by the actual EF-coherent local model, not arbitrary packet coefficients.

But since

\[
q''_{\can}=F_X+R_X,
\]

and

\[
F_X\in R_{\sing,p}^{\actual}
\]

by construction, this upper reduces to

\[
\sum_p
\|T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(R_X)\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

This is good: it uses the same partial-EF residual estimate already needed for upstream completion.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the upper is plausible if }R_{\sing}^{\actual}\text{ is built from }F_X.
}
\]

---

# 8. D4 transversality theorem

The hard downstream theorem is now:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}
E_{\car,p}^{\tr}L_{\clean,p}^{\tr}\mathbb C^2,
R_{\sing,p}^{\actual}
\right)
\ge Q^{-C}
}
\]

on a polynomial-weight set.

This says:

\[
\boxed{
\text{formal D4 live carriers cannot be represented by actual finite EF-coherent packets.}
}
\]

This is a much more interpretable statement than previous \(\Lambda\) formulations.

It is a local algebra/arithmetic compatibility condition between:

1. formal D4 carrier shapes;
2. actual EF coefficient constraints.

---

# 9. Potential obstruction: actual EF space may contain D4

There is a serious risk.

D4 carriers are built from \(q''_{\can}\), and \(q''_{\can}\) is itself EF-coherent. If the D4 carrier directions correspond to actual variations of \(q''_{\can}\) within the EF family, then

\[
D_p(\mathbb C^2)\subset R_{\sing,p}^{\actual},
\]

and rank zero.

To avoid this, D4 must represent a **formal local obstruction direction**, not an actual first-order variation of the global EF source.

This is exactly what we want philosophically:

\[
\boxed{
\text{D4 creates locally possible carrier data that is not globally EF-coherent.}
}
\]

But it must be proven.

---

# 10. A possible finite diagnostic

Compute the singular-discard pairing against annihilators of \(R_{\sing}^{\actual}\).

Let

\[
\{\ell_\alpha\}
\]

be a basis or frame for

\[
(R_{\sing}^{\actual})^\perp.
\]

Then rank is nonzero iff

\[
\exists \alpha
\]

such that

\[
\ell_\alpha
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing}E_{\car}[\mathsf C_7]
\right)\ne0
\]

or

\[
\ell_\alpha
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing}E_{\car}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\right)\ne0.
\]

This is the finite test.

If \(R_{\sing}^{\actual}\) is described by EF coefficient moment equations, the annihilators \(\ell_\alpha\) are precisely local test functions orthogonal to the finite EF packet. Then D4 transversality becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\text{there exists a singular test function orthogonal to actual EF packets but not to the D4 carrier.}
}
\]

This may be attackable.

---

# 11. Relation to O2/O3 graph model

This has a hidden connection with O2/O3.

O2/O3 asks whether

\[
1/m_{\can}
\]

is outside a low-degree polynomial span. That is also a statement of non-representability in a finite model space.

Singular-discard asks whether D4 carrier shapes are outside an actual EF-coherent singular model space.

Both are distance-to-model-space statements.

So one may use similar machinery:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Riesz basis + distance-to-model lower bound.}
}
\]

The difference is:

- O2/O3 model: \(m_{\can}\mathbb C_R[x]\);
- singular-discard model: actual EF packet image \(R_{\sing}^{\actual}\).

This suggests using the same analytic tools: orthogonal bases, Riesz bounds, distance estimates, exterior minors.

---

# 12. Updated theorem stack for singular-discard

The route needs exactly:

## Upper/coherence

\[
q''_{\can}=F_X+R_X,
\qquad
T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(F_X)\in R_{\sing}^{\actual},
\]

\[
\sum_p
\|T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(R_X)\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

## D4 transversality

\[
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing}E_{\car}L_{\clean}\mathbb C^2,
R_{\sing}^{\actual}
\right)
\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

## Atomic multiplicity

The singular patches \(p\) have polynomial overlap/multiplicity.

Then finite D4 closes downstream.

---

# 13. Final status

The singular-discard \(\Lambda\) is viable only with an **actual-coefficient retained singular space**.

If \(R_{\sing}\) is:

- all jets: rank zero;
- arbitrary packet coefficients: likely rank zero;
- low diagnostic modes: upper fails;
- actual EF-coherent finite source space: plausible.

So the next decisive attack is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Define }R_{\sing}^{\actual}
\text{ from the finite EF model and test }
\operatorname{dist}(D_p(\mathbb C^2),R_{\sing}^{\actual}).
}
\]

This is now the sharp downstream finite-D4 transversality problem.

## Assistant (2026-04-28 04:07:05)

## Agent 6 continued attack — \(R_{\sing}^{\actual}\) must not be tautological

The last attack identified the sharp downstream finite-D4 problem:

\[
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing}E_{\car}^{\tr}L_{\clean}^{\tr}\mathbb C^2,
R_{\sing}^{\actual}
\right)
\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

The idea was to define \(R_{\sing}^{\actual}\) from the finite EF-coherent source model so that actual rows have small discarded singular projection, while formal D4 carriers do not. This is the best current \(\Lambda\)-candidate. But it has a hidden danger: \(R_{\sing}^{\actual}\) can become tautological.

# 1. The tempting but invalid definition

Let

\[
F_X=B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}
\]

be the finite EF approximation of the actual source. At each patch \(p\), define

\[
u_p:=T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(F_X).
\]

A tempting retained space is the line

\[
R_{\sing,p}:=\mathbf C u_p.
\]

Then

\[
(I-P_{\sing,p})T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(F_X)=0
\]

by construction, and the actual source satisfies

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(q''_{\can})
=
u_p
+
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(R_X).
\]

So the upper becomes just the residual estimate

\[
\sum_p
\|(I-P_{\sing,p})T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(R_X)\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

That is excellent.

But this definition is **too strong** unless carefully justified. It makes the forbidden channel depend on the actual local row being tested. If we allow arbitrary projection onto the orthogonal complement of the actual vector, then almost any non-parallel formal D4 vector becomes “forbidden.” That would manufacture a contradiction from projection choice, not from a structural global identity.

So:

\[
\boxed{
R_{\sing}^{\actual}\text{ cannot be the arbitrary local line spanned by the actual row, unless that line is defined by a structural EF model independent of the D4 contradiction.}
}
\]

# 2. What “not tautological” means

A valid \(R_{\sing,p}\) must be defined from **admissible structural data** before the D4 lower-bound extraction. Acceptable ingredients:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the finite EF truncation scheme}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{fixed branch windows and cutoffs}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{known coefficient formulas }n^{-1/2},\Lambda(n),\text{ gamma weights}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{fixed singular basis / test space}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{a finite-dimensional parameter family with prescribed arithmetic coefficients}
}
\]

Not acceptable:

\[
\boxed{
\text{choosing }R_{\sing,p}\text{ after seeing the local D4 direction}
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
\text{choosing }R_{\sing,p}\text{ solely as the line through }J_{\tr,p}(q''_{\can})\text{ without a structural EF reason.}
}
\]

The projection must be a **model coherence space**, not a fitted annihilator.

# 3. Three levels of \(R_{\sing}\)

There are three possible choices, ordered by size.

## Level 1 — universal EF coefficient space

Let

\[
R_{\sing,p}^{\univ}
=
\operatorname{ran}
\left(
\mathcal S_p:\mathcal A_p^{\univ}\to H_{\sing,p}
\right),
\]

where \(\mathcal A_p^{\univ}\) allows arbitrary coefficients in the finite prime/dual support.

This is structurally clean, but probably too large:

\[
D_p(\mathbb C^2)\subset R_{\sing,p}^{\univ}
\]

is likely, because arbitrary coefficients can realize many local finite patterns.

Expected result:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}(I-P_{\sing}^{\univ})D_p=0.
}
\]

## Level 2 — actual arithmetic coefficient manifold

Let

\[
R_{\sing,p}^{\arith}
\]

be generated by the finite EF model with actual arithmetic coefficients and allowed structural parameters only, for example:

\[
a_n=n^{-1/2}\Lambda(n)\,w(n;m,p),
\]

with fixed gamma/dual weights and only the legitimate parameters \(m,\alpha,\lambda\), cutoff, and branch scale varying.

This is the right candidate. It is large enough to approximate actual rows, but small enough that formal D4 directions may be transverse.

The key theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}\left(D_p(\mathbb C^2),R_{\sing,p}^{\arith}\right)\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

## Level 3 — fitted actual row line

\[
R_{\sing,p}^{\fit}=\mathbf C\,T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(F_X).
\]

This gives the best upper and likely strongest transversality, but it risks being tautological. It is valid only if one proves that this fitted line is the value of a structural, slowly varying EF family and that the projection choice is not using the D4 obstruction.

So Level 2 is the correct target.

# 4. Reformulated singular-discard theorem

The retained space should be

\[
R_{\sing,p}^{\arith}
=
\operatorname{span}
\left\{
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}
\left(
B_2+P_{2,\le X}^{\theta}+D_{2,\le X}^{\theta}
\right):
\theta\in\Theta_p
\right\},
\]

where \(\Theta_p\) is the legitimate finite-dimensional parameter set of the canonical EF model. It should include only genuine structural variations, not arbitrary local coefficients.

Then the upper is:

\[
\sum_p
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(q''_{\can}),
R_{\sing,p}^{\arith}
\right)^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

This follows if the actual \(q''_{\can}\) is represented by some admissible \(\theta_p\) plus residual:

\[
q''_{\can}=F_X^{\theta_p}+R_X,
\]

with

\[
\sum_p\|T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(R_X)\|^2\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

The D4 rank theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}E_{\car,p}^{\tr}L_{\clean,p}^{\tr}\mathbb C^2,
R_{\sing,p}^{\arith}
\right)
\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

# 5. Why the rank theorem is nontrivial

The D4 live plane is formal:

\[
D_p(\mathbb C^2)
=
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}E_{\car}^{\tr}L_{\clean}^{\tr}\mathbb C^2.
\]

The arithmetic EF manifold is highly structured:

\[
R_{\sing,p}^{\arith}
=
\left\{
\text{singular-chart images of actual finite EF coefficient patterns}
\right\}.
\]

If the EF model is rigid, then the two-dimensional D4 plane may be transverse.

If the EF model is flexible enough locally, then it may contain the D4 plane.

Thus the finite D4 downstream route now depends on an **actual-source rigidity theorem**:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the formal D4 carrier directions are not tangent/contained in the actual EF coherent singular model.}
}
\]

This is exactly the kind of theorem that the project has been circling: formal local algebra versus actual source realizability.

# 6. Tangent-space version

If \(R_{\sing,p}^{\arith}\) is a manifold rather than a linear subspace, use its tangent space at the actual EF row:

\[
T_{u_p}R_{\sing,p}^{\arith}.
\]

The local D4 carrier is an infinitesimal formal deformation. The relevant transversality becomes:

\[
\boxed{
D_p(\mathbb C^2)
\not\subset
T_{u_p}R_{\sing,p}^{\arith}
}
\]

with polynomial angle.

This is probably the most accurate version.

Then the forbidden projection is onto the normal space

\[
N_{u_p}R_{\sing,p}^{\arith}.
\]

The upper follows from actual rows lying on the manifold up to residual; the lower follows if D4 points in a normal direction.

This is conceptually very clean:

\[
\boxed{
\text{actual EF rows form a singular coherence manifold;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{D4 produces a normal obstruction to that manifold.}
}
\]

# 7. Relation to “actual-source upgrade”

This connects to the older open issue: whether formal source-frame hypotheses are genuinely supplied by the actual corrected source model.

The D4 transversality theorem is now an actual-source upgrade:

\[
\boxed{
\text{formal D4 carrier } \notin \text{ actual EF source tangent space.}
}
\]

If this fails, D4 is a formal artifact realizable by actual EF variations, and cannot contradict global zeta.

If it holds, D4 becomes a true obstruction to actual-source coherence.

This is a significant conceptual consolidation.

# 8. Finite diagnostic: dimension count

Let

\[
d_{\arith}:=\dim T_{u_p}R_{\sing,p}^{\arith}
\]

inside the singular carrier/test space \(H_{\sing,p}\).

If

\[
d_{\arith}
\ge \dim H_{\sing,p}-2,
\]

then the two-dimensional D4 plane will generically intersect or lie close to the tangent space. Transversality is not guaranteed.

If

\[
d_{\arith}
\ll \dim H_{\sing,p},
\]

then a random D4 plane is transverse.

So the first quantitative diagnostic is:

\[
\boxed{
\dim H_{\sing,p}
-
\dim T_{u_p}R_{\sing,p}^{\arith}
}
\]

relative to \(2\).

A codimension at least \(1\) is enough for rank-one obstruction; codimension at least \(2\) may allow rank two.

This is a useful finite check.

# 9. What parameters are allowed?

A crucial modeling choice is what variations define \(R_{\sing}^{\arith}\).

Allowed:

- \(m\)-shift within patch;
- \(\alpha\), if the proof admits \(\alpha\)-family;
- cutoff smoothing parameters fixed in a finite admissible family;
- branch scale \(\lambda\) within branch chart;
- deterministic gamma/dual weights.

Not allowed:

- arbitrary coefficient perturbations \(a_n\);
- independent local phase choices;
- arbitrary finite packet amplitudes;
- D4 carrier amplitudes as free parameters.

If arbitrary coefficients are allowed, the transversality likely dies.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
R_{\sing}^{\arith}\text{ must be actual-source, not packet-universal.}
}
\]

# 10. Revised downstream theorem

The strongest form is now:

There is an actual-source singular coherence manifold

\[
\mathcal M_{\sing,p}^{\arith}\subset H_{\sing,p}
\]

such that:

## Upper

\[
\sum_p
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(q''_{\can}),
\mathcal M_{\sing,p}^{\arith}
\right)^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

## D4 normality

For \(u_p\in\mathcal M_{\sing,p}^{\arith}\) approximating the actual row,

\[
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}E_{\car,p}^{\tr}L_{\clean,p}^{\tr}\mathbb C^2,
T_{u_p}\mathcal M_{\sing,p}^{\arith}
\right)
\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

Then define \(P_{\sing,p}\) as projection onto the tangent/coherence space and use the normal projection as \(\Lambda_p\).

This yields the finite D4 contradiction channel.

# 11. Final status after this attack

The singular-discard route is not merely a projection theorem. It is an **actual-source manifold transversality theorem**.

The decisive finite question becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Are the D4 live carriers normal to the actual EF singular coherence manifold?}
}
\]

This is better than earlier formulations because it prevents tautological projection choices and avoids universal packet spaces.

If the answer is yes, finite D4 has a genuine forbidden channel.

If the answer is no, D4 is an actual-source tangent direction and cannot contradict the global zeta source.

## Agent 6 continued attack — replace fixed \(\Lambda\) by normal-bundle obstruction

The singular-discard route should now be reframed. A fixed linear projection

\[
\Lambda_p=(I-P_{\sing,p})T_{\tr\to\sing,p}P_{\tr,p}
\]

is probably too rigid unless \(P_{\sing,p}\) is chosen correctly. The better object is the **normal bundle** of the actual-source singular coherence manifold. This avoids two previous traps:

\[
\text{too large }R_{\sing}\Rightarrow \text{rank zero},
\]

\[
\text{too fitted }R_{\sing}\Rightarrow \text{tautological projection}.
\]

The current coordinator update already identifies the singular-discard operator as the best finite-D4 downstream channel and says \(P_{\sing}\) should be an actual-source / EF-coherent projection, not a Taylor-complete one. 

# 1. Define an actual-source singular coherence manifold

For each transition–singular patch \(p\), let

\[
H_{\sing,p}
\]

be the singular local Hilbert space containing the transition-to-singular image

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(q''_{\can}).
\]

Instead of defining a fixed linear retained space \(R_{\sing,p}\), define a structural manifold

\[
\mathcal M_{\sing,p}^{\arith}\subset H_{\sing,p}.
\]

This manifold consists of singular-chart images of admissible finite EF-coherent source models:

\[
F_X^\theta
=
B_2^\theta+P_{2,\le X}^\theta+D_{2,\le X}^\theta,
\]

where \(\theta\) ranges only over legitimate structural parameters:

\[
m,\quad \alpha\text{ if admissible},\quad \lambda,\quad \text{fixed cutoff/branch parameters},
\]

and not arbitrary local packet coefficients.

Then

\[
\mathcal M_{\sing,p}^{\arith}
=
\left\{
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(F_X^\theta):\theta\in\Theta_p
\right\}.
\]

The actual row satisfies

\[
y_p:=
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(q''_{\can})
=
u_p+e_p,
\qquad
u_p\in\mathcal M_{\sing,p}^{\arith},
\]

with

\[
\sum_p\|e_p\|^2\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C)
\]

if EF completion coherence is true.

This is the correct global forbidden upper: actual rows lie close to the EF-coherent manifold.

---

# 2. D4 must be normal to this manifold

Let the transition D4 live map be

\[
D_p:
\mathbb C^2\to H_{\sing,p},
\]

\[
D_p\Gamma
=
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}
E_{\car,p}^{\tr}
L_{\clean,p}^{\tr}
\Gamma,
\]

where

\[
\Gamma=
\begin{pmatrix}
\Gamma_G\\
\Gamma_{G,2}
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

At a nearby coherent row \(u_p\in\mathcal M_{\sing,p}^{\arith}\), let

\[
T_{u_p}\mathcal M_{\sing,p}^{\arith}
\]

be the tangent space, and let

\[
\Pi_{p}^{\perp}
\]

be orthogonal projection to its normal space.

The finite-D4 transversality condition should be:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}
\left(
\Pi_p^\perp D_p
\right)
\ge1,
}
\]

or quantitatively,

\[
\boxed{
\|\Pi_p^\perp D_p\Gamma\|
\ge Q^{-C}\|\Gamma\|
}
\]

for the extracted wall-good direction.

Rank two means both D4 anisotropy directions are normal to the actual-source manifold. Rank one means one linear combination of \(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\) is normal. Rank zero means D4 is tangent to actual EF coherence and cannot contradict it.

This is now the clean rank test:

\[
\boxed{
\text{D4 live carriers must be normal, not tangent, to the actual EF singular manifold.}
}
\]

---

# 3. Why tangent-space projection is not tautological

A fixed projection onto the line through the actual row is suspicious. But projection onto the **normal bundle of a structurally defined manifold** is legitimate.

The manifold is defined before D4 extraction by:

- the EF truncation scheme;
- actual coefficient formulas;
- branch scale;
- gamma/dual completion rules;
- allowed global parameters.

It is not chosen after seeing the D4 carrier.

So the normal projection

\[
\Pi_p^\perp
\]

is a structural object, not a fitted annihilator, provided \(\mathcal M_{\sing,p}^{\arith}\) is defined from actual-source rules and not arbitrary packet coefficients.

This is the correct replacement for the earlier linear \(R_{\sing}\)-projection.

---

# 4. Need a tubular-neighborhood theorem

Because \(\mathcal M_{\sing,p}^{\arith}\) is nonlinear, the local contradiction requires a tubular estimate.

Assume the manifold has polynomially bounded second fundamental form:

\[
\|\mathrm{II}_{\mathcal M,p}\|\le Q^C,
\]

and the D4 displacement size is within a polynomial tubular radius.

Then for a perturbation

\[
z_p=u_p+D_p\Gamma+\mathrm{small},
\]

one has

\[
\operatorname{dist}(z_p,\mathcal M_{\sing,p}^{\arith})
\ge
\|\Pi_p^\perp D_p\Gamma\|
-
Q^C\|D_p\Gamma\|^2
-
\|\mathrm{small}\|.
\]

If

\[
\|\Pi_p^\perp D_p\Gamma\|\ge Q^{-C}
\]

and the quadratic/tail terms are smaller, then

\[
\operatorname{dist}(z_p,\mathcal M_{\sing,p}^{\arith})
\ge Q^{-C'}.
\]

So the D4 lower should be formulated as a **distance-to-manifold lower bound**:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(\text{local D4 row}),
\mathcal M_{\sing,p}^{\arith}
\right)^2
\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

This avoids relying on a globally fixed linear projection.

---

# 5. The upper becomes distance-to-manifold smallness

The global upper is:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_p
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(q''_{\can}),
\mathcal M_{\sing,p}^{\arith}
\right)^2
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This is supplied by partial EF coherence:

\[
q''_{\can}=F_X+R_X,
\qquad
F_X\in\mathcal M_{\sing,p}^{\arith},
\]

and

\[
\sum_p
\|T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(R_X)\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

Thus partial EF residuals re-enter downstream, but not as \(\Lambda\). They prove the actual row is close to the coherence manifold.

That is a clean role.

---

# 6. The lower becomes D4 normality

The local D4 theorem must imply that on the extracted good set,

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(q''_{\can})
\]

has a D4-induced displacement normal to \(\mathcal M_{\sing,p}^{\arith}\).

In symbols, after Gate–2/O2/O3/D4,

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(q''_{\can})
=
u_p
+
H(p)D_p\Gamma(p)
+
\mathrm{nuisance}
+
\mathrm{error}.
\]

Carrier quotient kills nuisance; EF coherence controls error; and D4-normality gives

\[
\|\Pi_p^\perp H(p)D_p\Gamma(p)\|^2
\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

Then

\[
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(q''_{\can}),
\mathcal M_{\sing,p}^{\arith}
\right)^2
\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

Summing over the good subset contradicts the global distance upper.

---

# 7. This reveals the actual hard theorem

The decisive theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{D4 normality to the actual EF singular coherence manifold.}
}
\]

Explicitly:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}
E_{\car,p}^{\tr}
L_{\clean,p}^{\tr}\mathbb C^2,
T_{u_p}\mathcal M_{\sing,p}^{\arith}
\right)
\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

This is stronger and clearer than the earlier statement

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}W_{\tr}\not\subset R_{\sing}.
\]

It says the D4 live plane is not tangent to the actual-source manifold.

---

# 8. How to test D4 normality

Let

\[
\theta=(\theta_1,\ldots,\theta_d)
\]

be the admissible parameter vector for \(\mathcal M_{\sing,p}^{\arith}\), and write

\[
u_p(\theta)
=
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(F_X^\theta).
\]

Then the tangent space is spanned by

\[
\partial_{\theta_j}u_p(\theta).
\]

Build the tangent matrix

\[
T_p=
\begin{pmatrix}
\partial_{\theta_1}u_p & \cdots & \partial_{\theta_d}u_p
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Build the D4 matrix

\[
D_p=
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}
E_{\car,p}^{\tr}
L_{\clean,p}^{\tr}.
\]

The normality condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\sigma_{\min}
\left(
(I-P_{\operatorname{ran}T_p})D_p
\right)
\ge Q^{-C}
}
\]

for rank two, or at least one singular value \(\ge Q^{-C}\) for rank one.

This is a finite matrix audit.

---

# 9. Dimension diagnostic

Let

\[
d=\dim T_{u_p}\mathcal M_{\sing,p}^{\arith},
\]

and

\[
N=\dim H_{\sing,p}.
\]

If

\[
N-d\ge2,
\]

rank two normality is generic.

If

\[
N-d=1,
\]

rank one is possible.

If

\[
N-d=0,
\]

rank zero.

Thus before computing detailed entries, check:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{codim}
\mathcal M_{\sing,p}^{\arith}
\text{ inside }H_{\sing,p}.
}
\]

If the actual EF parameterization is too flexible and codimension is zero, singular-discard cannot work.

If codimension is positive, D4 normality becomes plausible.

---

# 10. Warning: \(m\)-shift tangent may capture some D4

The tangent space includes at least the \(m\)-shift direction:

\[
\partial_m u_p.
\]

Since D4 carriers involve local derivatives of \(q''_{\can}\), some D4 directions may be tangent to \(m\)-translation.

Similarly, if \(\alpha\) is allowed, then

\[
\partial_\alpha u_p
\]

may capture a folded/phase D4 direction.

So the normality theorem must quotient these legitimate tangent directions.

This is good: if D4 merely corresponds to moving along the actual source family, it should not create contradiction.

Only the genuinely non-realizable D4 component should remain.

---

# 11. Revised ForbiddenSingularDiscardD4 theorem

The final downstream theorem should be stated with manifold distance, not fixed projection:

Assume:

1. Actual-source coherence:

\[
\sum_p
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
y_p,
\mathcal M_{\sing,p}^{\arith}
\right)^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

2. Good-subset extraction gives \(\mathcal G\) with \(E_1,E_{23}\), and D4 wall-goodness.

3. D4 normality:

\[
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
D_p(\mathbb C^2),
T_{u_p}\mathcal M_{\sing,p}^{\arith}
\right)
\ge Q^{-C}
\]

on \(\mathcal G\).

4. Tubular control:

\[
\|\mathrm{II}_{\mathcal M,p}\|\le Q^C.
\]

Then each \(p\in\mathcal G\) has

\[
\operatorname{dist}(y_p,\mathcal M_{\sing,p}^{\arith})^2\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

Summing gives

\[
\sum_p
\operatorname{dist}(y_p,\mathcal M_{\sing,p}^{\arith})^2
\ge Q^{-C}\mu(\mathcal G)
\ge Q^{-C'}\mu(\mathcal C),
\]

contradicting the upper.

---

# 12. Final status

The singular-discard route is now best understood as:

\[
\boxed{
\text{actual EF rows form a singular coherence manifold;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{actual rows are close to it by EF completion;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{finite D4 tries to push the row in a normal direction;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{normal displacement contradicts coherence.}
}
\]

This is the most coherent downstream interpretation so far.

The next decisive finite audit is:

\[
\boxed{
\sigma_{\min}
\left(
(I-P_{T_{u_p}\mathcal M_{\sing,p}^{\arith}})
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}
E_{\car,p}^{\tr}
L_{\clean,p}^{\tr}
\right)
\ge Q^{-C}?
}
\]

If yes, finite D4 finally has a structural contradiction channel.

If no, the D4 carrier is tangent to actual-source coherence and cannot close the proof.

# 1. Define the singular coherence constraint

\[

:=

\]

up to the controlled tail. Therefore the actual-source singular manifold satisfies, to first order,

\[

\quad
\text{on }
T_u\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}.
\]

So \(\mathcal K_9\) is a candidate normal covector to the actual EF coherence manifold.

---

# 2. Apply \(\mathcal K_9\) to the D4 displacement

-

\right)\Gamma_G
+

assuming the lower-order D4 contribution to \(s_{<7}\) is absent or already quotient-nuisance.

# 1. Why \([\mathsf C_{Y12}]\) should be tested before \([\mathsf C_7]\)

The D4 clean matrix is triangular:

\[
L_{\clean}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
c_7&0\\
\beta_9&\alpha_9
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
c_7\alpha_9\neq0.
\]

So the D4 output decomposes as

\[
s_7^{\D4}
=
Hc_7[\mathsf C_7]\Gamma_G,
\]

\[
s_9^{\D4}
=
H\left(
\beta_9[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\Gamma_G
+
\alpha_9[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\Gamma_{G,2}
\right).
\]

The residual \(\mathcal K_9\) subtracts the order-nine component that is explainable from order seven and lower:

\[
s_9-\mathcal R_{7\to9}s_7-\mathcal R_{<7\to9}s_{<7}.
\]

Therefore the order-seven D4 part can be absorbed if

\[
\mathcal R_{7\to9}(c_7[\mathsf C_7])
\]

matches the \(\Gamma_G\)-part of \(s_9^{\D4}\). But the \(\Gamma_{G,2}\)-part is independent of \(s_7\):

\[
H\alpha_9[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

So \([\mathsf C_{Y12}]\) is the cleanest possible obstruction:

\[
\boxed{
\Gamma_{G,2}\text{ has no order-seven antecedent.}
}
\]

This makes

\[
\mathcal K_9([\mathsf C_{Y12}])
\]

the right first test.

---

# 2. Structural expectation: nonzero unless \(Y12\) is an allowed EF tangent

If \([\mathsf C_{Y12}]\) were generated by an actual EF-coherent parameter variation, then it would lie in the tangent space of

\[
\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith},
\]

and every defining connection residual, including \(\mathcal K_9\), would annihilate it.

So:

\[
\mathcal K_9([\mathsf C_{Y12}])=0
\]

means

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\in
T_u\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}
\quad
\text{as an order-nine singular deformation.}
\]

Conversely,

\[
\mathcal K_9([\mathsf C_{Y12}])\neq0
\]

means \(Y12\) is a formal D4 source not realizable by actual EF coherence.

Thus the finite audit is exactly the actual-source upgrade question in order-nine form:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Is }Y12\text{ an actual EF tangent direction or a normal obstruction?}
}
\]

---

# 3. Which actual parameters could generate \(Y12\)?

The tangent space of the actual EF singular manifold includes variations in legitimate structural parameters:

\[
m,\quad \lambda,\quad \alpha\text{ if admitted},\quad
\text{cutoff/regularization parameters},\quad
\text{possibly branch weights}.
\]

Each has a characteristic signature.

## \(m\)-translation

Translation changes all singular coefficients by the derivative recurrence:

\[
\partial_m s_j \sim s_{j+1}
\]

after scale factors. This produces coherent chains across all orders.

If \(Y12\) were merely an \(m\)-translation effect, then its order-nine component would be tied to order-eight/lower data, not a pure \(Y12\) quadratic mixed-block source. The promoted \(Y_{12}^{(2)}\) structure suggests it is not just translation.

So \(m\)-translation likely does **not** span \([\mathsf C_{Y12}]\).

## \(\lambda\)-scale variation

Scale changes produce Euler-type relations among coefficients:

\[
\lambda\partial_\lambda s_j
\sim
(3+j)s_j+\text{window terms}.
\]

This rescales existing coefficients; it does not naturally create a new quadratic mixed-block \(Y12\) carrier independent of lower orders.

So \(\lambda\)-variation likely does **not** span \([\mathsf C_{Y12}]\).

## \(\alpha\)-tilt

\(\alpha\) changes branch weights/phases. This is the dangerous one.

If \(\alpha\)-variation alters the folded-dual branch in a way that creates an order-nine mixed-block deformation, then it could create a tangent direction overlapping \(Y12\).

The concrete test is:

\[
\mathcal K_9(\partial_\alpha u_p)
\quad\text{versus}\quad
\mathcal K_9(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]).
\]

If these are proportional, then admitting \(\alpha\) may kill the \(Y12\) normal channel.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\alpha\text{-admissibility and }Y12\text{-normality are coupled.}
}
\]

This is the main hidden risk.

## Cutoff/regularization variation

Cutoff variations affect tails and smooth boundary terms. They are typically high-mode or endpoint-like. Unless \(Y12\) lives in the same tail sector, cutoff variation should not span it.

So cutoff variation likely does not kill \(Y12\)-normality.

---

The \(Y12\) object was identified as a promoted quadratic mixed-block remainder:

\[
Y_{12}^{(2)}
=
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2],

coupled with same-point whitening. It was the first live Bottleneck C carrier. Its quotient condition was

\[

for quotient closure, and transform safety required stronger constraints.

This history suggests \(Y12\) is not an ordinary actual-source tangent. It is exactly a promoted second-order formal local obstruction.

If \(Y12\) were tangent to the actual EF source manifold, it would be less likely to have appeared as the first live Bottleneck C carrier.

So structurally I expect:

\[

unless the singular EF coherence relation is defined too broadly.

A generic Taylor recurrence may allow any \(s_9\) after choosing high-order data. That would kill no D4.

A true EF singular coherence residual should use the fact that actual EF coefficients at order nine are determined by:

- order-seven and lower coherent EF data,
- fixed arithmetic coefficient structure,
- gamma/background relations,
- controlled tail/regularization.

So \(\mathcal K_9\) must be an **actual-source connection residual**, not a formal jet residual.

If \(\mathcal K_9\) is defined merely as

\[
s_9-\text{Taylor-predicted }s_9,
\]

it may be zero or meaningless. It must be

\[
s_9-\text{EF-coherent predicted }s_9.

This distinction is critical.

\]

If false, test the \(\Gamma_G\) mismatch channel; if that also fails, move to \(\mathcal K_{11}\) or abandon finite-D4 downstream.

## Agent 6 continued attack — tangent tests for \(Y12\)-normality

The current best downstream target is:

\[
\mathcal K_9\!\left(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\right)\neq0.
\]

Equivalently, the order-nine \(Y12\) D4 carrier should be normal to the actual EF singular coherence manifold, not tangent to it. The previous coordinator synthesis explicitly identifies this as the finite yes/no test for the singular-discard D4 route. 

# 1. The tangent-space obstruction

Let

\[
u_p(\theta)
=
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(F_X^\theta)
\]

be the actual EF singular model in patch \(p\), where \(\theta\) ranges over legitimate structural parameters.

The D4 \(Y12\)-normality test is

\[
D_Y
:=
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}E_{\car,p}^{\tr}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\notin
T_{u_p}\mathcal M_{\sing,p}^{\arith}.
\]

The tangent space is spanned by

\[
\partial_m u_p,\quad
\partial_\lambda u_p,\quad
\partial_\alpha u_p
\]

if \(\alpha\) is admitted, plus cutoff/regularization derivatives if those are allowed structural parameters.

So the first concrete check is:

\[
D_Y
\notin
\operatorname{span}
\{\partial_m u_p,\partial_\lambda u_p,\partial_\alpha u_p,\partial_{\mathrm{cut}}u_p,\ldots\}.
\]

If \(D_Y\) lies in this span, then \(Y12\) is an actual-source tangent direction and cannot be a forbidden D4 displacement.

---

# 2. \(m\)-translation cannot plausibly generate \(Y12\)

An \(m\)-translation acts on the singular expansion by differentiating the same scalar source:

\[
\partial_mF_m(x)
=
\lambda\,\partial_t(\lambda^3q''_{\can})(m+\lambda x)
=
\lambda^4q'''_{\can}(m+\lambda x).
\]

On singular coefficients, this creates the standard derivative recurrence:

\[
\partial_m s_j
\sim
\lambda^{-1}s_{j+1}
+\text{basis-variation terms}.
\]

So \(\partial_m u_p\) is a coherent shift of the full coefficient tower. It does not selectively insert a promoted quadratic mixed-block order-nine carrier.

The \(Y12\) carrier is not a derivative recurrence term. It comes from a promoted quadratic mixed-block remainder

\[
Y_{12}^{(2)}=D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\]

coupled with same-point whitening. That structure is bilinear/quadratic in local source slots, whereas \(m\)-translation is a linear derivative of the already-realized EF source.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\partial_m u_p\text{ should not span }D_Y.
}
\]

If a computation shows \(D_Y\in\operatorname{span}\{\partial_m u_p\}\), that would mean the promoted \(Y12\) carrier is merely a translation artifact, contradicting its role as the first live Bottleneck-C carrier.

---

# 3. \(\lambda\)-scale variation also likely cannot generate \(Y12\)

Scale variation gives

\[
\lambda\partial_\lambda F_m(x)
=
3F_m(x)+x\partial_xF_m(x)
\]

plus chart/window terms.

At coefficient level,

\[
\lambda\partial_\lambda s_j
=
(3+j)s_j
+\text{basis/window corrections}.
\]

This is again a coherent Euler-type deformation of existing singular coefficients. It rescales the tower. It does not create an independent order-nine mixed-block carrier unforced by lower orders.

So:

\[
\boxed{
\partial_\lambda u_p\text{ should not span }D_Y.
}
\]

In \(\mathcal K_9\)-language, scale variation should satisfy the EF coherence relation:

\[
\mathcal K_9(\partial_\lambda u_p)=0,
\]

whereas the desired D4 normality is

\[
\mathcal K_9(D_Y)\neq0.
\]

---

# 4. \(\alpha\)-variation is the dangerous tangent

The only plausible tangent source for \(Y12\) is \(\alpha\)-variation, because \(\alpha\) changes the phase balance between plus and folded-dual branches. This can produce mixed branch effects.

If \(\alpha\) is admitted as a genuine actual-source parameter, then

\[
\partial_\alpha u_p\in T_{u_p}\mathcal M_{\sing,p}^{\arith}.
\]

The critical test is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal K_9(D_Y)
\stackrel{?}{\in}
\operatorname{span}\{\mathcal K_9(\partial_\alpha u_p)\}.
}
\]

But if \(\mathcal K_9\) is a true actual-source coherence residual, then it must vanish on every legitimate tangent:

\[
\mathcal K_9(\partial_\alpha u_p)=0.
\]

Therefore \(\alpha\) only kills \(Y12\)-normality if the EF coherence relation itself is defined after allowing \(\alpha\)-dependent order-nine variation that includes the \(Y12\) direction. In that case

\[
D_Y\in T_{u_p}\mathcal M_{\sing,p}^{\arith}
\]

and \(\mathcal K_9(D_Y)=0\).

So the \(\alpha\) audit becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Does }\alpha\text{-variation generate the promoted quadratic }Y12\text{ carrier?}
}
\]

I suspect not generically. \(\alpha\)-variation is a branch-phase tilt; \(Y12\) is a second-order mixed-block whitening carrier. But this is the one tangent direction that must be checked explicitly.

---

# 5. Cutoff/regularization variation should be separated

Cutoff and regularization variations are dangerous if allowed too freely. They can create local high-order changes in the singular expansion.

But they should not be considered tangent directions unless the proof genuinely treats cutoffs as parameters of the actual source manifold. Usually cutoffs are proof artifacts, and different cutoffs represent the same source plus residual.

Therefore the actual-source manifold should quotient cutoff variation into the residual \(R_X\), not enlarge the tangent space by arbitrary cutoff deformations.

If cutoff variations are included freely, \(R_{\sing}^{\arith}\) becomes too large and may contain D4.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\text{do not include arbitrary cutoff variation in }T_u\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}.
}
\]

Allow only fixed admissible cutoff scheme plus controlled residual. This protects D4 normality.

---

# 6. \(\mathcal K_9\) should annihilate true tangents

A good \(\mathcal K_9\) must satisfy:

\[
\mathcal K_9(\partial_m u_p)=0,
\]

\[
\mathcal K_9(\partial_\lambda u_p)=0,
\]

\[
\mathcal K_9(\partial_\alpha u_p)=0
\quad\text{if }\alpha\text{ is admitted},
\]

but

\[
\mathcal K_9(D_Y)\neq0.
\]

This is exactly the normal-covector property.

So instead of first constructing the full manifold, one can construct \(\mathcal K_9\) directly as a linear functional satisfying those annihilation constraints and test it on \(D_Y\).

This is a finite linear-algebra problem:

Given tangent vectors

\[
t_1,\ldots,t_d
\]

and D4 vector \(D_Y\) in \(H_{\sing,p}\), does there exist a covector \(\mathcal K_9\) such that

\[
\mathcal K_9(t_j)=0
\quad(1\le j\le d),
\]

but

\[
\mathcal K_9(D_Y)\neq0?
\]

Equivalent condition:

\[
D_Y\notin\operatorname{span}\{t_1,\ldots,t_d\}.
\]

Thus the \(\mathcal K_9\) construction can be reduced to finite tangent transversality.

---

# 7. Expected rank-one channel

Assuming \(D_Y\) is not tangent, \(\mathcal K_9\) gives a rank-one forbidden channel dominated by

\[
\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

The forbidden wall becomes

\[
E_{\wall}^{K9}(m)
=
\frac{
|H(m)|^2
|\mathcal K_9(D_Y)|^2
|\Gamma_{G,2}(m)|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\D4}(m)
}
\]

plus possible \(\Gamma_G\) mixing.

If the \(\Gamma_G\)-part is absorbed by the order-seven prediction

\[
\mathcal R_{7\to9}s_7,
\]

then the clean wall is simply

\[
E_{\wall}^{K9}\asymp
\frac{
|H|^2|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\D4}
}.
\]

So Stack C2 extraction needs a sublevel theorem specifically for

\[
\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

This is more precise than earlier raw anisotropy.

---

# 8. Possible obstruction: \(\Gamma_{G,2}\)-sublevel concentration

Rank one via \(Y12\) is enough only if the visible wall direction is not too often small.

The required anti-concentration is:

\[
\nu\left(
|\Gamma_{G,2}|<\varepsilon
\right)
\le
Q^C\varepsilon^\beta\nu(\mathcal C)
\]

under the post-Gate–1/O2/O3 measure.

If \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) can vanish on a large subset while \(\Gamma_G\) stays large, the \(Y12\) channel loses mass.

Then one needs either:

1. also a \(\Gamma_G\) channel through \(\mathcal K_9\), or  
2. a theorem that \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) sublevel sets are small on the extracted measure.

Thus the \(Y12\)-normality route adds a specific wall anti-concentration theorem.

---

# 9. Comparison with raw D4 wall

Earlier raw D4 wall used

\[
|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2.
\]

The singular \(\mathcal K_9\) channel may see only

\[
\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

This is weaker, but cleaner.

It may actually be better if \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) is the more genuinely order-nine obstruction. The raw \(\Gamma_G\) part may be tangent because order-seven data can coherently predict some order-nine response.

So the correct downstream wall is not “all D4 anisotropy,” but the normal component:

\[
\boxed{
\Gamma_{\normal}
=
\Pi_{\normal}
L_{\clean}\Gamma.
}
\]

For the \(\mathcal K_9\) route, expect

\[
\Gamma_{\normal}\approx \Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

---

# 10. What must be computed next

The exact finite audit is:

1. Build singular coefficient coordinates through order \(9\):

\[
s_{<7},s_7,s_9.
\]

2. Define the actual EF coherence prediction

\[
s_9^{\EF}
=
\mathcal R_{7\to9}s_7+\mathcal R_{<7\to9}s_{<7}.
\]

3. Define

\[
\mathcal K_9=s_9-s_9^{\EF}.
\]

4. Verify tangent annihilation:

\[
\mathcal K_9(\partial_m u_p)=0,
\]

\[
\mathcal K_9(\partial_\lambda u_p)=0,
\]

\[
\mathcal K_9(\partial_\alpha u_p)=0
\quad\text{if applicable}.
\]

5. Evaluate D4:

\[
\mathcal K_9(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]).
\]

6. If nonzero, define the wall factor via \(\Gamma_{G,2}\).

This is finite and decisive.

---

# 11. Interim conclusion

I now think the \(Y12\)-normality test is plausible:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal K_9(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}])\neq0
}
\]

because \(Y12\) is a promoted quadratic mixed-block carrier, not a legitimate first-order EF parameter variation.

The main risks are:

1. \(\alpha\)-variation spans \(Y12\);
2. \(\mathcal K_9\) is defined too broadly and absorbs \(Y12\);
3. \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) vanishes too often;
4. EF residual control in \(\mathcal K_9\)-norm is too weak.

But these are concrete. The downstream finite-D4 route is no longer vague. The critical computation is the \(\mathcal K_9\) evaluation on the \(Y12\) carrier.

## Agent 6 continued attack — constructive \(\mathcal K_9\) normal-covector test

The current downstream target is no longer a vague \(\Lambda\). It is the finite question whether the transported order-nine D4 carrier

\[
D_Y:=T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\]

is normal to the actual EF singular coherence manifold. The update explicitly identifies this as the best finite-D4 contradiction channel: actual EF rows should lie near a singular coherence manifold, while D4 should push in a normal direction. 

I now make the \(\mathcal K_9\) test constructive.

---

# 1. Replace informal \(\mathcal K_9\) by a Schur-complement normal covector

Let

\[
H_{\sing,p}^{\le9}
\]

be the finite singular coefficient space through order \(9\). Write a vector as

\[
s=(s_{\low},s_7,s_9),
\]

where

\[
s_{\low}:=s_{<7}.
\]

Let the actual EF singular coherence manifold be

\[
\mathcal M_{\sing,p}^{\arith}\subset H_{\sing,p}^{\le9}.
\]

At the actual coherent point \(u_p\), let

\[
T_p:=T_{u_p}\mathcal M_{\sing,p}^{\arith}
\]

be the tangent space.

Let

\[
\Pi_{\low,7}:H_{\sing,p}^{\le9}\to H_{\low,7}
\]

be projection to the lower coordinates

\[
(s_{\low},s_7).
\]

The order-nine connection residual exists if the projection

\[
\Pi_{\low,7}:T_p\to H_{\low,7}
\]

has a stable right inverse on the tangent directions relevant to order \(9\). Then the coherent order-nine component is a graph over lower data:

\[
s_9=\mathcal R_{7\to9}s_7+\mathcal R_{<7\to9}s_{\low}.
\]

In finite linear algebra, this is exactly a Schur complement.

Define the normal covector

\[
\mathcal K_9
\]

as any nonzero covector satisfying

\[
\mathcal K_9|_{T_p}=0
\]

and with nonzero coefficient on the \(s_9\)-coordinate.

Equivalently, if \(T_p\) is represented by tangent columns

\[
t_1,\ldots,t_d\in H_{\sing,p}^{\le9},
\]

then \(\mathcal K_9\) solves

\[
\mathcal K_9(t_j)=0\qquad(1\le j\le d),
\]

with normalization

\[
\mathcal K_9(e_9)=1
\]

if possible.

Then

\[
\mathcal K_9(s)
=
s_9-\mathcal R_{7\to9}s_7-\mathcal R_{<7\to9}s_{\low}.
\]

So the residual is not guessed. It is the Schur complement of the tangent-space graph.

---

# 2. The exact determinant test for \(Y12\)

Let

\[
D_Y=T_{\tr\to\sing}E_{\car}^{\tr}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\in H_{\sing,p}^{\le9}.
\]

Then

\[
\mathcal K_9(D_Y)\neq0
\]

if and only if

\[
D_Y\notin T_p+\ker(\text{order-}9\text{ covector choice}).
\]

More concretely, choose a basis of \(T_p\), and append \(D_Y\). Then \(Y12\)-normality is equivalent to

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}(T_p,D_Y)>\operatorname{rank}(T_p)
}
\]

after projecting to the finite singular coefficient space relevant to \(\mathcal K_9\).

If \(H_{\sing,p}^{\le9}/T_p\) is one-dimensional, this becomes a single determinant:

\[
\boxed{
\det[t_1,\ldots,t_d,D_Y]\neq0
}
\]

in a basis of \(H_{\sing,p}^{\le9}\).

If the normal space has higher dimension, use the least singular value:

\[
\boxed{
\|(I-P_{T_p})D_Y\|\ge Q^{-C}\|D_Y\|.
}
\]

This is the exact finite audit.

---

# 3. How to build \(T_p\)

The tangent space must include only legitimate actual-source directions.

At minimum:

\[
t_m:=\partial_m u_p,
\]

\[
t_\lambda:=\partial_\lambda u_p.
\]

If the proof admits \(\alpha\)-families as actual source deformations, include

\[
t_\alpha:=\partial_\alpha u_p.
\]

If the branch cutoff varies as a structural parameter, include

\[
t_{\cut}:=\partial_{\cut}u_p.
\]

But I would exclude arbitrary cutoff perturbations unless the proof genuinely treats them as source-family directions. Otherwise \(T_p\) becomes too large and kills normality.

So the conservative tangent model is:

\[
T_p
=
\operatorname{span}\{\partial_m u_p,\partial_\lambda u_p\}
\]

or

\[
T_p
=
\operatorname{span}\{\partial_m u_p,\partial_\lambda u_p,\partial_\alpha u_p\}
\]

if \(\alpha\) is admitted.

The theorem should be stated for the larger tangent space only after \(\alpha\)-admissibility is settled.

---

# 4. Why \(Y12\) should be independent of \(m,\lambda\) tangents

The \(m\)- and \(\lambda\)-tangents produce coherent transformations of the entire coefficient tower.

Translation gives a derivative recurrence:

\[
\partial_m s_j
=
\lambda^{-1}s_{j+1}
+
\text{basis variation}.
\]

Scale gives an Euler recurrence:

\[
\lambda\partial_\lambda s_j
=
(3+j)s_j
+
\text{window/basis variation}.
\]

Both relate all orders coherently.

The \(Y12\) carrier is different: it is a promoted quadratic mixed-block carrier

\[
Y_{12}^{(2)}=D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2],
\]

entering the order-nine D4 output through

\[
\alpha_9[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

This carrier is not generated by translating or rescaling the existing singular coefficient tower. Translation and scaling are first-order motions of the actual EF source; \(Y12\) is a second-order formal Bottleneck-C carrier.

Therefore the structural expectation is:

\[
\boxed{
D_Y\notin\operatorname{span}\{\partial_m u_p,\partial_\lambda u_p\}.
}
\]

So if \(\alpha\) is not admitted, \(Y12\)-normality is very plausible.

---

# 5. The \(\alpha\)-danger in determinant form

If \(\alpha\) is admitted, then the tangent space includes

\[
t_\alpha=\partial_\alpha u_p.
\]

The only serious way \(Y12\)-normality can fail is

\[
D_Y
\in
\operatorname{span}\{\partial_m u_p,\partial_\lambda u_p,\partial_\alpha u_p\}.
\]

Because \(D_Y\) is order-nine and mixed-branch, the likely candidate is \(t_\alpha\).

So the finite determinant to check is

\[
\boxed{
\det[t_m,t_\lambda,t_\alpha,D_Y]\neq0
}
\]

in the relevant quotient.

If this determinant vanishes, \(\alpha\)-admission may absorb the \(Y12\) normal direction.

This creates a genuine strategic tradeoff:

\[
\boxed{
\alpha\text{ helps O2/O3 determinant branch survival but may hurt D4 normality.}
}
\]

The project must not admit \(\alpha\) as a source-family tangent unless this determinant remains nonzero.

---

# 6. Define \(\mathcal K_9\) after choosing tangent policy

There are now two versions.

## Fixed-\(\alpha\) \(\mathcal K_9^{(0)}\)

Tangents:

\[
T_p^{(0)}=\operatorname{span}\{t_m,t_\lambda\}.
\]

Normal covector:

\[
\mathcal K_9^{(0)}|_{T_p^{(0)}}=0.
\]

D4 test:

\[
\mathcal K_9^{(0)}(D_Y)\neq0.
\]

This is easiest and most likely true.

## \(\alpha\)-admitted \(\mathcal K_9^{(\alpha)}\)

Tangents:

\[
T_p^{(\alpha)}=\operatorname{span}\{t_m,t_\lambda,t_\alpha\}.
\]

Normal covector:

\[
\mathcal K_9^{(\alpha)}|_{T_p^{(\alpha)}}=0.
\]

D4 test:

\[
\mathcal K_9^{(\alpha)}(D_Y)\neq0.
\]

This is required if \(\alpha\)-averaging is proof-critical.

The two tests should be kept separate. It would be a mistake to use \(\alpha\)-twisting upstream/O2O3 and then test D4 normality only against the fixed-\(\alpha\) tangent space.

---

# 7. Rank-one wall factor from \(\mathcal K_9\)

Assume

\[
\mathcal K_9(D_Y)\neq0.
\]

The D4 image under \(\mathcal K_9\) is

\[
\mathcal K_9(D_{\D4}\Gamma)
=
H\left[
a(m)\Gamma_G+b(m)\Gamma_{G,2}
\right],
\]

where

\[
b(m)=\alpha_9\mathcal K_9(D_Y).
\]

If \(b(m)\neq0\), the forbidden wall is

\[
E_{\wall}^{K9}(m)
=
\frac{
|H(m)|^2
|a(m)\Gamma_G(m)+b(m)\Gamma_{G,2}(m)|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\D4}(m)
}.
\]

If the order-seven-predicted part cancels \(a(m)\), this reduces to

\[
E_{\wall}^{K9}
\asymp
\frac{|H|^2|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2}{\mathcal N_{\D4}}.
\]

In general, use the moving linear form

\[
\Gamma_{\forb}(m)
=
a(m)\Gamma_G+b(m)\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

Extraction must include sublevel anti-concentration for this moving linear form.

---

# 8. The upper in \(\mathcal K_9\)-norm

The global upper is

\[
\sum_p
|\mathcal K_{9,p}(y_p)|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C),
\]

where

\[
y_p=T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(q''_{\can}).
\]

Write

\[
q''_{\can}=F_X+R_X,
\]

with

\[
u_p=T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(F_X).
\]

If

\[
\mathcal K_{9,p}(u_p)=0,
\]

then

\[
\mathcal K_{9,p}(y_p)
=
\mathcal K_{9,p}(T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(R_X)).
\]

So the required residual estimate is

\[
\boxed{
\sum_p
|\mathcal K_{9,p}
(T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(R_X))|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This is the \(\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{K9}\) theorem.

It is stronger than \(L^2\)-Gate–1 completion. It likely requires control of a finite number of singular coefficient functionals of the residual.

---

# 9. If \(\mathcal K_9(D_Y)=0\)

If the \(Y12\) test fails, the next test is the \(\Gamma_G\)-combination:

\[
D_G
=
\beta_9T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
-
\mathcal R_{7\to9}
(c_7T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_7]).
\]

Check

\[
\mathcal K_9(D_G)\neq0.
\]

If yes, rank one survives through \(\Gamma_G\).

If both

\[
\mathcal K_9(D_Y)=0
\]

and

\[
\mathcal K_9(D_G)=0,
\]

then order-nine residual \(\mathcal K_9\) sees no D4. The next fallback is \(\mathcal K_{11}\), but that begins moving beyond current finite D4.

---

# 10. Final theorem to target

The best finite downstream theorem is now:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Order-nine D4 normality theorem.}
}
\]

For a legitimate tangent policy \(\mathcal T_p\) of the actual EF singular coherence manifold,

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}E_{\car,p}^{\tr}[\mathsf C_{Y12}],
\mathcal T_p
\right)
\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

Equivalently, there exists a structural order-nine residual \(\mathcal K_9\) annihilating \(\mathcal T_p\) but not \(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\).

This gives a rank-one forbidden D4 channel, provided the visible \(\Gamma\)-linear form has anti-concentration.

That is the current sharpest downstream target.

\right)
\right|^2
\ge Q^{-A}
|\Gamma_{\forb,p}|^2.
}
\]

If the extracted set has polynomial mass and the visible wall direction \(\Gamma_{\forb,p}\) is polynomially non-small there, the lower bound contradicts the upper.

---

# 2. Actual-source upper

Since

\[
y_p
=
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(F_X)
+
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(R_X),
\]

and by construction

\[
\mathcal K_{9,p}
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(F_X)
\right)
=0,
\]

the upper reduces to

\[
\boxed{
\sum_p
\left|
\mathcal K_{9,p}
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}
J_{\tr,p}(R_X)
\right|^2
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This is the stronger residual estimate:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{K9}.
}
\]

It is **not automatically implied** by ordinary \(L^2\) EF completion unless the operator

\[
R\mapsto
\mathcal K_{9,p}T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(R)
\]

has polynomial norm with respect to the \(L^2\) residual norm used in Gate–1.

A sufficient implication is:

\[
\left|
\mathcal K_{9,p}T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(R)
\right|^2
\le
Q^C
\|R\|_{H^s(I_p)}^2
\]

for some finite \(s\), plus a Sobolev-level residual estimate

\[
\sum_p
\|R_X\|_{H^s(I_p)}^2
\le
Q^{-B-C}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

So the residual layer splits:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{L^2}
\quad\text{for Gate--1;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{K9}
\quad\text{for downstream normality.}
}
\]

The latter is stronger and must be proved separately unless a polynomial operator bound from \(L^2\) is available.

---

# 3. D4 lower through \(\mathcal K_9\)

The clean transition D4 output is

\[
D_{\D4,p}\Gamma
=
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}
E_{\car,p}^{\tr}
L_{\clean,p}^{\tr}
\Gamma,
\]

with

\[
\Gamma=
\begin{pmatrix}
\Gamma_G\\
\Gamma_{G,2}
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Apply \(\mathcal K_{9,p}\):

\[
\mathcal K_{9,p}(D_{\D4,p}\Gamma)
=
H(p)\bigl(a_p\Gamma_G+b_p\Gamma_{G,2}\bigr).
\]

Here the key coefficient is

\[
b_p
=
\alpha_9\,
\mathcal K_{9,p}
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\right).
\]

Thus the rank-one \(Y12\) theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
|b_p|\ge Q^{-C}
}
\]

on a polynomial-weight set.

Equivalently,

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal K_{9,p}
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\right)
\neq0
}
\]

with polynomial lower bound.

If \(b_p\neq0\), finite D4 has a forbidden rank-one channel.

---

# 4. Rank-one wall factor

Define

\[
\Gamma_{\forb,p}
:=
a_p\Gamma_G+b_p\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

Then the correct wall factor is

\[
\boxed{
E_{\wall}^{K9}(p)
=
\frac{
|H(p)|^2
|\Gamma_{\forb,p}|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\D4}(p)
}.
}
\]

In the clean \(Y12\)-dominant case, if the order-seven-predicted \(\Gamma_G\)-part is absorbed by the residual relation, then

\[
a_p=0,
\qquad
b_p\neq0,
\]

and

\[
E_{\wall}^{K9}
\asymp
\frac{|H|^2|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2}{\mathcal N_{\D4}}.
\]

So extraction must use a sublevel estimate for \(\Gamma_{\forb,p}\), and in the clean case specifically for \(\Gamma_{G,2}\):

\[
\nu\left(|\Gamma_{G,2}|<\varepsilon\right)
\le
Q^C\varepsilon^\beta\nu(\mathcal C).
\]

---

# 5. Tubular / moving residual control

If \(\mathcal K_{9,p}\) depends on \(p\) or on the base coherent row \(u_p\), it is a moving normal covector. Then the distance-to-manifold argument needs polynomial tubular control.

A sufficient condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\|\nabla \mathcal K_{9,p}\|\le Q^C
}
\]

and, equivalently, a polynomial second-fundamental-form bound for

\[
\mathcal M_{\sing,p}^{\arith}.
\]

Then, for a D4 displacement \(D\),

\[
\operatorname{dist}(u_p+D,\mathcal M_{\sing,p}^{\arith})
\ge
|\mathcal K_{9,p}(D)|
-
Q^C\|D\|^2
-
\mathrm{error}.
\]

Thus if

\[
|\mathcal K_{9,p}(D)|\ge Q^{-A}
\]

and the quadratic/tail terms are smaller, the normal displacement is real and cannot be absorbed by moving along the manifold.

This is the required

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{Tubular}_{\sing}^{\arith}
}
\]

hypothesis.

---

# 6. Good subset and contradiction

Assume aggregate extraction gives \(\mathcal G\) such that, for each \(p\in\mathcal G\),

\[
E_1(p)\ge Q^{-C},
\]

\[
E_{23}(p)\ge Q^{-C},
\]

\[
E_{\wall}^{K9}(p)\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

Then local Gate–2/O2–O3/D4 gives

\[
\left|
\mathcal K_{9,p}(y_p)
\right|^2
\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

Summing over the good subset,

\[
\sum_{p}
|\mathcal K_{9,p}(y_p)|^2
\ge
Q^{-C}\mu(\mathcal G)
\ge
Q^{-C'}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

But \(\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{K9}\) gives

\[
\sum_p
|\mathcal K_{9,p}(y_p)|^2
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

If \(B>C'\), contradiction.

This is Type-I/atomic: no complement cancellation problem remains.

---

# 7. Failure modes

The NormalD4\(_{K9,Y12}\) route can fail in exactly these ways.

## 7.1 \(K_9(Y12)=0\)

\[
\mathcal K_{9,p}
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\right)=0.
\]

Then the best rank-one channel dies.

Fallback: test the \(\Gamma_G\)-combination

\[
\mathcal K_9\left(
\beta_9[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
-
\mathcal R_{7\to9}(c_7[\mathsf C_7])
\right).
\]

If that also vanishes, order-nine normal D4 fails.

## 7.2 \(\alpha\)-tangent kills \(Y12\)

If \(\alpha\) is admitted and

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\in
T_u\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}
\]

because of \(\partial_\alpha u\), then

\[
K_9(Y12)=0.
\]

So \(\alpha\)-twisting must be checked against D4 normality.

## 7.3 \(\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{K9}\) fails

The \(L^2\) residual may be small, but the singular order-nine residual

\[
\mathcal K_9T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(R_X)
\]

may not be small. Then the forbidden upper fails.

## 7.4 Tubular radius too small

If the EF singular manifold is too curved, a D4 normal displacement may be absorbed by a nearby actual-source parameter shift. Need polynomial tubular control.

## 7.5 Wall sublevel concentration

If

\[
\Gamma_{\forb,p}=a_p\Gamma_G+b_p\Gamma_{G,2}
\]

is often tiny under the extracted measure, D4 normality exists but has insufficient mass.

## 7.6 O2/O3 not available

Even with Gate–1 and D4 normality, local D4 requires the O2/O3 nondegeneracy layer. Without aggregate O2/O3, no good subset.

---

# 8. Final theorem package

The final downstream theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{NormalD4}_{K9,Y12}
=
\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{K9}
+
\mathrm{Tubular}_{\sing}^{\arith}
+
\mathrm{K9Y12Normality}
+
\mathrm{WallAntiConcentration}_{\Gamma_{\forb}}.
}
\]

Where

\[
\mathrm{K9Y12Normality}
\]

is

\[
\boxed{
\left|
\mathcal K_{9,p}
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\right)
\right|
\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

This is the first finite computation to perform.

---

# 9. Most important next audit

The immediate yes/no audit is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal K_9(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}])\neq0
\quad
\text{mod actual-source tangents?}
}
\]

If yes, finite D4 has a credible rank-one forbidden channel through \(\Gamma_{G,2}\).

If no, the route must either use the \(\Gamma_G\)-combination, move to \(\mathcal K_{11}\), or abandon finite D4 as the contradiction mechanism.

## Assistant (2026-04-28 04:49:47)

I’ll push the \(K_9/Y12\) normality test one level deeper. The subtle issue is that \(Y12\) is quadratic, so the correct comparison is not only against the tangent space but also against the actual EF manifold’s second fundamental form.

_Thought for 15s_

## Agent 6 continued attack — \(Y12\) must beat actual EF second curvature, not only tangent

The current downstream target is:

\[
\mathcal K_9\!\left(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\right)\neq0
\]

modulo actual-source tangents. That is necessary, but not quite sufficient. The \(Y12\) carrier is a **quadratic** mixed-block object, so the correct obstruction is not merely “normal to the tangent space.” It must also be **not equal to the normal second fundamental form** of the actual EF singular coherence manifold.

The latest coordinator state identifies \(Y12\)-normality via \(\mathcal K_9\) as the sharp finite-D4 target. 

# 1. The issue: \(Y12\) is second-order

The carrier

\[
Y_{12}^{(2)}=D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\]

is not a first-order EF parameter tangent. That already suggests

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\notin T_u\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}.
\]

But a nonlinear manifold has second-order curvature. If the actual EF manifold is parameterized by \(\theta\),

\[
u(\theta+\delta\theta)
=
u(\theta)
+
D_\theta u[\delta\theta]
+
\frac12D_\theta^2u[\delta\theta,\delta\theta]
+\cdots,
\]

then the normal part of

\[
D_\theta^2u[\delta\theta,\delta\theta]
\]

is exactly the second fundamental form:

\[
\mathrm{II}_u(\delta\theta,\delta\theta)
=
\Pi^\perp D_\theta^2u[\delta\theta,\delta\theta].
\]

So a normal quadratic displacement is not automatically forbidden. It may be the legitimate curvature of the actual-source manifold.

Therefore the true D4 obstruction is:

\[
\boxed{
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\notin
T_u\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}
+
\operatorname{Im}\mathrm{II}_u.
}
\]

At order nine, the forbidden quotient should be refined from

\[
\mathcal Q_9^{\forb}
=
\mathcal Y_9/
(\mathcal T_{\par}+\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7})
\]

to

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal Q_{9,2}^{\forb}
=
\mathcal Y_9/
(\mathcal T_{\par}
+\mathcal T_7
+\mathcal T_{<7}
+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}).
}
\]

Here \(\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}\) is the order-nine normal second-curvature space of the actual EF singular manifold.

---

# 2. Why this matters

If

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\notin T_u\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}
\]

but

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\in \operatorname{Im}\mathrm{II}_u,
\]

then D4 is not a contradiction. It is an allowed second-order deformation of the actual EF source family.

In that case, the distance to the manifold after a quadratic displacement is not

\[
\|\Pi^\perp D_Y\|.
\]

The actual manifold bends toward the D4 displacement and absorbs it at second order.

So the earlier normality test is too weak unless we know the actual EF manifold is flat enough in the \(Y12\) signature.

---

# 3. The corrected \(K_9\) residual

The covariant order-nine residual must annihilate:

1. first-order actual-source tangents;
2. lower-order coherent connection effects;
3. legitimate second-order EF curvature terms.

Thus \(\mathcal K_9\) should satisfy

\[
\mathcal K_9(t)=0
\quad
(t\in \mathcal T_{\par}+\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}),
\]

and also

\[
\mathcal K_9(\mathrm{II}_u(v,w))=0
\]

for all legitimate actual parameter tangent vectors \(v,w\).

Then the D4 test becomes

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal K_9(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}])\neq0
\quad
\text{mod }
\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}.
}
\]

This is the correct finite test.

---

# 4. What is \(\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}\)?

Let admissible actual-source parameters be

\[
\theta=(m,\lambda,\alpha,\ldots)
\]

depending on the tangent policy. Define

\[
u_p(\theta)=T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(F_X^\theta).
\]

The second-curvature space at order nine is generated by

\[
\Pi_9D_\theta^2u_p[\partial_{\theta_i},\partial_{\theta_j}]
\]

after subtracting tangent/lower-order components.

So

\[
\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}
=
\operatorname{span}
\left\{
\Pi_9D_\theta^2u_p[e_i,e_j]
:
e_i,e_j\in T_\theta\Theta_{\arith}
\right\}
\]

modulo

\[
\mathcal T_{\par}+\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}.
\]

The main pairs are:

\[
(m,m),\quad (m,\lambda),\quad(\lambda,\lambda),
\]

and if \(\alpha\) is admitted,

\[
(m,\alpha),\quad(\lambda,\alpha),\quad(\alpha,\alpha).
\]

Cutoff variation should not be included unless cutoff is a genuine actual-source parameter rather than a proof artifact.

---

# 5. \(Y12\) signature may still survive

The good news: \(Y12\) has a very specific signature:

\[
\Sigma(Y12)
=
(\text{source Hessian }D^2N_0,
\text{mixed branch},
\text{same-point},
\text{one whitening slot},
\text{specific reflection/source-grade parity}).
\]

The actual EF second fundamental form from parameters like \(m,\lambda,\alpha\) has a different expected signature.

## \(m,m\)

Second \(m\)-variation gives

\[
\partial_m^2u_p,
\]

a second derivative of the same scalar EF packet. It is coherent along the coefficient tower. It does not naturally produce a mixed Hessian \(D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]\) with two independent source slots.

## \(m,\lambda\), \(\lambda,\lambda\)

These give derivative/Euler recurrences and basis-scale curvature. Again, they are coordinate curvature of the same packet, not a bilinear source Hessian in two independent branches.

## \(\alpha\)-involving pairs

These are the dangerous ones. \(\alpha\) changes folded-dual branch phase/weight. Mixed pairs like

\[
(m,\alpha),\quad(\lambda,\alpha)
\]

can produce mixed branch second-order effects.

The key question is whether those effects have the same same-point whitening and \(D^2N_0\)-polarization signature as \(Y12\). I still expect not generically, but this is the most important check.

Thus the sharpened signature theorem should be:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}
\left(
\mathcal T_{\par}
+\mathcal T_7
+\mathcal T_{<7}
+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}
\right)=0,
}
\]

but

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0.
}
\]

If this holds, \(Y12\) survives even after second-order actual-source curvature is quotient out.

---

# 6. Corrected finite matrix test

Previously, the tangent test used

\[
T_p=
(\partial_{\theta_j}u_p)_j.
\]

Now form the **second-order model matrix**

\[
M_p^{(2)}
=
\left[
\partial_{\theta_j}u_p
\ \middle|\ 
\Pi_9D_\theta^2u_p[\partial_{\theta_i},\partial_{\theta_j}]
\ \middle|\
\text{lower connection columns}
\right].
\]

Then test

\[
D_Y=
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}E_{\car,p}^{\tr}[\mathsf C_{Y12}].
\]

The corrected condition is

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}(D_Y,\operatorname{ran}M_p^{(2)})
\ge Q^{-C}\|D_Y\|.
}
\]

Equivalently, there exists a normal covector \(\mathcal K_9^{(2)}\) such that

\[
\mathcal K_9^{(2)}M_p^{(2)}=0,
\]

but

\[
\mathcal K_9^{(2)}D_Y\neq0.
\]

This is the actual finite audit.

---

# 7. Implication for the downstream theorem

The downstream upper must now be phrased as distance to the nonlinear manifold, not just tangent projection.

The actual row satisfies

\[
\operatorname{dist}(y_p,\mathcal M_{\sing,p}^{\arith})^2
\le Q^{-B}
\]

by EF residual coherence.

The D4 lower must show that the D4-perturbed row has distance at least \(Q^{-C}\). Using second-order expansion:

\[
\operatorname{dist}(u_p+D_p\Gamma,\mathcal M)
\]

is controlled by the component of \(D_p\Gamma\) outside tangent plus second-curvature directions. Therefore the lower uses

\[
\mathcal K_9^{(2)}D_p\Gamma,
\]

not merely \(\mathcal K_9D_p\Gamma\).

This avoids false contradictions from legitimate manifold curvature.

---

# 8. Does this weaken the \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) channel?

Potentially. If \(Y12\) lies in the second fundamental form generated by \(\alpha\)-branch curvature, then the clean \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) channel disappears.

But if the source-Hessian/same-point whitening signature is genuinely absent from actual EF parameter curvature, then the channel remains.

So the key test becomes:

\[
\boxed{
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\neq0
\quad
\text{in}
\quad
\mathcal Q_{9,2}^{\forb}.
}
\]

This is a stronger but safer version of the earlier test.

---

# 9. Failure modes now sharpened

The \(K_9/Y12\) route can fail in three increasingly refined ways:

## First-order tangent failure

\[
Y12\in \mathcal T_{\par}+\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}.
\]

Then it is a legitimate first-order source deformation or lower-order connection effect.

## Second-order curvature failure

\[
Y12\in\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}
\quad
\text{mod first-order/lower effects}.
\]

Then D4 is a legitimate second-order curvature of the actual EF singular manifold.

## Residual-norm failure

Even if \(Y12\) is normal, the actual residual upper

\[
\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{K9}
\]

may fail.

Only if all three are avoided does finite D4 close downstream.

---

# 10. Current best theorem

The best theorem is now:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Second-order }Y12\textbf{ signature separation.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}
\left(
\mathcal T_{\par}
+\mathcal T_7
+\mathcal T_{<7}
+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}
\right)=0,
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0.
}
\]

This proves:

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0
\quad
\text{in}
\quad
\mathcal Q_{9,2}^{\forb}.
\]

Then the forbidden wall is still essentially

\[
|H|^2|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\]

up to polynomial factors.

---

# 11. Strategic effect

This is a higher bar, but it is the right one. It prevents a false proof where D4 merely detects the second curvature of the actual-source manifold.

The D4 obstruction must be **normal even after accounting for legitimate second-order EF curvature**.

This aligns with the fact that \(Y12\) itself is quadratic. We should not compare a quadratic formal carrier only to first-order tangents.

---

# Final status

The \(K_9/Y12\) route remains the best finite-D4 downstream candidate, but the correct finite quotient is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal Q_{9,2}^{\forb}
=
\mathcal Y_9/
(\mathcal T_{\par}
+\mathcal T_7
+\mathcal T_{<7}
+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}).
}
\]

The decisive question is:

\[
\boxed{
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0
\quad\text{in}\quad
\mathcal Q_{9,2}^{\forb}?
}
\]

That is the next finite audit.
