## Agent 6 continued attack — rank-two joint wall as a better trap

Assume the favorable local result:

\[
B_Y=0.
\]

Then the D4 output is vector-valued:

\[
F_{2Y}
=
H
\begin{pmatrix}
c_7\Gamma_G\\
\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

On coefficient-good patches,

\[
\|F_{2Y}\|^2
\asymp_{Q^A}
|H|^2\left(|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2\right).
\]

The rank-two route replaces the scalar moving wall

\[
\Gamma_{G,2}+\rho_\Gamma\Gamma_G
\]

with the joint wall

\[
(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}).
\]

That is not only simpler; its failure is much more rigid.

---

# 1. Rank-two failure gives a coupled algebraic jet trap

Rank-two wall failure means

\[
\Gamma_G\approx0,
\qquad
\Gamma_{G,2}\approx0.
\]

Recall

\[
\Gamma_G=q''_{\can}-6q_0+2q_0^3,
\]

\[
\Gamma_{G,2}=(1-q_0^2)q''_{\can}-\frac16q^{(4)}.
\]

The exact simultaneous wall equations are

\[
q''=6q_0-2q_0^3,
\]

\[
q^{(4)}
=
6(1-q_0^2)q''.
\]

Substitute the first into the second:

\[
q^{(4)}
=
6(1-q_0^2)(6q_0-2q_0^3).
\]

Thus

\[
\boxed{
q^{(4)}
=
36q_0-48q_0^3+12q_0^5.
}
\]

So rank-two wall failure imposes the coupled trap

\[
\boxed{
q''=6q_0-2q_0^3,
\qquad
q^{(4)}=36q_0-48q_0^3+12q_0^5.
}
\]

This is stronger than the rank-one forced wall ODE, because it constrains both \(q''\) and \(q^{(4)}\) algebraically from \(q_0\).

---

# 2. The second equation is not independent if the first holds identically

If

\[
q''=6q-2q^3
\]

held identically, differentiating twice gives

\[
q^{(4)}
=
(6-6q^2)q''-12q(q')^2.
\]

Using

\[
q''=6q-2q^3
\]

this becomes

\[
q^{(4)}
=
6(1-q^2)(6q-2q^3)-12q(q')^2.
\]

But the rank-two trap requires

\[
q^{(4)}
=
6(1-q^2)(6q-2q^3).
\]

Therefore both conditions imply

\[
\boxed{
q(q')^2=0
}
\]

if the first equation holds as an identity.

Thus, on any interval where \(q\neq0\), the simultaneous trap forces

\[
q'=0.
\]

Then the first equation forces

\[
0=q''=6q-2q^3=2q(3-q^2),
\]

so

\[
q=\pm\sqrt3
\]

or \(q=0\).

This is extremely rigid.

So a persistent rank-two wall trap would force \(q\) into a constant special state, which is incompatible with a nontrivial canonical zeta source on ordinary intervals.

This is a major advantage of rank two.

---

# 3. Approximate version: rank-two trap is finite-type impossible unless \(q\) is nearly constant-special

If

\[
|\Gamma_G|\le\varepsilon,
\qquad
|\Gamma_{G,2}|\le\varepsilon
\]

on a sufficiently long interval, then

\[
q''\approx 6q-2q^3,
\]

\[
q^{(4)}\approx 6(1-q^2)q''.
\]

Differentiating the approximate first relation twice and comparing with the approximate second gives

\[
12q(q')^2\approx0
\]

up to derivative-control errors.

Thus either:

\[
q\approx0
\]

or

\[
q'\approx0.
\]

If \(q'\approx0\), the approximate first relation forces

\[
q\approx0
\quad\text{or}\quad
q^2\approx3.
\]

So the approximate joint trap confines \(q\) near

\[
\boxed{
q\approx0,\quad q\approx\sqrt3,\quad q\approx-\sqrt3
}
\]

with small derivative.

This should be much easier to rule out using the actual canonical source than the rank-one moving ODE.

---

# 4. Rank-two wall anti-concentration theorem can be stronger

The rank-two wall theorem can be stated as:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{JointD4Wall}_{2}:
\quad
\int
\min\left(
Q^A|H|^2(|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2),
1
\right)d\nu
\ge Q^{-B}\nu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

A sufficient theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\nu(|\Gamma_G|<\varepsilon,\ |\Gamma_{G,2}|<\varepsilon)
\le Q^C\varepsilon^\theta\nu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This can be proved by finite type of the map

\[
m\mapsto(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}).
\]

Unlike rank one, failure of finite type implies a near-constant-special state rather than a broad forced ODE family.

This is a much more favorable anti-concentration problem.

---

# 5. Source determinant condition

A rank-two finite-type witness is

\[
W_{G,G2}
=
\Gamma_G\Gamma_{G,2}'-\Gamma_G'\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

If

\[
|\Gamma_G|+|\Gamma_{G,2}|\ge Q^{-C}
\]

or

\[
|W_{G,G2}|\ge Q^{-C}
\]

on routed mass, then joint sublevel is polynomial.

At common near-zeros, use derivatives of the vector map:

\[
D(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}),
\quad
D^2(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}),
\ldots
\]

Finite-type failure through bounded order means the coupled jet trap above persists.

So the rank-two wall theorem can be recast as:

\[
\boxed{
(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})
\text{ is not finite-type flat on polynomial mass.}
}
\]

---

# 6. Consequence for O2/O3

Rank two uses the wall weight

\[
W_{\bd}^{(2)}
=
N_{\sm}|H|^2(|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2).
\]

This is a sum of two bounded-charge weights. It can be decomposed dyadically into whichever component dominates:

\[
|\Gamma_G|^2\ge|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\]

or

\[
|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2\ge|\Gamma_G|^2.
\]

Thus O2/O3 can be run with one scalar component at a time, losing only a polynomial factor.

The branch bandwidth is

\[
d_0^{(2)}
=
d_N+2d_H+2\max(d_{\Gamma_G},d_{\Gamma_{G,2}}).
\]

No moving coefficient \(\rho_\Gamma\) appears. This is cleaner for the bounded-charge near-pure Schur block.

---

# 7. Amplitude model in rank two is also less dangerous

If \(B_Y=0\), then the local primitive fiber has no actual-source/nuisance tangent. This strongly suggests that

\[
\mathcal C_{2Y}^{\arith}
\]

is absent or fixed. A finite coherent amplitude space could still arise from global completion or labelled-realization residuals, but it cannot come from local fiber motion.

So rank two makes the expected amplitude classification more favorable:

\[
\mathcal C_{2Y}^{\arith}=0/\text{fixed}
\]

is plausible.

If finite coherent remains, the vector amplitude Schur is:

\[
\operatorname{dist}_{L^2(\nu)}
(F_{2Y},\mathcal C_{2Y}^{\arith})^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\|F_{2Y}\|^2.
\]

But this is still easier than the scalar rank-one forced-wall family, because the target is two-dimensional.

---

# 8. The rank-two route avoids \(\rho_Y\) after classification

If \(B_Y=0\), we do not need

\[
\rho_Y
\]

or

\[
\rho_\Gamma.
\]

We only need to know that no actual-source graph has been quotiented.

This removes the low-order WallHonesty audit.

Thus, proving

\[
B_Y=0
\]

saves:

1. \(\rho_Y\)-low-order proof;
2. \(\rho_\Gamma\)-low-order proof;
3. moving-line wall finite type;
4. forced ODE trap Schur;
5. effective-wall branch-charge complications.

This is a substantial simplification.

---

# 9. Rank-two theorem package

The preferred downstream theorem is now:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{RankTwoY12D4}.
}
\]

Hypotheses:

1. Live dual pair exists:

\[
(e_7,e_Y)
\]

are independent modulo nuisance/source-linear directions.

2. \(B_Y=0\):

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}
(\mathcal N_{\sing}+T\mathcal M_Y^{\arith}
+\mathcal T_{E_2^Y}^{\local}
+\mathcal T_{\lab,Y}^{\local})
=0.
\]

3. Coefficient conditioning:

\[
|c_7|,\ |c_7^{-1}|,\ |\alpha_9|,\ |\alpha_9^{-1}|\le Q^A.
\]

4. No locally free vector amplitude model.

5. Joint D4 wall anti-concentration:

\[
(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})
\]

has polynomial wall mass.

6. O2/O3 with vector wall.

Then finite D4 gives a direct rank-two atomic contradiction.

---

# 10. Current tactical conclusion

The next proof attempt should aggressively pursue:

\[
\boxed{
B_Y=0.
}
\]

The strongest subgoal is:

\[
\boxed{
\ell_7(T\mathcal M_Y^{\arith})=0
\quad\text{and}\quad
\ell_9^Y(T\mathcal M_Y^{\arith})=0.
}
\]

If this passes for:

\[
\mathcal T_{\par}^{\actual},
\quad
\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9},
\quad
\mathcal T_{<7}^{\arith},
\quad
\mathcal T_{E_2^Y}^{\local},
\quad

then rank two becomes the main route.

\[
\rho_Y=\text{lower }Y12\text{ graph slope},\qquad

## Agent 6 continued attack — a candidate proof of \(B_Y=0\)

The best route is now rank two. That means proving

\[
B_Y=0,
\]

where

\[
B_Y
=
\Pi_{\fib,Y}
\left[
\mathcal N_{\sing}
+
T\mathcal M_Y^{\arith}
+
\mathcal T_{E_2^Y}^{\local}
+
\mathcal T_{\lab,Y}^{\local}
\right]
\subset\mathbb C^2_{(s_7,s_9^Y)}.
\]

The key correction remains: the formal \(C_7\to Y12\) connection does **not** enter \(B_Y\) unless \(s_7\) is actual-source motion. So the immediate target is

\[
\ell_7(U)=0,\qquad \ell_9^Y(U)=0
\]

for every actual-source/nuisance/local contaminant \(U\). 

---

# 1. The core separation principle

The live pair

\[
(e_7,e_Y)
\]

should be treated as a **carrier obstruction pair**, not as ordinary Taylor coordinates.

That is:

\[
s_7(v)=\ell_7(v)
\]

does not mean “the order-seven Taylor coefficient of \(v\).” It means the component of \(v\) in the live \(C_7\)-carrier class after quotienting nuisance/source-linear directions.

Likewise,

\[
s_9^Y(v)=\ell_9^Y(v)
\]

does not mean “the order-nine Taylor coefficient of \(v\).” It means the primitive \(Y12\)-signature component:

\[
(o_N,o_W)
=
(D^2N_0,DW_{\same}),
\]

mixed branch \(12\), same-point, one whitening slot.

So ordinary source variations may have order-seven/order-nine Taylor mass and still satisfy

\[
\ell_7(v)=\ell_9^Y(v)=0.
\]

This is the main reason \(B_Y=0\) is plausible.

---

# 2. Actual-source first tangents

Let

\[
v_\theta=\partial_\theta J_{\sing}(F_X^\theta)
\]

for actual parameters

\[
\theta\in\{m,\lambda,\text{phase/chart/normalization/background/gamma}\}.
\]

These are source-linear variations of the canonical EF packet:

\[
F_X=B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}.
\]

They have origins:

\[
\mathrm{EF\text{-}linear},
\quad
\mathrm{gamma/background},
\quad
\mathrm{chart/coordinate}.
\]

They do **not** have primitive origin pair

\[
(D^2N_0,DW_{\same}).
\]

Therefore they should satisfy

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}v_\theta=0.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\boxed{
\ell_7(v_\theta)=0,\qquad \ell_9^Y(v_\theta)=0.
}
\]

This gives

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_{\par}^{\actual}=0.
\]

The only caveat is \(\alpha\). If \(\alpha\) is proof-thickening only, exclude it from \(T\mathcal M_Y^{\arith}\). If \(\alpha\) is internal actual-source motion, it must pass the same origin-pair test. I would not include it by default.

---

# 3. Actual second curvature

Actual second curvature has the schematic form

\[
D_\theta^2 J_{\sing}(F_X^\theta)[\eta_1,\eta_2].
\]

Even though it is second-order in parameters, it differentiates the EF packet, weights, phase, chart map, gamma/background, or fold—not the nonlinear source Hessian \(D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]\) in the primitive \(Y12\) slot.

The dangerous primitive \(Y12\) form is

\[
DW_{\same}[a_Y]\,D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2],
\]

with the correct mixed \(12\), same-point, one-whitening signature.

For actual fixed-\(\alpha\) parameter curvature, the slot-rank test says we would need

\[
a_Y\in V_W,\qquad
\xi_1\in V_1,\qquad
\xi_2\in V_2.
\]

For ordinary \(m,\lambda,\) chart/phase/background/gamma directions, at least one of these slots should be absent. Thus

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}=0
}
\]

under fixed-\(\alpha\).

This supports rank two.

---

# 4. Lower actual directions below \(7\)

The old Bottleneck-C statement should be used carefully.

It does **not** prove that the formal \(C_7\)-carrier is actual-source motion.

It does support this weaker but useful theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\text{No actual lower direction below \(7\) produces an independent primitive \(Y12\)-fiber column.}
}
\]

In the current rank-two audit we need more:

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_{<7}^{\arith}=0.
\]

This should follow if all actual lower directions below \(7\) are source-linear, chart, gamma/background, or nuisance directions without primitive

\[
(D^2N_0,DW_{\same})
\]

origin.

If there is a lower actual direction with nonzero \(s_7\), then rank two may collapse to rank one. But that direction must be **actual**, not merely formal.

So the test is:

\[
\boxed{
\ell_7(u)=0
\quad\text{for every }u\in\mathcal T_{<7}^{\arith}\cup\mathcal T_7^{\arith}.
}
\]

If true, lower actual directions do not enter \(B_Y\).

---

# 5. \(E_2^Y\) local contribution

The completion term \(E_2^Y\) has to be split into:

\[
\text{local fiber freedom}
\]

versus

\[
\text{finite coherent amplitude}
\]

versus

\[
\text{residual}.
\]

For rank two, local fiber contribution must vanish:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_{E_2^Y}^{\local}=0.
}
\]

A fixed \(E_2^Y\) basepoint is not a tangent and does not enter \(B_Y\).

A finite coherent amplitude

\[
\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}
\]

does not enter \(B_Y\); it enters the amplitude-Schur layer.

Only a local derivative of \(E_2^Y\) in the primitive fiber enters \(B_Y\).

Using the coefficient map

\[
c_E(\theta;p)=\frac{\Pi_YE_{\model}(\theta;p)}{y_Y(p)},
\]

rank two requires

\[
Dc_E=0,\qquad D^2c_E=0
\]

in the primitive \(Y12\)-fiber grade for actual local parameters.

If true, \(E_2^Y\) does not block rank two.

---

# 6. Labelled realization local contribution

The labelled/raw mismatch is

\[
R_{\lab}
=
R_{\norm}+R_{\whit}+R_{\proj}+R_{\trunc}+R_{\chart}+R_{\tail}.
\]

For rank two, the **local fiber** part must vanish:

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_{\lab,Y}^{\local}=0.
\]

The commutator form is

\[
\mathfrak C_Y
=
\mathfrak C_Y^N+\mathfrak C_Y^L+\mathfrak C_Y^W.
\]

Rank-two-compatible outcomes:

\[
\mathfrak C_Y=0,
\]

or

\[
\mathfrak C_Y
\]

is fixed/coherent amplitude, not local fiber tangent.

Rank-two-incompatible outcomes:

\[
\mathfrak C_Y
\in \mathbf C(1,\rho_Y),
\]

which collapses to rank one;

\[
\mathfrak C_Y
\in \mathbf C(0,1),
\]

which kills \(Y12\);

or rank two local absorption.

Expected favorable result:

- \(\mathfrak C_Y^L=0\) if \(L\) is affine or wrong-origin;
- \(\mathfrak C_Y^N=0\) by tensorial naturality;
- \(\mathfrak C_Y^W=0\) by origin-pair separation of moving-whitening artifacts.

Thus labelled realization likely does not enter \(B_Y\).

---

# 7. Singular nuisance

Need

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal N_{\sing}=0.
\]

This is the quotient-faithfulness condition. If \(e_7\) or \(e_Y\) lies in nuisance, rank two fails for quotient-design reasons.

This is not arithmetic absorption unless the nuisance is genuinely actual-source coherent. It may be repairable by refining the quotient.

So classify nuisance failure separately:

\[
\boxed{
\text{nuisance overcollapse}
\neq
\text{actual-source coherence}.
}
\]

---

# 8. Proposed \(B_Y=0\) theorem

The theorem should be:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{NoActualPrimitiveY12FiberMotion}.
}
\]

Hypotheses:

1. Live dual pair exists and is conditioned.
2. Actual fixed-target first tangents are source-linear/background/chart-origin, hence killed by \(\Pi_{\fib,Y}\).
3. Fixed-\(\alpha\) actual second curvature fails the \(Y12\) slot-rank test.
4. Actual lower directions below or at \(7\) do not carry live \(C_7\) or primitive \(Y12\) fiber coordinates.
5. \(E_2^Y\) has no local fiber derivative; it is absent/fixed/residual/finite coherent amplitude.
6. Labelled-realization commutator has no local fiber component.
7. Nuisance quotient is origin-pair faithful.

Conclusion:

\[
\boxed{
B_Y=0.
}
\]

Then rank-two D4 survives.

---

# 9. If one hypothesis fails

## Actual \(s_7\)-motion exists

Then

\[
B_Y=\mathbf C(1,\rho_Y)
\]

if its \(Y12\) continuation is unique. Rank one.

## Vertical \(s_9^Y\)-motion exists

Then current \(Y12\) route dies.

## Second slope exists

Then actual-source model spans too much. Current route dies locally.

## \(E_2^Y\) finite coherent amplitude exists

Rank two local still survives, but amplitude Schur is needed.

# 2. RankTwoY12D4 theorem package

The rank-two theorem should be stated as:

\[
\boxed{\mathrm{RankTwoY12D4}.}
\]

It requires the following hypotheses.

## 2.1 TwoGradeLiveQuotient

Construct

\[
\mathcal Q_{2Y}=\mathcal J_{7,9}/\mathcal D_{7,9}
\]

with live classes

\[
e_7,\quad e_Y
\]

surviving independently:

\[
\lambda_{\min}(G_{\live})\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

This gives bounded dual covectors

\[
\ell_7,\quad \ell_9^Y.
\]

## 2.2 CanonicalImageAvoidsLiveFiber

The actual EF/gamma/chart/tail generated source has zero live fiber image:

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}T\mathcal M_Y^{\arith}=0.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\ell_7(U)=\ell_9^Y(U)=0
\]

for all canonical actual-source tangent and curvature generators \(U\).

## 2.3 NoLocalCompletionFiberFreedom

Completion terms may be fixed, finite coherent, or residual, but they must not give local fiber tangent:

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_{E_2^Y}^{\local}=0.
\]

## 2.4 TypedCommutator\(_{2Y}\)

The labelled/raw transition mismatch must have no local live-fiber component:

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}R_{\lab}^{\local}=0.
\]

Finite coherent or residual labelled errors are allowed, but local graph/vertical fiber errors are not compatible with rank two.

## 2.5 NuisancePreservesLiveFiber

The nuisance quotient must not kill or identify the live pair:

\[
\mathcal N_{\sing}\cap\operatorname{span}\{e_7,e_Y\}=0
\]

with polynomial angle.

## 2.6 Coefficient conditioning

Need

\[
|c_7|,\ |c_7^{-1}|,\ |\alpha_9|,\ |\alpha_9^{-1}|,\ |\beta_9|
\le Q^A
\]

on the chart-good set.

## 2.7 Vector amplitude Schur, if needed

If a finite coherent vector amplitude model exists,

\[
\mathcal C_{2Y}^{\arith}\neq0,
\]

need

\[
\operatorname{dist}_{L^2(\nu)}
(F_{2Y},\mathcal C_{2Y}^{\arith})^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\|F_{2Y}\|^2.
\]

If \(\mathcal C_{2Y}^{\arith}=0/\text{fixed}\), this layer disappears.

## 2.8 JointFlatnessStability

Need

\[
(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})\approx0
\Longrightarrow
q''_{\can}\approx0
\]

in the relevant local norm, so Gate–1 excludes rank-two wall failure.

## 2.9 O2/O3 with vector wall

Use

\[
W_{\bd}^{(2)}
=
N_{\sm}|H|^2
\bigl(|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2\bigr).
\]

The branch bandwidth is

\[
\boxed{
d_0^{(2)}
=
d_N+2d_H+2\max(d_{\Gamma_G},d_{\Gamma_{G,2}}).
}
\]

If exact degrees remain unknown, keep the conservative requirement

\[
\mathbb C_{d_{\max}}[y]\subset V_{\tr},
\qquad
d_{\max}=14.
\]

---

# 3. Why rank two is preferable

If \(B_Y=0\), rank two bypasses:

\[
\rho_Y\text{ low-order audit},
\]

\[
\rho_\Gamma\text{ WallHonesty},
\]

\[
\text{moving-line finite type},
\]

\[
\text{forced ODE trap},
\]

\[
\mathrm{TrapSchur}_J.
\]

The scalar rank-one wall

\[
\Gamma_{G,2}+\rho_\Gamma\Gamma_G
\]

is replaced by the vector wall

\[
(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}).
\]

This is structurally stronger.

---

# 4. Rank-two joint wall and Gate–1

Under the current normalization,

\[
\Gamma_G=-6q''+36q-12q^3,
\]

so

\[
\Gamma_G=0
\iff
q''=6q-2q^3=:f(q).
\]

Also,

\[
\Gamma_{G,2}=q^{(4)}-6(1-q^2)q''
=q^{(4)}-f'(q)q''.
\]

Let

\[
E_1=q''-f(q),
\qquad
E_2=q^{(4)}-f'(q)q''.
\]

Then

\[
E_2-E_1''
=
f''(q)(q')^2.
\]

Since

\[
f''(q)=-12q,
\]

joint flatness implies

\[
q(q')^2\approx0.
\]

Together with

\[
q''=f(q)+E_1,
\]

small \(E_1,E_2\) should force \(q''\) small in \(L^2\), after treating the \(q\approx0\) and \(|q|\gtrsim Q^{-C}\) regions separately.

Thus the needed stability lemma is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{JointFlatnessStability}:
\quad
\|\Gamma_G\|_{C^2(I)}+\|\Gamma_{G,2}\|_{C^0(I)}
\le\varepsilon
\Rightarrow
\|q''\|_{L^2(I)}
\le Q^C\varepsilon^\theta |I|^{1/2}.
}
\]

Then Gate–1 gives the rank-two wall lower.

---

# 5. Rank-one fallback

If \(B_Y=\mathbf C(1,\rho_Y)\), resume the rank-one tower:

1. prove \(\rho_Y\) is formal and low-order;
2. prove

\[
\rho_\Gamma=\frac{\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7}{\alpha_9}\in\mathcal A_{\low};
\]

3. prove moving-wall finite type for

\[
\Gamma_{G,2}+\rho_\Gamma\Gamma_G;
\]

4. if finite type fails, use the forced ODE / TrapSchur hierarchy.

This fallback should be activated only if an actual \(s_7\)-tangent is found.

---

The next work should focus on proving or breaking

\[
B_Y=0.
\]

Recommended six tasks:

1. **TwoGradeLiveQuotient:** construct \(\mathcal Q_{2Y}\), prove \(e_7,e_Y\) survive independently.

2. **CanonicalImageAvoidsLiveFiber:** prove EF/gamma/chart/tail actual-source tangents and curvatures have zero \((s_7,s_9^Y)\)-fiber image.

3. **NoLocalCompletionFiberFreedom:** classify \(E_2^Y\), prove no local live-fiber derivatives.

4. **TypedCommutator\(_{2Y}\):** prove labelled/raw mismatch has no local live-fiber component.

5. **JointFlatnessStability:** prove joint rank-two wall failure implies \(q''\) small.

6. **Rank-two O2/O3:** formulate O2/O3 with vector wall and conservative \(d_{\max}\).

---

The preferred branch is now:

\[
\boxed{
B_Y=0
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
\text{rank-two vector wall}.
}
\]

Only if an actual \(s_7\)-motion appears should we return to the rank-one moving wall.

## Agent 6 continued attack — proof skeleton for \(\mathrm{JointFlatnessStability}\)

Assume the rank-two branch:

\[
B_Y=0.
\]

Then the D4 wall is vector-valued:

\[
F_{2Y}
=
H
\begin{pmatrix}
c_7\Gamma_G\\
\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}
\end{pmatrix}
\asymp_{Q^A}
H(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}).
\]

The current rank-two route needs the stability lemma:

\[
\boxed{
(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})\approx0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
q''_{\can}\approx0.
}
\]

This would let Gate–1 rule out rank-two wall failure. This is exactly why \(B_Y=0\) is such a valuable branch: the scalar moving-wall/ODE-trap layer disappears. 

---

# 1. Normalize the wall equations

Use the current rank-two normalization:

\[
\Gamma_G=-6q''+36q-12q^3.
\]

Define

\[
f(q):=6q-2q^3.
\]

Then

\[
\Gamma_G=-6(q''-f(q)).
\]

So set

\[
E_1:=q''-f(q).
\]

Then

\[
\Gamma_G=-6E_1.
\]

Also

\[
\Gamma_{G,2}=q^{(4)}-6(1-q^2)q''.
\]

But

\[
f'(q)=6-6q^2=6(1-q^2),
\]

so define

\[
E_2:=q^{(4)}-f'(q)q''.
\]

Then

\[
\Gamma_{G,2}=E_2.
\]

Thus joint wall smallness is exactly

\[
E_1\approx0,\qquad E_2\approx0.
\]

---

# 2. Exact identity

Compute

\[
E_1''
=
q^{(4)}-(f(q))''.
\]

Since

\[
(f(q))''
=
f''(q)(q')^2+f'(q)q'',
\]

we get

\[
E_1''
=
q^{(4)}-f'(q)q''-f''(q)(q')^2.
\]

Therefore

\[
E_2-E_1''
=
f''(q)(q')^2.
\]

Now

\[
f(q)=6q-2q^3,
\]

so

\[
f''(q)=-12q.
\]

Hence

\[
\boxed{
E_2-E_1''
=
-12q(q')^2.
}
\]

This is the key rank-two identity.

---

# 3. Exact flatness

If

\[
E_1=0,\qquad E_2=0
\]

on an interval, then

\[
q(q')^2=0.
\]

On any open subinterval where \(q\neq0\),

\[
q'=0.
\]

Then

\[
q''=0.
\]

But

\[
E_1=0
\]

also gives

\[
q''=6q-2q^3.
\]

So

\[
6q-2q^3=0,
\]

hence

\[
q\in\{0,\sqrt3,-\sqrt3\}.
\]

Thus exact joint flatness forces the constant special states

\[
\boxed{
q\equiv0,\quad q\equiv\sqrt3,\quad q\equiv-\sqrt3,
}
\]

and in every case

\[
q''\equiv0.
\]

So exact rank-two wall failure contradicts Gate–1 source energy.

---

# 4. Approximate stability lemma

The needed analytic lemma is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{JointFlatnessStability}.
}
\]

A usable form is:

Let \(I\) be a chart-good interval. Assume polynomial a priori bounds

\[
\|q\|_{C^K(I)}\le Q^C
\]

for fixed \(K\), and

\[
\|E_1\|_{C^2(I)}+\|E_2\|_{C^0(I)}
\le \varepsilon.
\]

Then for some fixed \(\theta>0\),

\[
\boxed{
\|q''\|_{L^2(I)}
\le
Q^C\varepsilon^\theta |I|^{1/2}.
}
\]

This is exactly the bridge from rank-two wall smallness to Gate–1 contradiction.

---

# 5. Proof mechanism for the approximate lemma

From the identity,

\[
|q(q')^2|
\le
C\varepsilon.
\]

Split \(I\) into two regions.

## Region A: \(|q|\le\eta\)

There,

\[
|f(q)|
=
|6q-2q^3|
\le C\eta.
\]

Since

\[
q''=f(q)+E_1,
\]

we get

\[
|q''|\le C\eta+\varepsilon.
\]

So

\[
\|q''\|_{L^2(\{|q|\le\eta\})}
\le
(C\eta+\varepsilon)|I|^{1/2}.
\]

## Region B: \(|q|\ge\eta\)

Then

\[
(q')^2
\le
C\varepsilon/\eta,
\]

so

\[
|q'|\le C(\varepsilon/\eta)^{1/2}.
\]

Thus \(q\) varies only slowly on this region.

Now if

\[
|f(q)|\ge \tau,
\]

then

\[
|q''|
=
|f(q)+E_1|
\ge \tau/2
\]

provided \(\varepsilon\ll\tau\). But if \(q''\) has magnitude \(\gtrsim\tau\) over a component of length \(L\), then \(q'\) changes by \(\gtrsim \tau L\), unless there are rapid sign oscillations. Under the chart-good finite-type or \(C^K\)-boundedness hypothesis, such oscillations have polynomially bounded complexity, so each such component has length

\[
L\le Q^C\frac{(\varepsilon/\eta)^{1/2}}{\tau}.
\]

Hence the region where both

\[
|q|\ge\eta
\]

and

\[
|f(q)|\ge\tau
\]

has small total measure.

On the remaining region,

\[
|f(q)|\le\tau,
\]

and therefore

\[
|q''|\le \tau+\varepsilon.
\]

Choosing, for example,

\[
\eta=\varepsilon^{a},\qquad \tau=\varepsilon^{b}
\]

with small fixed exponents gives

\[
\|q''\|_{L^2(I)}
\le Q^C\varepsilon^\theta |I|^{1/2}.
\]

The exact exponent is not important; polynomial decay is enough.

---

# 6. What technical input remains

The only nontrivial analytic input in that proof is the control of oscillatory components where

\[
|q|\ge\eta,\qquad |f(q)|\ge\tau.
\]

We need one of:

## Option 1: finite-type control

A finite-type bound on

\[
q,\quad q',\quad f(q)
\]

on each chart.

## Option 2: bounded zero complexity

A bound that \(q'\), \(f(q)\), or \(q''\) has at most \(Q^C\) sign changes on a chart.

## Option 3: local Sobolev/Markov inequality

A Markov-type inequality in the finite carrier/source approximation space, converting \(C^K\) bounds and small \(q'\) into measure control for the high-\(q''\) region.

Any of these is sufficient.

This is much more manageable than the rank-one forced ODE branch.

---

# 7. Jet-enhanced wall formulation

The stability lemma uses

\[
E_1''.
\]

Since

\[
E_1=-\Gamma_G/6,
\]

one convenient wall energy is a jet-enhanced rank-two wall:

\[
\boxed{
E_{\wall}^{(2),\jet}
=
|H|^2
\left(
|\Gamma_G|^2
+
|\partial_m\Gamma_G|^2
+
|\partial_m^2\Gamma_G|^2
+
|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\right).
}
\]

If this wall is small, then

\[
\|E_1\|_{C^2}
\]

and

\[
\|E_2\|_{C^0}
\]

are small after local Sobolev/Markov conversion.

Then JointFlatnessStability applies.

This suggests a strengthened rank-two D4 wall should possibly include the \(2\)-jet of \(\Gamma_G\), not only \(\Gamma_G\) itself.

---

# 8. Does D4 supply \(\partial_m\Gamma_G\) and \(\partial_m^2\Gamma_G\)?

This is the next finite question.

The rank-two local D4 output directly sees

\[
(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}).
\]

It may not directly see

\[
\partial_m\Gamma_G,\quad \partial_m^2\Gamma_G.
\]

But the derivatives can be supplied analytically if:

1. \(\Gamma_G\) is controlled in a finite-dimensional local source space;
2. local Markov inequalities give \(C^2\) control from \(L^2\)-smallness over enough neighboring atoms;
3. wall extraction is block-based rather than pointwise.

So a practical route is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{use block \(L^2\) smallness of }\Gamma_G
\text{ to infer derivative smallness by finite-dimensionality.}
}
\]

This converts the rank-two pointwise wall into a block wall.

Again, this is acceptable and still much simpler than rank-one.

---

# 9. Rank-two closure theorem

The rank-two wall closure can be stated as:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{RankTwoGateClosure}.
}
\]

On each good block \(\Omega\), if

\[
\|H\Gamma_G\|_{L^2(\Omega)}
+
\|H\Gamma_{G,2}\|_{L^2(\Omega)}
\]

is too small, then by finite-dimensional Markov/Sobolev control,

\[
\|\Gamma_G\|_{C^2(\Omega')}
+
\|\Gamma_{G,2}\|_{C^0(\Omega')}
\]

is small on a subblock \(\Omega'\), hence

\[
\|q''\|_{L^2(\Omega')}\le Q^{-A}.
\]

This contradicts Gate–1 on the same block.

Therefore rank-two wall mass follows from Gate–1.

---

# 10. Current conclusion

The rank-two branch can likely close the wall layer by:

\[
\boxed{
\text{JointFlatnessStability}
+
\text{finite-dimensional Markov/Sobolev control}.
}
\]

The exact identity

\[
E_2-E_1''=-12q(q')^2
\]

is the key.

This makes rank-two dramatically preferable. The remaining local algebraic theorem

\[
B_Y=0
\]

is now the true bottleneck. If \(B_Y=0\) closes, the wall layer looks tractable.

## Agent 6 continued attack — rank-two wall lower as a coercivity theorem

The rank-two branch reduces the wall layer to the following coercivity problem:

\[
\boxed{
\|q''\|_{L^2(\Omega)}
\lesssim_{Q^C}
\|\Gamma_G\|_{\mathcal X(\Omega)}
+
\|\Gamma_{G,2}\|_{\mathcal Y(\Omega)}
}
\]

on each routed block \(\Omega\), for suitable local norms \(\mathcal X,\mathcal Y\). If this coercivity holds, then Gate–1 source energy forces rank-two wall mass.

This is better than a sublevel theorem. It is a deterministic local estimate.

---

# 1. Normalize the rank-two wall variables

Use

\[
f(q)=6q-2q^3.
\]

Define

\[
E_1:=q''-f(q),
\]

\[
E_2:=q^{(4)}-f'(q)q''.
\]

Then

\[
\Gamma_G=-6E_1,
\]

\[
\Gamma_{G,2}=E_2.
\]

The key identity is

\[
\boxed{
E_2-E_1''=f''(q)(q')^2=-12q(q')^2.
}
\]

So joint wall smallness implies

\[
q(q')^2\approx0.
\]

Together with

\[
q''=f(q)+E_1,
\]

this forces \(q''\) small, unless \(q\) lives near one of the special constant equilibria.

---

# 2. Coercivity statement to target

A clean local theorem would be:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{RankTwoCoercivity}.
}
\]

Let \(I\) be a chart-good interval. Suppose \(q\) has polynomial \(C^K\)-bounds on \(I\). Then

\[
\boxed{
\|q''\|_{L^2(I)}
\le
Q^C
\left(
\|E_1\|_{H^2(I)}
+
\|E_2\|_{L^2(I)}
\right)^\theta
|I|^{1/2-\theta'}
}
\]

for fixed \(\theta,\theta'>0\), or more simply with normalized local scales,

\[
\boxed{
\|q''\|_{L^2(I)}
\le
Q^C
\left(
\|E_1\|_{H^2(I)}
+
\|E_2\|_{L^2(I)}
\right)^\theta .
}
\]

Since

\[
E_1=-\Gamma_G/6,\qquad E_2=\Gamma_{G,2},
\]

this becomes

\[
\boxed{
\|q''\|_{L^2(I)}
\le
Q^C
\left(
\|\Gamma_G\|_{H^2(I)}
+
\|\Gamma_{G,2}\|_{L^2(I)}
\right)^\theta .
}
\]

This is the exact bridge from rank-two wall to Gate–1.

---

# 3. Proof skeleton in \(L^2\) form

From

\[
E_2-E_1''=-12q(q')^2,
\]

we get

\[
\|q(q')^2\|_{L^2(I)}
\le
C\left(
\|E_2\|_{L^2(I)}
+
\|E_1\|_{H^2(I)}
\right).
\]

Let

\[
\delta:=\|E_2\|_{L^2(I)}+\|E_1\|_{H^2(I)}.
\]

Then

\[
\|q(q')^2\|_{L^2(I)}\le C\delta.
\]

Split \(I\) into

\[
I_0=\{|q|\le \eta\},
\qquad
I_1=\{|q|>\eta\}.
\]

On \(I_0\),

\[
|f(q)|\le C\eta.
\]

Thus

\[
|q''|
\le |f(q)|+|E_1|
\le C\eta+|E_1|.
\]

So

\[
\|q''\|_{L^2(I_0)}
\le C\eta |I|^{1/2}+\|E_1\|_{L^2(I)}.
\]

On \(I_1\),

\[
|q|>\eta
\]

gives

\[
\|(q')^2\|_{L^2(I_1)}
\le C\eta^{-1}\delta.
\]

Hence

\[
\|q'\|_{L^4(I_1)}^2
\le C\eta^{-1}\delta.
\]

So \(q'\) is small in \(L^4\) on the region where \(q\) is not small.

Now use

\[
q''=f(q)+E_1.
\]

If \(f(q)\) were large on a substantial part of \(I_1\), then \(q''\) would be large while \(q'\) is \(L^4\)-small. This is only possible over short intervals, because large acceleration changes velocity. This is where a one-dimensional interpolation lemma enters.

---

# 4. Needed interpolation lemma

The missing analytic lemma is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{AccelerationVelocityLemma}.
}
\]

Let \(q\) have polynomial \(C^3\)-bounds on \(I\). If

\[
\|q'\|_{L^4(I)}\le \epsilon
\]

and

\[
q''=f(q)+e
\]

with

\[
\|e\|_{L^2(I)}\le \epsilon,
\]

then

\[
\|f(q)\|_{L^2(I)}
\le
Q^C\epsilon^\theta.
\]

Since

\[
q''=f(q)+E_1,
\]

this yields

\[
\|q''\|_{L^2(I)}
\le
Q^C(\epsilon^\theta+\|E_1\|_{L^2}).
\]

This closes the coercivity estimate.

Intuition: if \(f(q)\) is nontrivial on a thick set, \(q''\) forces \(q'\) to accumulate variation. Small \(q'\) in \(L^4\) prevents this, except on a small set controlled by \(C^3\)-bounds.

---

# 5. Avoiding pointwise \(C^2\) wall assumptions

Earlier I stated a strong condition:

\[
\|E_1\|_{C^2}+\|E_2\|_{C^0}\le\varepsilon.
\]

But for the proof architecture, an \(L^2/H^2\) version is better:

\[
\boxed{
\|\Gamma_G\|_{H^2(I)}
+
\|\Gamma_{G,2}\|_{L^2(I)}
\text{ small}
\Rightarrow
\|q''\|_{L^2(I)}
\text{ small}.
}
\]

This aligns with block energy methods and avoids needing pointwise wall extraction.

The price is that the rank-two wall energy should be slightly jet-enhanced:

\[
\boxed{
E_{\wall}^{(2),\jet}
=
|H|^2
\left(
|\Gamma_G|^2
+
|\partial_m\Gamma_G|^2
+
|\partial_m^2\Gamma_G|^2
+
|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\right).
}
\]

Then block \(L^2\) smallness of \(E_{\wall}^{(2),\jet}\) implies the \(H^2/L^2\) smallness needed for coercivity.

---

# 6. Does D4 provide the jet-enhanced wall?

The local rank-two D4 directly gives

\[
(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}).
\]

It does not automatically give

\[
\partial_m\Gamma_G,\quad \partial_m^2\Gamma_G.
\]

But we may not need D4 to provide them as separate carriers. If \(\Gamma_G\) lies in a finite-dimensional chart-local source class, then Markov/Sobolev inequalities give

\[
\|\partial_m^j\Gamma_G\|_{L^2(I)}
\le
Q^C
\|\Gamma_G\|_{L^2(I^*)}
\]

on a slightly enlarged block \(I^*\), for \(j\le2\).

Thus

\[
\|\Gamma_G\|_{H^2(I)}
\le
Q^C\|\Gamma_G\|_{L^2(I^*)}.
\]

If such a Markov inequality holds, the non-jet vector wall

\[
|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\]

is enough at block scale.

So the required theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{LocalMarkov}_{\Gamma_G}:
\quad
\|\Gamma_G\|_{H^2(I)}
\le Q^C\|\Gamma_G\|_{L^2(I^*)}.
}
\]

This should follow from the same finite carrier/Riesz machinery used elsewhere.

---

# 7. Rank-two wall lower from Gate–1

Assume by contradiction that rank-two wall mass is small on a block:

\[
\|\Gamma_G\|_{L^2(I^*)}
+
\|\Gamma_{G,2}\|_{L^2(I)}
\le \delta.
\]

By LocalMarkov,

\[
\|\Gamma_G\|_{H^2(I)}
+
\|\Gamma_{G,2}\|_{L^2(I)}
\le Q^C\delta.
\]

By RankTwoCoercivity,

\[
\|q''\|_{L^2(I)}
\le Q^C\delta^\theta.
\]

But Gate–1 gives

\[
\|q''\|_{L^2(I)}
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Choosing losses gives contradiction unless

\[
\delta\ge Q^{-A'}.
\]

Thus

\[
\boxed{
\|\Gamma_G\|_{L^2(I^*)}^2+\|\Gamma_{G,2}\|_{L^2(I)}^2
\ge Q^{-A'}
}
\]

on Gate–1-good blocks.

This proves rank-two wall mass from Gate–1.

---

# 8. Consequence for downstream architecture

If \(B_Y=0\), then the wall layer reduces to:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{LocalMarkov}_{\Gamma_G}
+
\mathrm{RankTwoCoercivity}
+
\mathrm{Gate1}.
}
\]

No sublevel theorem is required. No moving-line finite type. No trap-Schur.

This is a large simplification.

---

# 9. What remains to prove for rank-two wall closure

The rank-two route now needs two analytic lemmas:

## 9.1 LocalMarkov for \(\Gamma_G\)

\[
\|\Gamma_G\|_{H^2(I)}
\le Q^C\|\Gamma_G\|_{L^2(I^*)}.
\]

Need to specify the function class containing \(\Gamma_G\). It is built from

\[
q,\quad q'',\quad q^3.
\]

If \(q\) and \(q''\) are in the same finite source chart with polynomial Riesz/Markov constants, this is plausible.

## 9.2 RankTwoCoercivity

\[
\|q''\|_{L^2(I)}
\le
Q^C
\left(
\|\Gamma_G\|_{H^2(I)}
+
\|\Gamma_{G,2}\|_{L^2(I)}
\right)^\theta.
\]

This is mostly ODE/real-variable analysis using the identity

\[
E_2-E_1''=-12q(q')^2.
\]

These are much more concrete than the rank-one wall problem.

---

# 10. Current conclusion

If \(B_Y=0\), the downstream proof can be reorganized as:

\[
\mathrm{RankTwoY12D4}
+
\mathrm{LocalMarkov}_{\Gamma_G}
+
\mathrm{RankTwoCoercivity}
+
\mathrm{Gate1}
\Rightarrow
\text{wall lower}.
\]

Then O2/O3 uses the vector wall weight:

\[
N_{\sm}|H|^2(|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2).
\]

The rank-two route is now the strongest visible path. The decisive remaining algebraic theorem is still

\[
B_Y=0.
\]

But once that is established, the analytic wall layer looks tractable.

\|\Gamma_G\|_{L^2(I^*)}.

\|\Gamma_G\|_{L^2},\|\Gamma_{G,2}\|_{L^2}

+

The main technical concern is whether

\[
\|\Gamma_G\|_{H^2(I)}
\le Q^C\|\Gamma_G\|_{L^2(I^*)}
\]

is valid, because

\[
\Gamma_G=-6q''+36q-12q^3
\]

is nonlinear in \(q\), not just a linear finite EF packet.

---

# 1. Why \(\Gamma_G\) should still satisfy a Markov inequality

Although \(\Gamma_G\) contains \(q^3\), it is still a finite-complexity expression in the local source chart.

If \(q\) lies in a finite-dimensional local chart space

\[
\mathcal V_I
\]

with polynomial Riesz/Markov constants, then

\[
q,\quad q'',\quad q^3
\]

belong to a controlled algebra generated by \(\mathcal V_I\).

The relevant function class is not \(\mathcal V_I\) itself but

\[
\mathcal A_I
=
\operatorname{span}
\left\{
q'',\ q,\ q^3
:
q\in \mathcal V_I
\right\}
\]

or, more accurately, the finite algebra closure of the actual one-dimensional source family inside the local chart.

Since the expression is polynomial of fixed degree \(3\), its derivative complexity remains polynomial.

So the target is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{AlgebraMarkov}_{\Gamma_G}:
\quad
\|\Gamma_G\|_{H^2(I)}
\le Q^C\|\Gamma_G\|_{L^2(I^*)}.
}
\]

This is a finite-dimensional algebra Markov inequality.

---

# 2. Sufficient local chart assumptions

A clean sufficient package is:

## Finite-dimensional Riesz chart

There is a local basis

\[
\varphi_1,\ldots,\varphi_N
\]

with

\[
N\le Q^C
\]

such that \(q\) is represented in this chart with polynomial coefficient norm.

## Markov control

For every

\[
v\in\mathcal V_I,
\]

\[
\|D^jv\|_{L^2(I)}
\le Q^C\|v\|_{L^2(I^*)},
\qquad
0\le j\le 4.
\]

## Algebra closure

Products of at most three chart functions satisfy

\[
\|D^j(v_1v_2v_3)\|_{L^2(I)}
\le Q^C
\prod_{k=1}^3\|v_k\|_{\mathcal V(I^*)}
\]

and, more importantly, the actual set of possible \(\Gamma_G\) values lies in a finite-dimensional space

\[
\mathcal W_I
\]

with

\[
\dim\mathcal W_I\le Q^C
\]

and Markov constants \(Q^C\).

Then

\[
\Gamma_G\in\mathcal W_I
\]

and LocalMarkov follows.

---

# 3. The possible problem: small \(L^2\) of \(\Gamma_G\) does not control \(q\)

One cannot prove

\[
\|\Gamma_G\|_{H^2}\le Q^C\|\Gamma_G\|_{L^2}
\]

by bounding \(q,q'',q^3\) separately, because cancellations inside

\[
-6q''+36q-12q^3
\]

may make \(\Gamma_G\) small while \(q\) is large.

But Markov inequality is about the function \(\Gamma_G\) itself, not about its components.

Thus we need \(\Gamma_G\) to belong to a finite-dimensional family with polynomial derivative control **as a function**. We do not need invertibility of the expression.

This is why \(\mathcal W_I\) is the right object.

---

# 4. Constructing \(\mathcal W_I\)

If \(q\) lies in an \(N\)-dimensional local source space

\[
\mathcal V_I,
\]

then \(q^3\) lies in the degree-three symmetric product space

\[
\mathrm{Sym}^3\mathcal V_I,
\]

whose dimension is

\[
\binom{N+2}{3}.
\]

If \(N=O(1)\) or \(N\le R^C\), this remains polynomial:

\[
\dim\mathrm{Sym}^3\mathcal V_I\le Q^C.
\]

Then

\[
\Gamma_G\in
D^2\mathcal V_I+\mathcal V_I+\mathrm{Sym}^3\mathcal V_I
=:\mathcal W_I.
\]

If \(\mathcal V_I\) has polynomial Markov constants and the product basis is controlled, \(\mathcal W_I\) also has polynomial Markov constants.

This gives

\[
\|\Gamma_G\|_{H^2(I)}
\le Q^C\|\Gamma_G\|_{L^2(I^*)}.
\]

So the local Markov theorem reduces to:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal V_I\text{ has polynomial-dimensional algebra closure through degree }3.
}
\]

This is plausible because all degrees are fixed.

---

# 5. If \(q\) is not in a finite-dimensional chart

If \(q\) is only approximated by a finite EF packet plus residual,

\[
q=q_X+r_X,
\]

then

\[
\Gamma_G(q)
=
\Gamma_G(q_X)
+
\mathrm{linear/quadratic/cubic\ residual\ terms}.
\]

Then use:

\[
\Gamma_G(q_X)\in\mathcal W_I
\]

and control the residual terms separately.

The required estimate becomes:

\[
\|\Gamma_G(q)\|_{H^2(I)}
\le
Q^C\|\Gamma_G(q)\|_{L^2(I^*)}
+
\mathrm{Residual}_{\Gamma_G}.
\]

If the residual is below the Gate–1 scale, the argument survives.

Thus the rank-two stack also needs a mild completion estimate for the nonlinear wall class, but only through \(\Gamma_G\), not a full \(K_9\)-type singular residual.

---

# 6. RankTwoCoercivity with \(H^2/L^2\) input

Assuming LocalMarkov, Gate–1 closure is:

If the plain wall is small:

\[
\|\Gamma_G\|_{L^2(I^*)}
+
\|\Gamma_{G,2}\|_{L^2(I)}
\le \delta,
\]

then

\[
\|\Gamma_G\|_{H^2(I)}
+
\|\Gamma_{G,2}\|_{L^2(I)}
\le Q^C\delta.
\]

Then RankTwoCoercivity gives

\[
\|q''\|_{L^2(I)}
\le Q^C\delta^\theta.
\]

Gate–1 says

\[
\|q''\|_{L^2(I)}
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Therefore

\[
\delta\ge Q^{-A'}.
\]

So

\[
\boxed{
\|\Gamma_G\|_{L^2(I^*)}^2+
\|\Gamma_{G,2}\|_{L^2(I)}^2
\ge Q^{-A'}.
}
\]

This proves wall mass on Gate–1-good blocks.

---

# 7. O2/O3 compatibility of block enlargement

The Markov inequality uses an enlarged interval

\[
I^*.
\]

This affects extraction but not seriously:

- use overlapping blocks \(I^*\) with bounded overlap;
- prove wall lower on smaller subblocks \(I\);
- O2/O3 smooth weights can be assigned to \(I^*\) and transferred to \(I\) at polynomial loss.

This is standard in local energy arguments.

Thus LocalMarkov does not break the O2/O3 bounded-charge setup.

---

# 8. Jet wall fallback revisited

If AlgebraMarkov is hard, use jet wall:

\[
W_{\bd}^{(2),\jet}
=
N_{\sm}|H|^2
\left(
|\Gamma_G|^2
+
|D\Gamma_G|^2
+
|D^2\Gamma_G|^2
+
|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\right).
\]

This removes the need for LocalMarkov but increases the wall factor complexity.

Because only two derivatives are added, the branch-charge increase is fixed. So O2/O3 still remains bounded-charge, likely with a slightly larger

\[
d_{\max}.
\]

Thus there is no catastrophic fallback.

---

# 9. Recommended analytic theorem hierarchy

The rank-two analytic package should be:

## Primary

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{AlgebraMarkov}_{\Gamma_G}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{RankTwoCoercivity}
}
\]

These prove rank-two wall lower from plain wall plus Gate–1.

## Fallback

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{JetRankTwoWall}
}
\]

Use the jet-enhanced wall directly in O2/O3.

---

# 10. Current conclusion

The plain vector wall is still the preferred rank-two O2/O3 input.

The only new theorem needed is a finite-dimensional algebra Markov inequality for

\[
\Gamma_G=-6q''+36q-12q^3.
\]

This is plausible because \(\Gamma_G\) lies in a fixed-degree algebra closure of the local source chart.

So the rank-two branch remains strong:

\[
B_Y=0
\Rightarrow
\text{plain vector wall}
+
\mathrm{AlgebraMarkov}_{\Gamma_G}
+
\mathrm{RankTwoCoercivity}
+
\mathrm{Gate1}.
\]

# 1. Restate the normalized variables

Use

\[
f(q)=6q-2q^3.
\]

Define

\[
E_1=q''-f(q),
\]

\[

Then

\[
\Gamma_G=-6E_1,
\]

and

\[
\Gamma_{G,2}=E_2.

The exact identity is

\[
E_2-E_1''
=
f''(q)(q')^2
=
-12q(q')^2.
\]

So if \(E_1\) is small in \(H^2\) and \(E_2\) is small in \(L^2\), then

\[
q(q')^2
\]

is small in \(L^2\).

---

# 2. A sharper coercivity target

The earlier target was

\[
\|q''\|_{L^2(I)}
\le
Q^C
\left(
\|E_1\|_{H^2(I)}
+
\|E_2\|_{L^2(I)}
\right)^\theta.
\]

This is slightly too optimistic without excluding very short intervals or allowing scale normalization. The safer block-normalized theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\|q''\|_{L^2(I)}
\le
Q^C
\left(
\|E_1\|_{H^2(I)}
+
\|E_2\|_{L^2(I)}
\right)^\theta
+
Q^C |I|^{-2}\|E_1\|_{L^2(I)}.
}
\]

The last term is harmless after scale-normalization or if \(I\) is a fixed chart-scale interval. It accounts for the fact that \(E_1''\) control in \(H^2\) is scale-sensitive.

In normalized local coordinates, this becomes the clean version:

\[
\boxed{
\|q''\|_{L^2(I)}
\le
Q^C
\left(
\|E_1\|_{H^2(I)}
+
\|E_2\|_{L^2(I)}
\right)^\theta.
}
\]

---

\[
q''=f(q)+E_1

is a perturbed conservative ODE.

\[

This normalization is not quite aligned. Better choose \(U\) with

\[
U'(q)=-f(q)= -6q+2q^3.
\]

Then

\[
U(q)=-3q^2+\frac12q^4.
\]

For the exact equation \(q''=f(q)\),

\[
\frac12(q')^2+U(q)
\]

is conserved up to sign convention:

\[
\frac{d}{dm}
\left(
\frac12(q')^2-U(q)
\right)
=
q'(q''-f(q))
=
q'E_1.
\]

So if \(E_1\) is small and \(q'\) is small on \(|q|>\eta\), then \(q\) cannot cross regions where \(f(q)\) is bounded away from zero without accumulating velocity.

This gives a stronger version of the acceleration/velocity lemma.

---

# 4. The exact obstruction set

The zeros of

\[
f(q)=6q-2q^3=2q(3-q^2)
\]

are

\[
q=0,\quad q=\pm \sqrt3.
\]

If

\[
q''=f(q)+E_1
\]

and \(q''\) is not small, then \(q\) must be away from these three equilibria.

But from

\[
q(q')^2
\]

small, either

\[
q\approx0
\]

or

\[
q'\approx0.
\]

If \(q\) is away from \(0\) and away from \(\pm\sqrt3\), then \(f(q)\) is bounded below. Hence

\[
q''\approx f(q)
\]

forces nontrivial acceleration, which contradicts \(q'\approx0\) on a thick interval.

So the interval decomposes into:

\[
\{|q|\le \eta\},
\]

\[
\{|q^2-3|\le \tau\},
\]

and a remaining region where

\[
|q|\ge\eta,\qquad |q^2-3|\ge\tau.
\]

On the first two regions,

\[
|f(q)|
\le
C(\eta+\tau).
\]

Therefore

\[
|q''|\le C(\eta+\tau)+|E_1|.
\]

On the remaining region,

\[
|f(q)|\ge c\eta\tau.
\]

But \(q'\) is small there, because \(|q|\ge\eta\) and \(q(q')^2\) is small. This region cannot have large measure unless the \(C^K\) bounds allow extremely oscillatory acceleration. Chart-good finite-dimensionality rules that out.

This gives the coercivity estimate.

---

# 5. Finite-dimensional substitute for the hard real-variable step

The remaining technical step is:

\[
|q|\ge\eta,\quad |q^2-3|\ge\tau
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\text{region has small measure if }q'\text{ is small and }q''\approx f(q).
\]

A clean way to prove this is a finite-dimensional Remez/Markov lemma.

Let

\[
\mathcal V_I
\]

be the local chart class containing \(q\). Then \(q'\), \(q''\), and \(f(q)\) lie in a finite algebraic closure

\[
\mathcal W_I
=
\mathcal V_I
+
D\mathcal V_I
+
D^2\mathcal V_I
+
\mathrm{Sym}^3\mathcal V_I.
\]

Assume \(\mathcal W_I\) has polynomial Remez constants:

If \(w\in\mathcal W_I\) satisfies

\[
|w|\le \epsilon
\]

on a set of relative measure \(1-\delta\), then

\[
\|w\|_{L^2(I)}
\le
Q^C(\epsilon+\delta^\kappa\|w\|_{L^2(I)}).
\]

Apply this to

\[
w=f(q).
\]

On most of the region where \(q'\) is small and \(q''=f(q)+E_1\), acceleration cannot persist. Remez/Markov converts this into

\[
\|f(q)\|_{L^2(I)}
\le Q^C\delta^\theta.
\]

Then

\[
\|q''\|_{L^2}
\le
\|f(q)\|_{L^2}+\|E_1\|_{L^2}
\]

is small.

This is the right proof technology: finite-dimensional Remez rather than delicate pointwise ODE topology.

---

# 6. Final analytic package for rank-two wall

The analytic package should be:

## 6.1 AlgebraMarkov

\[
\boxed{
\|\Gamma_G\|_{H^2(I)}
\le
Q^C\|\Gamma_G\|_{L^2(I^*)}.
}
\]

This gives the derivative control needed for \(E_1''\).

## 6.2 AlgebraRemez

For the local algebra generated by \(q,q',q'',q^3\), smallness on large sets propagates with polynomial loss.

## 6.3 RankTwoCoercivity

\[
\boxed{
\|q''\|_{L^2(I)}
\le
Q^C
\left(
\|\Gamma_G\|_{H^2(I)}
+
\|\Gamma_{G,2}\|_{L^2(I)}
\right)^\theta.
}
\]

## 6.4 Gate–1 contradiction

Gate–1 gives

\[
\|q''\|_{L^2(I)}\ge Q^{-A}
\]

on good blocks, so rank-two wall cannot be small.

---

# 7. Why this is better than jet wall by default

If AlgebraMarkov holds, the plain wall

\[
|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\]

is enough.

This keeps O2/O3 simpler:

\[
d_0^{(2)}
=
d_N+2d_H+2\max(d_{\Gamma_G},d_{\Gamma_{G,2}}).
\]

The jet wall should remain a fallback. It is safe but adds derivative wall components to O2/O3.

---

# 8. Rank-two branch after this attack

The rank-two proof stack becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{LiveFiberAvoidance}_{2Y}:B_Y=0
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{RankTwoY12D4}:F_{2Y}\asymp H(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{AlgebraMarkov}+\mathrm{AlgebraRemez}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{RankTwoCoercivity}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{Gate1}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{rank-two wall lower}.
}
\]

This is now a coherent analytic route. The central remaining uncertainty is local algebraic, not wall analytic:

\[
\boxed{
B_Y=0?
}
\]

_Thought for 7s_

I’ll keep the current convention:

\[
\rho_Y=\text{lower }Y12\text{ graph slope},\qquad
\rho_\Gamma=\frac{\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7}{\alpha_9},\qquad
R_{\fold}(x)=\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}.
\]

## Agent 6 continued attack — proving the acceleration/velocity lemma

The rank-two analytic path now rests on:

\[
B_Y=0
\Longrightarrow
F_{2Y}\asymp H(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})
\]

and then

\[
(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})\text{ small}
\Longrightarrow
q''_{\can}\text{ small}.
\]

The previous attack isolated the remaining real-variable gap: if

\[
q(q')^2\approx 0
\]

and

\[
q''\approx f(q),\qquad f(q)=6q-2q^3,
\]

then \(q''\) should be small. I now push that lemma harder. 

---

# 1. Restate the core identities

Define

\[
E_1=q''-f(q),
\qquad
f(q)=6q-2q^3,
\]

and

\[
E_2=q^{(4)}-f'(q)q''.
\]

Then

\[
\Gamma_G=-6E_1,
\qquad
\Gamma_{G,2}=E_2.
\]

The exact identity is

\[
\boxed{
E_2-E_1''=-12q(q')^2.
}
\]

Thus, if the rank-two wall is small in the needed norms,

\[
\|E_1\|_{H^2}+\|E_2\|_{L^2}\le \delta,
\]

then

\[
\boxed{
\|q(q')^2\|_{L^2}\le C\delta.
}
\]

The target is

\[
\boxed{
\|q''\|_{L^2}\le Q^C\delta^\theta.
}
\]

---

# 2. Split by equilibrium neighborhoods

The zeros of

\[
f(q)=2q(3-q^2)
\]

are

\[
0,\quad \sqrt3,\quad -\sqrt3.
\]

Choose small parameters

\[
\eta,\tau>0.
\]

Decompose \(I\) into:

\[
I_0=\{|q|\le \eta\},
\]

\[
I_+=\{|q-\sqrt3|\le \tau\},
\]

\[
I_-=\{|q+\sqrt3|\le \tau\},
\]

and the remaining region

\[
I_{\mathrm{away}}
=
I\setminus(I_0\cup I_+\cup I_-).
\]

On

\[
I_0\cup I_+\cup I_-,
\]

we have

\[
|f(q)|\le C(\eta+\tau).
\]

Since

\[
q''=f(q)+E_1,
\]

\[
\|q''\|_{L^2(I_0\cup I_+\cup I_-)}
\le
C(\eta+\tau)|I|^{1/2}
+
\|E_1\|_{L^2(I)}.
\]

So it remains to control \(I_{\mathrm{away}}\).

---

# 3. On the away region, \(q'\) is small

On \(I_{\mathrm{away}}\), in particular \(|q|\ge \eta\). Hence

\[
|q'|^2
\le
\eta^{-1}|q(q')^2|.
\]

In \(L^2\)-integrated form:

\[
\|(q')^2\|_{L^2(I_{\mathrm{away}})}
\le
\eta^{-1}\|q(q')^2\|_{L^2(I)}
\le
C\eta^{-1}\delta.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\boxed{
\|q'\|_{L^4(I_{\mathrm{away}})}
\le
C\eta^{-1/2}\delta^{1/2}.
}
\]

On \(I_{\mathrm{away}}\), also

\[
|f(q)|\ge c\eta\tau,
\]

because \(q\) is away from all three simple roots of \(f\).

Thus if \(I_{\mathrm{away}}\) had large measure, then

\[
q''=f(q)+E_1
\]

would have persistent nontrivial acceleration while \(q'\) is \(L^4\)-small. That is the contradiction mechanism.

---

# 4. A direct interval argument

Let \(J\) be a connected component of \(I_{\mathrm{away}}\) where

\[
|E_1|\le \frac12 |f(q)|.
\]

Then

\[
|q''|\ge c\eta\tau
\]

on \(J\), and \(q''\) has a fixed sign if \(f(q)\) has fixed sign. Since \(f\) has no zero on \(I_{\mathrm{away}}\), \(f(q)\) is sign-stable on each connected component of \(I_{\mathrm{away}}\).

Therefore \(q'\) changes by at least

\[
c\eta\tau |J|
\]

across \(J\), up to endpoint orientation.

But the \(L^4\)-smallness of \(q'\) bounds the possible variation. Specifically, if \(q'\) is monotone with slope at least \(a=c\eta\tau\), then

\[
\int_J |q'|^4
\gtrsim
a^4 |J|^5
\]

unless \(q'\) starts and ends with large cancellation. A standard convexity estimate gives

\[
\boxed{
|J|
\le
C
a^{-4/5}
\|q'\|_{L^4(J)}^{4/5}.
}
\]

Summing over components and using a bounded-component-count or finite-type/Remez bound gives

\[
|I_{\mathrm{away}}|
\le
Q^C
(\eta\tau)^{-4/5}
\|q'\|_{L^4(I_{\mathrm{away}})}^{4/5}
+
\text{bad set where }|E_1|>\tfrac12|f(q)|.
\]

The bad set is controlled by

\[
|E_1|>\frac12c\eta\tau
\]

so

\[
|\mathrm{bad}|
\le
C(\eta\tau)^{-2}\|E_1\|_{L^2}^2.
\]

Thus

\[
|I_{\mathrm{away}}|
\le
Q^C
(\eta\tau)^{-4/5}
(\eta^{-1/2}\delta^{1/2})^{4/5}
+
C(\eta\tau)^{-2}\delta^2.
\]

That is

\[
|I_{\mathrm{away}}|
\le
Q^C
\eta^{-6/5}\tau^{-4/5}\delta^{2/5}
+
C\eta^{-2}\tau^{-2}\delta^2.
\]

On \(I_{\mathrm{away}}\), \(q''\) is polynomially bounded by chart-goodness:

\[
|q''|\le Q^C.
\]

Therefore

\[
\|q''\|_{L^2(I_{\mathrm{away}})}
\le
Q^C |I_{\mathrm{away}}|^{1/2}.
\]

Choose

\[
\eta=\tau=\delta^a
\]

with \(a>0\) small. Then

\[
\|q''\|_{L^2(I_{\mathrm{away}})}
\le
Q^C\delta^\theta
\]

for some fixed \(\theta>0\).

Combining with the equilibrium neighborhoods gives

\[
\boxed{
\|q''\|_{L^2(I)}
\le
Q^C\delta^\theta.
}
\]

This is the desired coercivity.

---

# 5. Remaining technical assumption

The direct interval argument used one hidden input:

\[
\boxed{
I_{\mathrm{away}}\text{ has polynomially many connected components.}
}
\]

This can be supplied by any of:

1. finite-dimensional chart/Remez control for \(q\);
2. polynomial zero-count for \(q\), \(q-\sqrt3\), \(q+\sqrt3\);
3. a Markov inequality plus real-analyticity/finite EF packet control.

This is a much weaker requirement than rank-one wall finite type. It concerns only the level sets of \(q\) near three fixed constants.

So the required auxiliary lemma is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{EquilibriumComponentBound}:
\quad
\{|q|>\eta,\ |q^2-3|>\tau\}
\text{ has }Q^C\text{ components on chart blocks.}
}
\]

This should follow from the same local finite-dimensional source machinery used for O2/O3.

---

# 6. Final RankTwoCoercivity theorem

A proof-quality statement is:

Let \(I\) be a chart-good block. Suppose:

\[
\|q\|_{C^4(I)}\le Q^C,
\]

the equilibrium complement

\[
\{|q|>\eta,\ |q^2-3|>\tau\}
\]

has at most \(Q^C\eta^{-C}\tau^{-C}\) connected components, and

\[
\|E_1\|_{H^2(I)}+\|E_2\|_{L^2(I)}\le \delta.
\]

Then there exist constants \(C,\theta>0\) such that

\[
\boxed{
\|q''\|_{L^2(I)}
\le
Q^C\delta^\theta |I|^{1/2}.
}
\]

Since

\[
E_1=-\Gamma_G/6,
\qquad
E_2=\Gamma_{G,2},
\]

this gives

\[
\boxed{
\|q''\|_{L^2(I)}
\le
Q^C
\left(
\|\Gamma_G\|_{H^2(I)}
+
\|\Gamma_{G,2}\|_{L^2(I)}
\right)^\theta.
}
\]

---

# 7. Integration with LocalMarkov

If

\[
\mathrm{LocalMarkov}_{\Gamma_G}
\]

holds:

\[
\|\Gamma_G\|_{H^2(I)}
\le
Q^C\|\Gamma_G\|_{L^2(I^*)},
\]

then

\[
\|q''\|_{L^2(I)}
\le
Q^C
\left(
\|\Gamma_G\|_{L^2(I^*)}
+
\|\Gamma_{G,2}\|_{L^2(I)}
\right)^\theta.
\]

Thus, on a Gate–1-good block where

\[
\|q''\|_{L^2(I)}\ge Q^{-A},
\]

one must have

\[
\|\Gamma_G\|_{L^2(I^*)}
+
\|\Gamma_{G,2}\|_{L^2(I)}
\ge Q^{-A'}.
\]

This is the rank-two wall lower.

---

# 8. Strategic result

The wall layer for rank two is now reduced to two manageable lemmas:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{LocalMarkov}_{\Gamma_G}
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{EquilibriumComponentBound}.
}
\]

The coercive identity

\[
E_2-E_1''=-12q(q')^2
\]

does the main work.

So the rank-two analytic route is now substantially credible. The real bottleneck remains:

\[
\boxed{
B_Y=0.
}
\]

## Agent 6 continued attack — reducing \(B_Y=0\) to two exactness tests

The analytic rank-two wall layer now looks plausible:

\[
(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})\text{ small}
\Rightarrow q''_{\can}\text{ small}
\Rightarrow \text{Gate--1 contradiction}.
\]

So the main remaining bottleneck is local algebra:

\[
\boxed{B_Y=0.}
\]

The latest prompt’s architecture points directly at this: rank two survives if the actual-source/nuisance/local model has no image in the live two-grade fiber \((s_7,s_9^Y)\). 

---

# 1. Split \(B_Y=0\) into two row tests

Recall

\[
B_Y
=
\Pi_{\fib,Y}
\left[
\mathcal N_{\sing}
+
T\mathcal M_Y^{\arith}
+
\mathcal T_{E_2^Y}^{\local}
+
\mathcal T_{\lab,Y}^{\local}
\right]
\subset \mathbf C^2_{(s_7,s_9^Y)}.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
B_Y=0
\]

means both rows vanish:

\[
\boxed{
\ell_7(\mathcal A_{\actual})=0,
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\ell_9^Y(\mathcal A_{\actual})=0,
}
\]

where

\[
\mathcal A_{\actual}
=
\mathcal N_{\sing}
+
T\mathcal M_Y^{\arith}
+
\mathcal T_{E_2^Y}^{\local}
+
\mathcal T_{\lab,Y}^{\local}.
\]

So we need two exactness statements:

\[
\boxed{\mathrm{C7Exactness}}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{\mathrm{Y12Exactness}}.
\]

---

# 2. \(C7\)-exactness

The \(s_7\) row is not raw order-seven Taylor data. It is the live \(C_7\)-carrier coordinate after quotienting dead order-seven directions.

Define

\[
\mathcal Q_7^C
=
\mathcal J_7/\mathcal D_7,
\]

where \(\mathcal D_7\) contains:

\[
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{EF\text{-}lin}},
\quad
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{EF\text{-}curv}},
\quad
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{chart}},
\quad
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{chart\text{-}curv}},
\]

\[
\mathcal J_7^{\gamma/\mathrm{bg}},
\quad
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{norm}},
\quad
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{norm\text{-}curv}},
\quad
\mathcal N_7.
\]

Then \(C7\)-exactness is:

\[
\boxed{
[\mathcal A_{\actual}]_7=0
\quad\text{in}\quad
\mathcal Q_7^C.
}
\]

Equivalently,

\[
A_7\subseteq \operatorname{ran}D_7,
\]

where \(A_7\) is the order-seven actual-source image.

But live survival requires

\[
\boxed{
[C_7]\neq0
\quad\text{in}\quad
\mathcal Q_7^C.
}
\]

So the \(C_7\)-row theorem is a denominator-saturation theorem:

\[
\boxed{
A_7\subseteq \operatorname{ran}D_7,
\qquad
c_7^{\live}\notin \operatorname{ran}D_7.
}
\]

This is finite linear algebra.

---

# 3. \(Y12\)-exactness

The \(s_9^Y\) row is the primitive \(Y12\)-signature coordinate:

\[
(D^2N_0,DW_{\same},\mathrm{mixed}\ 12,\mathrm{same\ point},w=1).
\]

Thus

\[
\ell_9^Y(U)=0
\]

for any \(U\) whose origin is:

\[
\mathrm{EF\text{-}linear},
\quad
\gamma/\mathrm{background},
\quad
\mathrm{chart},
\quad
DN_0\text{-origin},
\quad
D^2W\text{-origin},
\quad
\text{wrong mixed branch},
\quad
\text{transported-point rather than same-point}.
\]

So \(Y12\)-exactness is:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}\mathcal A_{\actual}=0
}
\]

modulo fixed/coherent/residual pieces that do not enter local fiber tangent.

The dangerous terms are only those with the exact primitive signature:

\[
DW_{\same}[a_Y]D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2].
\]

For fixed-\(\alpha\) actual-source variations, the slot-rank test should rule these out:

\[
a_Y\notin V_W
\quad\text{or}\quad
\xi_1\notin V_1
\quad\text{or}\quad
\xi_2\notin V_2.
\]

Thus the target theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\ell_9^Y(T\mathcal M_Y^{\arith})=0
}
\]

and likewise for local \(E_2^Y\), labelled transition, and nuisance.

---

# 4. The actual-source tangent \(T\mathcal M_Y^{\arith}\)

For rank two, the actual-source tangent model must be constrained to the **fixed target**:

Included:

\[
m,\lambda,\text{chart/normalization/background/gamma variations}
\]

if they represent coordinate/source descriptions of the same canonical object.

Excluded:

\[
\text{formal package coordinates},
\]

\[
\text{D4 carrier amplitudes},
\]

\[
\text{free local corrected-package coefficients},
\]

\[
\alpha\text{ if it is only O2/O3 proof-thickening}.
\]

This is critical. If the actual-source model is enlarged to include free package coordinates, \(B_Y=0\) fails by definition.

So we need:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{NoFreePackageCoordinatesInActualSource}.
}
\]

This theorem says:

\[
T\mathcal M_Y^{\arith}
\subset
\mathrm{EF/gamma/chart/tail\ generated\ directions},
\]

not corrected-package carrier directions.

If this is accepted, then both \(C7\)-exactness and \(Y12\)-exactness become plausible.

---

# 5. \(E_2^Y\) local versus amplitude split

For rank two, local \(E_2^Y\) freedom must vanish:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_{E_2^Y}^{\local}=0.
}
\]

But this does not require \(E_2^Y=0\).

Permitted:

\[
E_2^Y\text{ fixed basepoint},
\]

\[
E_2^Y\text{ finite coherent amplitude},
\]

\[
E_2^Y\text{ residual-controlled}.
\]

Forbidden for rank two:

\[
E_2^Y\text{ has local graph derivative }\mathbf C(1,\rho_Y),
\]

because this collapses rank two to rank one;

\[
E_2^Y\text{ has vertical derivative }\mathbf C(0,1),
\]

because this kills the \(Y12\) vertical direction;

\[
E_2^Y\text{ has full local }\mathbf C^2\text{ freedom}.
\]

Thus the exact local condition is:

\[
D_{\loc}\Pi_{\fib,Y}E_2=0.
\]

Finite coherent amplitude belongs later in

\[
\mathcal C_{2Y}^{\arith}.
\]

---

# 6. Labelled transition local exactness

Similarly, the labelled/raw mismatch

\[
R_{\lab}
\]

may be nonzero, but rank two only requires

\[
\boxed{
D_{\loc}
\begin{pmatrix}
\ell_7(R_{\lab})\\
\ell_9^Y(R_{\lab})
\end{pmatrix}
=0.
}
\]

The typed commutator

\[
\mathfrak C_Y=\mathfrak C_Y^N+\mathfrak C_Y^L+\mathfrak C_Y^W
\]

must route to:

\[
0,
\quad
\mathcal C_{2Y}^{\arith},
\quad
R_X^Y,
\]

not to local fiber tangent.

This weakens the labelled-realization burden: exact equality is not required.

---

# 7. Nuisance exactness

Need

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal N_{\sing}\cap\operatorname{span}\{e_7,e_Y\}=0
}
\]

with polynomial angle.

If nuisance kills \(e_7\), rank two may collapse to rank one or fail.

If nuisance kills \(e_Y\), the primitive \(Y12\) route dies.

If nuisance identifies \(e_7\) and \(e_Y\), the live pair loses conditioning.

So nuisance must be included in the two-grade quotient denominator only if it is typed-faithful.

---

# 8. \(B_Y=0\) theorem package

The local theorem should be:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{LiveFiberAvoidance}_{2Y}.
}
\]

Hypotheses:

1. Two-grade quotient \(\mathcal Q_{2Y}\) exists and \(e_7,e_Y\) survive independently.
2. \(C7\)-exactness:

\[
[\mathcal A_{\actual}]_7=0\quad\text{in }\mathcal Q_7^C.
\]

3. \(Y12\)-exactness:

\[
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}\mathcal A_{\actual}=0
\]

for local fiber directions.
4. No local \(E_2^Y\) fiber derivative.
5. No local labelled-transition fiber derivative.
6. Nuisance preserves the live fiber.

Conclusion:

\[
\boxed{
B_Y=0.
}
\]

---

# 9. What if \(C7\)-exactness fails?

If

\[
\ell_7(T\mathcal M_Y^{\arith})\neq0
\]

but the \(Y12\) continuation is unique, then

\[
B_Y=\mathbf C(1,\rho_Y)
\]

and rank one is forced.

If

\[
\ell_7\neq0
\]

and a second slope appears, the route dies locally.

Thus failure of \(C7\)-exactness is not automatically fatal, but it destroys rank two.

The audit must report which of these occurs.

---

# 10. Current best path

The rank-two route now reduces to:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{TwoGradeLiveQuotient}
+
\mathrm{LiveFiberAvoidance}_{2Y}
}
\]

for local algebra, and

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{RankTwoCoercivity}
+
\mathrm{LocalMarkov}_{\Gamma_G}
}
\]

for wall closure.

This is a compact proof architecture.

The main remaining local algebraic tasks are:

\[
\boxed{
A_7\subseteq\operatorname{ran}D_7
\quad\text{but}\quad
C_7\notin D_7;
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}\mathcal A_{\actual}=0.
}
\]

If these close, rank two becomes the primary route.

No nomenclature changes. I’ll focus on making O2/O3 compatible with either the plain rank-two wall or the jet-enhanced fallback. 

## Agent 6 — FiniteWallFamilyO2/O3 and plain/jet wall decision

The right abstraction is:

\[
\boxed{\mathrm{FiniteWallFamilyO2/O3}.}
\]

Instead of hard-coding one wall, allow a finite wall family

\[
\mathcal G_{\wall}
=
\{G_1,\ldots,G_M\}
\]

with \(M=O(1)\), bounded branch charge, and polynomial coefficient conditioning. Then define

\[
\boxed{
W_{\bd}^{(\mathcal G)}
=
N_{\sm}|H|^2\sum_{G\in\mathcal G_{\wall}}|G|^2.
}
\]

This lets us use the plain wall by default and switch to the jet wall without rewriting the O2/O3 architecture.

---

# 1. Finite wall family requirements

Require:

\[
|\mathcal G_{\wall}|=O(1),
\]

each \(G\in\mathcal G_{\wall}\) has branch charge at most

\[
d_\Gamma,
\]

derivatives are scale-normalized if present, and all coefficient normalizations are polynomially bounded on chart-good regions.

Then the O2/O3 branch bandwidth is

\[
\boxed{
d_0^{(\mathcal G)}
=
d_N+2d_H+2d_\Gamma.
}
\]

The transition basis requirement is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathbb C_{d_0^{(\mathcal G)}}[y]\subset V_{\tr}
}
\]

with \(R^C\)-conditioning.

Until exact degrees are finalized, keep the conservative condition

\[
\boxed{
d_0^{(\mathcal G)}\le d_{\max},\qquad d_{\max}=14
}
\]

unless a later degree audit forces a larger \(d_{\max}\).

---

# 2. Plain rank-two wall

For the rank-two branch \(B_Y=0\), the natural wall family is

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal G_{\wall}^{\plain}
=
\{\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\}.
}
\]

Then

\[
W_{\bd}^{(2)}
=
N_{\sm}|H|^2
\left(
|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\right).
\]

This is the preferred wall for O2/O3.

Its bandwidth is

\[
\boxed{
d_0^{(2)}
=
d_N+2d_H+2\max(d_{\Gamma_G},d_{\Gamma_{G,2}}).
}
\]

This wall avoids \(\rho_\Gamma\), moving-line finite type, and trap-Schur dependencies.

---

# 3. The needed LocalMarkov bridge

The plain wall gives only

\[
\|\Gamma_G\|_{L^2}
+
\|\Gamma_{G,2}\|_{L^2}.
\]

RankTwoCoercivity wants

\[
\|\Gamma_G\|_{H^2}
+
\|\Gamma_{G,2}\|_{L^2}.
\]

So the bridge is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{LocalMarkov}_{\Gamma_G}:
\quad
\|\Gamma_G\|_{H^2(I)}
\le
Q^C
\|\Gamma_G\|_{L^2(I^*)}.
}
\]

This is plausible because

\[
\Gamma_G=-6q''+36q-12q^3
\]

belongs to a fixed-degree algebra closure of the local source chart. Proving this keeps O2/O3 cleaner than using derivative walls directly.

Thus the preferred analytic chain is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{plain wall}
+
\mathrm{LocalMarkov}_{\Gamma_G}
+
\mathrm{RankTwoCoercivity}
+
\mathrm{Gate1}.
}
\]

---

# 4. Jet-enhanced fallback

If \(\mathrm{LocalMarkov}_{\Gamma_G}\) cannot be proved in the needed function class, use

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal G_{\wall}^{\jet}
=
\{\Gamma_G,D\Gamma_G,D^2\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\},
}
\]

with \(D\) scale-normalized.

Then

\[
W_{\bd}^{(2),\jet}
=
N_{\sm}|H|^2
\left(
|\Gamma_G|^2+
|D\Gamma_G|^2+
|D^2\Gamma_G|^2+
|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\right).
\]

The bandwidth is

\[
\boxed{
d_0^{(2),\jet}
=
d_N+2d_H+2d_{\Gamma,\jet},
}
\]

where

\[
d_{\Gamma,\jet}
=
\max
\{
d(\Gamma_G),
d(D\Gamma_G),
d(D^2\Gamma_G),
d(\Gamma_{G,2})
\}.
\]

Expected:

\[
\boxed{
d_{\Gamma,\jet}=O(1).
}
\]

The derivatives should add only fixed finite complexity, not an \(R\)-scale branch forest. This still keeps O2/O3 bounded-charge.

---

# 5. Near-pure O2/O3 statement

The O2/O3 theorem should be formulated uniformly:

For every finite wall family \(\mathcal G_{\wall}\) with

\[
d_0^{(\mathcal G)}\le d_{\max},
\]

the bounded-charge graph/O2O3 stack gives aggregate survival under

\[
W_{\bd}^{(\mathcal G)}
=
N_{\sm}|H|^2\sum_{G\in\mathcal G_{\wall}}|G|^2.
\]

The fixed finite moment separation remains:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}_{L^2(\mu_{\sub})}
\left(
1,
\operatorname{span}
\{R_{\fold},R_{\fold}^2,\ldots,R_{\fold}^{d_0}\}
\right)
\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

Here

\[
R_{\fold}(x)=\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}.
\]

Thus O2/O3 should prove the statement for all fixed

\[
d_0\le d_{\max}.
\]

---

# 6. Finite coherent vector amplitude model

If a finite coherent vector amplitude model exists,

\[
\mathcal C_{2Y}^{\arith}\neq0,
\]

do not project before O2/O3 unless projection branch charge is controlled.

Use raw finite wall family first:

\[
W_{\bd}^{(\mathcal G)}
=
N_{\sm}|H|^2\sum |G|^2.
\]

Then apply vector amplitude Schur after O2/O3:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}_{L^2(\nu_{\bd})}
(F_{2Y},\mathcal C_{2Y}^{\arith})^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\|F_{2Y}\|^2.
}
\]

This preserves bounded-charge O2/O3.

---

# 7. Failure modes

## \(d_0>d_{\max}\)

Enlarge \(d_{\max}\). This remains acceptable if the required value is \(O(1)\).

## Derivative components have unbounded branch charge

Then the jet wall is not acceptable for O2/O3. Fall back to proving \(\mathrm{LocalMarkov}_{\Gamma_G}\), or avoid derivative walls.

## LocalMarkov fails and jet wall is not O2/O3-compatible

Then rank-two wall closure needs a different analytic bridge. This would be a serious obstruction.

## Coherent projection contaminates O2/O3

Avoid by using raw wall for O2/O3 and amplitude Schur afterward.

---

# 8. Final recommendation

Use:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal G_{\wall}^{\plain}
=
\{\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\}
}
\]

as the default O2/O3 wall family.

Prove O2/O3 in the uniform form:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{FiniteWallFamilyO2/O3}(d_0\le d_{\max}).
}
\]

Set provisionally:

\[
\boxed{
d_{\max}=14.
}
\]

Then prove:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{LocalMarkov}_{\Gamma_G}
}
\]

to connect the plain wall to RankTwoCoercivity.

Keep:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal G_{\wall}^{\jet}
=
\{\Gamma_G,D\Gamma_G,D^2\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\}
}
\]

as the fallback if LocalMarkov fails.

So the rank-two downstream path should be:

\[
\boxed{
B_Y=0
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\Rightarrow
\mathcal G_{\wall}^{\plain}=\{\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\Rightarrow
\mathrm{FiniteWallFamilyO2/O3}
+
\mathrm{LocalMarkov}_{\Gamma_G}
+
\mathrm{RankTwoCoercivity}.
}
\]

## Agent 6 continued attack — reducing finite wall families to scalar O2/O3 weights

The current preferred rank-two wall family is

\[
\mathcal G_{\wall}^{\plain}
=
\{\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\},
\]

with smooth wall weight

\[
W_{\bd}^{(2)}
=
N_{\sm}|H|^2
\left(
|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\right).
\]

The question is whether O2/O3 can handle a **sum of two wall components** without losing the finite near-pure Schur structure. I think yes, and it reduces to a scalar dominant-component argument.

---

# 1. Finite wall family reduction

Let

\[
\mathcal G_{\wall}
=
\{G_1,\ldots,G_M\},
\qquad M=O(1),
\]

and define

\[
W_{\bd}^{(\mathcal G)}
=
N_{\sm}|H|^2\sum_{j=1}^{M}|G_j|^2.
\]

Then by pigeonhole,

\[
\int W_{\bd}^{(\mathcal G)}\,d\nu
=
\sum_{j=1}^M
\int N_{\sm}|H|^2|G_j|^2\,d\nu
\]

implies that for some \(j\),

\[
\int N_{\sm}|H|^2|G_j|^2\,d\nu
\ge
M^{-1}
\int W_{\bd}^{(\mathcal G)}\,d\nu.
\]

Since \(M=O(1)\), this loses only a polynomial constant. Thus, for O2/O3, a finite wall family can be reduced to finitely many scalar wall runs:

\[
W_j=N_{\sm}|H|^2|G_j|^2.
\]

For rank two:

\[
G_1=\Gamma_G,\qquad G_2=\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

So one can run O2/O3 separately on

\[
N_{\sm}|H|^2|\Gamma_G|^2
\]

and

\[
N_{\sm}|H|^2|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2,
\]

then keep the branch with larger mass.

This avoids needing to expand the sum as a single mixed object.

---

# 2. Branch-charge bandwidth

For each scalar component

\[
W_j=N_{\sm}|H|^2|G_j|^2,
\]

the branch-charge bandwidth is

\[
d_0^{(j)}
=
d_N+2d_H+2d_{G_j}.
\]

For the family,

\[
d_0^{(\mathcal G)}
=
\max_j d_0^{(j)}
=
d_N+2d_H+2\max_j d_{G_j}.
\]

For the rank-two plain wall:

\[
\boxed{
d_0^{(2)}
=
d_N+2d_H+2\max(d_{\Gamma_G},d_{\Gamma_{G,2}}).
}
\]

Thus the finite wall family produces no new branch-combinatorics beyond the worst scalar component.

This confirms the \(d_{\max}\)-style O2/O3 theorem is the right abstraction:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{O2/O3}\text{ should be proved uniformly for all scalar walls with }d_0\le d_{\max}.
}
\]

Then a finite wall family follows by pigeonhole.

---

# 3. Consequence for central transition basis

The central transition basis requirement should be:

\[
\mathbb C_{d_{\max}}[y]\subset V_{\tr},
\]

not a wall-specific basis.

If

\[
d_0^{(j)}\le d_{\max}
\]

for every wall component \(G_j\), then each scalar run is covered.

For the rank-two plain family, it is enough to check

\[
d_N+2d_H+2d_{\Gamma_G}\le d_{\max},
\]

\[
d_N+2d_H+2d_{\Gamma_{G,2}}\le d_{\max}.
\]

For the jet fallback, check the same for

\[
G_j\in\{\Gamma_G,D\Gamma_G,D^2\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\}.
\]

This keeps central routing independent of the final wall choice.

---

# 4. Rank-two wall lower from Gate–1

The wall lower should be stated at family level:

\[
\int
N_{\sm}|H|^2
\left(
|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\right)
\,d\nu
\ge
Q^{-A}\nu(\mathcal C).
\]

By the family reduction, this implies that one scalar component has mass:

\[
\int N_{\sm}|H|^2|G_j|^2\,d\nu
\ge Q^{-A'}\nu(\mathcal C).
\]

O2/O3 can then run with that scalar component.

So the rank-two proof does not require a vector-valued O2/O3 theorem. It only requires:

1. vector wall lower from Gate–1/coercivity;
2. scalar O2/O3 for whichever component carries mass.

This is useful because the existing O2/O3 machinery is scalar-weight based.

---

# 5. Plain wall versus jet wall

The same reduction works for the jet family:

\[
\mathcal G_{\wall}^{\jet}
=
\{\Gamma_G,D\Gamma_G,D^2\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\}.
\]

If RankTwoCoercivity requires jet control and LocalMarkov fails, we use the family wall lower

\[
\int
N_{\sm}|H|^2
\sum_{G\in\mathcal G_{\wall}^{\jet}}
|G|^2
\,d\nu
\ge Q^{-A}\nu(\mathcal C).
\]

Then pigeonhole gives a scalar component

\[
G\in\mathcal G_{\wall}^{\jet}
\]

with enough mass.

O2/O3 runs on

\[
N_{\sm}|H|^2|G|^2.
\]

Therefore the jet fallback is O2/O3-compatible as long as every derivative component \(G\) has bounded branch charge.

---

# 6. Avoiding projected-wall complications

If there is a finite coherent vector amplitude model

\[
\mathcal C_{2Y}^{\arith},
\]

do not insert

\[
(I-P_{\mathcal C_{2Y}^{\arith}})F_{2Y}
\]

into O2/O3 unless projection complexity is controlled.

Instead:

1. use raw finite family wall for O2/O3;
2. prove vector amplitude Schur afterward:

\[
\operatorname{dist}_{L^2(\nu_{\bd})}
(F_{2Y},\mathcal C_{2Y}^{\arith})^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\|F_{2Y}\|^2;
\]

3. extract blockwise from the residual.

This is the same topology as the rank-one finite coherent case, but vector-valued and likely easier.

---

# 7. Updated theorem stack for rank two

The clean rank-two downstream stack becomes:

## Local algebra

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{LiveFiberAvoidance}_{2Y}:B_Y=0.
}
\]

## D4 vector wall

\[
\boxed{
F_{2Y}\asymp H(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}).
}
\]

## Gate–1 coercive wall lower

Either:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal G_{\wall}^{\plain}
=
\{\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\}
}
\]

with LocalMarkov, or:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal G_{\wall}^{\jet}
=
\{\Gamma_G,D\Gamma_G,D^2\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\}
}
\]

without LocalMarkov.

## Scalar O2/O3 by pigeonhole

For some

\[
G\in\mathcal G_{\wall},
\]

run O2/O3 with scalar weight

\[
N_{\sm}|H|^2|G|^2.
\]

## Contradiction

Atomic if no coherent amplitude model; block-Schur if finite coherent vector amplitudes remain.

---

# 8. Current best default

The default remains:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal G_{\wall}^{\plain}
=
\{\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\}
}
\]

plus

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{LocalMarkov}_{\Gamma_G}.
}
\]

But the O2/O3 theorem should be written for a finite wall family from the beginning. That makes the jet fallback painless.

---

# 9. Final operational recommendation

The next proof work should split as:

1. **Local exactness**: prove \(B_Y=0\).
2. **Coercive wall lower**: prove either plain-wall LocalMarkov + RankTwoCoercivity or jet-wall RankTwoCoercivity.
3. **FiniteWallFamily O2/O3**: prove scalar O2/O3 uniformly for every wall component with \(d_0\le d_{\max}\).
4. **Pigeonhole**: reduce finite family wall mass to one scalar wall run.
5. **Amplitude Schur**, if needed.

This keeps the rank-two route modular and avoids reintroducing the rank-one moving-wall machinery.

## Agent 6 continued attack — finite wall family, component selection, and extraction order

The rank-two branch currently has the stack:

\[
B_Y=0
\Longrightarrow
F_{2Y}\asymp H(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})
\Longrightarrow
\text{Gate--1/coercivity gives vector wall mass}.
\]

The next hidden issue is ordering. A finite wall family can be reduced to a scalar component by pigeonhole, but that choice must happen at the **smooth aggregate stage**, not after hard extraction.

---

# 1. Rank-two wall family

The plain rank-two wall family is

\[
\mathcal G_{\wall}^{\plain}
=
\{\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\}.
\]

The jet fallback family is

\[
\mathcal G_{\wall}^{\jet}
=
\{\Gamma_G,D\Gamma_G,D^2\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\}.
\]

For either family, define

\[
W_{\bd}^{(\mathcal G)}
=
N_{\sm}|H|^2
\sum_{G\in\mathcal G_{\wall}} |G|^2.
\]

The vector wall lower should be proved as

\[
\int W_{\bd}^{(\mathcal G)}\,d\nu
\ge Q^{-A}\nu(\mathcal C).
\]

Then, since \(|\mathcal G_{\wall}|=O(1)\), there exists a component

\[
G_\star\in\mathcal G_{\wall}
\]

such that

\[
\boxed{
\int N_{\sm}|H|^2|G_\star|^2\,d\nu
\ge Q^{-A'}\nu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This component \(G_\star\) is then fixed for the O2/O3 run.

---

# 2. Component selection must be pre-extraction

Do **not** extract good atoms first and then choose the locally dominant component.

That would create a moving wall selector

\[
G_\star=G_\star(p),
\]

which could break bounded-charge O2/O3.

Correct order:

1. Prove aggregate finite-family wall lower.
2. Choose one global component \(G_\star\) by pigeonhole.
3. Run O2/O3 with scalar weight

\[
W_{\bd}^{G_\star}
=
N_{\sm}|H|^2|G_\star|^2.
\]

4. Only after O2/O3 and wall mass are established, perform hard extraction.

This preserves the bounded-charge branch structure.

---

# 3. O2/O3 bandwidth for chosen component

For the chosen scalar component

\[
G_\star,
\]

the branch bandwidth is

\[
d_0(G_\star)
=
d_N+2d_H+2d_{G_\star}.
\]

The uniform theorem needed is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{BoundedChargeGraph/O2O3}_{23}
\text{ holds for every scalar wall }G
\text{ with }d_0(G)\le d_{\max}.
}
\]

Then the finite-family case follows immediately.

For the plain family,

\[
d_0^{\plain}
=
d_N+2d_H+2\max(d_{\Gamma_G},d_{\Gamma_{G,2}}).
\]

For the jet family,

\[
d_0^{\jet}
=
d_N+2d_H+2
\max\{d_{\Gamma_G},d_{D\Gamma_G},d_{D^2\Gamma_G},d_{\Gamma_{G,2}}\}.
\]

The conservative transition condition remains

\[
\mathbb C_{d_{\max}}[y]\subset V_{\tr}.
\]

---

# 4. Interaction with Gate–1/coercivity

The vector wall lower from Gate–1/coercivity is block-level:

\[
\sum_\Omega
\int_\Omega
|H|^2\sum_{G\in\mathcal G_{\wall}}|G|^2
\,d\nu
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

A global component \(G_\star\) may not dominate every block, but it dominates on average:

\[
\sum_\Omega
\int_\Omega
|H|^2|G_\star|^2
\,d\nu
\ge Q^{-A'}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

That is sufficient for aggregate O2/O3. Pointwise dominance is unnecessary.

So we do not need a component selector per block.

---

# 5. What if O2/O3 requires blockwise wall mass?

If the O2/O3 extraction requires wall mass on each routed block, use a finite partition:

\[
\mathcal C_j
=
\left\{
\Omega:
\int_\Omega |H|^2|G_j|^2
\ge
|\mathcal G_{\wall}|^{-1}
\int_\Omega |H|^2\sum_k|G_k|^2
\right\}.
\]

There are only \(O(1)\) classes. One class has polynomial total mass.

Then fix that component \(G_j\) and restrict to the union of its blocks.

This is still pre-extraction and preserves bounded charge because the component is constant on the selected block family.

So even blockwise O2/O3 is compatible with finite wall families.

---

# 6. Vector amplitude Schur after scalar component selection

If there is a finite coherent vector amplitude space

\[
\mathcal C_{2Y}^{\arith},
\]

the scalar component selected for O2/O3 may not alone represent the full vector residual after amplitude projection.

Therefore the clean sequence is:

1. Use raw vector family wall to get aggregate mass.
2. Pigeonhole to scalar \(G_\star\) for O2/O3.
3. Prove O2/O3 under scalar \(G_\star\).
4. Apply vector amplitude Schur to \(F_{2Y}\) under the smooth post-O2/O3 measure.
5. Extract block residual.

This avoids projecting before O2/O3 and avoids requiring scalar component-wise amplitude Schur.

If \(\mathcal C_{2Y}^{\arith}=0\), step 4 disappears.

---

# 7. Why scalar component O2/O3 is enough for vector D4

A possible concern: if O2/O3 is proved using only

\[
G_\star=\Gamma_G,
\]

does the D4 lower still know about the full vector wall?

Yes. The scalar component was chosen because it carries a polynomial fraction of the vector wall mass. The D4 lower is ultimately the vector wall norm:

\[
\|F_{2Y}\|^2
\asymp
|H|^2(|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2).
\]

The scalar component is only a device for feeding bounded-charge O2/O3. It does not replace the local D4 obstruction.

Thus the rank-two contradiction still uses the vector lower; O2/O3 only uses a scalar branch-compatible witness.

---

# 8. Rank-two finite-family theorem

The clean theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{FiniteWallFamilyReduction}.
}
\]

Let

\[
\mathcal G_{\wall}=\{G_1,\ldots,G_M\},
\qquad M=O(1),
\]

with each \(G_j\) branch charge \(\le d_\Gamma\). If

\[
\int N_{\sm}|H|^2\sum_j |G_j|^2\,d\nu
\ge Q^{-A}\nu(\mathcal C),
\]

then there exists a fixed component \(G_\star\) and a routed subcollection of polynomial mass such that

\[
\int N_{\sm}|H|^2|G_\star|^2\,d\nu
\ge Q^{-A'}\nu(\mathcal C),
\]

and

\[
d_0(G_\star)
\le
d_N+2d_H+2d_\Gamma.
\]

Therefore any O2/O3 theorem uniform for scalar walls up to \(d_{\max}\) applies to the family.

---

# 9. Updated rank-two downstream order

The correct order is now:

\[
\boxed{
B_Y=0
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\Rightarrow
F_{2Y}\asymp H(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\Rightarrow
\mathrm{RankTwoCoercivity}
+
\mathrm{LocalMarkov/JetWall}
+
\mathrm{Gate1}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\Rightarrow
\text{finite-family wall mass}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\Rightarrow
\text{select scalar component }G_\star
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\Rightarrow
\mathrm{O2/O3}\text{ with }N_{\sm}|H|^2|G_\star|^2
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\Rightarrow
\text{hard extraction and contradiction}.
}
\]

---

# 10. Current conclusion

The finite wall family does not create a new obstacle.

The rank-two route remains:

\[
\boxed{
\text{local exactness }B_Y=0
}
\]

plus

\[
\boxed{
\text{coercive vector wall lower from Gate--1}
}
\]

plus

\[
\boxed{
\text{scalar O2/O3 after finite pigeonhole}.
}
\]

The next true bottleneck is still local exactness:

\[
\boxed{
B_Y=0.
}
\]

Everything downstream is now modular and tractable if \(B_Y=0\) closes.
