The rank-two branch now has a clean local target:

\[
B_Y=0.
\]

The problem is to stop treating this as a slogan and turn it into a finite certificate. The latest synthesis says the downstream route is modular if \(B_Y=0\): rank-two vector wall, Gate–1 coercivity, scalar O2/O3 after finite pigeonhole. So the live obstruction is now local exactness. 

---

# 1. Matrix form of \(B_Y=0\)

Choose a finite list of actual/local candidates:

\[
U_1,\ldots,U_M
\]

spanning

\[
\mathcal N_{\sing}
+
T\mathcal M_Y^{\arith}
+
\mathcal T_{E_2^Y}^{\local}
+
\mathcal T_{\lab,Y}^{\local}.
\]

Then

\[
B_Y
=
\begin{pmatrix}
\ell_7(U_1)&\cdots&\ell_7(U_M)\\
\ell_9^Y(U_1)&\cdots&\ell_9^Y(U_M)
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Rank two requires:

\[
\boxed{
B_Y=0.
}
\]

So the finite certificate is simply:

\[
\boxed{
\ell_7(U_j)=0,\qquad \ell_9^Y(U_j)=0
\quad\text{for every }j.
}
\]

But each row has a different proof mechanism.

---

# 2. The \(s_7\)-row certificate

The \(s_7\)-row is the \(C_7\)-carrier quotient.

Let

\[
\mathcal Q_7^C=\mathcal J_7/\mathcal D_7.
\]

Let \(D_7\) be a matrix spanning the dead denominator:

\[
\mathcal D_7
=
\mathrm{EF\text{-}lin}
+
\mathrm{EF\text{-}curv}
+
\mathrm{chart}
+
\mathrm{chart\text{-}curv}
+
\gamma/\mathrm{bg}
+
\mathrm{norm}
+
\mathrm{norm\text{-}curv}
+
\mathcal N_7.
\]

Let \(A_7\) be the matrix of actual-source order-seven candidates.

Then the desired row certificate is:

\[
\boxed{
(I-P_{D_7})A_7=0.
}
\]

Live survival is the separate condition:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}(c_7^{\live},\operatorname{ran}D_7)
\ge Q^{-A}|c_7^{\live}|.
}
\]

So \(C_7\)-faithfulness has two sides:

\[
\boxed{
\text{actual order-seven drift is dead;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{live \(C_7\) is not dead.}
}
\]

This is exactly the denominator-balance problem.

The important guardrail remains:

\[
[z^7]\partial_\theta q''_{\can}\neq0
\quad\not\Rightarrow\quad
\ell_7(\partial_\theta q''_{\can})\neq0.
\]

Raw order-seven motion is harmless if it lies in \(\mathcal D_7\).

---

# 3. The \(s_9^Y\)-row certificate

The \(s_9^Y\)-row is the primitive \(Y12\)-origin quotient.

The fastest certificate is through the whitening slot.

Let

\[
\mathcal Q_W^Y=W/W_{\dead}^{\fixed}.
\]

Let \(a_Y\) be the same-point whitening input of the primitive \(Y12\) carrier.

The desired test is:

\[
\boxed{
[P_W V_{\arith}^{\fixed}]=0
\quad\text{in }\mathcal Q_W^Y,
}
\]

but

\[
\boxed{
[a_Y]\neq0
\quad\text{in }\mathcal Q_W^Y.
}
\]

Equivalently, construct a covector

\[
\omega_Y
\]

such that

\[
\omega_Y(a_Y)=1,
\qquad
\omega_Y(P_WV_{\arith}^{\fixed})=0.
\]

If this passes, fixed-\(\alpha\) actual second curvature cannot produce the primitive \(Y12\) row. We do not need the full bilinear \(D^2N_0\)-slot rank test unless the whitening-slot test fails.

Thus the \(s_9^Y\)-certificate should be ordered:

\[
\boxed{
\text{whitening slot separation first;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{source-Hessian slot separation only if needed.}
}
\]

---

# 4. \(E_2^Y\) and \(R_{\lab}\): local derivative test

For rank two, fixed or coherent contaminant terms are not local fiber tangents. So the condition is not

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}E_2=0
\]

or

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}R_{\lab}=0.
\]

The condition is:

\[
\boxed{
D_{\loc}\Pi_{\fib,Y}E_2=0,
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
D_{\loc}\Pi_{\fib,Y}R_{\lab}=0.
}
\]

This is a major reduction.

Define for any contaminant \(X\):

\[
c_X(p)=
\begin{pmatrix}
\ell_7(X(p))\\
\ell_9^Y(X(p))
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Then rank two needs:

\[
\boxed{
D_{\loc}c_X=0.
}
\]

If

\[
c_X\neq0
\]

but is fixed or finite coherent, it goes to the vector amplitude model

\[
\mathcal C_{2Y}^{\arith}
\]

and does not affect \(B_Y\).

Fatal/local cases:

\[
D_{\loc}c_X\in\mathbf C(1,\rho_Y)
\]

collapses rank two to rank one;

\[
D_{\loc}c_X\in\mathbf C(0,1)
\]

kills \(Y12\);

two independent local directions kill the fiber.

---

# 5. Nuisance exactness

The nuisance condition should be part of the live quotient, not checked afterward.

The two tests are:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}(e_7,\mathcal D_{7,9})\ge Q^{-A}|e_7|,
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}(e_Y,\mathcal D_{7,9}+\mathbf C e_7)
\ge Q^{-A}|e_Y|.
}
\]

These imply

\[
\lambda_{\min}(G_{\live})\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

If nuisance kills \(e_7\) or \(e_Y\), that is not an analytic failure; it is a quotient-design failure unless the killed direction is genuinely actual-source coherent.

---

# 6. The finite exactness certificate

Put everything into one matrix.

Let

\[
D_{2Y}
\]

span the dead typed denominator in the two-grade carrier space.

Let

\[
A_{2Y}
\]

span actual/local candidates:

\[
A_{2Y}
=
[
A_{\can}^{(1)},
A_{\can}^{(2)},
A_{E_2,\loc},
A_{\lab,\loc},
A_{\nuis}
].
\]

Let

\[
L_{2Y}
=
[e_7,e_Y]
\]

be the live matrix.

Then rank-two exactness is:

\[
\boxed{
(I-P_{D_{2Y}})A_{2Y}=0,
}
\]

and live survival is:

\[
\boxed{
\lambda_{\min}
\left(
L_{2Y}^*(I-P_{D_{2Y}})L_{2Y}
\right)
\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

This is the cleanest finite formulation of \(B_Y=0\).

It says:

\[
\boxed{
\text{actual/local candidates vanish in the live quotient;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{the D4 live pair survives in the same quotient.}
}
\]

---

# 7. Consequences

If the finite exactness certificate passes, then:

\[
B_Y=0.
\]

Therefore:

\[
F_{2Y}
=
H
\begin{pmatrix}
c_7\Gamma_G\\
\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}
\end{pmatrix}
\]

and

\[
|F_{2Y}|^2\asymp_{Q^A}|H|^2(|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2).
\]

Then the downstream proof is no longer a wall-selection problem. It is:

\[
\mathrm{RankTwoCoercivity}
+
\mathrm{Gate1}
+
\mathrm{FiniteWallFamilyO2/O3}.
\]

---

# 8. If the exactness certificate fails

The failure type determines the branch.

## \(A_{2Y}\) has image \(\mathbf C(1,\rho_Y)\)

Rank two collapses to rank one. Then proceed with the moving-wall route.

## \(A_{2Y}\) contains \(\mathbf C(0,1)\)

The primitive \(Y12\) vertical direction is actual-source/local. Current \(Y12\) route dies locally.

## \(A_{2Y}\) has rank two

The full live fiber is actual-source/local. Current \(Y12\) route dies locally.

## \(L_{2Y}\) fails survival

The quotient kills the live pair. Refine denominator/nuisance or abandon this quotient.

---

# 9. Immediate mathematical next step

The next proof should write down the finite matrices:

\[
D_{2Y},\quad A_{2Y},\quad L_{2Y}.
\]

The most important rows are:

\[
D_7,\quad A_7,\quad c_7^{\live}
\]

for \(C_7\)-faithfulness, and

\[
W_{\dead}^{\fixed},\quad P_WV_{\arith}^{\fixed},\quad a_Y
\]

for \(Y12\)-faithfulness.

If those two pass, \(B_Y=0\) is very close, because \(E_2\) and labelled mismatch only need the weaker no-local-derivative condition.

---

# 10. Current conclusion

The rank-two local algebra can now be attacked as one finite exactness problem:

\[
\boxed{
(I-P_{D_{2Y}})A_{2Y}=0,
\qquad
\lambda_{\min}
(L_{2Y}^*(I-P_{D_{2Y}})L_{2Y})
\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

This is the right next target. If it closes, the rank-two route becomes the main proof path.

## Agent 6 continued attack — stratifying the finite exactness certificate

The current rank-two local target is the finite certificate

\[
(I-P_{D_{2Y}})A_{2Y}=0,
\]

and

\[
\lambda_{\min}
\left(
L_{2Y}^*(I-P_{D_{2Y}})L_{2Y}
\right)
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Here \(D_{2Y}\) is the dead typed denominator, \(A_{2Y}\) is the actual/local candidate matrix, and

\[
L_{2Y}=[e_7,e_Y]
\]

is the live D4 carrier pair. This is the clean finite form of \(B_Y=0\). 

The next step is to stratify the certificate into columns that vanish by **type**, columns that vanish by **locality**, and columns that need a genuine **Schur determinant**.

---

# 1. Split \(A_{2Y}\) into four column blocks

Write

\[
A_{2Y}
=
[A_{\can},A_{\curv},A_{\loc},A_{\nuis}],
\]

where:

\[
A_{\can}
=
\text{first actual-source tangents},
\]

\[
A_{\curv}
=
\text{second actual-source curvature},
\]

\[
A_{\loc}
=
\text{local \(E_2^Y\) and labelled-transition local derivatives},
\]

\[
A_{\nuis}
=
\text{singular nuisance representatives}.
\]

Then

\[
(I-P_{D_{2Y}})A_{2Y}=0
\]

splits into:

\[
(I-P_{D_{2Y}})A_{\can}=0,
\]

\[
(I-P_{D_{2Y}})A_{\curv}=0,
\]

\[
(I-P_{D_{2Y}})A_{\loc}=0,
\]

\[
(I-P_{D_{2Y}})A_{\nuis}=0.
\]

These four blocks require different arguments.

---

# 2. \(A_{\can}\): type-zero by EF/gamma/chart origin

First actual-source tangents are generated by:

\[
m,\lambda,\text{phase},\text{chart},\text{normalization},\text{gamma/background}.
\]

Their typed origins are:

\[
\mathrm{EF\text{-}linear},
\quad
\mathrm{chart},
\quad
\mathrm{normalization},
\quad
\gamma/\mathrm{background}.
\]

They should lie inside the dead denominator by definition:

\[
A_{\can}\subset D_{2Y}.
\]

So for this block, the proof is not a determinant calculation. It is a denominator-design theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{ActualFirstTangentsAreDead}_{2Y}.
}
\]

The key guardrail is:

\[
[z^7]\partial_\theta q''_{\can}\neq0
\]

does not imply

\[
\ell_7(\partial_\theta q''_{\can})\neq0.
\]

The actual tangent may have raw order-seven drift, but its type is EF/chart/gamma/norm, so it should vanish in \(\mathcal Q_{2Y}\).

---

# 3. \(A_{\curv}\): mostly type-zero, with one slot-rank fallback

Second actual-source curvature contains two kinds of terms.

## 3.1 Dead-origin curvature

Terms like:

\[
D N_0[\partial_{\theta_i\theta_j}\xi],
\]

chart curvature,

normalization curvature,

gamma/background curvature,

and EF coefficient curvature.

These are dead by origin:

\[
\mathrm{DN_0}\neq \mathrm{D^2N_0},
\]

\[
\mathrm{chart/norm/gamma}\neq (D^2N_0,DW_{\same}).
\]

So they belong to \(D_{2Y}\).

## 3.2 True Hessian terms

The only dangerous terms are:

\[
DW_{\same}[a]D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2].
\]

For these, use the whitening-slot shortcut:

\[
[P_WV_{\arith}^{\fixed}]=0
\quad\text{in}\quad
\mathcal Q_W^Y,
\]

while

\[
[a_Y]\neq0
\quad\text{in}\quad
\mathcal Q_W^Y.
\]

If the whitening covector \(\omega_Y\) exists, then all fixed-\(\alpha\) actual curvature misses \(s_9^Y\).

Thus the curvature block is killed by:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{DeadCurvatureOrigin}_{2Y}
+
\mathrm{SamePointWhiteningExactness}_Y.
}
\]

No full bilinear source-slot rank computation is needed unless the whitening test fails.

---

# 4. \(A_{\loc}\): killed by locality, not type

This block is subtler.

For

\[
E_2^Y
\]

and

\[
R_{\lab},
\]

we do **not** need their total projection to vanish:

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}E_2
\]

or

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}R_{\lab}
\]

may be fixed, finite coherent, or residual-controlled.

For \(B_Y=0\), we need only:

\[
D_{\loc}\Pi_{\fib,Y}E_2=0,
\]

\[
D_{\loc}\Pi_{\fib,Y}R_{\lab}=0.
\]

So

\[
A_{\loc}
\]

should contain only **local derivative columns**, not fixed/coherent/residual columns.

Then the exactness condition is:

\[
A_{\loc}=0.
\]

This is a locality theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{NoAtomFitting}_{2Y}.
}
\]

It says completion, transition, normal-form, and whitening data are fixed pre-extraction or block-coherent; they do not carry atom-local live-fiber coordinates.

This is an important simplification: fixed \(E_2^Y\) or finite coherent labelled mismatch belongs to \(\mathcal C_{2Y}^{\arith}\), not \(B_Y\).

---

# 5. \(A_{\nuis}\): true Schur/angle check

The nuisance block is the least “type automatic.”

For nuisance we need:

\[
\operatorname{dist}(e_7,D_{2Y})\ge Q^{-A}|e_7|,
\]

and

\[
\operatorname{dist}(e_Y,D_{2Y}+\mathbf C e_7)\ge Q^{-A}|e_Y|.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\lambda_{\min}
\left(
L_{2Y}^*(I-P_{D_{2Y}})L_{2Y}
\right)
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

This is the genuine finite Schur determinant. It decides whether the live pair survives after nuisance/dead quotient.

So the local exactness proof has one hard linear-algebra block:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{NuisancePreservesLivePair}_{2Y}.
}
\]

Everything else should be killed by type/locality if the setup is correct.

---

# 6. Refined proof architecture for \(B_Y=0\)

The proof should be organized as:

## Step 1: build the two-grade quotient

\[
\mathcal Q_{2Y}=\mathcal J_{7,9}/D_{2Y}.
\]

## Step 2: prove live pair survival

\[
[e_7],[e_Y]\text{ independent in }\mathcal Q_{2Y}.
\]

This gives \(\ell_7,\ell_9^Y\).

## Step 3: prove actual first tangents are dead

\[
A_{\can}\subset D_{2Y}.
\]

## Step 4: prove actual second curvature is dead

Use dead-origin classification plus same-point whitening exactness.

## Step 5: prove no local atom-fitting

\[
A_{\loc}=0.
\]

## Step 6: ensure nuisance is already accounted for in \(D_{2Y}\) without killing live pair.

Then conclude

\[
B_Y=0.
\]

---

# 7. What would falsify rank two?

The finite exactness certificate can fail in four distinct ways.

## 7.1 Actual first tangent has live \(C_7\) class

\[
(I-P_{D_{2Y}})A_{\can}\neq0
\]

in the \(s_7\) row.

Then \(B_Y\) may contain \(s_7\)-motion and rank two collapses to rank one if the continuation is one-slope.

## 7.2 Actual second curvature has primitive \(Y12\) slot

This means the fixed actual parameter variations really span:

\[
DW_{\same}[a_Y]D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2].
\]

Then \(Y12\) may be actual-source curvature, and the route likely dies locally.

## 7.3 Local atom-fitting exists

If \(E_2\) or labelled transition has local columns:

\[
\eta_7(p)e_7+\eta_Y(p)e_Y,
\]

then \(B_Y\) is nonzero. Graph-local terms collapse to rank one; vertical/full terms kill the route.

## 7.4 Nuisance overcollapses live pair

If the denominator kills \(e_7\) or \(e_Y\), rank two fails for quotient-design reasons. This may be repairable by refining \(D_{2Y}\).

---

# 8. The most likely bottleneck

The main non-formal bottleneck is now:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{NuisancePreservesLivePair}_{2Y}.
}
\]

The type arguments for actual/gamma/chart terms are plausible. The locality arguments for \(E_2,R_{\lab}\) are modelling guardrails. But the finite Schur angle

\[
\lambda_{\min}(G_{\live})\ge Q^{-A}
\]

must actually be checked.

So the highest-value finite computation is:

\[
\boxed{
\det G_{\live}
\neq0
\quad\text{at polynomial scale.}
}
\]

where

\[
G_{\live}
=
L_{2Y}^*(I-P_{D_{2Y}})L_{2Y}.
\]

---

# 9. Rank-two downstream after exactness

Once

\[
B_Y=0
\]

is established, the rest is modular:

1. \(F_{2Y}\asymp H(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})\).
2. Gate–1 plus RankTwoCoercivity gives finite-family wall mass.
3. Pigeonhole selects a scalar component \(G_\star\).
4. O2/O3 runs with \(N_{\sm}|H|^2|G_\star|^2\).
5. If finite coherent amplitude exists, apply vector amplitude Schur after O2/O3.
6. Extract and contradict.

No rank-one moving wall is needed.

---

# 10. Current conclusion

The rank-two local exactness theorem is now split into:

\[
\boxed{
\text{type-dead actual tangents/curvatures}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{locality-dead completion/transition defects}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Schur-live \(e_7,e_Y\) pair}
}
\]

The first two should be structural. The third is the decisive finite linear-algebra computation.

Thus, the next best mathematical attack is:

\[
\boxed{
\lambda_{\min}
\left(
L_{2Y}^*(I-P_{D_{2Y}})L_{2Y}
\right)
\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

If that live-pair Schur lower holds, \(B_Y=0\) becomes highly plausible.

I’ll keep the current convention:

\[
\rho_Y=\text{lower }Y12\text{ graph slope},\qquad
\rho_\Gamma=\frac{\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7}{\alpha_9},\qquad
R_{\fold}(x)=\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}.
\]

## Agent 6 continued attack — live-pair Schur lower

The current local bottleneck is no longer wall analysis. It is the finite Schur lower

\[
\lambda_{\min}
\left(
L_{2Y}^*(I-P_{D_{2Y}})L_{2Y}
\right)
\ge Q^{-A},
\]

where

\[
L_{2Y}=[e_7,e_Y].
\]

This proves that the live pair survives the dead/nuisance denominator and gives the dual covectors

\[
\ell_7,\ell_9^Y.
\]

If this holds, and the actual/local candidates are dead by type/locality, then

\[
B_Y=0.
\]

---

# 1. What can make the live Schur fail?

The live-pair Schur can fail in exactly three ways.

## 1.1 \(e_7\) is dead

\[
e_7\in D_{2Y}.
\]

Then the \(C_7\)-carrier row dies. Rank two is impossible, though a \(Y12\)-only route might remain.

## 1.2 \(e_Y\) is dead modulo \(e_7\)

\[
e_Y\in D_{2Y}+\mathbf C e_7.
\]

Then the primitive \(Y12\) direction is not independent. Rank two collapses or dies.

## 1.3 Poor polynomial conditioning

The pair is independent, but the angle is too small:

\[
\operatorname{dist}(e_Y,D_{2Y}+\mathbf Ce_7)
<
Q^{-A}|e_Y|.
\]

Then the quotient is formally correct but quantitatively unusable.

So the finite live theorem must prove:

\[
\boxed{
e_7\notin D_{2Y},
\qquad
e_Y\notin D_{2Y}+\mathbf Ce_7,
}
\]

at polynomial angle.

---

# 2. Type labels should separate \(e_7\) and \(e_Y\)

The two live vectors have distinct carrier types.

\[
e_7:
\quad
\text{live corrected-package \(C_7\)-carrier, order seven}.
\]

\[
e_Y:
\quad
(D^2N_0,DW_{\same}),\ \text{mixed branch }12,\ \text{same-point},\ w=1,\ \text{order nine}.
\]

The denominator \(D_{2Y}\) contains:

\[
\mathrm{EF\text{-}linear},
\quad
\mathrm{EF\text{-}curvature},
\quad
\mathrm{chart},
\quad
\mathrm{chart\text{-}curvature},
\quad
\gamma/\mathrm{background},
\quad
\mathrm{normalization},
\quad
\mathrm{wrong\ origin},
\quad
\mathrm{nuisance}.
\]

Thus the expected separation is **typed**, not accidental:

\[
\operatorname{type}(e_7)
\notin
\operatorname{type}(D_{2Y}),
\]

\[
\operatorname{type}(e_Y)
\notin
\operatorname{type}(D_{2Y}+\mathbf Ce_7).
\]

This is stronger than a coefficient calculation. A coefficient calculation is only needed inside a type sector if labels collide.

---

# 3. The \(e_7\) survival test

The \(e_7\) survival test is:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}(e_7,D_{2Y})\ge Q^{-A}|e_7|.
}
\]

Since \(e_7\) lives in order seven, this reduces to the \(C_7\)-carrier quotient:

\[
\mathcal Q_7^C=\mathcal J_7/\mathcal D_7.
\]

So

\[
e_7\notin D_{2Y}
\]

is equivalent to

\[
[C_7]\neq0
\quad
\text{in}\quad
\mathcal Q_7^C.
\]

The denominator \(\mathcal D_7\) must be large enough to kill actual order-seven EF/chart/gamma/norm drift, but not so large that it kills \(C_7\).

This is the denominator-balance theorem:

\[
\boxed{
A_7\subseteq \operatorname{ran}D_7,
\qquad
\operatorname{dist}(c_7^{\live},\operatorname{ran}D_7)\ge Q^{-A}|c_7^{\live}|.
}
\]

Here \(A_7\) is actual-source order-seven drift, not D4 carrier data.

---

# 4. The \(e_Y\) survival test

The \(e_Y\) survival test is:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}(e_Y,D_{2Y}+\mathbf Ce_7)
\ge Q^{-A}|e_Y|.
}
\]

Because \(e_Y\) has order nine and primitive \(Y12\)-origin labels, it should be separated from \(e_7\) by:

1. order:
   \[
   9\neq 7;
   \]

2. origin:
   \[
   (D^2N_0,DW_{\same})
   \neq
   C_7\text{-carrier};
   \]

3. branch:
   mixed \(12\);

4. same-point support;

5. one whitening slot.

So, unless the quotient deliberately identifies order-seven and order-nine live sectors, \(e_Y\) cannot lie in \(\mathbf Ce_7\).

The real risk is denominator overcollapse:

\[
e_Y\in D_{2Y}.
\]

The whitening quotient shortcut addresses this:

\[
[a_Y]\neq0
\quad
\text{in}
\quad
\mathcal Q_W^Y.
\]

If \(a_Y\) survives the whitening quotient, then \(e_Y\) survives the full \(Y12\) quotient, provided the \(D^2N_0\)-source slots are not killed.

---

# 5. Polynomial conditioning

Typed separation usually gives exact orthogonality in an abstract graded decomposition. But in the realized analytic carrier space, the basis may be nonorthogonal.

So we need a Riesz condition:

\[
\boxed{
\text{typed decomposition has polynomial condition number.}
}
\]

In finite terms, if

\[
D_{2Y}
\]

is the denominator matrix and

\[
L_{2Y}=[e_7,e_Y],
\]

we need the Schur complement

\[
G_{\live}
=
L_{2Y}^*(I-P_{D_{2Y}})L_{2Y}
\]

to satisfy

\[
\lambda_{\min}(G_{\live})\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

A sufficient condition is:

1. each type sector has a polynomial Riesz basis;
2. distinct type sectors have polynomial angle;
3. the live sectors are not included in the denominator sectors.

So the finite live-pair theorem is really:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{TypedRiesz}_{2Y}
+
\mathrm{LiveTypeExclusion}_{2Y}
\Rightarrow
\lambda_{\min}(G_{\live})\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

---

# 6. What if the types are not orthogonal?

If realized types mix, then use explicit Schur residuals.

Define:

\[
\delta_7^2
=
\|e_7\|^2-e_7^*D(D^*D)^\dagger D^*e_7.
\]

Need

\[
\delta_7^2\ge Q^{-A}\|e_7\|^2.
\]

Then define

\[
D_7'=[D,e_7].
\]

Need

\[
\delta_Y^2
=
\|e_Y\|^2-e_Y^*D_7'(D_7'^*D_7')^\dagger D_7'^*e_Y
\ge Q^{-A}\|e_Y\|^2.
\]

The determinant form is:

\[
\delta_7^2=\frac{\det G_{D,e_7}}{\det G_D},
\]

\[
\delta_Y^2=\frac{\det G_{D,e_7,e_Y}}{\det G_{D,e_7}}.
\]

This gives a direct finite fallback if the typed decomposition is not exact enough.

---

# 7. Nuisance overcollapse test

The nuisance part \(\mathcal N_{\sing}\) must be audited carefully.

If \(\mathcal N_{\sing}\) is typed as source-linear/chart/gauge, it belongs in \(D_{2Y}\) safely.

But if \(\mathcal N_{\sing}\) contains representatives with live \(C_7\) or primitive \(Y12\) labels, it will kill rank two.

So we need:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\mathrm{live}}
\mathcal N_{\sing}=0.
}
\]

More concretely:

\[
\ell_7(N)=0,\qquad \ell_9^Y(N)=0
\]

for every nuisance generator \(N\).

This can be checked either by type labels or by the Schur complement above.

If it fails, ask whether this is:

1. legitimate actual-source coherence;
2. gauge redundancy that should be quotiented;
3. quotient overcollapse that must be repaired.

Only the first is an arithmetic failure.

---

# 8. Live-pair theorem package

The theorem should be:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{TwoGradeLivePair}_{2Y}.
}
\]

Hypotheses:

1. finite typed carrier decomposition exists;
2. denominator \(D_{2Y}\) contains all dead EF/chart/gamma/norm/nuisance types;
3. live \(C_7\)-type is not in \(D_{2Y}\);
4. primitive \(Y12\)-type is not in \(D_{2Y}+\mathbf Ce_7\);
5. type sectors have polynomial Riesz angles.

Conclusion:

\[
\lambda_{\min}
\left(
L_{2Y}^*(I-P_{D_{2Y}})L_{2Y}
\right)
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

This constructs the dual coordinates \(\ell_7,\ell_9^Y\) with polynomial norm.

---

# 9. Relation to \(B_Y=0\)

Once \(\ell_7,\ell_9^Y\) are constructed, \(B_Y=0\) becomes checking:

\[
\ell_7(U_j)=\ell_9^Y(U_j)=0
\]

for actual/local candidates \(U_j\).

But if the candidates are all in \(D_{2Y}\), this is automatic because \(\ell_7,\ell_9^Y\) kill \(D_{2Y}\).

So the complete local proof is:

1. build \(D_{2Y}\) so that actual/local candidates lie in \(D_{2Y}\);
2. prove \(e_7,e_Y\) survive modulo \(D_{2Y}\).

That is clean.

---

# 10. Current bottleneck

The live-pair Schur lower is probably the single finite computation that decides rank two.

If it passes, all type-dead actual candidates are automatically killed.

If it fails, diagnose:

- \(e_7\) dead: rank-two loses \(C_7\);
- \(e_Y\) dead: \(Y12\) route dies;
- \(e_Y\sim e_7\): rank collapses;
- poor conditioning: quantitative failure.

So the next practical step is to build \(D_{2Y}\) explicitly enough to compute

\[
G_{\live}
=
L_{2Y}^*(I-P_{D_{2Y}})L_{2Y}.
\]

This is the decisive local algebra audit.

## Assistant (2026-04-28 13:32:22)

_Thought for 6s_

I will use the existing convention:

\[
\rho_Y=\text{lower }Y12\text{ graph slope},\qquad
\rho_\Gamma=\frac{\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7}{\alpha_9},\qquad

\]

No nomenclature changes. I will handle the finite wall family/O2O3 integration and scalar component selection. The rank-two proof now rests on the finite exactness certificate plus the identity \(E_2-E_1''=-12q(q')^2\); O2/O3 only needs to support the resulting finite wall family. 

## Agent 6 — FiniteWallFamilyO2O3 and scalar selection protocol

The correct O2/O3 abstraction is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{FiniteWallFamilyO2O3}.
}
\]

Let

\[
\mathcal G_{\wall}=\{G_1,\ldots,G_M\},
\qquad M=O(1).
\]

Define the smooth pre-extraction wall weight

\[
\boxed{
W_{\bd}^{(\mathcal G)}
=
N_{\sm}|H|^2\sum_{j=1}^M |G_j|^2.
}
\]

O2/O3 does not need to be vector-valued. A finite wall family reduces to one scalar wall component by pigeonhole at the smooth aggregate stage.

---

# 1. Wall family requirements

Each component \(G_j\) must satisfy:

\[
d(G_j)\le d_\Gamma,
\]

with \(d_\Gamma=O(1)\), and all derivative components must be scale-normalized.

Coefficient conditioning must be polynomial on chart-good regions:

\[
|H|,\ |H|^{-1},\ |G_j|\text{ normalizations}\le Q^C
\]

where applicable.

The O2/O3 bandwidth is

\[
\boxed{
d_0^{(\mathcal G)}
=
d_N+2d_H+2d_\Gamma.
}
\]

O2/O3 must be proved uniformly for all scalar wall components satisfying

\[
\boxed{
d_0(G)=d_N+2d_H+2d_G\le d_{\max}.
}
\]

Keep the current conservative target

\[
\boxed{
d_{\max}=14
}
\]

unless a degree audit forces a larger bound.

---

# 2. Default rank-two wall family

Given the rank-two coercivity proof wants \(\Gamma_G\) in \(H^2\), the robust default is the jet family:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal G_{\wall}^{\jet}
=
\{\Gamma_G,\ D\Gamma_G,\ D^2\Gamma_G,\ \Gamma_{G,2}\}.
}
\]

Here \(D\) is the scale-normalized local derivative.

The corresponding wall weight is

\[
\boxed{
W_{\bd}^{(2),\jet}
=
N_{\sm}|H|^2
\left(
|\Gamma_G|^2+
|D\Gamma_G|^2+
|D^2\Gamma_G|^2+
|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\right).
}
\]

This avoids relying on a separate \(\mathrm{LocalMarkov}_{\Gamma_G}\).

---

# 3. Plain wall as optimization

If

\[
\boxed{
\|\Gamma_G\|_{H^2(I)}
\le Q^C\|\Gamma_G\|_{L^2(I^*)}
}
\]

is proved, then use the smaller plain family:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal G_{\wall}^{\plain}
=
\{\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\}.
}
\]

The plain wall is simpler:

\[
W_{\bd}^{(2)}
=
N_{\sm}|H|^2
\left(
|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\right).
\]

But until \(\mathrm{LocalMarkov}_{\Gamma_G}\) is proven, the jet family is the safer default.

---

# 4. Finite-family-to-scalar reduction

Assume the rank-two wall lower has been established in finite-family form:

\[
\int
N_{\sm}|H|^2\sum_{j=1}^M |G_j|^2
\,d\nu
\ge
Q^{-A}\nu(\mathcal C).
\]

Since \(M=O(1)\), there exists a fixed component

\[
G_\star\in\mathcal G_{\wall}
\]

such that

\[
\boxed{
\int
N_{\sm}|H|^2 |G_\star|^2
\,d\nu
\ge
Q^{-A'}\nu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

Then run O2/O3 with the scalar weight

\[
\boxed{
W_\star
=
N_{\sm}|H|^2 |G_\star|^2.
}
\]

Its bandwidth is

\[
\boxed{
d_0(G_\star)
=
d_N+2d_H+2d_{G_\star}.
}
\]

---

# 5. Selection order guardrail

The component \(G_\star\) must be selected **before hard extraction**, using the smooth aggregate measure.

Do not choose \(G_\star=G_\star(p)\) atom by atom after extraction. That would create a moving wall selector and could destroy bounded branch-charge structure.

Correct order:

\[
\boxed{
\text{finite-family wall lower}
\to
\text{global scalar component selection}
\to
\text{O2/O3 with fixed scalar wall}
\to
\text{hard extraction}.
}
\]

If blockwise selection is necessary, partition blocks by their dominant component first:

\[
\Omega\in\mathcal B_j
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\int_\Omega |H|^2|G_j|^2
\ge
M^{-1}\int_\Omega |H|^2\sum_k|G_k|^2.
\]

There are only \(O(1)\) block classes. Choose one class with polynomial total mass and one fixed component \(G_j\). This is still pre-extraction and branch-compatible.

---

# 6. Scalar O2/O3 theorem needed

The O2/O3 theorem should be stated as:

For every scalar wall component \(G\) satisfying

\[
d_N+2d_H+2d_G\le d_{\max},
\]

the smooth weight

\[
N_{\sm}|H|^2|G|^2
\]

preserves the O2/O3 near-pure Schur lower.

Equivalently, the determinant survival reduces to

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}_{L^2(\mu_{\sub})}
\left(
1,\operatorname{span}
\{R_{\fold},R_{\fold}^2,\ldots,R_{\fold}^{d_0}\}
\right)
\ge Q^{-A},
}
\]

uniformly for all

\[
d_0\le d_{\max}.
\]

---

# 7. Finite coherent vector amplitude model

If a finite coherent vector amplitude model exists,

\[
\mathcal C_{2Y}^{\arith}\neq0,
\]

do **not** use the projected wall inside O2/O3 unless its branch-charge complexity is explicitly controlled.

Use the raw finite wall family for O2/O3. After O2/O3, apply vector amplitude Schur:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}_{L^2(\nu_{\bd})}
(F_{2Y},\mathcal C_{2Y}^{\arith})^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\|F_{2Y}\|^2.
}
\]

This keeps O2/O3 scalar and bounded-charge.

---

# 8. Degree risks

The only serious O2/O3 risks are:

## 8.1 Derivative components have unbounded branch charge

If \(D\Gamma_G\) or \(D^2\Gamma_G\) creates high branch complexity, the jet wall is not acceptable.

Then either prove \(\mathrm{LocalMarkov}_{\Gamma_G}\) and use the plain wall, or enlarge the transition basis if the growth remains \(O(1)\).

## 8.2 \(d_0>d_{\max}\)

If

\[
d_N+2d_H+2d_\Gamma>d_{\max},
\]

then raise \(d_{\max}\) and prove central transition visibility for the larger fixed degree.

This is acceptable only if the required \(d_{\max}\) remains \(O(1)\).

## 8.3 Scalar component mass appears only post-extraction

This is not allowed. The scalar component must be selected from smooth aggregate mass.

---

# 9. Recommended default

Use:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal G_{\wall}^{\jet}
=
\{\Gamma_G,D\Gamma_G,D^2\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\}
}
\]

as the robust rank-two wall family.

Prove O2/O3 uniformly for all scalar components of this family with

\[
d_0\le d_{\max}.
\]

If \(\mathrm{LocalMarkov}_{\Gamma_G}\) is later proven, downgrade to the plain family:

\[
\{\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\}.
\]

This choice keeps the proof robust and avoids making wall closure depend on LocalMarkov.

---

# 10. Final theorem statement

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{FiniteWallFamilyO2O3}.
}
\]

Let \(\mathcal G_{\wall}\) be a fixed finite wall family of \(O(1)\) scale-normalized components with branch charge \(\le d_\Gamma\). If

\[
d_N+2d_H+2d_\Gamma\le d_{\max},
\]

and if

\[
\int
N_{\sm}|H|^2\sum_{G\in\mathcal G_{\wall}}|G|^2\,d\nu
\ge Q^{-A}\nu(\mathcal C),
\]

then some fixed \(G_\star\in\mathcal G_{\wall}\) supports a scalar O2/O3 run with

\[
N_{\sm}|H|^2|G_\star|^2,
\]

losing only polynomial factors.

That is the correct O2/O3 interface for the rank-two D4 branch.

## Agent 6 continued attack — derivative wall branch-charge audit

The rank-two O2/O3 interface is now:

\[
\mathcal G_{\wall}^{\jet}
=
\{\Gamma_G,D\Gamma_G,D^2\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\},
\]

with

\[
W_{\bd}^{(2),\jet}
=
N_{\sm}|H|^2
\sum_{G\in\mathcal G_{\wall}^{\jet}}|G|^2.
\]

The remaining O2/O3 risk is whether adding

\[
D\Gamma_G,\quad D^2\Gamma_G
\]

keeps bounded branch charge. If yes, the jet wall is the robust default and avoids the separate \(\mathrm{LocalMarkov}_{\Gamma_G}\) theorem. If no, we must fall back to the plain wall plus LocalMarkov. The latest architecture explicitly allows a finite wall family and scalar component selection, but only when each component has bounded branch charge. 

---

# 1. Expand the jet components

Recall

\[
\Gamma_G=-6q''+36q-12q^3.
\]

Then

\[
D\Gamma_G
=
-6Dq''
+
36Dq
-
36q^2Dq.
\]

And

\[
D^2\Gamma_G
=
-6D^2q''
+
36D^2q
-
72q(Dq)^2
-
36q^2D^2q.
\]

So the jet wall uses the finite list

\[
q,\quad Dq,\quad D^2q,\quad q'',\quad Dq'',\quad D^2q'',\quad q^2Dq,\quad q(Dq)^2,\quad q^2D^2q.
\]

This is still a fixed-degree algebra generated by the local source chart. No new infinite branch family appears.

Thus the expected branch-charge conclusion is:

\[
\boxed{
d(D^j\Gamma_G)\le d(\Gamma_G)+O_j(1),\qquad j\le2.
}
\]

Since \(j\le2\) is fixed, the jet family remains \(O(1)\)-charge.

---

# 2. What derivatives can do to branch charge

There are three possible effects.

## 2.1 Differentiating amplitudes

If a branch term is

\[
a_b(m)e^{i\phi_b(m)},
\]

then

\[
D(a_be^{i\phi_b})
=
(Da_b+i(D\phi_b)a_b)e^{i\phi_b}.
\]

This changes the coefficient but not the branch phase label. So branch charge is unchanged.

## 2.2 Differentiating products

For products such as \(q^3\),

\[
D(q^3)=3q^2Dq,
\]

\[
D^2(q^3)=6q(Dq)^2+3q^2D^2q.
\]

The number of factors increases only up to a fixed degree. If \(q\) has branch-charge bandwidth \(d_q\), then \(q^3\) has bandwidth at most \(3d_q\), and derivatives do not increase this except by fixed coefficient labels.

Thus

\[
d(D^j(q^3))\le 3d_q+O(j).
\]

## 2.3 Differentiating scale-normalized charts

If \(D\) is the scale-normalized derivative on the transition chart, differentiation may introduce powers of the local scale but not additional branch sectors. Those powers belong to coefficient complexity, not branch charge.

So the jet wall remains bounded-charge.

---

# 3. Degree estimate for the jet wall

Let

\[
d_q
\]

be the branch-charge degree of the local \(q\)-source representation, and let

\[
d_{q''}
\]

be the degree of \(q''\). Since \(q''\) is obtained by differentiating \(q\) twice, it should have the same branch support, with differentiated coefficients. Thus usually

\[
d_{q''}=d_q.
\]

Then

\[
d_{\Gamma_G}
\le
\max(d_{q''},d_q,3d_q)
=
3d_q.
\]

Similarly,

\[
d_{D\Gamma_G}\le3d_q+O(1),
\]

\[
d_{D^2\Gamma_G}\le3d_q+O(1).
\]

For

\[
\Gamma_{G,2}=q^{(4)}-6(1-q^2)q'',
\]

we have terms

\[
q^{(4)},\quad q'',\quad q^2q''.
\]

So

\[
d_{\Gamma_{G,2}}
\le
\max(d_q,3d_q)
=
3d_q
\]

up to fixed derivative bookkeeping.

Therefore

\[
\boxed{
d_{\Gamma,\jet}
=
\max_{G\in\mathcal G_{\wall}^{\jet}}d_G
\le 3d_q+O(1).
}
\]

If the current branch bookkeeping has \(d_q\le 2\) or \(3\), then \(d_{\Gamma,\jet}\) remains within a plausible \(d_{\max}=14\). The exact value must be checked against the project’s branch-charge convention, but there is no structural blow-up.

---

# 4. Consequence for \(d_0\)

The jet wall bandwidth is

\[
d_0^{(2),\jet}
=
d_N+2d_H+2d_{\Gamma,\jet}.
\]

Using the estimate above,

\[
d_0^{(2),\jet}
\le
d_N+2d_H+6d_q+O(1).
\]

So the finite question is now:

\[
\boxed{
d_N+2d_H+6d_q+O(1)\le d_{\max}?
}
\]

If yes, keep

\[
d_{\max}=14.
\]

If no, either:

1. increase \(d_{\max}\) to the required fixed value; or  
2. use the plain wall plus \(\mathrm{LocalMarkov}_{\Gamma_G}\) to lower \(d_{\Gamma}\).

This is now a finite bookkeeping audit, not a conceptual obstacle.

---

# 5. Possible simplification: dyadic scalar component choice reduces degree pressure

The jet wall family is finite:

\[
\{\Gamma_G,D\Gamma_G,D^2\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\}.
\]

After the finite-family wall lower, one scalar component

\[
G_\star
\]

is selected. O2/O3 only needs the bandwidth of that selected component.

Thus even if

\[
D^2\Gamma_G
\]

has the largest degree, it matters only if it is the selected component.

In a proof, we can partition into four scalar cases:

\[
G_\star=\Gamma_G,
\quad
G_\star=D\Gamma_G,
\quad
G_\star=D^2\Gamma_G,
\quad
G_\star=\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

Then run O2/O3 conditionally for each component. The theorem need only support the maximum degree across these four. Again, finite.

---

# 6. Recommended O2/O3 theorem statement

The best O2/O3 statement is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{BoundedChargeGraph/O2O3}_{23}(d_{\max})
}
\]

For every scalar wall \(G\) in a fixed finite family with

\[
d_N+2d_H+2d_G\le d_{\max},
\]

the O2/O3 near-pure Schur lower survives under the smooth weight

\[
N_{\sm}|H|^2|G|^2.
\]

Then both plain and jet rank-two wall choices are covered automatically.

This avoids re-proving O2/O3 for each wall.

---

# 7. Jet wall and amplitude Schur

If a finite coherent vector amplitude model exists, and we use the jet family, the amplitude model should also be lifted consistently.

The vector target becomes

\[
F_{2Y}^{\jet}
=
H(\Gamma_G,D\Gamma_G,D^2\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})
\]

or the corresponding D4-normalized version.

A coherent amplitude model

\[
\mathcal C_{2Y}^{\arith}
\]

must be derivative-lifted with the same coefficients if it is meant to absorb jet components.

This is the same guardrail as the trap-Schur layer:

\[
\boxed{
\text{same coefficient vector across derivative components.}
}
\]

Do not allow independent coherent amplitudes for \(\Gamma_G\), \(D\Gamma_G\), and \(D^2\Gamma_G\). That would create artificial local freedom.

So if jet wall is used, the vector amplitude-Schur model must be:

\[
\operatorname{span}
\{(\psi_a,D\psi_a,D^2\psi_a,\chi_a)\}_a
\]

with coherent derivative linkage, not independent spans at each derivative level.

---

# 8. Strategic recommendation update

The default should now be:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Use jet wall if }d_{\Gamma,\jet}\le d_{\max}\text{ can be verified.}
}
\]

If this finite degree audit passes, we no longer need to prove \(\mathrm{LocalMarkov}_{\Gamma_G}\) before using O2/O3.

If the degree audit fails, use:

\[
\boxed{
\text{plain wall + LocalMarkov}_{\Gamma_G}.
}
\]

Thus the next immediate finite audit is:

\[
\boxed{
d_{\Gamma,\jet}\le d_{\max}-d_N-2d_H \over 2.
}
\]

More concretely:

\[
\boxed{
d_0^{(2),\jet}=d_N+2d_H+2d_{\Gamma,\jet}\le14?
}
\]

If yes, jet wall should be the robust path.

---

# 9. Current conclusion

The rank-two O2/O3 wall issue is now reduced to finite branch-charge bookkeeping.

There are two viable paths:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Path A: jet wall, no LocalMarkov needed, slightly larger }d_0.
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Path B: plain wall, LocalMarkov needed, smaller }d_0.
}
\]

I now favor Path A as default **if** the derivative branch-charge audit stays within \(d_{\max}\). It is analytically safer and still structurally finite.

## Agent 6 continued attack — choose jet wall as default, but prevent derivative-amplitude overfitting

The rank-two route currently has two wall options:

\[
\mathcal G_{\wall}^{\plain}
=
\{\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\},
\]

or

\[
\mathcal G_{\wall}^{\jet}
=
\{\Gamma_G,D\Gamma_G,D^2\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\}.
\]

I now think the jet wall should be the default **provided** its branch-charge degree stays below the fixed \(d_{\max}\). It directly supplies the \(H^2/L^2\) norms needed by RankTwoCoercivity and avoids the extra LocalMarkov theorem. The main new guardrail is that any coherent amplitude model must be derivative-linked, not independently fitted at each derivative level.

---

# 1. Jet wall gives the right coercivity input

RankTwoCoercivity wants control of

\[
\|E_1\|_{H^2(I)}+\|E_2\|_{L^2(I)},
\]

where

\[
E_1=q''-(6q-2q^3),
\qquad
E_2=q^{(4)}-6(1-q^2)q''.
\]

Since

\[
\Gamma_G=-6E_1,
\qquad
\Gamma_{G,2}=E_2,
\]

the natural wall is

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal G_{\wall}^{\jet}
=
\{\Gamma_G,D\Gamma_G,D^2\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\}.
}
\]

Then small jet wall means directly:

\[
\Gamma_G\text{ small in }H^2,
\qquad
\Gamma_{G,2}\text{ small in }L^2.
\]

So the analytic wall closure becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal G_{\wall}^{\jet}\text{ small}
\Rightarrow
q''\text{ small}
\Rightarrow
\text{Gate--1 contradiction}.
}
\]

No LocalMarkov bridge is needed.

---

# 2. Branch-charge audit for jet wall

The jet wall is acceptable if

\[
d_0^{(2),\jet}
=
d_N+2d_H+2d_{\Gamma,\jet}
\le d_{\max}.
\]

Here

\[
d_{\Gamma,\jet}
=
\max\{
d(\Gamma_G),
d(D\Gamma_G),
d(D^2\Gamma_G),
d(\Gamma_{G,2})
\}.
\]

The finite audit is:

\[
\boxed{
d_{\Gamma,\jet}=O(1)
}
\]

and preferably

\[
\boxed{
d_N+2d_H+2d_{\Gamma,\jet}\le14.
}
\]

Why this is plausible:

\[
\Gamma_G=-6q''+36q-12q^3.
\]

Then

\[
D\Gamma_G=-6Dq''+36Dq-36q^2Dq,
\]

\[
D^2\Gamma_G=-6D^2q''+36D^2q-72q(Dq)^2-36q^2D^2q.
\]

These terms live in the fixed-degree algebra generated by \(q,Dq,D^2q,q'',Dq'',D^2q''\). Derivatives change coefficients and add fixed algebraic products, but they do not create a new unbounded family of branch sectors.

So derivative wall branch charge should remain bounded.

---

# 3. O2/O3 stays scalar by finite-family reduction

Even with the jet wall, O2/O3 does not need to be vector-valued.

If

\[
\int N_{\sm}|H|^2
\sum_{G\in\mathcal G_{\wall}^{\jet}}|G|^2\,d\nu
\ge Q^{-A}\nu(\mathcal C),
\]

then one fixed component

\[
G_\star\in
\{\Gamma_G,D\Gamma_G,D^2\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\}
\]

has

\[
\int N_{\sm}|H|^2|G_\star|^2\,d\nu
\ge Q^{-A'}\nu(\mathcal C).
\]

Choose \(G_\star\) at the smooth aggregate stage, before hard extraction.

Then run O2/O3 with scalar weight

\[
N_{\sm}|H|^2|G_\star|^2.
\]

The only O2/O3 theorem needed is uniform scalar O2/O3 for all \(G\) with

\[
d_N+2d_H+2d_G\le d_{\max}.
\]

---

# 4. Derivative amplitude guardrail

If a finite coherent amplitude model exists, rank two with jet wall should not allow independent coherent amplitudes for each derivative component.

Suppose a coherent amplitude basis contributes to the non-jet vector as

\[
\phi_a
\]

in the \(\Gamma_G\)-component and

\[
\chi_a
\]

in the \(\Gamma_{G,2}\)-component.

Then the jet-lifted coherent model must use linked coefficients:

\[
\boxed{
(\phi_a,D\phi_a,D^2\phi_a,\chi_a)
}
\]

with the same coefficient \(c_a\) across all derivative components.

Thus the coherent jet amplitude space is

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal C_{2Y,\jet}^{\arith}
=
\operatorname{span}
\{(\phi_a,D\phi_a,D^2\phi_a,\chi_a)\}_a.
}
\]

Forbidden model:

\[
\operatorname{span}\{\phi_a\}
\oplus
\operatorname{span}\{D\phi_a\}
\oplus
\operatorname{span}\{D^2\phi_a\}
\oplus
\operatorname{span}\{\chi_a\}
\]

with independent coefficients in each slot. That would create artificial derivative-level local freedom.

This is the same “same coefficient vector across derivatives” guardrail from TrapSchur, now applied earlier to vector amplitude Schur.

---

# 5. Jet vector amplitude Schur

If

\[
\mathcal C_{2Y,\jet}^{\arith}\neq0,
\]

the amplitude theorem becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}_{L^2(\nu)}
\left(
H(\Gamma_G,D\Gamma_G,D^2\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}),
\mathcal C_{2Y,\jet}^{\arith}
\right)^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\|H(\Gamma_G,D\Gamma_G,D^2\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})\|^2.
}
\]

If this fails, then with coherent forcing

\[
E_1\approx \sum_a c_a\phi_a,
\]

\[
E_1'\approx \sum_a c_a\phi_a',
\]

\[
E_1''\approx \sum_a c_a\phi_a'',
\]

\[
E_2\approx \sum_a c_a\chi_a.
\]

Using

\[
E_2-E_1''=-12q(q')^2,
\]

we get

\[
q(q')^2
\approx
-\frac{1}{12}
\sum_a c_a(\chi_a-\phi_a'').
\]

This is a very rigid coherent forcing model, not a free local fit.

So even if amplitude Schur is needed, the jet linkage makes the fallback controlled.

---

# 6. Plain wall remains optimization, not default

Plain wall:

\[
\{\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\}
\]

is attractive because it has smaller \(d_0\). But it requires

\[
\mathrm{LocalMarkov}_{\Gamma_G}:
\quad
\|\Gamma_G\|_{H^2(I)}
\le Q^C\|\Gamma_G\|_{L^2(I^*)}.
\]

This is plausible, but it is another theorem.

Jet wall removes that theorem at the cost of a finite derivative-degree audit.

So the decision rule is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{If }d_0^{(2),\jet}\le d_{\max},
\text{ use jet wall by default.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{If not, prove LocalMarkov and use the plain wall.}
}
\]

---

# 7. Updated rank-two downstream stack

Assuming \(B_Y=0\) and jet degree is acceptable:

\[
\boxed{
B_Y=0
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\Rightarrow
F_{2Y}^{\jet}
=
H(\Gamma_G,D\Gamma_G,D^2\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\Rightarrow
\mathrm{RankTwoCoercivity}
+
\mathrm{Gate1}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\Rightarrow
\int N_{\sm}|H|^2
\sum_{G\in\mathcal G_{\wall}^{\jet}}|G|^2
\ge Q^{-A}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\Rightarrow
\text{choose scalar }G_\star\text{ by smooth pigeonhole}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\Rightarrow
\mathrm{O2/O3}
\text{ with }N_{\sm}|H|^2|G_\star|^2.
}
\]

This is now a fully scalar-compatible O2/O3 interface.

---

# 8. Remaining finite audits

The remaining rank-two wall-side audits are:

1. Compute/confirm

\[
d_{\Gamma,\jet}
\]

under the branch-charge convention.

2. Verify

\[
d_N+2d_H+2d_{\Gamma,\jet}\le d_{\max}.
\]

3. If finite coherent amplitudes exist, define the derivative-linked amplitude model

\[
\mathcal C_{2Y,\jet}^{\arith}
\]

with same coefficients across derivative slots.

4. Prove scalar O2/O3 uniformly for all jet components.

These are finite bookkeeping/model-space tasks, not conceptual obstructions.

---

# 9. Current conclusion

The preferred rank-two downstream wall is now:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal G_{\wall}^{\jet}
=
\{\Gamma_G,D\Gamma_G,D^2\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\}.

This makes RankTwoCoercivity direct and leaves O2/O3 scalar by finite-family pigeonhole.

## Agent 6 continued attack — correction: jet wall is not automatically a D4 wall

I need to correct the prior rank-two wall recommendation.

The jet family

\[
\mathcal G_{\wall}^{\jet}
=
\{\Gamma_G,D\Gamma_G,D^2\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\}
\]

is analytically convenient for proving coercivity, because RankTwoCoercivity wants

\[
\Gamma_G\in H^2,
\qquad
\Gamma_{G,2}\in L^2.
\]

But the D4 local output, under \(B_Y=0\), directly gives only

\[
F_{2Y}
=
H
\begin{pmatrix}
c_7\Gamma_G\\
\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}
\end{pmatrix}
\asymp
H(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}).
\]

It does **not** automatically give

\[
D\Gamma_G,\qquad D^2\Gamma_G
\]

as forbidden D4 channels.

So the default D4 wall must remain the **plain vector wall** unless we can prove a derivative-channel theorem.

This is an important correction to the previous step. The finite wall family abstraction is valid only for wall components that are legitimately available to the forbidden lower, or for analytic components that can be controlled from the legitimate D4 components.

---

# 1. Correct rank-two wall

The legitimate rank-two D4 wall is

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal G_{\wall}^{D4}
=
\{\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\}.
}
\]

The D4 lower is

\[
\boxed{
\|F_{2Y}\|^2
\asymp
|H|^2
\left(
|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\right).
}
\]

This is the wall that can feed extraction and O2/O3 directly.

Therefore O2/O3 should use

\[
\boxed{
W_{\bd}^{(2)}
=
N_{\sm}|H|^2
\left(
|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\right).
}
\]

Then finite-family pigeonhole reduces this to a scalar component

\[
G_\star\in\{\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\}
\]

chosen at the smooth aggregate stage.

---

# 2. Where the jet family belongs

The jet family belongs to the **analytic coercivity proof**, not automatically to the D4 wall.

It can be used in one of two legitimate ways.

## Option A — analytic auxiliary via LocalMarkov

Use the legitimate D4 wall

\[
\{\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\}.
\]

Then prove

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{LocalMarkov}_{\Gamma_G}:
\quad
\|\Gamma_G\|_{H^2(I)}
\le
Q^C
\|\Gamma_G\|_{L^2(I^*)}.
}
\]

This upgrades the D4-supplied \(L^2\) control of \(\Gamma_G\) to the \(H^2\) control required by RankTwoCoercivity.

Then the analytic chain is valid:

\[
\|\Gamma_G\|_{L^2}
+
\|\Gamma_{G,2}\|_{L^2}
\text{ small}
\]

\[
\Longrightarrow
\|\Gamma_G\|_{H^2}
+
\|\Gamma_{G,2}\|_{L^2}
\text{ small}
\]

\[
\Longrightarrow
\|q''\|_{L^2}\text{ small}.
\]

This is the cleanest route if LocalMarkov holds.

## Option B — prove derivative D4 channels

If we want to use

\[
D\Gamma_G,\quad D^2\Gamma_G
\]

as wall components in O2/O3, we need a new finite theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{DerivativeD4Channels}_{2Y}.
}
\]

It would state that the local D4 obstruction, perhaps after neighboring-block difference quotients or differentiated singular extractors, sees

\[
H D\Gamma_G,\qquad H D^2\Gamma_G
\]

as forbidden components with polynomial normalization.

Without such a theorem, putting derivative terms into the D4 wall is not justified.

---

# 3. Revised wall closure theorem

The correct rank-two closure should be:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{PlainWallRankTwoClosure}.
}
\]

Hypotheses:

\[
H(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}).
\]

2. LocalMarkov:

\[
\|\Gamma_G\|_{H^2(I)}
\le
Q^C
\|\Gamma_G\|_{L^2(I^*)}.
\]

3. RankTwoCoercivity:

\[
\|q''\|_{L^2(I)}
\le
Q^C
\left(
\|\Gamma_G\|_{H^2(I)}
+
\|\Gamma_{G,2}\|_{L^2(I)}
\right)^\theta.
\]

4. Gate–1:

\[
\|q''\|_{L^2(I)}\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Conclusion:

\[
\boxed{
\|\Gamma_G\|_{L^2(I^*)}
+
\|\Gamma_{G,2}\|_{L^2(I)}
\ge Q^{-A'}.
}
\]

Thus the legitimate D4 wall has mass.

---

# 4. LocalMarkov becomes necessary, not optional

Given the correction above, \(\mathrm{LocalMarkov}_{\Gamma_G}\) is no longer merely an optimization if we want the plain D4 wall.

It is the analytic bridge between:

\[
\text{D4 supplies }\Gamma_G\text{ in }L^2
\]

and

\[
\text{coercivity needs }\Gamma_G\text{ in }H^2.
\]

So the rank-two analytic package is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{LocalMarkov}_{\Gamma_G}
+
\mathrm{RankTwoCoercivity}
+
\mathrm{Gate1}.
}
\]

The jet wall remains a fallback only if derivative D4 channels are separately established.

---

# 5. Is LocalMarkov plausible?

Yes, still plausible.

\[
\Gamma_G=-6q''+36q-12q^3
\]

belongs to the finite algebra generated by the local EF source chart. If the local chart has polynomial Riesz/Markov constants, then the fixed-degree algebra closure should satisfy

\[
\|\Gamma_G\|_{H^2(I)}
\le
Q^C
\|\Gamma_G\|_{L^2(I^*)}.
\]

The important point is that this must be proved for \(\Gamma_G\) as a function class element, not for \(q,q'',q^3\) separately. Cancellations inside \(\Gamma_G\) are harmless if \(\Gamma_G\) itself lies in a finite-dimensional Markov class.

A sufficient theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\Gamma_G\in \mathcal W_I,
\qquad
\dim\mathcal W_I\le Q^C,
\qquad
\mathrm{Markov}(\mathcal W_I)\le Q^C.
}
\]

where

\[
\mathcal W_I
=
D^2\mathcal V_I+\mathcal V_I+\mathrm{Sym}^3\mathcal V_I
\]

for the local chart space \(\mathcal V_I\).

---

# 6. O2/O3 wall family reverts to plain rank-two

The finite wall family for O2/O3 should now be

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal G_{\wall}^{(2)}
=
\{\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\}.
}
\]

The bandwidth is

\[
\boxed{
d_0^{(2)}
=
d_N+2d_H+2\max(d_{\Gamma_G},d_{\Gamma_{G,2}}).
}
\]

This is the wall family directly supported by D4.

The scalar component selection remains valid:

if

\[
\int N_{\sm}|H|^2
\left(
|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\right)d\nu
\ge Q^{-A},
\]

then either

\[
\Gamma_G
\]

or

\[
\Gamma_{G,2}
\]

carries polynomial mass. Choose that scalar component before hard extraction.

---

# 7. When can the jet wall be used?

The jet wall can be used only under one of these added theorems.

## 7.1 DerivativeD4Channels

D4 or a legitimate differentiated D4 extractor gives forbidden channels

\[
D\Gamma_G,\quad D^2\Gamma_G.
\]

Then the jet family is a true D4 wall.

## 7.2 Block-difference D4 propagation

If D4 gives \(\Gamma_G\) on neighboring atoms and the block structure controls differences, then finite differences of the D4 wall might supply

\[
D\Gamma_G,\quad D^2\Gamma_G.
\]

This would be a block-level derivative-channel theorem.

## 7.3 Pure analytic auxiliary only

If neither holds, jet terms are only analytic auxiliaries and cannot be placed inside the D4/O2O3 wall weight.

## Agent 6 continued attack — LocalMarkov\(_{\Gamma_G}\) is now the analytic bottleneck

After the correction, the honest rank-two wall is the plain D4 wall:

\[
\mathcal G_{\wall}^{(2)}=\{\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\}.
\]

The jet family

\[
\{\Gamma_G,D\Gamma_G,D^2\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\}

B_Y=0

then

\[
\mathrm{LocalMarkov}_{\Gamma_G}
+

\Longrightarrow

\boxed{

The latest coordinator synthesis explicitly put the rank-two branch on this exactness/coercivity track rather than the rank-one moving-wall tower. 

---

# 1. Why LocalMarkov is plausible

We have

\[
\Gamma_G=-6q''+36q-12q^3.
\]

The issue is that \(\Gamma_G\) is nonlinear in \(q\). But a Markov estimate is a statement about the **function class containing \(\Gamma_G\)**, not about inverting the expression in terms of \(q\).

If \(q\) belongs locally to a finite chart class

\[
\mathcal V_I,
\]

then

\[
q'',\quad q,\quad q^3
\]

belong to the finite algebraic closure

\[
\mathcal W_I
=
D^2\mathcal V_I+\mathcal V_I+\operatorname{Sym}^3\mathcal V_I.
\]

Thus

\[
\Gamma_G\in\mathcal W_I.
\]

If \(\mathcal W_I\) has polynomial Riesz/Markov constants, then

\[
\|\Gamma_G\|_{H^2(I)}
\le
Q^C
\|\Gamma_G\|_{L^2(I^*)}.
\]

So LocalMarkov reduces to:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal W_I
\text{ has polynomial dimension and polynomial Markov constants.}
}
\]

This is a finite-algebra lemma, not a zeta-specific miracle.

---

# 2. Finite-dimensional algebra closure

Assume local source representation:

\[
q|_{I^*}\in\mathcal V_I,
\qquad
\dim\mathcal V_I\le Q^C.
\]

If \(\mathcal V_I\) has basis

\[
\varphi_1,\ldots,\varphi_N,
\]

then

\[
q^3\in \operatorname{Sym}^3\mathcal V_I,
\]

whose dimension is

\[
\binom{N+2}{3}.
\]

If \(N=O(1)\), trivial. If \(N\le Q^C\), still polynomial.

Then

\[
\mathcal W_I
=
D^2\mathcal V_I+\mathcal V_I+\operatorname{Sym}^3\mathcal V_I
\]

has

\[
\dim\mathcal W_I\le Q^C.
\]

The remaining condition is the Riesz/Markov bound:

\[
\boxed{
\|D^j w\|_{L^2(I)}
\le Q^C\|w\|_{L^2(I^*)},
\qquad
w\in\mathcal W_I,\quad 0\le j\le2.
}
\]

This should follow if \(\mathcal V_I\) is a controlled local EF/AFE chart with polynomially bounded frequencies/scales and \(I^*\) is a fixed enlargement of \(I\).

---

# 3. The exact theorem to ask for

The theorem should be:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{AlgebraMarkov}_{3}(I,I^*).
}
\]

Let \(\mathcal V_I\) be the local source chart class. Suppose:

\[
\dim\mathcal V_I\le Q^C,
\]

\[
\|D^jv\|_{L^2(I)}
\le Q^C\|v\|_{L^2(I^*)},
\qquad
v\in\mathcal V_I,\quad 0\le j\le4,
\]

and product/Riesz control holds through degree \(3\). Then for

\[
\mathcal W_I
=
D^2\mathcal V_I+\mathcal V_I+\operatorname{Sym}^3\mathcal V_I,
\]

we have

\[
\boxed{
\|w\|_{H^2(I)}
\le Q^C\|w\|_{L^2(I^*)},
\qquad
w\in\mathcal W_I.
}
\]

Apply to

\[
w=\Gamma_G.
\]

This gives LocalMarkov.

---

# 4. Why component cancellation is harmless

One might worry that

\[
-6q''+36q-12q^3
\]

can have cancellations, making \(\Gamma_G\) small while the components are large.

That does **not** harm Markov. Markov says:

\[
\|\Gamma_G\|_{H^2}
\le Q^C\|\Gamma_G\|_{L^2}.
\]

It is a property of \(\Gamma_G\) as an element of \(\mathcal W_I\). It does not require bounding \(q\), \(q''\), and \(q^3\) separately by \(\|\Gamma_G\|\).

Thus the finite-dimensional space must contain \(\Gamma_G\) itself and have a stable Riesz/Markov norm. Once that holds, internal cancellation is irrelevant.

---

# 5. Residual/completion issue

If the actual \(q\) is not exactly in \(\mathcal V_I\), write

\[
q=q_X+r_X.
\]

Then

\[
\Gamma_G(q)
=
\Gamma_G(q_X)
+
D\Gamma_G[q_X](r_X)
+
\text{quadratic/cubic residual terms}.
\]

The Markov theorem applies to \(\Gamma_G(q_X)\). The residual terms require a wall-residual estimate:

\[
\boxed{
\|\Gamma_G(q)-\Gamma_G(q_X)\|_{H^2(I)}
\le Q^{-B}.
}
\]

This is much weaker than full \(K_9\)-completion. It is only a local nonlinear source-wall residual estimate.

If this estimate is unavailable, the rank-two wall closure must be stated for the finite source model first, with an additional completion layer.

---

# 6. RankTwoCoercivity then closes the wall

Assume:

\[
\|\Gamma_G\|_{L^2(I^*)}
+
\|\Gamma_{G,2}\|_{L^2(I)}
\le \delta.
\]

By LocalMarkov,

\[
\|\Gamma_G\|_{H^2(I)}
\le Q^C\delta.
\]

Then RankTwoCoercivity gives

\[
\|q''\|_{L^2(I)}
\le Q^C\delta^\theta.
\]

Gate–1 gives

\[
\|q''\|_{L^2(I)}
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Therefore

\[
\delta\ge Q^{-A'}.
\]

So

\[
\boxed{
\|\Gamma_G\|_{L^2(I^*)}
+
\|\Gamma_{G,2}\|_{L^2(I)}
\ge Q^{-A'}.
}
\]

This is exactly the plain rank-two wall lower.

---

# 7. Relation to O2/O3

Once the plain wall lower is known:

\[
\int
N_{\sm}|H|^2
\left(
|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\right)
\,d\nu
\ge Q^{-A}\nu(\mathcal C),
\]

choose by smooth aggregate pigeonhole either:

\[
G_\star=\Gamma_G
\]

or

\[
G_\star=\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

Then run scalar O2/O3 with

\[
N_{\sm}|H|^2|G_\star|^2.
\]

The bandwidth is

\[
d_0^{(2)}
=
d_N+2d_H+2\max(d_{\Gamma_G},d_{\Gamma_{G,2}}).
\]

No derivative wall components enter O2/O3.

---

# 8. Fallback: derivative D4 channels

If LocalMarkov fails, there are two possible fallback routes.

## 8.1 Prove derivative D4 channels

Show D4 also supplies legitimate forbidden components

\[
HD\Gamma_G,\qquad HD^2\Gamma_G.
\]

Then the jet family becomes a true D4 wall.

## 8.2 Block-difference propagation

Use D4 wall values on neighboring atoms to control finite differences of \(\Gamma_G\). This would make the derivative wall a consequence of multiple legitimate D4 observations, not an added wall.

Both are extra theorems. Until then, jet terms are analytic auxiliaries only.

---

# 9. Current theorem stack

The rank-two branch now has two hard but clean components.

## Local algebra

\[
(I-P_{D_{2Y}})A_{2Y}=0,
\]

\[
\lambda_{\min}
(L_{2Y}^*(I-P_{D_{2Y}})L_{2Y})
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

This gives

\[
B_Y=0.
\]

## Analytic wall closure

\[
\mathrm{AlgebraMarkov}_{\Gamma_G}
+
\mathrm{RankTwoCoercivity}
+
\mathrm{Gate1}.
\]

This gives wall mass for the honest D4 wall

\[
\{\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\}.
\]

---

# 10. Current conclusion

The rank-two path remains the best route, but the jet wall should not be used as a D4/O2O3 wall without a derivative-channel theorem.

The corrected default is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{plain rank-two D4 wall}
+
\mathrm{LocalMarkov}_{\Gamma_G}
+
\mathrm{RankTwoCoercivity}.
}
\]

So the next analytic target is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{AlgebraMarkov}_{\Gamma_G}
}
\]

for the finite algebraic closure containing

\[
\Gamma_G=-6q''+36q-12q^3.
\]

## Assistant (2026-04-28 13:58:34)

_Thought for 12s_

I’ll keep the current convention:

\[
\rho_Y=\text{lower }Y12\text{ graph slope},\qquad
\rho_\Gamma=\frac{\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7}{\alpha_9},\qquad
R_{\fold}(x)=\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}.
\]

## Agent 6 continued attack — proving \(\mathrm{AlgebraMarkov}_{\Gamma_G}\) without overclaiming

The current rank-two route is:

\[
B_Y=0
\Longrightarrow
F_{2Y}\asymp H(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})
\]

and then

\[
\mathrm{AlgebraMarkov}_{\Gamma_G}
+
\mathrm{RankTwoCoercivity}
+
\mathrm{Gate1}
\Longrightarrow
\text{rank-two wall lower}.
\]

The correction from the prior step is important: the honest D4 wall is only

\[
\{\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\},
\]

not automatically the jet family. Derivative terms are analytic auxiliaries unless a derivative-D4 theorem is separately proved. 

So the analytic target is now:

\[
\boxed{
\|\Gamma_G\|_{H^2(I)}
\le Q^C\|\Gamma_G\|_{L^2(I^*)}.
}
\]

---

# 1. The correct function class for \(\Gamma_G\)

We should not try to prove Markov by estimating

\[
q,\quad q'',\quad q^3
\]

separately from \(\Gamma_G\). That would fail under internal cancellation.

Instead define the class

\[
\mathcal W_I
:=
D^2\mathcal V_I+\mathcal V_I+\operatorname{Sym}^3\mathcal V_I,
\]

where \(\mathcal V_I\) is the local finite source chart containing \(q\).

Then

\[
\Gamma_G=-6q''+36q-12q^3
\in \mathcal W_I.
\]

The desired theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\|w\|_{H^2(I)}
\le Q^C\|w\|_{L^2(I^*)}
\qquad
(w\in\mathcal W_I).
}
\]

Then apply it to \(w=\Gamma_G\).

This avoids any need to invert the nonlinear expression for \(\Gamma_G\).

---

# 2. Sufficient hypotheses

A proof-quality \(\mathrm{AlgebraMarkov}_{\Gamma_G}\) can be stated with four inputs.

## 2.1 Finite chart dimension

\[
\dim\mathcal V_I\le Q^C.
\]

## 2.2 Riesz stability on an enlarged block

There is a basis of \(\mathcal V_I\) whose Gram matrices on \(I\) and \(I^*\) have condition number at most \(Q^C\).

Equivalently,

\[
\|v\|_{L^2(I^*)}
\]

is a stable norm for \(v\in\mathcal V_I\).

## 2.3 Markov bounds on \(\mathcal V_I\)

For fixed \(0\le j\le 4\),

\[
\|D^jv\|_{L^2(I)}
\le Q^C\|v\|_{L^2(I^*)}
\qquad(v\in\mathcal V_I).
\]

The \(j\le4\) bound is enough because \(\Gamma_G\) contains \(q''\), and we need two further derivatives of \(\Gamma_G\).

## 2.4 Algebra closure through degree \(3\)

The product space

\[
\operatorname{Sym}^3\mathcal V_I
\]

has polynomial Riesz/Markov constants on \(I\subset I^*\).

Then

\[
\mathcal W_I
\]

inherits polynomial Markov constants.

---

# 3. Why algebra closure is plausible

If

\[
\dim \mathcal V_I=N\le Q^C,
\]

then

\[
\dim\operatorname{Sym}^3\mathcal V_I
=
\binom{N+2}{3}
\le Q^C
\]

after changing the polynomial exponent.

Differentiating products uses Leibniz:

\[
D(q^3)=3q^2Dq,
\]

\[
D^2(q^3)=6q(Dq)^2+3q^2D^2q.
\]

So derivative order stays fixed, and product degree stays at most \(3\). There is no unbounded branch cascade.

The only real requirement is that the local chart algebra has polynomial Riesz constants. This is the same kind of finite-dimensional control already needed for central routing and O2/O3.

---

# 4. Residual version

If the actual source is not exactly finite-chart but

\[
q=q_X+r_X,
\]

then

\[
\Gamma_G(q)
=
\Gamma_G(q_X)
+
\mathcal L_{q_X}(r_X)
+
\mathcal Q(q_X,r_X)
+
\mathcal C(r_X),
\]

where

\[
\mathcal L_{q_X}(r_X)
=
-6r_X''+36r_X-36q_X^2r_X,
\]

and \(\mathcal Q,\mathcal C\) are quadratic/cubic residual terms.

Then the Markov estimate becomes

\[
\|\Gamma_G(q)\|_{H^2(I)}
\le
Q^C\|\Gamma_G(q)\|_{L^2(I^*)}
+
\mathrm{Err}_{\Gamma_G}(I).
\]

The needed residual theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{\Gamma_G}:
\quad
\mathrm{Err}_{\Gamma_G}(I)\le Q^{-B}
}
\]

on routed blocks.

This is much weaker than a full \(K_9\)-type completion. It controls only the nonlinear rank-two wall component \(\Gamma_G\).

---

# 5. How this feeds RankTwoCoercivity

Assume

\[
\|\Gamma_G\|_{L^2(I^*)}
+
\|\Gamma_{G,2}\|_{L^2(I)}
\le \delta.
\]

By AlgebraMarkov,

\[
\|\Gamma_G\|_{H^2(I)}
\le Q^C\delta+\mathrm{Err}_{\Gamma_G}.
\]

Then RankTwoCoercivity gives

\[
\|q''\|_{L^2(I)}
\le
Q^C
\left(
\|\Gamma_G\|_{H^2(I)}
+
\|\Gamma_{G,2}\|_{L^2(I)}
\right)^\theta.
\]

So

\[
\|q''\|_{L^2(I)}
\le
Q^C(\delta+\mathrm{Err}_{\Gamma_G})^\theta.
\]

Gate–1 supplies

\[
\|q''\|_{L^2(I)}\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Therefore

\[
\delta\ge Q^{-A'}
\]

unless the residual error is too large.

This proves the rank-two plain wall lower.

---

# 6. Where the proof can fail

## 6.1 The local chart is not finite-dimensional enough

If \(\mathcal V_I\) has dimension or frequency content too large, \(\mathcal W_I\) may not have polynomial Markov constants.

Then the plain wall route fails, and we need either derivative-D4 channels or a different local chart.

## 6.2 Product space is ill-conditioned

Even if \(\mathcal V_I\) is well-conditioned, \(\operatorname{Sym}^3\mathcal V_I\) might have high leverage or near-linear dependence on the routed interval.

Then \(\Gamma_G\in\mathcal W_I\) but Markov constants may be too large.

This is a finite Riesz audit.

## 6.3 Residual is not controlled in \(H^2\)

If \(r_X\) is small in \(L^2\) but not enough to control

\[
r_X'',\quad q_X^2r_X,
\]

then \(\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{\Gamma_G}\) fails.

Then rank-two wall closure needs stronger local completion.

## 6.4 \(I^*\) cannot be used

If the block enlargement \(I^*\) crosses transition-chart boundaries or zero-side singularities, the Markov estimate may fail.

Then use smaller blocks or a partition with bounded overlap.

---

# 7. The theorem to assign to another agent

The next precise analytic theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{AlgebraMarkov}_{3,\mathrm{EF}}.
}
\]

For every routed block pair \(I\subset I^*\), let \(\mathcal V_I\) be the local finite EF chart. Define

\[
\mathcal W_I=D^2\mathcal V_I+\mathcal V_I+\operatorname{Sym}^3\mathcal V_I.
\]

Then

\[
\|w\|_{H^2(I)}
\le Q^C\|w\|_{L^2(I^*)}
\qquad(w\in\mathcal W_I).
\]

Additionally, the actual source satisfies

\[
\operatorname{dist}_{H^2(I)}
(\Gamma_G(q),\mathcal W_I)
\le Q^{-B}.
\]

This gives \(\mathrm{LocalMarkov}_{\Gamma_G}\).

---

# 8. Current status

The rank-two branch now needs two main certificates:

Local algebra:

\[
(I-P_{D_{2Y}})A_{2Y}=0,
\]

\[
\lambda_{\min}
(L_{2Y}^*(I-P_{D_{2Y}})L_{2Y})
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Analytic wall closure:

\[
\mathrm{AlgebraMarkov}_{3,\mathrm{EF}}
+
\mathrm{RankTwoCoercivity}
+
\mathrm{Gate1}.
\]

The first is the algebraic bottleneck. The second is now a concrete finite-dimensional Markov/completion theorem, not a moving-wall problem.

## Agent 6 continued attack — make \(\mathrm{AlgebraMarkov}_{3,\mathrm{EF}}\) conditional on the same finite model non-absorption machinery

The rank-two route now has two core certificates:

\[
(I-P_{D_{2Y}})A_{2Y}=0,\qquad
\lambda_{\min}\!\left(L_{2Y}^*(I-P_{D_{2Y}})L_{2Y}\right)\ge Q^{-A},
\]

and

\[
\|\Gamma_G\|_{H^2(I)}
\le Q^C\|\Gamma_G\|_{L^2(I^*)}.
\]

The latest synthesis correctly treats the first as the local typed exactness certificate and the second as the analytic bridge from the honest D4 wall \(\{\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\}\) to RankTwoCoercivity. 

I now think the Markov theorem should be formulated using the same finite-model non-absorption infrastructure as O2/O3 and amplitude Schur.

---

# 1. The exact Markov object

Let \(\mathcal V_I\) be the finite local source chart on an enlarged interval \(I^*\). Define

\[
\mathcal W_I
=
D^2\mathcal V_I+\mathcal V_I+\operatorname{Sym}^3\mathcal V_I.
\]

Then

\[
\Gamma_G(q_X)\in \mathcal W_I
\]

for the finite source approximation \(q_X\).

The needed estimate is

\[
\boxed{
\|w\|_{H^2(I)}
\le Q^C\|w\|_{L^2(I^*)}
\qquad(w\in\mathcal W_I).
}
\]

This is not an estimate about \(q\). It is a **Riesz/Markov property of \(\mathcal W_I\)**.

So the theorem should be stated as:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{RieszMarkov}(\mathcal W_I,I,I^*).
}
\]

---

# 2. Finite-dimensional proof template

Choose a basis

\[
\psi_1,\ldots,\psi_M
\]

for \(\mathcal W_I\), with

\[
M\le Q^C.
\]

Let

\[
G_0=(\langle \psi_i,\psi_j\rangle_{L^2(I^*)})_{ij},
\]

\[
G_2=(\langle \psi_i,\psi_j\rangle_{H^2(I)})_{ij}.
\]

Then

\[
\|w\|_{H^2(I)}^2\le Q^C\|w\|_{L^2(I^*)}^2
\]

for all \(w\in\mathcal W_I\) is equivalent to the generalized eigenvalue bound

\[
\boxed{
G_2\preceq Q^C G_0.
}
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\boxed{
\lambda_{\max}(G_0^{-1/2}G_2G_0^{-1/2})\le Q^C.
}
\]

Thus \(\mathrm{AlgebraMarkov}_{3,\mathrm{EF}}\) is a finite Gram/Riesz condition. This is structurally the same kind of finite Schur/Riesz test already appearing in the live-pair and O2/O3 blocks.

---

# 3. How to build \(\mathcal W_I\) without exploding dimension

If

\[
\dim \mathcal V_I=N,
\]

then

\[
\dim \operatorname{Sym}^3\mathcal V_I
=
\binom{N+2}{3}.
\]

If \(N=O(1)\), this is harmless. If \(N\le Q^C\), it remains polynomial.

But for proof cleanliness, we do not need the full symmetric cube if \(q_X\) is a **single source function**, not an arbitrary element of \(\mathcal V_I\). There are two choices:

## Option A — full algebra closure

Use

\[
\mathcal W_I=D^2\mathcal V_I+\mathcal V_I+\operatorname{Sym}^3\mathcal V_I.
\]

This is robust and uniform over all \(q_X\in\mathcal V_I\).

## Option B — tangent algebra around the actual source

Use the smaller affine/tangent span generated by the actual finite packet:

\[
\mathcal W_I^{\actual}
=
\operatorname{span}\{
q_X'',q_X,q_X^3
\}
+
\text{first few parameter derivatives if needed}.
\]

This is lower-dimensional but less stable under perturbation.

For rank-two wall closure, Option A is safer. It gives a uniform theorem and avoids repeatedly tracking the specific finite packet.

---

# 4. Residual-controlled version

Let

\[
q=q_X+r.
\]

Then

\[
\Gamma_G(q)
=
\Gamma_G(q_X)
+
R_{\Gamma_G}.
\]

A useful explicit expansion is

\[
R_{\Gamma_G}
=
-6r''
+36r
-12\left((q_X+r)^3-q_X^3\right).
\]

That is

\[
R_{\Gamma_G}
=
-6r''
+36r
-36q_X^2r
-36q_Xr^2
-12r^3.
\]

So the residual theorem needed is:

\[
\boxed{
\|R_{\Gamma_G}\|_{H^2(I)}
\le Q^{-B}.
}
\]

Sufficient conditions:

\[
\|r\|_{H^4(I)}\le Q^{-B'},
\]

with polynomial \(H^4\)-bounds on \(q_X\), or a tailored nonlinear residual estimate for the cubic terms.

This is still much narrower than full singular \(K_9\)-completion.

---

# 5. Avoid a hidden circularity

There is a possible circularity:

- Gate–1 controls \(q''\) energy.
- AlgebraMarkov needs \(q\) in a finite chart with \(H^4\)-type control.
- If the finite chart is chosen using \(q''\)-energy localization after extraction, it may become wall-dependent.

So the finite chart \(\mathcal V_I\) and enlarged block \(I^*\) must be fixed by the routed atlas before wall extraction.

The correct order is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{choose routed chart }I\subset I^*
\to
\text{define }\mathcal V_I,\mathcal W_I
\to
\text{prove RieszMarkov}
\to
\text{use wall/Gate--1 extraction}.
}
\]

No chart may be chosen to make \(\Gamma_G\) small.

This is the same no-atom-fitting principle reappearing analytically.

---

# 6. RankTwoCoercivity after Markov

Assume

\[
\|\Gamma_G(q)\|_{L^2(I^*)}
+
\|\Gamma_{G,2}(q)\|_{L^2(I)}
\le \delta.
\]

By residual-corrected AlgebraMarkov,

\[
\|\Gamma_G(q)\|_{H^2(I)}
\le Q^C\delta+Q^{-B}.
\]

Then RankTwoCoercivity gives

\[
\|q''\|_{L^2(I)}
\le
Q^C
(\delta+Q^{-B})^\theta.
\]

Gate–1 gives

\[
\|q''\|_{L^2(I)}\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Thus, choosing \(B\) large enough,

\[
\delta\ge Q^{-A'}.
\]

So the honest D4 wall has block mass:

\[
\boxed{
\|\Gamma_G\|_{L^2(I^*)}
+
\|\Gamma_{G,2}\|_{L^2(I)}
\ge Q^{-A'}.
}
\]

This is the final wall lower needed for the finite-family scalar O2/O3 selection.

---

# 7. What if \(\mathrm{AlgebraMarkov}_{3,\mathrm{EF}}\) fails?

Failure means one of the following:

## 7.1 \(\mathcal W_I\) has high dimension

Then the routed chart is too large. Need a smaller local chart or multiscale split.

## 7.2 \(\mathcal W_I\) has high leverage

Then there are near-localized algebra functions in the source chart. This is a finite-model non-absorption failure and should be handled like O2/O3 leverage failure.

## 7.3 Residual lacks \(H^4\) control

Then the finite source approximation is insufficient for rank-two wall closure. Need a stronger completion estimate for \(\Gamma_G\), or use a different wall.

## 7.4 \(I^*\) crosses singular/transition boundaries

Then the chart routing is wrong. Split blocks or enlarge the transition atlas.

None of these is the rank-one moving-wall problem. They are finite atlas/regularity failures.

---

# 8. Integration with finite wall family

Once wall mass is known for

\[
\{\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\},
\]

the finite-family reduction selects

\[
G_\star=\Gamma_G
\quad\text{or}\quad
G_\star=\Gamma_{G,2}
\]

at smooth aggregate level.

Then scalar O2/O3 uses

\[
N_{\sm}|H|^2|G_\star|^2.
\]

Thus the O2/O3 bandwidth is

\[
d_0^{(2)}
=
d_N+2d_H+2\max(d_{\Gamma_G},d_{\Gamma_{G,2}}).
\]

No jet derivative component enters O2/O3 unless a derivative D4 channel theorem is added later.

---

# 9. Current clean theorem stack

The rank-two route now has this clean stack:

## Local exactness

\[
(I-P_{D_{2Y}})A_{2Y}=0,
\]

\[
\lambda_{\min}
(L_{2Y}^*(I-P_{D_{2Y}})L_{2Y})
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Conclusion:

\[
B_Y=0.
\]

## D4 vector wall

\[
F_{2Y}\asymp H(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}).
\]

## AlgebraMarkov

\[
\Gamma_G\in \mathcal W_I,
\quad
\mathcal W_I\text{ has polynomial Riesz/Markov constants}.
\]

## RankTwoCoercivity

\[
(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})\text{ small}
\Rightarrow q''\text{ small}.
\]

## Gate–1

\[
q''\text{ not small}.
\]

Conclusion:

\[
(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})\text{ has wall mass}.
\]

## FiniteWallFamily O2/O3

Select scalar component and run O2/O3.

---

# 10. Current next best attack

The next proof target should be:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{RieszMarkov}(\mathcal W_I,I,I^*):
G_2\preceq Q^C G_0.
}
\]

This is a finite matrix/Riesz theorem for

\[
\mathcal W_I=D^2\mathcal V_I+\mathcal V_I+\operatorname{Sym}^3\mathcal V_I.
\]

It is now the analytic analogue of the local exactness Schur certificate. Both are finite Gram statements.

## Agent 6 continued attack — reducing \(\mathrm{RieszMarkov}(\mathcal W_I)\) to Christoffel/leverage bounds

The rank-two route now has two finite Gram statements:

\[
(I-P_{D_{2Y}})A_{2Y}=0,\qquad
\lambda_{\min}\!\left(L_{2Y}^*(I-P_{D_{2Y}})L_{2Y}\right)\ge Q^{-A},
\]

for local exactness, and

\[
G_2\preceq Q^C G_0
\]

for analytic Markov control of

\[
\mathcal W_I=D^2\mathcal V_I+\mathcal V_I+\operatorname{Sym}^3\mathcal V_I.
\]

The second is now the analytic bottleneck for the plain rank-two wall, because D4 supplies only

\[
(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}),
\]

not derivative wall channels. 

---

# 1. Matrix form of \(\mathrm{RieszMarkov}\)

Let

\[
\psi_1,\ldots,\psi_M
\]

be a basis of

\[
\mathcal W_I.
\]

Define Gram matrices

\[
G_0=
\left(
\langle \psi_i,\psi_j\rangle_{L^2(I^*)}
\right)_{i,j},
\]

and

\[
G_2=
\left(
\langle \psi_i,\psi_j\rangle_{H^2(I)}
\right)_{i,j}.
\]

Then

\[
\|w\|_{H^2(I)}^2\le Q^C\|w\|_{L^2(I^*)}^2
\quad(w\in\mathcal W_I)
\]

is equivalent to

\[
\boxed{
G_2\preceq Q^C G_0.
}
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\boxed{
\lambda_{\max}
\left(
G_0^{-1/2}G_2G_0^{-1/2}
\right)
\le Q^C.
}
\]

So \(\mathrm{AlgebraMarkov}_{3,\mathrm{EF}}\) is not mysterious. It is a finite generalized eigenvalue bound.

---

# 2. Leverage formulation

Let

\[
K_{\mathcal W}(x,x)
\]

be the Christoffel/leverage kernel for \(\mathcal W_I\) relative to \(L^2(I^*)\):

\[
K_{\mathcal W}(x,x)
=
\Psi(x)^*G_0^{-1}\Psi(x),
\]

where

\[
\Psi(x)=(\psi_1(x),\ldots,\psi_M(x))^T.
\]

For derivatives, define

\[
K_{\mathcal W}^{(j)}(x,x)
=
D^j\Psi(x)^*G_0^{-1}D^j\Psi(x).
\]

Then

\[
G_2\preceq Q^C G_0
\]

follows if

\[
\boxed{
\int_I
\left(
K_{\mathcal W}^{(0)}(x,x)+K_{\mathcal W}^{(1)}(x,x)+K_{\mathcal W}^{(2)}(x,x)
\right)\,dx
\le Q^C.
}
\]

A pointwise sufficient bound is

\[
\boxed{
K_{\mathcal W}^{(j)}(x,x)\le Q^C|I|^{-1-2j}
\qquad (j=0,1,2).
}
\]

This is the leverage form of \(\mathrm{RieszMarkov}\).

So the analytic Markov problem is the same kind of problem as O2/O3 Christoffel spread:

\[
\boxed{
\text{finite model space + bounded leverage.}
}
\]

---

# 3. Why \(\mathcal W_I\) should have bounded leverage

If \(\mathcal V_I\) is the local EF/AFE chart with polynomial Christoffel spread, then products of a fixed number of functions from \(\mathcal V_I\) should still have polynomial leverage after enlarging the block from \(I\) to \(I^*\).

The important point is the block enlargement. A function in \(\mathcal W_I\) could concentrate near the boundary of \(I\), but if its norm is measured on \(I^*\), standard Remez/Markov behavior prevents uncontrolled derivative blow-up on \(I\).

Thus the correct theorem is not

\[
\|w\|_{H^2(I)}\le Q^C\|w\|_{L^2(I)}.
\]

It is

\[
\boxed{
\|w\|_{H^2(I)}\le Q^C\|w\|_{L^2(I^*)}.
}
\]

That enlargement is essential.

---

# 4. Product-space risk

The only real danger is that

\[
\operatorname{Sym}^3\mathcal V_I
\]

has high leverage even when

\[
\mathcal V_I
\]

does not.

This can happen if products generate near-spikes. So the theorem should include a product-leverage assumption:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{ProductLeverage}_{3}(\mathcal V_I):
\quad
\operatorname{Sym}^3\mathcal V_I
\text{ has polynomial Christoffel/leverage on }I\subset I^*.
}
\]

If this fails, the rank-two plain wall may fail even though the source chart itself is controlled.

But this failure is itself meaningful: it says the cubic algebra generated by the local source has near-local interpolation power. That would also threaten other finite-model Schur steps. It is a general finite-model pathology, not a D4-specific problem.

---

# 5. Alternative: use the actual cubic line instead of full \(\operatorname{Sym}^3\mathcal V_I\)

The full symmetric cube is robust but possibly over-large.

For the actual source,

\[
q^3
\]

is one function, not an arbitrary cubic combination of chart elements. So a smaller space is:

\[
\mathcal W_I^{\mathrm{act}}
=
\operatorname{span}\{q_X'',q_X,q_X^3\}
+
\operatorname{span}\{\text{allowed finite variations if needed}\}.
\]

Then

\[
\Gamma_G(q_X)\in \mathcal W_I^{\mathrm{act}},
\]

and the Markov estimate is easier.

But this smaller space may depend on the actual source and could become less stable under residual/completion perturbations.

Thus:

- use \(\mathcal W_I\) for a uniform theorem;
- use \(\mathcal W_I^{\mathrm{act}}\) as fallback if \(\operatorname{Sym}^3\mathcal V_I\) has poor leverage.

The fallback still suffices for \(\Gamma_G\), because we only need Markov for that actual wall function.

---

# 6. Residual-compatible Markov

Suppose

\[
\Gamma_G(q)=w+r_\Gamma,
\qquad
w\in\mathcal W_I,
\]

with

\[
\|r_\Gamma\|_{H^2(I)}\le Q^{-B}.
\]

Then

\[
\|\Gamma_G(q)\|_{H^2(I)}
\le
Q^C\|w\|_{L^2(I^*)}+Q^{-B}.
\]

But

\[
\|w\|_{L^2(I^*)}
\le
\|\Gamma_G(q)\|_{L^2(I^*)}
+
\|r_\Gamma\|_{L^2(I^*)}.
\]

So

\[
\boxed{
\|\Gamma_G(q)\|_{H^2(I)}
\le
Q^C\|\Gamma_G(q)\|_{L^2(I^*)}
+
Q^{-B+C}.
}
\]

This is the residual-corrected LocalMarkov theorem needed for rank two.

Thus the completion requirement is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{\Gamma_G}^{H^2}:
\quad
\operatorname{dist}_{H^2(I)}(\Gamma_G(q),\mathcal W_I)\le Q^{-B}.
}
\]

This is narrower than full singular completion.

---

# 7. Coercivity closure with residual

Let

\[
\delta
=
\|\Gamma_G(q)\|_{L^2(I^*)}
+
\|\Gamma_{G,2}(q)\|_{L^2(I)}.
\]

Residual-corrected LocalMarkov gives

\[
\|\Gamma_G(q)\|_{H^2(I)}
\le
Q^C\delta+Q^{-B}.
\]

RankTwoCoercivity gives

\[
\|q''\|_{L^2(I)}
\le
Q^C(\delta+Q^{-B})^\theta.
\]

Gate–1 gives

\[
\|q''\|_{L^2(I)}\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Hence

\[
\delta\ge Q^{-A'}
\]

if \(B\) is chosen large enough. This yields wall mass for the honest D4 wall family

\[
\{\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\}.
\]

---

# 8. Relation to O2/O3 finite model nonabsorption

The same leverage objects appear in three places:

1. \(\mathcal W_I\) for LocalMarkov;
2. \(\mathcal C_{2Y}^{\arith}\) for vector amplitude Schur;
3. \(\operatorname{span}\{R_{\fold}^k\}\) for O2/O3 near-pure separation.

So a single meta-theorem should be developed:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{FiniteModelRieszControl}.
}
\]

Inputs:

- finite model space \(\mathcal M\),
- fixed pre-extraction measure,
- enlarged block if derivatives are used,
- bounded leverage/Christoffel kernel,
- bounded dimension.

Outputs:

- Markov/Riesz inequalities,
- nonabsorption Schur estimates,
- stable projections.

This would unify the analytic control layer.

---

# 9. Updated rank-two analytic theorem stack

The analytic block should be:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{FiniteModelRieszControl}(\mathcal W_I)
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{\Gamma_G}^{H^2}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{RankTwoCoercivity}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{Gate1}
}
\]

Together they imply the rank-two wall lower.

No derivative D4 channels are needed.

No jet O2/O3 wall is needed.

---

# 10. Current conclusion

The rank-two analytic path is now reduced to a finite-model leverage theorem for

\[
\mathcal W_I=D^2\mathcal V_I+\mathcal V_I+\operatorname{Sym}^3\mathcal V_I
\]

or a smaller actual-source version of it.

This is the analytic analogue of the local exactness certificate:

\[
(I-P_{D_{2Y}})A_{2Y}=0,
\qquad
L_{2Y}^*(I-P_{D_{2Y}})L_{2Y}\gg Q^{-A}.
\]

Both are finite Gram/Riesz statements.

So the rank-two route now has a coherent finite-linear-algebra flavor throughout:

\[
\boxed{
\text{local exactness Schur}
+
\text{analytic Riesz/Markov}
+
\text{O2/O3 finite-model nonabsorption}.
}
\]
